zec.13.text: On that day a fountain shall be opened for the house of David and for the inhabitants of Jerusalem — for sin and for impurity.

"And on that day" — the word of YHWH of hosts — "I will cut off the names of the idols from the land, and they shall be remembered no more; and the prophets too, and the spirit of uncleanness, I will remove from the land.

And if a man still prophesies, his father and his mother who bore him shall say to him, 'You shall not live, for you have spoken falsehood in the name of YHWH' — and his father and his mother who bore him shall pierce him through when he prophesies.
And on that day the prophets shall each be ashamed of his vision when he prophesies; they shall not put on the hairy mantle in order to deceive,
but each shall say, 'I am no prophet — I am a tiller of the soil, for a man sold me in my youth.'
And if one says to him, 'What are these wounds between your hands?' he shall say, 'The wounds I received in the house of my friends.'"

Awake, O sword, against my shepherd, against the man who stands next to me — the word of YHWH of hosts. Strike the shepherd, that the sheep may be scattered — and I will turn my hand against the little ones.
"And in all the land" — the word of YHWH — "two thirds in it shall be cut off and perish, and one third shall be left in it.

And I will bring the third through the fire; I will refine them as silver is refined, and test them as gold is tested. They shall call on my name, and I will answer them. I will say, 'They are my people' — and they shall say, 'YHWH is my God.'"

zec.14.text: Behold, a day is coming for YHWH, when your spoil shall be divided in your midst.

And I will gather all the nations to Jerusalem for battle: the city shall be taken, the houses plundered, and the women violated. Half the city shall go out into exile — but the rest of the people shall not be cut off from the city.
Then YHWH shall go out and fight against those nations, as on the day he fights, on the day of battle.

And his feet shall stand on that day on the Mount of Olives, which faces Jerusalem on the east; and the Mount of Olives shall split in half, from east to west, into a very great valley — half the mountain shall move northward, and half southward.

And you shall flee by the valley of my mountains, for the valley of the mountains shall reach to Azal; and you shall flee as you fled from the earthquake in the days of Uzziah king of Judah. Then YHWH my God shall come — all the holy ones with you.
And on that day there shall be no light — the glorious ones shall congeal.
And there shall be one day — it is known to YHWH — not day and not night; and at evening time there shall be light.
And on that day living waters shall go out from Jerusalem, half to the eastern sea and half to the western sea — in summer and in winter it shall be.
And YHWH shall be king over all the earth; on that day YHWH shall be one, and his name one.

All the land shall turn into a plain, from Geba to Rimmon south of Jerusalem. But she shall rise high and dwell in her own place, from the Benjamin Gate to the place of the First Gate, to the Corner Gate, and from the Tower of Hananel to the king's winepresses.
And they shall dwell in her, and there shall be no more devotion-to-destruction — Jerusalem shall dwell secure.

And this shall be the plague with which YHWH strikes all the peoples that warred against Jerusalem: their flesh rotting while they stand on their feet, their eyes rotting in their sockets, and their tongue rotting in their mouth.
And on that day a great panic from YHWH shall be among them: each shall seize the hand of his neighbor, and his hand shall rise against the hand of his neighbor.
And Judah too shall fight at Jerusalem; and the wealth of all the nations around shall be gathered — gold and silver and garments in great abundance.
And so shall be the plague of the horse, the mule, the camel, and the donkey — every beast in those camps — like this plague.
And everyone left of all the nations that came against Jerusalem shall go up year by year to bow down to the King, YHWH of hosts, and to keep the Feast of Booths.
And whichever of the families of the earth does not go up to Jerusalem to bow down to the King, YHWH of hosts — upon them there shall be no rain.
And if the family of Egypt does not go up and does not come, upon them shall be the plague with which YHWH strikes the nations that do not go up to keep the Feast of Booths.
This shall be the punishment of Egypt, and the punishment of all the nations that do not go up to keep the Feast of Booths.
On that day there shall be upon the bells of the horses: HOLY TO YHWH. And the pots in the house of YHWH shall be like the bowls before the altar.

And every pot in Jerusalem and in Judah shall be holy to YHWH of hosts, and all who sacrifice shall come and take of them and boil in them. And there shall no longer be a trader in the house of YHWH of hosts on that day.

est.2.text: After these things, when the wrath of King Ahasuerus had subsided, he remembered Vashti — what she had done, and what had been decreed against her.
And the king's attendants who served him said: "Let beautiful young virgins be sought for the king.

And let the king appoint officers in all the provinces of his kingdom to gather every beautiful young virgin to Susa the citadel, to the house of the women, into the custody of Hegai the king's eunuch, keeper of the women — and let their cosmetics be provided.
And let the young woman who pleases the king reign in Vashti's place." The proposal pleased the king, and he did so.
There was a Jewish man in Susa the citadel whose name was Mordecai son of Jair, son of Shimei, son of Kish, a Benjaminite,
who had been carried into exile from Jerusalem with the exiles carried away with Jeconiah king of Judah, whom Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had exiled.

And he was foster father to Hadassah — that is, Esther — his uncle's daughter, for she had neither father nor mother. The young woman was lovely in form and beautiful to look upon; and when her father and mother died, Mordecai took her as his own daughter.

And when the king's word and his decree were proclaimed, and many young women were gathered to Susa the citadel into the custody of Hegai, Esther too was taken into the king's house, into the custody of Hegai, keeper of the women.

And the young woman pleased him and won his kindness. He quickly provided her cosmetics and her portions, and the seven chosen maids from the king's house — and he advanced her and her maids to the best place in the house of the women.
Esther had not made known her people or her kindred, for Mordecai had charged her not to tell.
And every single day Mordecai would walk about in front of the courtyard of the house of the women, to learn how Esther fared and what was being done with her.

Now when each young woman's turn came to go in to King Ahasuerus — after being twelve months under the regulations for the women, for so were the days of their beautifying completed: six months with oil of myrrh, and six months with perfumes and the cosmetics of women —
then in this way the young woman would go in to the king: whatever she asked for was given her to take with her from the house of the women to the king's house.

In the evening she would go in, and in the morning she would return to the second house of the women, into the custody of Shaashgaz, the king's eunuch, keeper of the concubines. She would not go in to the king again unless the king delighted in her and she was summoned by name.

And when the turn came for Esther — the daughter of Abihail, the uncle of Mordecai, who had taken her as his own daughter — to go in to the king, she asked for nothing except what Hegai the king's eunuch, keeper of the women, advised. And Esther won favor in the eyes of all who saw her.
And Esther was taken to King Ahasuerus, into his royal house, in the tenth month — the month of Tebeth — in the seventh year of his reign.
And the king loved Esther more than all the women; she won grace and kindness before him more than all the virgins. And he set the royal crown on her head and made her queen in place of Vashti.
And the king made a great feast for all his princes and servants — Esther's feast; and he granted a remission of taxes to the provinces, and gave gifts with royal liberality.
And when the virgins were gathered a second time, Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate.
Esther still had not made known her kindred or her people, as Mordecai had charged her; for Esther did what Mordecai said, just as when she was under his care.
In those days, while Mordecai was sitting at the king's gate, Bigthan and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs among the keepers of the threshold, grew furious and sought to lay hands on King Ahasuerus.
And the matter became known to Mordecai, and he told Queen Esther; and Esther told the king in Mordecai's name.
The matter was investigated and found to be so, and the two of them were hanged on a gallows-tree; and it was written in the book of the chronicles in the king's presence.

est.3.text: After these things, King Ahasuerus promoted Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite and advanced him, and set his seat above all the princes who were with him.
And all the king's servants at the king's gate would bow and prostrate themselves to Haman, for so the king had commanded concerning him. But Mordecai would not bow, and would not prostrate himself.
And the king's servants at the king's gate said to Mordecai, "Why do you transgress the king's command?"
And when they had spoken to him day after day and he would not listen to them, they told Haman, to see whether Mordecai's words would stand — for he had told them that he was a Jew.
And when Haman saw that Mordecai would not bow or prostrate himself to him, Haman was filled with fury.

But it was contemptible in his eyes to lay hands on Mordecai alone — for they had told him Mordecai's people — so Haman sought to destroy all the Jews throughout the whole kingdom of Ahasuerus: the people of Mordecai.

In the first month — the month of Nisan — in the twelfth year of King Ahasuerus, they cast pur (that is, the lot) before Haman, from day to day and from month to month, to the twelfth month — the month of Adar.

And Haman said to King Ahasuerus: "There is a certain people, scattered and dispersed among the peoples in all the provinces of your kingdom. Their laws differ from every other people's, and the king's laws they do not keep — and it does not profit the king to let them be.
If it please the king, let it be written that they be destroyed — and I will weigh out ten thousand talents of silver into the hands of those who do the work, to bring into the king's treasuries."
And the king removed his signet ring from his hand and gave it to Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, the enemy of the Jews.
And the king said to Haman: "The silver is given to you — and the people, to do with as seems good in your eyes."

Then the king's scribes were summoned, on the thirteenth day of the first month, and it was written according to all Haman commanded — to the king's satraps, and to the governors over each province, and to the princes of each people, to every province in its own script and every people in its own tongue. In the name of King Ahasuerus it was written, and sealed with the king's signet.

And letters were sent by couriers to all the king's provinces: to destroy, to slay, and to annihilate all the Jews — young and old, children and women — in one day, the thirteenth of the twelfth month, the month of Adar, and to plunder their spoil.
A copy of the writing was to be issued as law in every province, published to all the peoples, that they should be ready for that day.
The couriers went out in haste at the king's word, and the decree was issued in Susa the citadel. And the king and Haman sat down to drink — but the city of Susa was thrown into confusion.

est.4.text: And Mordecai learned all that had been done; and Mordecai tore his garments and put on sackcloth and ashes, and went out into the midst of the city, and cried a great and bitter cry.
And he came as far as the front of the king's gate — for none might enter the king's gate clothed in sackcloth.

And in every single province, wherever the king's word and his decree reached, there was great mourning among the Jews, with fasting and weeping and lamentation; sackcloth and ashes were spread out for the many.

And Esther's maids and her eunuchs came and told her, and the queen writhed in great anguish. She sent garments to clothe Mordecai, that he might take off his sackcloth — but he would not accept them.
Then Esther called for Hathach, one of the king's eunuchs whom he had appointed to attend her, and charged him concerning Mordecai — to learn what this was, and why it was.
So Hathach went out to Mordecai, to the city square in front of the king's gate.
And Mordecai told him all that had happened to him, and the exact sum of silver Haman had promised to weigh into the king's treasuries for the destruction of the Jews.

He also gave him a copy of the written decree issued in Susa for their destruction, to show Esther and to tell her — and to charge her to go in to the king, to plead with him and entreat him for her people.
And Hathach came and told Esther the words of Mordecai.
Then Esther spoke to Hathach and charged him to say to Mordecai:

"All the king's servants and the people of the king's provinces know that for any man or woman who comes to the king, into the inner court, without being summoned, there is one law: to be put to death — unless the king holds out to them the golden scepter, that they may live. And I have not been summoned to come to the king these thirty days."
And they told Mordecai Esther's words.
And Mordecai said to answer Esther: "Do not imagine in your soul that you, of all the Jews, will escape because you are in the king's house.

For if you keep utterly silent at this time, relief and deliverance will arise for the Jews from another place — but you and your father's house will perish. And who knows whether it was not for a time like this that you came to the kingdom?"
And Esther said to answer Mordecai:

"Go, gather all the Jews found in Susa, and fast for me. Do not eat and do not drink for three days, night and day; I also, with my maids, will fast the same. And then I will go in to the king, though it is against the law — and if I perish, I perish."
And Mordecai went his way, and did everything Esther had charged him.

est.5.text: And on the third day, Esther clothed herself in royalty and stood in the inner court of the king's house, facing the king's house; and the king was sitting on his royal throne in the royal house, facing the entrance of the house.

And when the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, she won grace in his eyes; and the king held out to Esther the golden scepter that was in his hand. And Esther drew near and touched the tip of the scepter.
And the king said to her: "What is it, Queen Esther? And what is your request? Up to half the kingdom — it shall be given you."
And Esther said: "If it please the king, let the king and Haman come today to the feast I have prepared for him."
And the king said, "Bring Haman quickly, to do Esther's word." So the king and Haman came to the feast Esther had prepared.
And the king said to Esther at the wine-feast: "What is your petition? It shall be granted you. And what is your request? Up to half the kingdom — it shall be done."
And Esther answered and said: "My petition and my request —

if I have found grace in the king's eyes, and if it please the king to grant my petition and to do my request: let the king and Haman come to the feast I will prepare for them, and tomorrow I will do according to the king's word."

And Haman went out that day joyful and glad of heart. But when Haman saw Mordecai at the king's gate — and he did not rise and did not tremble before him — Haman was filled with fury against Mordecai.
But Haman restrained himself and went home; and he sent and brought his friends and Zeresh his wife.
And Haman recounted to them the glory of his wealth and his many sons, and everything by which the king had promoted him and advanced him above the princes and the king's servants.
And Haman said: "What is more, Queen Esther brought no one with the king to the feast she prepared but me — and tomorrow too I am invited by her, with the king.
Yet all this is worth nothing to me, every moment I see Mordecai the Jew sitting at the king's gate."

Then Zeresh his wife and all his friends said to him: "Have a gallows-tree made, fifty cubits high, and in the morning speak to the king, and let Mordecai be hanged on it — then go with the king to the feast rejoicing." The proposal pleased Haman, and he had the gallows-tree made.

est.6.text: That night the king's sleep fled. And he ordered the book of records, the chronicles, to be brought — and they were read before the king.
And it was found written how Mordecai had informed on Bigthana and Teresh, two of the king's eunuchs among the keepers of the threshold, who had sought to lay hands on King Ahasuerus.
And the king said, "What honor and greatness has been done for Mordecai for this?" And the king's attendants who served him said, "Nothing has been done for him."
And the king said, "Who is in the court?" Now Haman had come into the outer court of the king's house, to speak to the king about hanging Mordecai on the gallows-tree he had prepared for him.
And the king's attendants said to him, "Behold, Haman is standing in the court." And the king said, "Let him come in."
So Haman came in. And the king said to him, "What should be done for the man whom the king delights to honor?" And Haman said in his heart, "Whom would the king delight to honor more than me?"
So Haman said to the king: "For the man whom the king delights to honor,
let them bring royal robes the king has worn, and a horse the king has ridden — one with a royal crown set on its head;

and let the robes and the horse be handed to one of the king's most noble princes. Let them robe the man whom the king delights to honor, and lead him on the horse through the city square, proclaiming before him: 'Thus shall it be done for the man whom the king delights to honor!'"
Then the king said to Haman: "Hurry — take the robes and the horse, as you have said, and do exactly so for Mordecai the Jew, who sits at the king's gate. Let nothing fail of all you have spoken."

So Haman took the robes and the horse, and robed Mordecai, and led him on horseback through the city square, proclaiming before him: "Thus shall it be done for the man whom the king delights to honor!"
Then Mordecai returned to the king's gate — but Haman hurried home, mourning, with his head covered.

And Haman recounted to Zeresh his wife and all his friends everything that had happened to him. And his wise men and Zeresh his wife said to him: "If Mordecai, before whom you have begun to fall, is of Jewish descent, you will not prevail against him — you will surely fall before him."
While they were still speaking with him, the king's eunuchs arrived, and hurried to bring Haman to the feast Esther had prepared.

est.7.text: So the king and Haman came to drink with Queen Esther.

And the king said to Esther again on the second day, at the wine-feast: "What is your petition, Queen Esther? It shall be granted you. And what is your request? Up to half the kingdom — it shall be done."
And Queen Esther answered and said: "If I have found grace in your eyes, O king, and if it please the king — let my life be given me as my petition, and my people as my request.

For we have been sold, I and my people, to be destroyed, slain, and annihilated. Had we been sold merely as male and female slaves, I would have kept silent — for such distress would not be worth the king's loss."
And King Ahasuerus spoke, and said to Queen Esther: "Who is he, and where is he — the one whose heart has filled him to do this?"
And Esther said: "An adversary and an enemy — this evil Haman!" And Haman was terrified before the king and the queen.
And the king rose in his fury from the wine-feast and went into the palace garden — while Haman stood to beg Queen Esther for his life, for he saw that evil was determined against him by the king.

And the king returned from the palace garden to the house of the wine-feast — and Haman had fallen upon the couch where Esther was. And the king said, "Would he even ravish the queen, with me in the house?" The word left the king's mouth — and they covered Haman's face.

And Harbona, one of the eunuchs attending the king, said: "What is more — behold, the gallows-tree that Haman made for Mordecai, whose word saved the king, stands at Haman's house, fifty cubits high." And the king said, "Hang him on it."
So they hanged Haman on the gallows-tree he had prepared for Mordecai — and the king's fury subsided.

est.8.text: That day King Ahasuerus gave Queen Esther the house of Haman, the enemy of the Jews. And Mordecai came before the king, for Esther had told what he was to her.
And the king removed his signet ring, which he had taken back from Haman, and gave it to Mordecai. And Esther set Mordecai over the house of Haman.
And Esther spoke again before the king. She fell at his feet and wept, and pleaded with him to avert the evil of Haman the Agagite and the scheme he had devised against the Jews.
And the king held out the golden scepter to Esther; and Esther rose and stood before the king.

And she said: "If it please the king, and if I have found grace before him, and the thing seems right before the king, and I am good in his eyes — let it be written to revoke the letters, the scheme of Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, which he wrote to destroy the Jews in all the king's provinces.
For how can I bear to see the evil that will find my people? And how can I bear to see the destruction of my kindred?"

And King Ahasuerus said to Queen Esther and to Mordecai the Jew: "Behold, I have given Haman's house to Esther, and him they have hanged on the gallows-tree, because he laid his hand against the Jews.

Now you — write concerning the Jews as seems good in your eyes, in the king's name, and seal it with the king's signet. For a writing written in the king's name and sealed with the king's signet cannot be revoked."

Then the king's scribes were summoned at that time, in the third month — the month of Sivan — on the twenty-third day; and it was written according to all Mordecai commanded, to the Jews and to the satraps and governors and princes of the provinces from India to Cush, a hundred and twenty-seven provinces — every province in its own script and every people in its own tongue, and to the Jews in their own script and their own tongue.
He wrote in the name of King Ahasuerus and sealed it with the king's signet, and sent letters by mounted couriers riding swift royal steeds bred of the mares:

that the king had granted the Jews in every single city to assemble and to stand for their lives — to destroy, slay, and annihilate any armed force of any people or province that might attack them, with children and women, and to plunder their spoil —
on one day in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus: the thirteenth of the twelfth month, the month of Adar.
A copy of the writing was to be issued as law in every single province, published to all the peoples — and the Jews were to be ready for that day, to take vengeance on their enemies.
The couriers, riding the swift royal steeds, went out urgently, pressed on by the king's word; and the decree was issued in Susa the citadel.
And Mordecai went out from the king's presence in royal robes of blue and white, with a great crown of gold and a mantle of fine linen and purple — and the city of Susa shouted and rejoiced.
For the Jews there was light and joy, gladness and honor.

And in every single province and every single city, wherever the king's word and his decree reached, there was joy and gladness for the Jews, a feast and a holiday. And many of the peoples of the land professed themselves Jews — for the dread of the Jews had fallen upon them.

est.9.text: And in the twelfth month — the month of Adar — on its thirteenth day, when the king's word and his decree were due to be carried out, on the very day the enemies of the Jews had hoped to master them, it was overturned: the Jews themselves mastered those who hated them.

The Jews assembled in their cities in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus, to lay hands on those who sought their harm; and no one could stand against them, for the dread of them had fallen upon all the peoples.
And all the princes of the provinces, the satraps and the governors and those who did the king's business, were supporting the Jews — for the dread of Mordecai had fallen upon them.
For Mordecai was great in the king's house, and his fame went out through all the provinces — for the man Mordecai grew greater and greater.
And the Jews struck all their enemies with the stroke of the sword, with slaughter and destruction; and they did to those who hated them as they pleased.
And in Susa the citadel the Jews slew and destroyed five hundred men,
including Parshandatha and Dalphon and Aspatha,
and Poratha and Adalia and Aridatha,
and Parmashta and Arisai and Aridai and Vaizatha —
the ten sons of Haman son of Hammedatha, the enemy of the Jews, they slew. But on the plunder they did not lay their hand.
That day the number of those slain in Susa the citadel came before the king.

And the king said to Queen Esther: "In Susa the citadel the Jews have slain and destroyed five hundred men, and the ten sons of Haman. What have they done in the rest of the king's provinces? And what is your petition? It shall be granted you. And what is your further request? It shall be done."
And Esther said: "If it please the king, let it be granted to the Jews in Susa tomorrow also to act according to today's decree — and let the ten sons of Haman be hanged on the gallows-tree."
And the king commanded it to be done so: a decree was issued in Susa, and the ten sons of Haman were hanged.
And the Jews in Susa assembled again on the fourteenth day of the month of Adar and slew three hundred men in Susa — but on the plunder they did not lay their hand.

And the rest of the Jews in the king's provinces assembled and stood for their lives, and had rest from their enemies, slaying seventy-five thousand of those who hated them — but on the plunder they did not lay their hand —
on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar; and they rested on its fourteenth, and made it a day of feasting and joy.
But the Jews in Susa assembled on the thirteenth and the fourteenth — and rested on the fifteenth, and made it a day of feasting and joy.
Therefore the Jews of the villages, who dwell in the unwalled towns, keep the fourteenth day of the month of Adar as a day of joy and feasting, a holiday, and of sending portions to one another.
And Mordecai wrote these things down, and sent letters to all the Jews in all the provinces of King Ahasuerus, near and far,
to establish among them that they should keep the fourteenth day of the month of Adar and its fifteenth day, every single year,

as the days on which the Jews had rest from their enemies, and the month that was turned for them from sorrow to joy and from mourning to holiday — to make them days of feasting and joy, of sending portions to one another and gifts to the poor.
And the Jews took upon themselves what they had begun to do, and what Mordecai had written to them.
For Haman son of Hammedatha the Agagite, the enemy of all the Jews, had schemed against the Jews to destroy them, and had cast pur — that is, the lot — to crush them and destroy them.
But when it came before the king, he said with the letter: "Let his evil scheme, which he devised against the Jews, return upon his own head" — and they hanged him and his sons on the gallows-tree.
Therefore they called these days Purim, after the name of the pur. Therefore — because of all the words of this letter, and what they had seen concerning this, and what had come upon them —

the Jews established and took upon themselves, and upon their descendants, and upon all who joined them, never to fail to keep these two days as written and at their appointed time, every single year;

and that these days should be remembered and kept in every generation, in every family, every province, and every city; and that these days of Purim should never pass away from among the Jews, nor their memory perish from their descendants.
And Queen Esther daughter of Abihail, with Mordecai the Jew, wrote with full authority to confirm this second letter of Purim.
And letters were sent to all the Jews, to the hundred and twenty-seven provinces of the kingdom of Ahasuerus — words of peace and truth —

to establish these days of Purim at their appointed times, as Mordecai the Jew and Queen Esther had established for them, and as they had established for themselves and their descendants: the matters of the fasts and their cry.
And the command of Esther confirmed these matters of Purim — and it was written in the book.

est.10.text: And King Ahasuerus laid tribute on the land and on the islands of the sea.

And all the acts of his power and his might, and the full account of the greatness of Mordecai, to which the king advanced him — are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Media and Persia?
For Mordecai the Jew was second to King Ahasuerus, and great among the Jews, and beloved by the multitude of his brothers — seeking the good of his people, and speaking peace to all his descendants.

rut.1.text: And it came to pass, in the days when the judges judged, that there was a famine in the land; and a man from Bethlehem of Judah went to sojourn in the fields of Moab — he and his wife and his two sons.
The man's name was Elimelech, his wife's name Naomi, and the names of his two sons Mahlon and Chilion — Ephrathites from Bethlehem of Judah. And they came to the fields of Moab and remained there.
And Elimelech, Naomi's husband, died; and she was left, with her two sons.
And they took Moabite wives: the name of the one was Orpah, and the name of the second was Ruth. And they dwelt there about ten years.
And the two of them also died — Mahlon and Chilion — and the woman was left, bereft of her two children and of her husband.
Then she arose, she and her daughters-in-law, to return from the fields of Moab; for she had heard in the field of Moab that YHWH had visited his people, to give them bread.
And she went out from the place where she had been, her two daughters-in-law with her, and they set out on the road to return to the land of Judah.
And Naomi said to her two daughters-in-law: "Go, return, each to her mother's house. May YHWH deal kindly with you, as you have dealt with the dead and with me.
May YHWH grant you rest, each in the house of her husband." And she kissed them — and they lifted their voices and wept.
And they said to her, "No — with you we will return to your people."
But Naomi said: "Turn back, my daughters. Why would you go with me? Do I still have sons in my womb, who could become husbands for you?
Turn back, my daughters; go — for I am too old to have a husband. Even if I said there was hope for me, and even were I with a husband tonight, and even bore sons —

would you wait for them until they grew up? Would you shut yourselves off for them, and have no husbands? No, my daughters — it is far more bitter for me than for you, for the hand of YHWH has gone out against me."
And they lifted their voices and wept again. And Orpah kissed her mother-in-law — but Ruth clung to her.
And she said, "Look — your sister-in-law has gone back to her people and to her gods. Turn back after your sister-in-law."
But Ruth said: "Do not press me to leave you, to turn back from following you. For wherever you go, I will go; and wherever you lodge, I will lodge. Your people is my people, and your God is my God.
Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried. So may YHWH do to me, and more also — for only death shall part me from you."
And when she saw that she was determined to go with her, she stopped speaking to her about it.
So the two of them went on until they came to Bethlehem. And when they came to Bethlehem, the whole town stirred over them, and the women said, "Is this Naomi?"
And she said to them: "Do not call me Naomi — Pleasant. Call me Mara — Bitter — for Shaddai has dealt very bitterly with me.
I went away full, and empty has YHWH brought me back. Why call me Naomi, when YHWH has testified against me, and Shaddai has done me evil?"
So Naomi returned, and with her Ruth the Moabite, her daughter-in-law, returning from the fields of Moab. And they came to Bethlehem at the beginning of the barley harvest.

rut.2.text: Now Naomi had a kinsman of her husband's, a man of standing and substance, of the family of Elimelech — and his name was Boaz.
And Ruth the Moabite said to Naomi, "Let me go to the field and glean among the ears of grain behind whoever shows me favor." And she said to her, "Go, my daughter."
So she went, and came and gleaned in the field behind the reapers. And her chance chanced upon the portion of field belonging to Boaz, who was of the family of Elimelech.
And behold, Boaz came from Bethlehem, and said to the reapers, "YHWH be with you!" And they said to him, "YHWH bless you!"
And Boaz said to his young man who was set over the reapers, "Whose is this young woman?"
And the young man set over the reapers answered and said: "She is the Moabite girl who came back with Naomi from the field of Moab.
And she said, 'Let me glean, please, and gather among the sheaves behind the reapers.' So she came, and she has been on her feet from morning until now — she has rested in the shelter only a little."
And Boaz said to Ruth: "Listen, my daughter. Do not go to glean in another field, and do not pass on from this one; stay close by my young women.

Keep your eyes on the field they are reaping and follow after them. Have I not charged the young men not to touch you? And when you are thirsty, go to the vessels and drink from what the young men draw."
And she fell on her face and bowed to the ground, and said to him, "Why have I found favor in your eyes, that you take notice of me — and I a foreigner?"

And Boaz answered and said to her: "It has been fully told me — all you have done for your mother-in-law since the death of your husband: how you left your father and your mother and the land of your birth, and came to a people you did not know before.
May YHWH repay your deed, and may your wages be full from YHWH, the God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge."
And she said, "Let me find favor in your eyes, my lord — for you have comforted me, and have spoken to the heart of your servant, though I am not even as one of your servant girls."

And at mealtime Boaz said to her, "Come here, and eat of the bread, and dip your morsel in the vinegar." So she sat beside the reapers, and he heaped up roasted grain for her; and she ate and was satisfied, and had some left over.
And when she rose to glean, Boaz charged his young men: "Let her glean even among the sheaves — and do not shame her.
And also pull out some for her from the bundles and leave it for her to glean — and do not rebuke her."
So she gleaned in the field until evening; and she beat out what she had gleaned, and it was about an ephah of barley.
And she carried it and came into the town, and her mother-in-law saw what she had gleaned. And she brought out and gave her what she had left over after being satisfied.

And her mother-in-law said to her, "Where did you glean today? Where did you work? Blessed be the one who took notice of you!" And she told her mother-in-law with whom she had worked, and said, "The name of the man I worked with today is Boaz."

And Naomi said to her daughter-in-law: "Blessed be he of YHWH, who has not forsaken his kindness to the living or to the dead." And Naomi said to her, "The man is a close relative of ours — he is one of our redeemers."
And Ruth the Moabite said, "He even said to me, 'Stay close by my young men until they have finished all my harvest.'"
And Naomi said to Ruth her daughter-in-law, "It is good, my daughter, that you go out with his young women — so that no one molests you in another field."
So she stayed close by the young women of Boaz, gleaning until the end of the barley harvest and the wheat harvest. And she lived with her mother-in-law.

rut.3.text: And Naomi her mother-in-law said to her: "My daughter, shall I not seek rest for you, that it may be well with you?
And now — is not Boaz our kinsman, with whose young women you have been? Behold, he is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight.
So wash and anoint yourself, put on your cloak, and go down to the threshing floor. Do not make yourself known to the man until he has finished eating and drinking.
And when he lies down, note the place where he lies; then go in, uncover his feet, and lie down — and he will tell you what to do."
And she said to her, "All that you say, I will do."
So she went down to the threshing floor and did just as her mother-in-law had charged her.
And Boaz ate and drank, and his heart was glad; and he went to lie down at the end of the grain heap. Then she came softly, uncovered his feet, and lay down.
And it came to pass at midnight that the man shuddered and twisted round — and behold, a woman lying at his feet!
And he said, "Who are you?" And she said, "I am Ruth, your servant. Spread the wing of your garment over your servant — for you are a redeemer."
And he said: "Blessed are you of YHWH, my daughter. You have made this last kindness greater than the first — in not going after the young men, whether poor or rich.
And now, my daughter, do not fear. All that you say, I will do for you — for the whole gate of my people knows that you are a woman of worth.
And now — it is true that I am a redeemer; but there is also a redeemer closer than I.
Stay the night. And in the morning, if he will redeem you — good, let him redeem. But if he does not desire to redeem you, then I will redeem you myself — as YHWH lives. Lie down until morning."
So she lay at his feet until morning, and rose before one person could recognize another; for he said, "Let it not be known that the woman came to the threshing floor."
And he said, "Bring the shawl you are wearing and hold it out." And she held it, and he measured out six measures of barley and laid it on her — and he went into the town.
And she came to her mother-in-law, who said, "How is it with you, my daughter?" And she told her all that the man had done for her,
and said, "These six measures of barley he gave me — for he said, 'Do not go back empty to your mother-in-law.'"
And she said: "Sit still, my daughter, until you know how the matter falls — for the man will not rest unless he settles the matter today."

rut.4.text: Now Boaz had gone up to the gate and sat down there — and behold, the redeemer of whom Boaz had spoken came passing by. And he said, "Turn aside, sit down here, friend so-and-so." And he turned aside and sat down.
And he took ten men of the elders of the town and said, "Sit down here." And they sat down.
And he said to the redeemer: "The portion of field that belonged to our brother Elimelech — Naomi, who has returned from the field of Moab, is selling it.

And I thought I would disclose it to you, saying: Buy it, before those sitting here and before the elders of my people. If you will redeem, redeem; but if it will not be redeemed, tell me, that I may know — for there is no one besides you to redeem it, and I am after you." And he said, "I will redeem it."
Then Boaz said: "On the day you buy the field from the hand of Naomi, you also acquire Ruth the Moabite, the wife of the dead — to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance."
And the redeemer said: "I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I ruin my own inheritance. Take my right of redemption for yourself — for I cannot redeem."

Now this was the custom in former times in Israel concerning redemption and exchange, to confirm any matter: a man drew off his sandal and gave it to the other — and this was the attestation in Israel.
So the redeemer said to Boaz, "Buy it for yourself" — and he drew off his sandal.
And Boaz said to the elders and all the people: "You are witnesses this day that I have bought from the hand of Naomi all that belonged to Elimelech and all that belonged to Chilion and Mahlon.

And Ruth the Moabite, the wife of Mahlon, I have also acquired as my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, so that the name of the dead may not be cut off from among his brothers and from the gate of his place. You are witnesses this day."

And all the people at the gate, and the elders, said: "Witnesses! May YHWH make the woman coming into your house like Rachel and like Leah, who between them built the house of Israel. Do worthily in Ephrathah, and win a name in Bethlehem!
And may your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah — from the seed YHWH will give you by this young woman."
So Boaz took Ruth, and she became his wife, and he went in to her; and YHWH granted her conception, and she bore a son.
And the women said to Naomi: "Blessed be YHWH, who has not left you without a redeemer this day! May his name be proclaimed in Israel.
He shall be to you a restorer of life and a sustainer of your old age — for your daughter-in-law who loves you has borne him, she who is better to you than seven sons."
And Naomi took the child and laid him on her bosom, and became his nurse.
And the neighbor women gave him a name, saying, "A son has been born to Naomi!" And they called his name Obed — he is the father of Jesse, the father of David.
And these are the generations of Perez: Perez begot Hezron,
and Hezron begot Ram, and Ram begot Amminadab,
and Amminadab begot Nahshon, and Nahshon begot Salmah,
and Salmon begot Boaz, and Boaz begot Obed,
and Obed begot Jesse — and Jesse begot David.

lam.1.text: How lonely sits the city that once was crowded with people! She has become like a widow, she that was great among the nations. A princess among the provinces, she is now a slave.
She weeps bitterly in the night, her tears upon her cheeks. Among all her lovers she has no one to comfort her; all her friends have betrayed her, they have become her enemies.
Judah has gone into exile out of affliction and hard servitude; she dwells among the nations and finds no resting place. All her pursuers overtook her in the midst of her distress.
The roads to Zion mourn, for none come to the appointed feasts. All her gates are desolate, her priests groan, her young women grieve, and she herself is in bitterness.
Her foes have become the head, her enemies are at ease, for YHWH has afflicted her for the multitude of her transgressions. Her little children have gone away, captives before the foe.
From the daughter of Zion all her splendor has departed. Her princes have become like deer that find no pasture; they flee without strength before the pursuer.

In the days of her affliction and her wandering Jerusalem remembers all the treasures that were hers in days of old, when her people fell into the hand of the foe and there was none to help her. The foes looked on her and mocked at her downfall.
Jerusalem has sinned grievously, therefore she has become an object of scorn. All who honored her despise her, for they have seen her nakedness; she herself groans and turns away.
Her filthiness clung to her skirts; she gave no thought to her end. Therefore her fall was astounding, with none to comfort her. Look, YHWH, upon my affliction, for the enemy has triumphed.
The foe has stretched out his hand over all her treasures; she has seen the nations invade her sanctuary, those whom you forbade to enter your assembly.
All her people groan as they search for bread; they trade their treasures for food to keep themselves alive. Look, YHWH, and behold, for I have become worthless.
Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was dealt out to me, which YHWH inflicted on the day of his burning anger.
From on high he sent fire into my bones, and it overpowered them. He spread a net for my feet and turned me back; he left me desolate, faint all the day long.
My transgressions were bound into a yoke; woven together by his hand, they came up upon my neck. He made my strength fail; the Lord gave me into the hands of those I cannot withstand.
The Lord has cast aside all my mighty men in my midst; he summoned an assembly against me to crush my young men. In the winepress the Lord has trodden the virgin daughter of Judah.
For these things I weep; my eyes, my eyes flow down with water, for a comforter is far from me, one to revive my soul. My children are desolate, for the enemy has prevailed.
Zion stretches out her hands, but there is none to comfort her. YHWH has commanded against Jacob that his neighbors should be his foes; Jerusalem has become a defiled thing among them.
YHWH is righteous, for I have rebelled against his word. Hear now, all you peoples, and behold my sorrow: my young women and my young men have gone into captivity.
I called to my lovers, but they betrayed me. My priests and my elders perished in the city while they searched for food to keep themselves alive.

Look, YHWH, for I am in distress; my inward parts are in turmoil, my heart is overturned within me, for I have been utterly rebellious. In the street the sword bereaves; in the house it is like death.
They have heard how I groan, with none to comfort me. All my enemies have heard of my ruin; they rejoice that you have done it. Bring the day you have proclaimed, and let them become like me.
Let all their evil come before you, and deal with them as you have dealt with me for all my transgressions; for my groans are many, and my heart is faint.

lam.2.text: How the Lord in his anger has covered the daughter of Zion with a cloud! He has hurled down from heaven to earth the splendor of Israel; he has not remembered his footstool on the day of his anger.

The Lord has swallowed up without pity all the dwellings of Jacob; in his wrath he has torn down the strongholds of the daughter of Judah. He has brought to the ground and defiled the kingdom and its princes.
In fierce anger he has cut down every horn of Israel; he has drawn back his right hand from before the enemy. He has burned through Jacob like a flaming fire that devours on every side.
He has bent his bow like an enemy, his right hand set like a foe; he has slain all that was pleasing to the eye. On the tent of the daughter of Zion he has poured out his wrath like fire.
The Lord has become like an enemy; he has swallowed up Israel. He has swallowed up all her palaces, laid waste her strongholds, and multiplied in the daughter of Judah mourning and lamentation.
He has broken down his booth like a garden, laid waste his meeting place. YHWH has made Zion forget her appointed feasts and her sabbaths, and in his fierce indignation has spurned king and priest.
The Lord has rejected his altar, disowned his sanctuary; he has handed over to the enemy the walls of her palaces. They raised a shout in the house of YHWH as on a day of appointed feast.
YHWH resolved to destroy the wall of the daughter of Zion; he stretched out the measuring line and did not withhold his hand from swallowing. He made rampart and wall lament; together they languish.
Her gates have sunk into the ground; he has ruined and shattered her bars. Her king and her princes are among the nations; the law is no more, and her prophets find no vision from YHWH.
The elders of the daughter of Zion sit on the ground in silence; they have thrown dust on their heads and put on sackcloth. The young women of Jerusalem have bowed their heads to the ground.

My eyes are spent with weeping, my inward parts are in turmoil; my heart is poured out on the ground over the ruin of the daughter of my people, as children and infants faint in the streets of the city.
They cry to their mothers, "Where is bread and wine?" as they faint like the wounded in the streets of the city, as their life is poured out on their mothers' breast.
What can I say for you, to what compare you, O daughter of Jerusalem? What can I liken to you, that I may comfort you, O virgin daughter of Zion? For your ruin is vast as the sea; who can heal you?
Your prophets saw for you visions false and worthless; they did not expose your iniquity to restore your fortunes, but saw for you false oracles and misleading visions.
All who pass along the way clap their hands at you; they hiss and wag their heads at the daughter of Jerusalem: "Is this the city that was called the perfection of beauty, the joy of all the earth?"
All your enemies open their mouths against you; they hiss and gnash their teeth, they cry, "We have swallowed her up. This is the day we longed for; we have found it, we have seen it."

YHWH has done what he purposed; he has carried out his word, which he decreed in days of old. He has torn down without pity; he has let the enemy rejoice over you, he has raised the horn of your foes.
Their heart cried out to the Lord. O wall of the daughter of Zion, let tears run down like a torrent day and night; give yourself no rest, let your eyes have no respite.

Arise, cry out in the night, at the beginning of the watches; pour out your heart like water before the face of the Lord. Lift up your hands to him for the lives of your little children, who faint from hunger at the head of every street.
Look, YHWH, and behold: to whom have you dealt out such treatment? Shall women eat their own offspring, the children they have cradled? Shall priest and prophet be slain in the sanctuary of the Lord?
The young and the old lie on the ground in the streets; my young women and my young men have fallen by the sword. You have slain them in the day of your anger; you have slaughtered without pity.
You summoned, as to a day of appointed feast, my terrors on every side. On the day of YHWH's anger none escaped or survived; those whom I cradled and reared my enemy has consumed.

lam.3.text: I am the man who has seen affliction under the rod of his wrath.
He has driven me and made me walk in darkness and not in light.
Against me alone he turns his hand again and again, all the day long.
He has worn away my flesh and my skin; he has broken my bones.
He has besieged me and surrounded me with bitterness and hardship.
He has made me dwell in darkness like those long dead.
He has walled me in so I cannot escape; he has weighed my chains heavy upon me.
Even when I cry out and plead for help, he shuts out my prayer.
He has walled up my ways with hewn stone; he has made my paths crooked.
He is to me like a bear lying in wait, like a lion in hiding.
He turned my ways aside and tore me to pieces; he has left me desolate.
He bent his bow and set me up as a target for the arrow.
He drove into my kidneys the arrows of his quiver.
I have become the laughingstock of all my people, their mocking song all the day long.
He has filled me with bitterness; he has sated me with wormwood.
He has broken my teeth on gravel and pressed me down into ashes.
My soul is bereft of peace; I have forgotten what happiness is.
So I said, "My endurance has perished, and so has my hope from YHWH."
Remember my affliction and my wandering, the wormwood and the gall.
My soul remembers well, and is bowed down within me.
But this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope:
The steadfast love of YHWH never ceases; his mercies never come to an end.
They are new every morning; great is your faithfulness.
"YHWH is my portion," says my soul, "therefore I will hope in him."
YHWH is good to those who wait for him, to the soul who seeks him.
It is good to wait quietly for the salvation of YHWH.
It is good for a man to bear the yoke in his youth.
Let him sit alone in silence when it is laid upon him.
Let him put his mouth in the dust; perhaps there is yet hope.
Let him give his cheek to the one who strikes him, and be filled with reproach.
For the Lord will not cast off forever.
Though he brings grief, he will have compassion according to the abundance of his steadfast love.
For he does not willingly afflict or grieve the children of men.
To crush underfoot all the prisoners of the earth,
to deny a man his rights before the face of the Most High,
to subvert a man in his cause—the Lord does not approve.
Who has spoken and it came to pass, unless the Lord has commanded it?
Is it not from the mouth of the Most High that both evil and good come?
Why should any living man complain, a man, about the punishment of his sins?
Let us search out and examine our ways, and return to YHWH.
Let us lift up our hearts and our hands to God in heaven.
We have transgressed and rebelled, and you have not forgiven.
You have wrapped yourself in anger and pursued us; you have slain without pity.
You have wrapped yourself in a cloud so that no prayer can pass through.
You have made us scum and refuse among the peoples.
All our enemies open their mouths against us.
Panic and pitfall have come upon us, devastation and ruin.
Streams of water run down from my eyes over the ruin of the daughter of my people.
My eyes flow without ceasing, without respite,
until YHWH looks down and sees from heaven.
What I see brings grief to my soul over all the daughters of my city.
Those who were my enemies without cause hunted me like a bird.
They cut off my life in a pit and cast stones upon me.
Waters closed over my head; I said, "I am cut off."
I called on your name, YHWH, from the depths of the pit.
You heard my voice: "Do not close your ear to my cry for relief, to my plea."
You drew near on the day I called to you; you said, "Do not fear."
You have taken up my cause, O Lord; you have redeemed my life.
You have seen, YHWH, the wrong done to me; judge my cause.
You have seen all their vengeance, all their schemes against me.
You have heard their taunts, YHWH, all their schemes against me,
the lips and thoughts of my assailants against me all the day long.
Whether they sit or rise, behold, I am their mocking song.
Repay them, YHWH, according to the work of their hands.
Give them anguish of heart; may your curse be upon them.
Pursue them in anger and destroy them from under the heavens of YHWH.

lam.4.text: How the gold has grown dim, the finest gold changed! The sacred stones lie scattered at the head of every street.
The precious sons of Zion, worth their weight in fine gold, how they are reckoned as clay jars, the work of a potter's hands!
Even the jackals offer the breast and nurse their young, but the daughter of my people has turned cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.
The tongue of the nursing infant clings to the roof of its mouth for thirst; the little children beg for bread, but no one gives them a piece.
Those who feasted on delicacies lie desolate in the streets; those brought up in purple embrace the ash heaps.
The iniquity of the daughter of my people is greater than the sin of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, though no hands were laid on her.
Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk; their bodies were more ruddy than coral, their form like sapphire.
Now their appearance is blacker than soot; they are not recognized in the streets. Their skin has shriveled on their bones; it has become dry as wood.
Better were those slain by the sword than those slain by hunger, who wasted away, pierced through for lack of the fruits of the field.
The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food in the ruin of the daughter of my people.
YHWH has vented his wrath; he has poured out his fierce anger, and he has kindled a fire in Zion that has consumed her foundations.
The kings of the earth did not believe, nor any of the world's inhabitants, that foe and enemy could enter the gates of Jerusalem.
It was for the sins of her prophets, the iniquities of her priests, who shed within her the blood of the righteous.
They wandered blind through the streets, defiled with blood, so that none could touch their garments.
"Away! Unclean!" people cried at them. "Away, away, do not touch!" So they fled and wandered; among the nations it was said, "They shall stay here no longer."
The face of YHWH has scattered them; he will regard them no more. No honor was shown to the priests, no favor to the elders.
Our eyes still failed, watching in vain for help; from our watchtower we watched for a nation that could not save.
They dogged our steps so we could not walk in our streets. Our end drew near, our days were finished, for our end had come.
Our pursuers were swifter than the eagles of the sky; they chased us over the mountains, they lay in ambush for us in the wilderness.
The breath of our nostrils, the anointed of YHWH, was caught in their pits—he of whom we said, "Under his shadow we shall live among the nations."
Rejoice and be glad, O daughter of Edom, you who dwell in the land of Uz; but to you also the cup shall pass; you shall become drunk and strip yourself bare.
The punishment of your iniquity is complete, O daughter of Zion; he will not prolong your exile. But he will punish your iniquity, O daughter of Edom; he will lay bare your sins.

lam.5.text: Remember, YHWH, what has befallen us; look, and see our disgrace.
Our inheritance has been turned over to strangers, our homes to foreigners.
We have become orphans, fatherless; our mothers are like widows.
We must pay for the water we drink; our wood comes only at a price.
With a yoke on our necks we are hard driven; we are weary, and are given no rest.
We have given our hand to Egypt and to Assyria, to have enough bread.
Our fathers sinned and are no more, and we bear their iniquities.
Slaves rule over us; there is none to deliver us from their hand.
We get our bread at the peril of our lives, because of the sword in the wilderness.
Our skin is hot as an oven with the burning heat of famine.
Women are ravished in Zion, young women in the towns of Judah.
Princes are hung up by their hands; no respect is shown to the elders.
Young men are made to grind at the mill, and boys stagger under loads of wood.
The elders have left the gate, the young men their music.
The joy of our hearts has ceased; our dancing has turned to mourning.
The crown has fallen from our head. Woe to us, for we have sinned!
For this our heart has grown faint; for these things our eyes have grown dim,
over Mount Zion, which lies desolate; jackals prowl over it.
But you, YHWH, reign forever; your throne endures from generation to generation.
Why do you forget us forever, why forsake us for so long?
Restore us to yourself, YHWH, that we may return; renew our days as of old,
unless you have utterly rejected us and are angry with us beyond measure.

isa.13.text: The burden of Babylon, which Isaiah son of Amoz saw in vision.
Raise a banner on the bare mountain; lift your voice to them, wave the hand — let them enter the gates of the nobles.
I myself have commanded my consecrated ones; I have summoned my warriors to answer my wrath, my proudly exulting ones.
A roar on the mountains, like the sound of a vast people! A roar of kingdoms, of nations gathering! YHWH of hosts is mustering an army for battle.
They come from a far country, from the end of the heavens — YHWH and the weapons of his fury — to lay waste the whole earth.
Wail, for the day of YHWH is near; it comes like devastation from the Almighty.
Therefore every hand goes limp, and every human heart melts away.
They are seized with terror; pangs and throes lay hold of them; they writhe like a woman in labor. Each stares aghast at his neighbor, their faces aflame.
Look — the day of YHWH is coming, cruel, with fury and burning wrath, to make the earth a desolation and to wipe her sinners from her face.
For the stars of the heavens and their constellations will not flash their light; the sun goes dark at its rising, and the moon will not shine its glow.
I will bring evil upon the world, and upon the wicked their guilt; I will silence the arrogance of the proud and lay low the haughtiness of tyrants.
I will make a mortal rarer than fine gold, a man rarer than the pure gold of Ophir.
Therefore I will make the heavens tremble, and the earth will quake from its place, in the fury of YHWH of hosts, in the day of his burning wrath.
And like a hunted gazelle, like sheep with none to gather them, each will turn to his own people, each flee to his own land.
Everyone found will be run through, and everyone caught will fall by the sword.
Their infants will be dashed to pieces before their eyes, their houses plundered, their wives ravished.
Look — I am rousing the Medes against them, who care nothing for silver and take no delight in gold.
Their bows will cut down the young men; they will show no mercy to the fruit of the womb, their eye will not pity children.
And Babylon, glory of kingdoms, the splendor and pride of the Chaldeans, will be like God's overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah.
Never again will she be inhabited, never dwelt in from age to age; no Arab will pitch his tent there, no shepherds bed their flocks there.
But wild beasts will lie down there, and their houses fill with howling creatures; there ostriches will dwell, and there the wild goats will dance.
Hyenas will cry in her palaces, and jackals in her halls of pleasure; her time is near to come, and her days will not be prolonged.

isa.14.text: For YHWH will have compassion on Jacob and will again choose Israel, and settle them on their own soil; the stranger will join them and cleave to the house of Jacob.

Peoples will take them and bring them to their place, and the house of Israel will possess them on YHWH's soil as menservants and maidservants; they will take their captors captive and rule over their oppressors.
And on the day YHWH gives you rest from your pain and your turmoil and the hard labor you were made to serve,
you will take up this taunt against the king of Babylon and say: How the oppressor has ceased, how the raging has ceased!
YHWH has broken the staff of the wicked, the scepter of rulers,
that struck peoples in fury with blows unceasing, that trod down nations in anger with relentless persecution.
Now all the earth rests and is quiet; they break into singing.
Even the cypresses rejoice over you, the cedars of Lebanon: Since you were laid low, no woodcutter comes up against us.
Sheol below is stirred to meet you at your coming; it rouses the shades for you, all the leaders of the earth; it raises from their thrones all the kings of the nations.
They all speak up and say to you: So you too are made weak as we are; you have become like us!
Your pomp is brought down to Sheol, the music of your harps; maggots are the bed spread beneath you, and worms your covering.
How you have fallen from heaven, O Shining One, son of the dawn! You are cut down to the earth, you who laid the nations low.
You said in your heart: I will scale the heavens; above the stars of God I will lift my throne; I will sit on the mount of assembly, in the far reaches of the north.
I will rise above the heights of the clouds; I will make myself like the Most High.
But down to Sheol you are brought, to the far reaches of the pit.
Those who see you stare at you, they ponder you: Is this the man who made the earth tremble, who shook kingdoms,
who made the world a wilderness and tore down its cities, who never loosed his prisoners homeward?
All the kings of the nations, every one of them, lie in honor, each in his own house.
But you are flung from your grave like a loathsome branch, clothed in the slain, in those pierced by the sword, who go down to the stones of the pit, like a trampled corpse.
You will not join them in burial, because you ruined your land, you murdered your people. Never again will the seed of evildoers be named.
Prepare a slaughter for his sons for the guilt of their fathers, lest they rise and possess the earth and fill the face of the world with cities.
I will rise against them, declares YHWH of hosts, and cut off from Babylon name and remnant, offspring and posterity, declares YHWH.
I will make her a possession of the hedgehog, and pools of water, and sweep her with the broom of destruction, declares YHWH of hosts.
YHWH of hosts has sworn: Surely as I have planned, so it will be, and as I have purposed, so it will stand:
I will break Assyria in my land, and on my mountains trample him down; his yoke will depart from them, and his burden depart from their shoulder.
This is the purpose purposed against all the earth, and this is the hand stretched out over all the nations.
For YHWH of hosts has purposed, and who can annul it? His hand is stretched out, and who can turn it back?
In the year King Ahaz died came this burden:
Do not rejoice, Philistia, all of you, that the rod that struck you is broken; for from the serpent's root will come a viper, and its fruit a flying seraph.
The firstborn of the poor will graze, and the needy lie down in safety; but I will kill your root with famine, and it will slay your remnant.
Wail, O gate! Cry out, O city! Melt in fear, Philistia, all of you! For smoke comes from the north, and no straggler in his ranks.
What answer will be given the messengers of the nation? That YHWH has founded Zion, and in her the afflicted of his people find refuge.

isa.15.text: The burden of Moab. In a night Ar of Moab is laid waste, undone; in a night Kir of Moab is laid waste, undone.
He has gone up to the temple, and Dibon to the high places, to weep; over Nebo and over Medeba Moab wails; every head is shaved bald, every beard cut off.
In her streets they gird on sackcloth; on her rooftops and in her squares everyone wails, streaming down with weeping.
Heshbon cries out, and Elealeh; their voice is heard as far as Jahaz; therefore the armed men of Moab cry aloud, his soul trembles within him.
My heart cries out for Moab; her fugitives flee as far as Zoar, to Eglath-shelishiyah. Up the ascent of Luhith they climb weeping; on the road to Horonaim they raise the cry of destruction.
The waters of Nimrim are a desolation; the grass is withered, the tender growth gone, nothing green remains.
Therefore the wealth they gained and stored they carry away over the Wadi of the Willows.
For the cry has gone round the border of Moab; its wailing reaches Eglaim, its wailing reaches Beer-elim.
The waters of Dimon run full of blood, yet I will bring still more upon Dimon — a lion for the survivors of Moab, for the remnant of the land.

isa.16.text: Send the tribute lamb to the ruler of the land, from Sela through the wilderness to the mount of daughter Zion.
Like fluttering birds driven from the nest, so are the daughters of Moab at the fords of the Arnon.
Give counsel, render a decision; make your shadow like night at high noon; hide the outcasts, do not betray the fugitive.
Let the outcasts of Moab dwell with you; be a shelter to them from the destroyer. When the oppressor is no more, and destruction has ceased, and the tramplers have vanished from the land,
then a throne will be established in steadfast love, and one will sit on it in faithfulness in the tent of David, a judge who seeks justice and is swift to do right.
We have heard of the pride of Moab, his exceeding pride, his arrogance, his conceit, his fury — his boasts are false.
Therefore Moab wails for Moab, all of it wails; for the raisin-cakes of Kir-hareseth you moan, utterly stricken.

For the fields of Heshbon languish, the vine of Sibmah; the lords of the nations have beaten down its branches, which reached as far as Jazer, straying into the wilderness; its shoots spread out, they crossed the sea.
Therefore I weep with the weeping of Jazer for the vine of Sibmah; I drench you with my tears, Heshbon and Elealeh; for over your summer fruit and your harvest the battle shout has fallen.
Joy and gladness are taken from the orchard; in the vineyards no one sings, no one shouts; no treader treads out wine in the presses; I have silenced the shout of joy.
Therefore my inmost being moans like a harp for Moab, and my heart for Kir-heres.
And when Moab appears, when he wears himself out on the high place and comes to his sanctuary to pray, he will not prevail.
This is the word YHWH spoke concerning Moab long ago.

But now YHWH speaks, saying: In three years, like the years of a hired laborer, the glory of Moab will be brought to shame, with all its vast multitude, and the remnant will be very few and feeble, of no account.

isa.17.text: The burden of Damascus. Look — Damascus is removed from being a city; it will become a heap of ruins.
The cities of Aroer are forsaken; they will be given to flocks that lie down with none to make them afraid.
The fortress will vanish from Ephraim, and the kingdom from Damascus; and the remnant of Aram will be like the glory of the children of Israel, declares YHWH of hosts.
And on that day the glory of Jacob will be brought low, and the fat of his flesh will waste away.
It will be as when the reaper gathers the standing grain, his arm harvesting the ears; it will be as when one gleans ears in the Valley of Rephaim.

Yet gleanings will be left in it, as when the olive tree is beaten: two or three berries at the top of the highest bough, four or five on the branches of the fruit tree, declares YHWH, the God of Israel.
On that day a man will look to his Maker, and his eyes will turn to the Holy One of Israel.
He will not look to the altars, the work of his hands, nor gaze at what his fingers made, the sacred poles and the incense stands.
On that day their strong cities will be like the abandoned places of the wood and the height, forsaken before the children of Israel, and it will be a desolation.
For you have forgotten the God of your salvation, and have not remembered the Rock of your refuge; therefore you plant pleasant plantings and set out slips of an alien vine.
On the day you plant it you make it grow, and in the morning you make your seed blossom; yet the harvest flees away in a day of sickness and incurable pain.
Ah, the roar of many peoples — they roar like the roaring of the seas! The rushing of nations — they rush like the rushing of mighty waters!
The nations rush like the rushing of many waters, but he rebukes them, and they flee far away, chased like chaff on the mountains before the wind, like whirling dust before the storm.
At evening — sudden terror! Before morning they are no more. This is the portion of those who plunder us, the lot of those who rob us.

isa.18.text: Ah, land of whirring wings, beyond the rivers of Cush,

who sends envoys by sea, in vessels of papyrus over the waters: Go, swift messengers, to a nation tall and smooth-skinned, to a people feared far and wide, a nation mighty and trampling, whose land the rivers divide.
All you inhabitants of the world, you who dwell on the earth — when a banner is raised on the mountains, look! When a trumpet is blown, listen!
For thus YHWH said to me: I will be still and gaze from my dwelling, like shimmering heat in the sunlight, like a cloud of dew in the heat of harvest.
For before the harvest, when the blossom is over and the flower becomes a ripening grape, he will cut off the shoots with pruning knives and lop off the spreading branches.
They will be left together to the birds of prey of the mountains and to the beasts of the earth; the birds of prey will summer upon them, and all the beasts of the earth will winter upon them.

At that time a gift will be brought to YHWH of hosts from a people tall and smooth-skinned, from a people feared far and wide, a nation mighty and trampling, whose land the rivers divide — to the place of the name of YHWH of hosts, Mount Zion.

isa.19.text: The burden of Egypt. Look — YHWH rides on a swift cloud and comes to Egypt; the idols of Egypt tremble before him, and the heart of Egypt melts within it.
I will stir up Egypt against Egypt; they will fight, each against his brother, each against his neighbor, city against city, kingdom against kingdom.
The spirit of Egypt will be emptied out within it, and I will swallow up its counsel; they will seek the idols and the spirits of the dead, the mediums and the wizards.
I will hand Egypt over to a cruel master, and a fierce king will rule over them, declares the Lord, YHWH of hosts.
The waters of the sea will fail, and the river run dry and parched.
The canals will stink; the streams of Egypt will dwindle and dry up; reed and rush will wither.
The bare places by the Nile, by the mouth of the Nile, and all that is sown along the Nile, will dry up, blow away, and be no more.
The fishermen will mourn; all who cast a hook in the Nile will lament, and those who spread nets on the water will languish.
The workers of combed flax will be dismayed, and the weavers of white cloth grow pale.
Her weavers will be crushed, all who work for wages sick at heart.
The princes of Zoan are nothing but fools; the wisest counselors of Pharaoh give senseless counsel. How can you say to Pharaoh, I am a son of the wise, a son of ancient kings?
Where then are your wise men? Let them tell you now, let them make known what YHWH of hosts has purposed against Egypt.
The princes of Zoan have become fools, the princes of Memphis are deceived; the cornerstones of her tribes have led Egypt astray.
YHWH has mixed within her a spirit of dizziness, and they have led Egypt astray in all she does, like a drunkard staggering in his vomit.
And Egypt will have nothing to do — nothing that head or tail, palm branch or reed can do.
On that day Egypt will be like women, trembling and afraid before the shaking of the hand of YHWH of hosts, which he raises against them.
The land of Judah will become a terror to Egypt; everyone who mentions it will be afraid, because of the purpose of YHWH of hosts, which he is planning against them.
On that day five cities in the land of Egypt will speak the language of Canaan and swear allegiance to YHWH of hosts; one will be called the City of the Sun.
On that day there will be an altar to YHWH in the midst of the land of Egypt, and a pillar to YHWH at its border.
It will be a sign and a witness to YHWH of hosts in the land of Egypt; when they cry to YHWH because of oppressors, he will send them a savior and defender, and rescue them.
YHWH will make himself known to Egypt, and the Egyptians will know YHWH on that day; they will worship with sacrifice and offering, and make vows to YHWH and fulfill them.
YHWH will strike Egypt, striking and healing; they will turn to YHWH, and he will listen to their pleas and heal them.
On that day there will be a highway from Egypt to Assyria; Assyria will come into Egypt, and Egypt into Assyria, and the Egyptians will worship with the Assyrians.
On that day Israel will be the third with Egypt and Assyria, a blessing in the midst of the earth,
whom YHWH of hosts has blessed, saying: Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Israel my inheritance.

isa.20.text: In the year the commander sent by Sargon king of Assyria came to Ashdod and fought against it and captured it —
at that time YHWH spoke through Isaiah son of Amoz, saying: Go, loose the sackcloth from your waist and strip the sandals from your feet. And he did so, walking naked and barefoot.
Then YHWH said: As my servant Isaiah has walked naked and barefoot three years, a sign and a portent against Egypt and Cush,
so the king of Assyria will lead away the captives of Egypt and the exiles of Cush, young and old, naked and barefoot, with buttocks bared — the shame of Egypt.
They will be shattered and ashamed because of Cush their hope and Egypt their splendor.
And the inhabitants of this coastland will say on that day: Look what has become of our hope, to whom we fled for help, to be delivered from the king of Assyria! How then will we escape?

isa.21.text: The burden of the wilderness of the sea. Like whirlwinds sweeping through the Negev, it comes from the wilderness, from a terrible land.
A harsh vision is told to me: the traitor betrays, the destroyer destroys. Go up, Elam! Lay siege, Media! I have put an end to all her groaning.
Therefore my loins are filled with anguish; pangs seize me, like the pangs of a woman in labor. I am too bent to hear, too dismayed to see.
My heart reels; horror overwhelms me; the twilight I longed for has become a terror to me.
They set the table, they spread the rugs, they eat, they drink. Rise up, princes! Oil the shields!
For thus the Lord said to me: Go, station the watchman; let him report what he sees.
And if he sees riders, horsemen in pairs, riders on donkeys, riders on camels, let him listen closely, with the keenest attention.
Then he cried like a lion: Upon the watchtower, my Lord, I stand continually by day, and at my post I keep station all the nights.
And look — here they come, riders, horsemen in pairs! And he answered and said: Fallen, fallen is Babylon, and all the images of her gods lie shattered on the ground.
O my threshed one, child of my threshing floor — what I have heard from YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel, I have declared to you.
The burden of Dumah. One calls to me from Seir: Watchman, what of the night? Watchman, what of the night?
The watchman says: Morning comes, and also night. If you would inquire, inquire; come back again.
The burden against Arabia. In the thickets of Arabia you will lodge, O caravans of Dedan.
Bring water to the thirsty, you who dwell in the land of Tema; meet the fugitive with bread.
For they have fled from the swords, from the drawn sword, from the bent bow, from the press of battle.
For thus the Lord said to me: Within a year, like the years of a hired laborer, all the glory of Kedar will come to an end.
And the remnant of the archers, the warriors of the sons of Kedar, will be few; for YHWH, the God of Israel, has spoken.

isa.22.text: The burden of the Valley of Vision. What is the matter with you now, that you have all gone up to the rooftops,
you town full of tumult, clamorous city, exultant town? Your slain were not slain by the sword, nor did they die in battle.
All your rulers fled together; without a bow they were captured; all who were found in you were captured together, though they had fled far away.
Therefore I said: Look away from me, let me weep bitterly; do not press to comfort me over the ruin of the daughter of my people.
For the Lord, YHWH of hosts, has a day of tumult and trampling and confusion in the Valley of Vision — a battering of walls and a cry to the mountain.
Elam took up the quiver with chariots and horsemen, and Kir bared the shield.
Your choicest valleys were full of chariots, and the horsemen took their stand at the gate.
He stripped away the covering of Judah, and on that day you looked to the weapons of the House of the Forest.
You saw the breaches in the city of David, that they were many, and you gathered the waters of the lower pool.
You counted the houses of Jerusalem, and you tore down houses to fortify the wall.
You made a reservoir between the two walls for the waters of the old pool; but you did not look to him who made it, nor regard him who fashioned it long ago.
On that day the Lord, YHWH of hosts, called for weeping and mourning, for shaved heads and girding with sackcloth.
But instead — joy and gladness, slaughtering oxen and killing sheep, eating meat and drinking wine: Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die!
YHWH of hosts has revealed himself in my ears: Surely this guilt will not be forgiven you until you die, says the Lord, YHWH of hosts.
