I spoke with my heart: "Behold, I have grown great and gathered wisdom beyond all who were before me over Jerusalem" — and my heart has seen much wisdom and knowledge.
And I set my heart to know wisdom — and to know madness and folly. I learned that this too is a chasing after wind.
For in much wisdom is much vexation, and whoever adds knowledge adds pain.

ecc.2.text: I said in my heart: "Come now, let me test you with joy — taste what is good." And behold, this too was vapor.
Of laughter I said, "Madness!" — and of joy, "What does this accomplish?"

I explored in my heart how to draw my flesh along with wine — my heart still steering by wisdom — and how to take hold of folly, until I might see what is good for the children of men to do under the heavens the few days of their lives.
I made my works great: I built myself houses; I planted myself vineyards;
I made myself gardens and orchards, and planted in them fruit trees of every kind;
I made myself pools of water, to water from them a forest sprouting trees;
I bought male and female slaves, and had slaves born in my house; I also had herds of cattle and flocks, more than all who were before me in Jerusalem;
I gathered for myself silver and gold too, the treasure of kings and provinces; I got myself singing men and singing women, and the delights of the children of men — concubine upon concubine.
So I grew great, greater than all who were before me in Jerusalem — and still my wisdom stood by me.
And nothing my eyes asked did I withhold from them; I kept my heart from no joy — for my heart rejoiced from all my toil, and this was my portion from all my toil.
Then I turned to face all the works my hands had done, and the toil I had toiled to do — and behold, all was vapor and a chasing after wind, and there was no gain under the sun.
And I turned to consider wisdom, and madness, and folly — for what can the man do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
And I saw that wisdom has an advantage over folly, as light has an advantage over darkness:
the wise man has his eyes in his head, and the fool walks in darkness. But I also came to know that one fate befalls them all.
And I said in my heart: "As the fool's fate is, so shall mine be — why then have I been so exceedingly wise?" And I said in my heart that this too is vapor.
For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise any more than the fool: in the days to come, everything is already forgotten. And how the wise man dies — just like the fool!
So I hated life — for the work done under the sun was grievous to me; for all is vapor and a chasing after wind.
And I hated all my toil at which I toil under the sun, seeing I must leave it to the man who comes after me —
and who knows whether he will be wise or a fool? Yet he will be master of all my toil, at which I toiled and was wise under the sun. This too is vapor.
So I turned to give my heart up to despair over all the toil at which I toiled under the sun.
For here is a man whose toil is with wisdom and knowledge and skill — and to a man who never toiled at it he must give it as his portion. This too is vapor, and a great evil.
For what does a man have from all his toil, and from the striving of his heart, at which he toils under the sun?
For all his days are pains, and his business is vexation; even at night his heart does not lie down. This too — it is vapor.
There is nothing better for a man than to eat and drink and let his soul see good in his toil. This too I saw — that it is from the hand of God.
For who can eat, and who can feel, apart from him?

For to the man who is good before him he gives wisdom and knowledge and joy; but to the sinner he gives the business of gathering and heaping up — only to hand it to one who is good before God. This too is vapor and a chasing after wind.

ecc.3.text: For everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under the heavens:
a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted;
a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to tear down, and a time to build;
a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;
a time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones; a time to embrace, and a time to keep far from embracing;
a time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away;
a time to tear, and a time to sew; a time to be silent, and a time to speak;
a time to love, and a time to hate; a time for war, and a time for peace.
What gain has the worker from that at which he toils?
I have seen the business God has given the children of men to be busy with.
He has made everything beautiful in its time; he has also set eternity in their heart — yet so that man cannot find out the work God has done from beginning to end.
I know that there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and to do good in one's life —
and also that every man who eats and drinks and sees good in all his toil: it is the gift of God.
I know that whatever God does, it shall be forever; nothing can be added to it, and nothing taken from it — and God has done it so that men should stand in awe before him.
What is, already has been; and what is to be, already has been — and God seeks out what has been pursued.
And again I saw under the sun: in the place of justice — wickedness there; and in the place of righteousness — wickedness there.
I said in my heart: God will judge the righteous and the wicked — for there is a time for every purpose and for every deed, there.
I said in my heart concerning the children of men: God is testing them, to show them that they, in themselves, are beasts.

For the fate of the children of men and the fate of the beast — one fate is theirs: as the one dies, so dies the other, and one breath is in them all; and man has no advantage over the beast — for all is vapor.
All go to one place: all came from the dust, and all return to the dust.
Who knows whether the breath of the children of men rises upward, and the breath of the beast goes down beneath, to the earth?
So I saw that there is nothing better than that a man rejoice in his works, for that is his portion — for who can bring him to see what will be after him?

ecc.4.text: And I turned and saw all the oppressed who are made under the sun: and behold, the tears of the oppressed — and they have no comforter; and in the hand of their oppressors, power — and they have no comforter.
So I praised the dead, who have already died, above the living, who are still alive;
and better than both is the one who has never yet been — who has not seen the evil deed that is done under the sun.
And I saw all the toil, and all the skill of work — that it is one man's envy of his neighbor. This too is vapor and a chasing after wind.
The fool folds his hands — and eats his own flesh.
Better one palm filled with rest than two fists filled with toil and a chasing after wind.
And I turned and saw a vapor under the sun:

there is one alone, without a second — no son, no brother — and there is no end to all his toil; his eye is never satisfied with riches. "For whom, then, am I toiling and depriving my soul of good?" This too is vapor — a sorry business it is.
Two are better than one, for they have a good reward for their toil:
for if they fall, the one will lift up his fellow — but woe to the one who falls with no second to lift him up.
Again: if two lie down together, they are warm — but for one alone, how shall he be warm?
And if someone overpowers the one, two can stand against him — and the threefold cord is not quickly snapped.
Better a poor and wise youth than an old and foolish king who no longer knows how to take warning.
For out of the prison-house he came to reign — though even in his own kingdom he was born poor.
I saw all the living who walk about under the sun with the second youth, who will stand in his place:
there is no end to all the people, to all before whom he stands — and yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this too is vapor and a chasing after wind.
Guard your steps when you go to the house of God: to draw near to listen is better than the fools' giving of sacrifice — for they do not know that they are doing evil.

ecc.5.text: Do not be hasty with your mouth, and let not your heart hurry to bring forth a word before God — for God is in the heavens, and you are on the earth; therefore let your words be few.
For the dream comes with much business, and the voice of a fool with many words.
When you vow a vow to God, do not delay to pay it — for there is no delight in fools. What you vow — pay!
Better that you not vow, than that you vow and do not pay.
Do not let your mouth make your flesh sin, and do not say before the messenger, "It was a mistake." Why should God be angry at your voice and destroy the work of your hands?
For in a multitude of dreams are vapors — and in many words. Rather: fear God.

If you see the oppression of the poor and the robbing of justice and righteousness in a province, do not be astonished at the matter — for one high official watches over another high one, and higher ones over them.
And the profit of a land is, in the whole, this: a king who serves the field.
Whoever loves silver is not satisfied with silver, nor the lover of abundance with income. This too is vapor.
When goods increase, those who eat them increase — and what skill has their owner, except the seeing of his eyes?
Sweet is the sleep of the laborer, whether he eats little or much; but the rich man's surfeit does not let him sleep.
There is a sick evil I have seen under the sun: riches kept by their owner to his own hurt —
and those riches perish in a bad venture; and he fathers a son, and there is nothing in his hand.
As he came out of his mother's womb, naked he shall return, to go as he came — and nothing from his toil shall he carry away in his hand.
And this too is a sick evil: exactly as he came, so shall he go — and what gain has he, who toils for the wind?
All his days, too, he eats in darkness — with much vexation, and sickness, and anger.

Behold what I have seen to be good, to be beautiful: to eat and to drink and to see good in all one's toil at which one toils under the sun, the few days of the life God has given him — for that is his portion.
And every man to whom God has given riches and possessions, and empowered him to eat of them, to take his portion and to rejoice in his toil — this is the gift of God.
For he will not much remember the days of his life — because God keeps him answered with the joy of his heart.

ecc.6.text: There is an evil I have seen under the sun, and it lies heavy upon humankind:

a man to whom God gives riches and possessions and honor, so that his soul lacks nothing of all it desires — yet God does not empower him to eat of it, for a stranger eats it. This is vapor, and a grievous sickness.

If a man fathers a hundred children and lives many years — many as the days of his years may be — but his soul is not satisfied with the good, and he has no burial: I say the stillborn child is better off than he.
For in vapor it comes and in darkness it goes, and in darkness its name is covered;
it has not even seen the sun or known it — yet it has more rest than he.
And though he live a thousand years twice over and see no good — do not all go to one place?
All a man's toil is for his mouth — and yet the appetite is never filled.
For what advantage has the wise man over the fool? What advantage has the poor man who knows how to walk before the living?
Better the sight of the eyes than the roving of the appetite. This too is vapor and a chasing after wind.
Whatever comes to be has already been called by name, and it is known what man is — and he cannot contend with one mightier than he.
For there are many words that multiply vapor — what advantage is that to a man?
For who knows what is good for a man in life, the few days of his vaporous life, which he spends like a shadow? Who can tell a man what will be after him under the sun?

ecc.7.text: A good name is better than good oil — and the day of death than the day of one's birth.
Better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting; for that is the end of every man — and the living will lay it to heart.
Better vexation than laughter — for by sadness of face the heart is made good.
The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, and the heart of fools in the house of mirth.
Better to hear the rebuke of the wise than to be a man who hears the song of fools.
For like the crackle of thorns under the pot, so is the laughter of the fool. And this too is vapor.
For extortion makes the wise man mad, and a bribe destroys the heart.
Better the end of a thing than its beginning; better the patient in spirit than the proud in spirit.
Do not be hasty in your spirit to be angry — for anger rests in the bosom of fools.
Do not say, "How is it that the former days were better than these?" — for it is not from wisdom that you ask this.
Wisdom is good with an inheritance — an advantage to those who see the sun.
For to be in the shade of wisdom is to be in the shade of silver; and the advantage of knowledge is this: wisdom keeps its owner alive.
Consider the work of God: who can straighten what he has bent?
On the day of good, be in the good; and on the day of evil, consider: God has made the one alongside the other — so that man may find nothing at all of what comes after him.
I have seen everything in my days of vapor: there is a righteous man perishing in his righteousness, and there is a wicked man living long in his evil.
Do not be righteous overmuch, and do not make yourself excessively wise — why should you be appalled?
Do not be wicked overmuch, and do not be a fool — why should you die before your time?
It is good to take hold of this, and from that too do not let your hand rest — for the one who fears God comes out with both.
Wisdom gives the wise man more strength than ten rulers in a city.
Surely there is no righteous man on earth who does good and never sins.
Also, do not give your heart to all the words people speak — lest you hear your own servant cursing you;
for your heart knows that many times you yourself have likewise cursed others.
All this I tested by wisdom. I said, "I will be wise" — and it was far from me.
Far off is that which is, and deep, deep — who can find it?
I turned, my heart with me, to know and to probe, to seek wisdom and the sum of things — and to know wickedness as folly, and foolishness as madness.
And I find more bitter than death the woman who is snares — her heart is nets, her hands are fetters. The one who is good before God escapes her; the sinner is caught by her.
"See, this I have found," says Qohelet — adding one to one to find the sum —
"what my soul still sought and I did not find: one man among a thousand I found, but a woman among all these I did not find.
Only see — this I have found: that God made humankind upright, but they have sought out many devices."

ecc.8.text: Who is like the wise man, and who knows the interpretation of a thing? A man's wisdom lights up his face, and the hardness of his face is transformed.
I say: keep the king's command — and that because of the oath of God.
Do not be hasty to leave his presence; do not stand in an evil matter — for he does whatever he pleases.
For the king's word carries power — and who can say to him, "What are you doing?"
The keeper of a command shall know no evil thing; and a wise heart knows both time and judgment.
For every purpose has its time and judgment — for the evil of man lies heavy upon him.
For he does not know what shall be; for who can tell him how it shall be?
No man rules over the wind, to restrain the wind; no one rules over the day of death; there is no discharge in war — and wickedness will not deliver its owner.
All this I saw, giving my heart to every deed done under the sun — a time when man rules over man to his hurt.
And so I saw the wicked buried — they came and went from the holy place, and were forgotten in the city where they had acted so. This too is vapor.
Because the sentence against an evil deed is not carried out quickly, the heart of the children of men is filled within them to do evil —
because a sinner does evil a hundred times, and his days are prolonged. Yet I also know that it shall be well with those who fear God, who fear before his face,
and that it shall not be well with the wicked man; like a shadow, he shall not lengthen his days — because he does not fear before God.

There is a vapor that is done on the earth: there are righteous ones who receive what befits the deeds of the wicked, and wicked ones who receive what befits the deeds of the righteous. I said: this too is vapor.

So I praised joy — for there is nothing good for a man under the sun but to eat and to drink and to rejoice; and this will accompany him in his toil through the days of his life that God gives him under the sun.
When I set my heart to know wisdom and to see the business done upon the earth — how neither by day nor by night do one's eyes see sleep —

then I saw all the work of God: that man cannot find out the work that is done under the sun. However much a man toils to seek it, he shall not find it; and even if the wise man claims to know — he cannot find it.

ecc.9.text: For all this I laid to my heart, to sift all this: that the righteous and the wise and their works are in the hand of God. Whether love or hatred — man does not know; everything lies before them.

Everything is the same for everyone: one fate for the righteous and the wicked, for the good and the pure and the impure, for the one who sacrifices and the one who does not sacrifice; as the good man, so the sinner; the one who swears, as the one who fears an oath.

This is the evil in everything done under the sun: that one fate comes to all — and moreover, the heart of the children of men is full of evil, and madness is in their hearts while they live; and after that — to the dead.
For whoever is joined to all the living has hope — for a living dog is better than a dead lion.
For the living know that they shall die; but the dead know nothing at all, and they have no more reward — for their remembrance is forgotten.
Their love, their hatred, their jealousy alike have already perished; and never again do they have a portion in anything done under the sun.
Go — eat your bread with joy, and drink your wine with a glad heart, for God has already approved your deeds.
At every season let your garments be white, and let oil not be lacking on your head.

See life with the woman you love, all the days of your vaporous life that he has given you under the sun — all your vaporous days; for that is your portion in life, and in your toil at which you toil under the sun.
Whatever your hand finds to do, do it with your strength — for there is no deed or reckoning or knowledge or wisdom in Sheol, where you are going.

Again I saw under the sun: the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the mighty; neither is bread to the wise, nor riches to the discerning, nor favor to the knowing — for time and chance befall them all.
For man does not even know his time: like fish caught in a cruel net, like birds seized in a snare — like them the children of men are trapped at a time of evil, when it falls on them suddenly.
This too I saw as wisdom under the sun, and it seemed great to me:
a little city, and few men in it; and a great king came against it and surrounded it and built great siegeworks against it.
And there was found in it a poor wise man, and he — he delivered the city by his wisdom. Yet no one remembered that poor man.
And I said: wisdom is better than strength — but the poor man's wisdom is despised, and his words go unheard.
The words of the wise, spoken gently, are heard above the shouting of a ruler among fools.
Wisdom is better than weapons of war — and one sinner destroys much good.

ecc.10.text: Dead flies make the perfumer's oil stink and ferment; a little folly outweighs wisdom and honor.
The heart of the wise inclines to his right, and the heart of the fool to his left.
Even on the road, as the fool walks, his heart fails him — and he tells everyone he is a fool.
If the ruler's temper rises against you, do not leave your place — for calmness lays great offenses to rest.
There is an evil I have seen under the sun — like an error that goes out from before the ruler:
folly is set in the great heights, and the rich sit in the low place.
I have seen slaves on horses, and princes walking on the ground like slaves.
Whoever digs a pit may fall into it, and whoever breaks through a wall — a snake may bite him.
Whoever quarries stones may be hurt by them; whoever splits logs may be endangered by them.
If the iron is blunt and one has not sharpened the edge, he must exert more strength — but wisdom is the advantage that brings success.
If the snake bites before it is charmed, there is no advantage to the master of the tongue.
The words of a wise man's mouth win grace — but the lips of a fool swallow him up:
the beginning of the words of his mouth is folly, and the end of his mouth is evil madness —
and the fool multiplies words. Man does not know what shall be; and what shall be after him, who can tell him?
The toil of fools wearies them — one who does not even know the way to town.
Woe to you, O land, whose king is a boy, and whose princes feast in the morning!
Happy are you, O land, whose king is a son of nobles, and whose princes eat at the proper time — for strength, and not for drunkenness!
Through slothfulness the roof-beams sag, and through idle hands the house leaks.
For laughter they make a feast, and wine gladdens the living — and silver answers everything.
Even in your thought do not curse a king, and in your bedchamber do not curse the rich — for a bird of the heavens may carry the voice, and a winged thing may report the word.

ecc.11.text: Send your bread out upon the face of the waters — for in the multitude of days you will find it again.
Give a portion to seven, and even to eight — for you do not know what evil may come upon the earth.
If the clouds are full of rain, they empty themselves on the earth; and if a tree falls to the south or to the north — in the place where the tree falls, there it lies.
Whoever watches the wind will not sow, and whoever gazes at the clouds will not reap.
Just as you do not know the way of the wind, or how the bones grow in the womb of her who is with child, so you do not know the work of God, who makes everything.
In the morning sow your seed, and toward evening do not let your hand rest — for you do not know which will prosper, this or that, or whether both alike will be good.
And sweet is the light, and good it is for the eyes to see the sun.
For if a man lives many years, let him rejoice in them all — and let him remember the days of darkness, for they shall be many. All that comes is vapor.

Rejoice, young man, in your youth, and let your heart make you glad in the days of your prime. Walk in the ways of your heart and in the sight of your eyes — and know that for all these things God will bring you into judgment.
So remove vexation from your heart, and put away evil from your flesh — for youth and the black-haired dawn of life are vapor.

ecc.12.text: And remember your Creator in the days of your youth — before the evil days come, and the years arrive of which you will say, "I have no delight in them";
before the sun darkens, and the light, and the moon, and the stars — and the clouds return after the rain;
in the day when the keepers of the house tremble, and the strong men are bent; when the grinding-women cease because they are few, and those who look through the windows are darkened;
when the doors to the street are shut and the sound of the mill grows low; when one rises at the voice of a bird, and all the daughters of song are bowed down;

when men fear the height, and terrors are in the road; when the almond tree blossoms, and the grasshopper drags itself along, and the caper-berry fails — for man is going to his everlasting house, and the mourners circle in the street —
before the silver cord is snapped, and the golden bowl is shattered, and the jar is broken at the spring, and the wheel broken at the cistern —
and the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the breath returns to God who gave it.
Vapor of vapors, says the Qohelet — all is vapor.
And besides being wise, Qohelet also taught the people knowledge; he weighed and searched out and set in order many proverbs.
Qohelet sought to find words of delight — and what is written here is upright: words of truth.
The words of the wise are like goads, and like nails driven deep are the masters of collections — given by one Shepherd.
And beyond these, my son, be warned: of making many books there is no end, and much study is a weariness of the flesh.
The end of the matter — all has been heard: fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole of man.
For God will bring every deed into judgment — with everything hidden, whether good or evil.

ezr.1.text: And in the first year of Cyrus king of Persia — that the word of YHWH from the mouth of Jeremiah might be fulfilled — YHWH stirred the spirit of Cyrus king of Persia, and he sent a proclamation through all his kingdom, and also in writing:
"Thus says Cyrus king of Persia: All the kingdoms of the earth YHWH, the God of the heavens, has given me — and he has charged me to build him a house in Jerusalem, which is in Judah.
Whoever is among you of all his people — may his God be with him — let him go up to Jerusalem, which is in Judah, and build the house of YHWH, the God of Israel; he is the God who is in Jerusalem.

And every survivor, in whatever place he sojourns — let the men of his place assist him with silver and gold, with goods and livestock, along with the freewill offering for the house of God which is in Jerusalem."

Then the heads of the fathers' houses of Judah and Benjamin arose, and the priests and the Levites — everyone whose spirit God had stirred — to go up and build the house of YHWH which is in Jerusalem.
And all who were around them strengthened their hands with vessels of silver, with gold, with goods and livestock and precious things — besides all that was freely offered.
And King Cyrus brought out the vessels of the house of YHWH, which Nebuchadnezzar had carried away from Jerusalem and put in the house of his gods.
Cyrus king of Persia brought them out by the hand of Mithredath the treasurer, who counted them out to Sheshbazzar, the prince of Judah.
And this was their number: thirty gold basins, a thousand silver basins, twenty-nine knives,
thirty gold bowls, four hundred and ten silver bowls of a second sort, a thousand other vessels.
All the vessels of gold and silver: five thousand four hundred. All these Sheshbazzar brought up when the exiles were brought up from Babylon to Jerusalem.

ezr.2.text: And these are the people of the province who came up from the captivity of the exile — whom Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon had carried into exile to Babylon — and who returned to Jerusalem and Judah, each to his own town,
who came with Zerubbabel, Jeshua, Nehemiah, Seraiah, Reelaiah, Mordecai, Bilshan, Mispar, Bigvai, Rehum, Baanah. The count of the men of the people of Israel:
the sons of Parosh — two thousand one hundred and seventy-two;
the sons of Shephatiah — three hundred and seventy-two;
the sons of Arah — seven hundred and seventy-five;
the sons of Pahath-moab, of the sons of Jeshua and Joab — two thousand eight hundred and twelve;
the sons of Elam — one thousand two hundred and fifty-four;
the sons of Zattu — nine hundred and forty-five;
the sons of Zaccai — seven hundred and sixty;
the sons of Bani — six hundred and forty-two;
the sons of Bebai — six hundred and twenty-three;
the sons of Azgad — one thousand two hundred and twenty-two;
the sons of Adonikam — six hundred and sixty-six;
the sons of Bigvai — two thousand and fifty-six;
the sons of Adin — four hundred and fifty-four;
the sons of Ater, of Hezekiah — ninety-eight;
the sons of Bezai — three hundred and twenty-three;
the sons of Jorah — one hundred and twelve;
the sons of Hashum — two hundred and twenty-three;
the sons of Gibbar — ninety-five;
the sons of Bethlehem — one hundred and twenty-three;
the men of Netophah — fifty-six;
the men of Anathoth — one hundred and twenty-eight;
the sons of Azmaveth — forty-two;
the sons of Kiriath-arim, Chephirah, and Beeroth — seven hundred and forty-three;
the sons of Ramah and Geba — six hundred and twenty-one;
the men of Michmas — one hundred and twenty-two;
the men of Bethel and Ai — two hundred and twenty-three;
the sons of Nebo — fifty-two;
the sons of Magbish — one hundred and fifty-six;
the sons of the other Elam — one thousand two hundred and fifty-four;
the sons of Harim — three hundred and twenty;
the sons of Lod, Hadid, and Ono — seven hundred and twenty-five;
the sons of Jericho — three hundred and forty-five;
the sons of Senaah — three thousand six hundred and thirty.
The priests: the sons of Jedaiah, of the house of Jeshua — nine hundred and seventy-three;
the sons of Immer — one thousand and fifty-two;
the sons of Pashhur — one thousand two hundred and forty-seven;
the sons of Harim — one thousand and seventeen.
The Levites: the sons of Jeshua and Kadmiel, of the sons of Hodaviah — seventy-four.
The singers: the sons of Asaph — one hundred and twenty-eight.
The sons of the gatekeepers: the sons of Shallum, the sons of Ater, the sons of Talmon, the sons of Akkub, the sons of Hatita, the sons of Shobai — in all, one hundred and thirty-nine.
The temple servants: the sons of Ziha, the sons of Hasupha, the sons of Tabbaoth,
the sons of Keros, the sons of Siaha, the sons of Padon,
the sons of Lebanah, the sons of Hagabah, the sons of Akkub,
the sons of Hagab, the sons of Shamlai, the sons of Hanan,
the sons of Giddel, the sons of Gahar, the sons of Reaiah,
the sons of Rezin, the sons of Nekoda, the sons of Gazzam,
the sons of Uzza, the sons of Paseah, the sons of Besai,
the sons of Asnah, the sons of Meunim, the sons of Nephisim,
the sons of Bakbuk, the sons of Hakupha, the sons of Harhur,
the sons of Bazluth, the sons of Mehida, the sons of Harsha,
the sons of Barkos, the sons of Sisera, the sons of Temah,
the sons of Neziah, the sons of Hatipha.
The sons of Solomon's servants: the sons of Sotai, the sons of Hassophereth, the sons of Peruda,
the sons of Jaalah, the sons of Darkon, the sons of Giddel,
the sons of Shephatiah, the sons of Hattil, the sons of Pochereth-hazzebaim, the sons of Ami.
All the temple servants and the sons of Solomon's servants — three hundred and ninety-two.
And these came up from Tel-melah, Tel-harsha, Cherub, Addan, and Immer — but they could not declare their fathers' houses or their descent, whether they were of Israel:
the sons of Delaiah, the sons of Tobiah, the sons of Nekoda — six hundred and fifty-two.
And of the sons of the priests: the sons of Habaiah, the sons of Hakkoz, the sons of Barzillai — who had taken a wife from the daughters of Barzillai the Gileadite and was called by their name.
These searched for their entries in the genealogical records, but they were not found; so they were excluded from the priesthood as defiled.
And the governor said to them that they were not to eat of the most holy things until a priest should stand with Urim and Thummim.
The whole assembly together: forty-two thousand three hundred and sixty,
besides their male and female servants — these were seven thousand three hundred and thirty-seven — and they had two hundred singing men and singing women.
Their horses: seven hundred and thirty-six; their mules: two hundred and forty-five;
their camels: four hundred and thirty-five; their donkeys: six thousand seven hundred and twenty.
And some of the heads of the fathers' houses, when they came to the house of YHWH which is in Jerusalem, gave freely for the house of God, to set it up on its site.
According to their ability they gave to the treasury of the work: sixty-one thousand darics of gold, five thousand minas of silver, and a hundred priestly garments.
And the priests and the Levites, and some of the people, and the singers and the gatekeepers and the temple servants settled in their towns — and all Israel in their towns.

ezr.3.text: And when the seventh month arrived — the children of Israel being in their towns — the people gathered as one man to Jerusalem.

Then Jeshua son of Jozadak arose, with his brothers the priests, and Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel with his brothers, and they built the altar of the God of Israel, to offer burnt offerings on it as written in the law of Moses, the man of God.
And they set the altar on its bases — for dread was upon them because of the peoples of the lands — and they offered on it burnt offerings to YHWH, burnt offerings morning and evening.
And they kept the Feast of Booths as written, with the burnt offering day by day, by number, according to the ordinance, each day's matter on its day;
and afterward the regular burnt offering, and those for the new moons and for all the consecrated appointed feasts of YHWH, and those of everyone bringing a freewill offering to YHWH.
From the first day of the seventh month they began to offer burnt offerings to YHWH — though the temple of YHWH was not yet founded.

And they gave silver to the quarrymen and craftsmen, and food and drink and oil to the Sidonians and Tyrians, to bring cedar logs from Lebanon to the sea at Joppa, by the grant of Cyrus king of Persia to them.

And in the second year of their coming to the house of God at Jerusalem, in the second month, Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Jeshua son of Jozadak began — with the rest of their brothers the priests and the Levites, and all who had come from the captivity to Jerusalem — and they appointed the Levites, from twenty years old and upward, to oversee the work of the house of YHWH.

And Jeshua stood, with his sons and brothers, and Kadmiel with his sons, the sons of Judah, as one — overseeing the workmen in the house of God — with the sons of Henadad, their sons and their brothers, the Levites.

And when the builders laid the foundation of the temple of YHWH, the priests stood in their vestments with trumpets, and the Levites, sons of Asaph, with cymbals, to praise YHWH according to the direction of David king of Israel.

And they sang responsively, praising and giving thanks to YHWH: "For he is good — for his steadfast love is forever upon Israel." And all the people shouted a great shout in praise to YHWH, because the house of YHWH was founded.

But many of the priests and Levites and heads of the fathers' houses — the old men who had seen the first house — wept with a loud voice when the foundation of this house was laid before their eyes, while many shouted aloud for joy;
and the people could not distinguish the sound of the joyful shout from the sound of the people's weeping — for the people shouted a great shout, and the sound was heard from far away.

ezr.4.text: And the adversaries of Judah and Benjamin heard that the children of the exile were building a temple to YHWH, the God of Israel;

and they approached Zerubbabel and the heads of the fathers' houses and said to them: "Let us build with you — for like you, we seek your God, and to him we have been sacrificing since the days of Esarhaddon king of Assyria, who brought us up here."

But Zerubbabel and Jeshua and the rest of the heads of the fathers' houses of Israel said to them: "It is not for you and for us to build a house for our God — for we alone will build to YHWH, the God of Israel, as King Cyrus, the king of Persia, has commanded us."
Then the people of the land weakened the hands of the people of Judah and terrified them from building,
and hired counselors against them to frustrate their plan — all the days of Cyrus king of Persia, even until the reign of Darius king of Persia.
And in the reign of Ahasuerus, at the beginning of his reign, they wrote an accusation against the inhabitants of Judah and Jerusalem.

And in the days of Artaxerxes: Bishlam, Mithredath, Tabeel, and the rest of his colleagues wrote to Artaxerxes king of Persia; and the writing of the letter was written in Aramaic, and translated. (Aramaic:)
Rehum the chancellor and Shimshai the scribe wrote a letter against Jerusalem to Artaxerxes the king, as follows —

then Rehum the chancellor and Shimshai the scribe and the rest of their colleagues: the judges, the envoys, the officials, the Persians, the men of Erech, the Babylonians, the men of Susa (that is, the Elamites),
and the rest of the peoples whom the great and honored Osnappar deported and settled in the city of Samaria and in the rest of the province Beyond-the-River — and now:
this is the copy of the letter they sent to him: "To Artaxerxes the king — your servants, the men of Beyond-the-River. And now:

be it known to the king that the Jews who came up from you to us have come to Jerusalem. They are rebuilding that rebellious and evil city; they are completing the walls and repairing the foundations.
Now be it known to the king that if that city is rebuilt and the walls completed, they will pay no tribute, tax, or toll — and in the end it will damage the kings.
Now, since we eat the salt of the palace, and it is not fitting for us to watch the king's dishonor, therefore we send and inform the king,

so that a search may be made in the book of the records of your fathers. You will find in the book of records and learn that that city is a rebellious city, damaging to kings and provinces, and that sedition has been stirred within her from days of old — for which cause that city was laid waste.
We inform the king that if that city is rebuilt and its walls completed, then you will have no portion in Beyond-the-River."
The king sent an answer: "To Rehum the chancellor and Shimshai the scribe and the rest of their colleagues dwelling in Samaria and the rest of Beyond-the-River: Peace. And now:
the letter you sent us has been read in translation before me,
and by my decree a search was made, and it was found that that city from days of old has risen against kings, and that rebellion and sedition have been made in her.
And mighty kings have been over Jerusalem, ruling over all Beyond-the-River — and tribute, tax, and toll were paid to them.
Now issue a decree to make these men cease, and let that city not be rebuilt until a decree is issued by me.
And take care not to be slack in this matter — why should the damage grow, to the hurt of the kings?"
Then, when the copy of King Artaxerxes' letter was read before Rehum and Shimshai the scribe and their colleagues, they went in haste to Jerusalem, to the Jews, and made them cease by force of arm.
Then the work on the house of God in Jerusalem stopped — and it remained stopped until the second year of the reign of Darius king of Persia.

ezr.5.text: And the prophets — Haggai the prophet and Zechariah son of Iddo — prophesied to the Jews in Judah and Jerusalem, in the name of the God of Israel who was over them.
Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Jeshua son of Jozadak arose and began to build the house of God in Jerusalem — and with them the prophets of God, supporting them.

At that time Tattenai, governor of Beyond-the-River, came to them, with Shethar-bozenai and their colleagues, and said to them: "Who issued you a decree to build this house and to complete this structure?"
Then, accordingly, we told them the names of the men who were building this building.
But the eye of their God was upon the elders of the Jews, and they did not make them cease until the report should go to Darius — and then an answer by letter be returned concerning it.
The copy of the letter that Tattenai, governor of Beyond-the-River, and Shethar-bozenai and his colleagues, the officials in Beyond-the-River, sent to Darius the king —
they sent him a report, and thus was written in it: "To Darius the king: all peace!

Be it known to the king that we went to the province of Judah, to the house of the great God. It is being built with huge stones, and timber is set in the walls; and this work is being done diligently and prospering in their hands.
Then we asked those elders, saying to them: 'Who issued you a decree to build this house and to complete this structure?'
We also asked them their names, to inform you, that we might write down the names of the men at their head.

And this is the answer they returned us: 'We are the servants of the God of the heavens and the earth, and we are rebuilding the house that was built many years before this — which a great king of Israel built and completed.

But because our fathers provoked the God of the heavens to wrath, he gave them into the hand of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, the Chaldean — and he destroyed this house and exiled the people to Babylon.
Yet in the first year of Cyrus king of Babylon, King Cyrus issued a decree to build this house of God.

And also the gold and silver vessels of the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar had taken from the temple in Jerusalem and brought to the temple of Babylon — King Cyrus took these from the temple of Babylon, and they were given to one named Sheshbazzar, whom he had appointed governor.
And he said to him: Take these vessels; go, deposit them in the temple in Jerusalem — and let the house of God be rebuilt on its site.
Then this Sheshbazzar came and laid the foundations of the house of God in Jerusalem — and from then until now it has been under construction, and it is not finished.'

And now, if it please the king, let a search be made in the king's treasure-house there in Babylon, whether it is so that a decree was issued by King Cyrus to build this house of God in Jerusalem — and let the king send us his pleasure concerning this."

ezr.6.text: Then Darius the king issued a decree, and a search was made in the house of the archives, where the treasures were stored in Babylon;
and at Ecbatana, in the citadel that is in the province of Media, a scroll was found — and thus was written in it: "A record:

In the first year of Cyrus the king, King Cyrus issued a decree: Concerning the house of God in Jerusalem — let the house be rebuilt as a place where sacrifices are offered, and let its foundations be retained; its height sixty cubits, its width sixty cubits,
with three courses of huge stones and one course of new timber; and let the cost be paid from the king's house.

And also the gold and silver vessels of the house of God, which Nebuchadnezzar took from the temple in Jerusalem and brought to Babylon — let them be returned, and go back to the temple in Jerusalem, to their place; and you shall deposit them in the house of God."
"Now therefore — Tattenai, governor of Beyond-the-River, Shethar-bozenai, and your colleagues the officials in Beyond-the-River: keep far from there.
Leave the work of this house of God alone. Let the governor of the Jews and the elders of the Jews build this house of God on its site.

And by me a decree is issued as to what you shall do for these elders of the Jews for the building of this house of God: from the royal revenue, the tribute of Beyond-the-River, let the cost be paid to these men diligently, without ceasing.

And whatever is needed — young bulls, rams, and lambs for burnt offerings to the God of the heavens; wheat, salt, wine, and oil, as the priests in Jerusalem require — let it be given them day by day without fail,
that they may offer pleasing sacrifices to the God of the heavens, and pray for the life of the king and his sons.

And by me a decree is issued that if anyone alters this edict, a beam shall be pulled out of his house, and he shall be lifted up and impaled upon it — and his house shall be made a dunghill for this.

And may the God who has made his name dwell there overthrow any king or people who puts out a hand to alter it, to destroy this house of God in Jerusalem. I, Darius, have issued the decree — let it be done diligently."
Then Tattenai, governor of Beyond-the-River, Shethar-bozenai, and their colleagues did diligently just as Darius the king had directed.

And the elders of the Jews built and prospered, through the prophesying of Haggai the prophet and Zechariah son of Iddo. They built and finished it by the decree of the God of Israel and by the decree of Cyrus and Darius and Artaxerxes king of Persia;
and this house was completed on the third day of the month of Adar, in the sixth year of the reign of Darius the king.
And the children of Israel — the priests and the Levites and the rest of the children of the exile — celebrated the dedication of this house of God with joy.

And they offered at the dedication of this house of God a hundred bulls, two hundred rams, four hundred lambs — and as a sin offering for all Israel, twelve male goats, according to the number of the tribes of Israel.
And they set the priests in their divisions and the Levites in their courses for the service of God in Jerusalem, as written in the book of Moses.
And the children of the exile kept the Passover on the fourteenth of the first month;

for the priests and the Levites had purified themselves together — all of them were pure — and they slaughtered the Passover lamb for all the children of the exile, for their brothers the priests, and for themselves.

And the children of Israel who had returned from the exile ate it, together with everyone who had separated himself to them from the impurity of the nations of the land, to seek YHWH, the God of Israel.

And they kept the Feast of Unleavened Bread seven days with joy — for YHWH had made them joyful, and had turned the heart of the king of Assyria toward them, to strengthen their hands in the work of the house of God, the God of Israel.

ezr.7.text: And after these things, in the reign of Artaxerxes king of Persia: Ezra son of Seraiah, son of Azariah, son of Hilkiah,
son of Shallum, son of Zadok, son of Ahitub,
son of Amariah, son of Azariah, son of Meraioth,
son of Zerahiah, son of Uzzi, son of Bukki,
son of Abishua, son of Phinehas, son of Eleazar, son of Aaron the chief priest —

this Ezra came up from Babylon. And he was a scribe skilled in the law of Moses, which YHWH, the God of Israel, had given; and the king granted him all his request, for the hand of YHWH his God was upon him.

And some of the children of Israel went up, with some of the priests and the Levites and the singers and the gatekeepers and the temple servants, to Jerusalem, in the seventh year of Artaxerxes the king.
And he came to Jerusalem in the fifth month — it was the seventh year of the king;
for on the first of the first month the going-up from Babylon was set, and on the first of the fifth month he came to Jerusalem — for the good hand of his God was upon him.
For Ezra had set his heart to seek the law of YHWH, and to do it, and to teach statute and judgment in Israel.
And this is the copy of the letter that King Artaxerxes gave to Ezra the priest, the scribe — the scribe of the words of YHWH's commandments and his statutes for Israel:
"Artaxerxes, king of kings, to Ezra the priest, scribe of the law of the God of the heavens — perfect peace. And now:
by me a decree is issued that everyone in my kingdom of the people of Israel — and of their priests and Levites — who freely offers to go to Jerusalem with you, may go.
For you are sent by the king and his seven counselors to inquire concerning Judah and Jerusalem, according to the law of your God which is in your hand,
and to carry the silver and gold that the king and his counselors have freely offered to the God of Israel, whose dwelling is in Jerusalem,
and all the silver and gold that you find in all the province of Babylon, along with the freewill offering of the people and the priests, freely offered for the house of their God in Jerusalem.
Therefore with this silver you shall diligently buy bulls, rams, and lambs, with their grain offerings and their drink offerings, and offer them on the altar of the house of your God in Jerusalem.
And whatever seems good to you and to your brothers to do with the rest of the silver and gold — do it, according to the will of your God.
And the vessels given you for the service of the house of your God — deliver in full before the God of Jerusalem.
And whatever else is needed for the house of your God, which falls to you to provide — provide it from the king's treasure-house.

And by me — I, Artaxerxes the king — a decree is issued to all the treasurers in Beyond-the-River: whatever Ezra the priest, scribe of the law of the God of the heavens, asks of you, let it be done diligently —
up to a hundred talents of silver, a hundred cors of wheat, a hundred baths of wine, a hundred baths of oil, and salt without limit.
Whatever is decreed by the God of the heavens, let it be done zealously for the house of the God of the heavens — for why should wrath come upon the kingdom of the king and his sons?
And we inform you that upon all the priests and Levites, singers, gatekeepers, temple servants, and servants of this house of God, it shall not be lawful to impose tribute, tax, or toll.

And you, Ezra, by the wisdom of your God which is in your hand: appoint magistrates and judges to judge all the people in Beyond-the-River — all who know the laws of your God; and whoever does not know them, you shall teach.
And whoever will not do the law of your God and the law of the king — let judgment be executed on him diligently, whether for death, or banishment, or confiscation of goods, or imprisonment."
Blessed be YHWH, the God of our fathers, who put such a thing as this into the heart of the king, to beautify the house of YHWH which is in Jerusalem,

and who extended steadfast love to me before the king and his counselors and all the king's mighty princes! And I took courage, for the hand of YHWH my God was upon me, and I gathered leaders from Israel to go up with me.

ezr.8.text: And these are the heads of their fathers' houses, and the genealogy of those who went up with me from Babylon in the reign of Artaxerxes the king:
of the sons of Phinehas — Gershom; of the sons of Ithamar — Daniel; of the sons of David — Hattush;
of the sons of Shecaniah, of the sons of Parosh — Zechariah, and with him were registered a hundred and fifty males;
of the sons of Pahath-moab — Eliehoenai son of Zerahiah, and with him two hundred males;
of the sons of Shecaniah — the son of Jahaziel, and with him three hundred males;
and of the sons of Adin — Ebed son of Jonathan, and with him fifty males;
and of the sons of Elam — Jeshaiah son of Athaliah, and with him seventy males;
and of the sons of Shephatiah — Zebadiah son of Michael, and with him eighty males;
of the sons of Joab — Obadiah son of Jehiel, and with him two hundred and eighteen males;
and of the sons of Shelomith — the son of Josiphiah, and with him a hundred and sixty males;
and of the sons of Bebai — Zechariah son of Bebai, and with him twenty-eight males;
and of the sons of Azgad — Johanan son of Hakkatan, and with him a hundred and ten males;
and of the sons of Adonikam, the last ones — these are their names: Eliphelet, Jeuel, and Shemaiah, and with them sixty males;
and of the sons of Bigvai — Uthai and Zabbud, and with him seventy males.
And I gathered them at the river that runs to Ahava, and we camped there three days. And I reviewed the people and the priests — and of the sons of Levi I found none there.
So I sent for Eliezer, Ariel, Shemaiah, Elnathan, Jarib, Elnathan, Nathan, Zechariah, and Meshullam — leaders — and for Joiarib and Elnathan, men of understanding,

and I sent them out to Iddo, the leader at the place Casiphia; and I put words in their mouths to speak to Iddo and his brothers, the temple servants at the place Casiphia, to bring us ministers for the house of our God.
And by the good hand of our God upon us they brought us a man of insight, of the sons of Mahli, son of Levi, son of Israel — Sherebiah, with his sons and brothers, eighteen;
and Hashabiah, and with him Jeshaiah, of the sons of Merari, his brothers and their sons, twenty;
and of the temple servants, whom David and the princes had given for the service of the Levites — two hundred and twenty temple servants, all of them designated by name.
Then I proclaimed a fast there at the river Ahava, that we might humble ourselves before our God, to seek from him a straight way for us and our little ones and all our possessions.

For I was ashamed to ask the king for a troop and horsemen to help us against the enemy on the way — because we had said to the king: "The hand of our God is upon all who seek him, for good; but his power and his anger are against all who forsake him."
So we fasted and sought our God for this — and he was entreated by us.
Then I set apart twelve of the chiefs of the priests: Sherebiah, Hashabiah, and ten of their brothers with them,

and I weighed out to them the silver and the gold and the vessels — the offering for the house of our God that the king and his counselors and his princes and all Israel who were present had contributed.
I weighed into their hand six hundred and fifty talents of silver, a hundred silver vessels worth talents, a hundred talents of gold,
twenty gold bowls worth a thousand darics, and two vessels of fine bright bronze, precious as gold.
And I said to them: "You are holy to YHWH, and the vessels are holy; and the silver and the gold are a freewill offering to YHWH, the God of your fathers.
Watch and keep them until you weigh them out before the chiefs of the priests and the Levites and the heads of the fathers' houses of Israel in Jerusalem, in the chambers of the house of YHWH."
So the priests and the Levites received the weighed-out silver and gold and vessels, to bring them to Jerusalem, to the house of our God.
And we set out from the river Ahava on the twelfth of the first month, to go to Jerusalem; and the hand of our God was upon us, and he delivered us from the hand of enemy and ambush along the way.
And we came to Jerusalem, and stayed there three days.

And on the fourth day the silver and the gold and the vessels were weighed out in the house of our God, into the hand of Meremoth son of Uriah the priest — with him Eleazar son of Phinehas, and with them Jozabad son of Jeshua and Noadiah son of Binnui, the Levites —
the whole by number and by weight; and the entire weight was written down at that time.

Those who came from the captivity, the children of the exile, offered burnt offerings to the God of Israel: twelve bulls for all Israel, ninety-six rams, seventy-seven lambs, twelve male goats as a sin offering — the whole a burnt offering to YHWH.
And they delivered the king's edicts to the king's satraps and the governors of Beyond-the-River — and these supported the people and the house of God.

ezr.9.text: And when these things were finished, the princes approached me, saying: "The people of Israel and the priests and the Levites have not separated themselves from the peoples of the lands and their abominations — the Canaanite, the Hittite, the Perizzite, the Jebusite, the Ammonite, the Moabite, the Egyptian, and the Amorite;

for they have taken some of their daughters for themselves and for their sons, and the holy seed has intermingled with the peoples of the lands. And the hand of the princes and the officials has been first in this faithlessness."
And when I heard this word, I tore my garment and my robe, and pulled hair from my head and my beard, and sat appalled.
And there gathered to me everyone who trembled at the words of the God of Israel over the faithlessness of the exile; and I sat appalled until the evening offering.
And at the evening offering I rose from my self-affliction — my garment and robe torn — and I bowed on my knees and spread out my hands to YHWH my God,
and said: "My God, I am ashamed and humiliated to lift my face to you, my God — for our iniquities have risen higher than our heads, and our guilt has grown up to the heavens.

From the days of our fathers to this day we have been in great guilt; and for our iniquities we — our kings, our priests — have been given into the hand of the kings of the lands, to the sword, to captivity, to plunder, and to open shame, as it is this day.

And now, for a brief moment, grace has come from YHWH our God, to leave us a remnant of survivors and to give us a tent-peg in his holy place — that our God might brighten our eyes and grant us a little reviving in our bondage.

For we are slaves — yet in our bondage our God has not forsaken us, but has extended steadfast love to us before the kings of Persia, to grant us reviving: to raise up the house of our God and repair its ruins, and to give us a wall in Judah and Jerusalem.
And now, our God — what shall we say after this? For we have forsaken your commandments,

which you commanded by your servants the prophets, saying: 'The land you are entering to possess is a land unclean with the uncleanness of the peoples of the lands — with their abominations that have filled it from end to end with their impurity.

So now, do not give your daughters to their sons, and do not take their daughters for your sons; and never seek their peace or their prosperity — that you may be strong and eat the good of the land, and leave it as an inheritance to your children forever.'
And after all that has come upon us for our evil deeds and our great guilt — though you, our God, have punished us less than our iniquities deserved, and have given us a remnant like this —
shall we again break your commandments and intermarry with the peoples of these abominations? Would you not be angry with us to the point of destruction, until there was no remnant and no survivor?
YHWH, God of Israel — you are righteous, for we are left a remnant of survivors as at this day. Behold, we are before you in our guilt — for none can stand before you because of this."

ezr.10.text: And while Ezra was praying and confessing, weeping and prostrating himself before the house of God, a very great assembly of Israel gathered to him — men and women and children — for the people wept with a great weeping.

And Shecaniah son of Jehiel, of the sons of Elam, answered and said to Ezra: "We have been faithless to our God and have married foreign women from the peoples of the land — yet even now there is hope for Israel concerning this.

So now let us cut a covenant with our God: to send away all these wives and their offspring, according to the counsel of my lord and of those who tremble at the commandment of our God — and let it be done according to the law.
Arise! For the matter rests on you, and we are with you. Be strong, and act."
So Ezra arose and made the chiefs of the priests, the Levites, and all Israel swear to do according to this word — and they swore.

Then Ezra rose from before the house of God and went to the chamber of Jehohanan son of Eliashib; and he went there eating no bread and drinking no water — for he was mourning over the faithlessness of the exile.
And they sent a proclamation through Judah and Jerusalem to all the children of the exile, to assemble at Jerusalem;
and whoever did not come within three days — by the counsel of the princes and the elders, all his property would be devoted to destruction, and he himself excluded from the assembly of the exile.

So all the men of Judah and Benjamin assembled at Jerusalem within the three days — it was the ninth month, on the twentieth of the month — and all the people sat in the square of the house of God, trembling because of the matter, and because of the rains.
And Ezra the priest stood up and said to them: "You have been faithless and have married foreign women, adding to the guilt of Israel.
Now then, give thanks and confession to YHWH, the God of your fathers, and do his will: separate yourselves from the peoples of the land, and from the foreign women."
And all the assembly answered and said with a great voice: "Yes — it is on us to do according to your word.
But the people are many, and it is the season of rains; there is no strength to stand outside — and the work is not for one day or two, for we have transgressed greatly in this matter.

Let our princes stand for the whole assembly; and let everyone in our towns who has married a foreign woman come at appointed times, and with them the elders and judges of each town — until the burning anger of our God over this matter is turned away from us."
Only Jonathan son of Asahel and Jahzeiah son of Tikvah stood against this — and Meshullam and Shabbethai the Levite supported them.

And the children of the exile did so. And Ezra the priest selected men, heads of the fathers' houses by their fathers' houses, all of them by name; and they sat down on the first day of the tenth month to examine the matter,
and they finished with all the men who had married foreign women by the first day of the first month.
And among the sons of the priests there were found who had married foreign women: of the sons of Jeshua son of Jozadak and his brothers — Maaseiah, Eliezer, Jarib, and Gedaliah;
and they gave their hand in pledge to send away their wives, and being guilty, they offered a ram of the flock for their guilt.
And of the sons of Immer: Hanani and Zebadiah;
and of the sons of Harim: Maaseiah, Elijah, Shemaiah, Jehiel, and Uzziah;
and of the sons of Pashhur: Elioenai, Maaseiah, Ishmael, Nethanel, Jozabad, and Elasah.
And of the Levites: Jozabad, Shimei, Kelaiah (that is, Kelita), Pethahiah, Judah, and Eliezer;
and of the singers: Eliashib; and of the gatekeepers: Shallum, Telem, and Uri.
And of Israel — of the sons of Parosh: Ramiah, Izziah, Malchijah, Mijamin, Eleazar, Malchijah, and Benaiah;
and of the sons of Elam: Mattaniah, Zechariah, Jehiel, Abdi, Jeremoth, and Elijah;
and of the sons of Zattu: Elioenai, Eliashib, Mattaniah, Jeremoth, Zabad, and Aziza;
and of the sons of Bebai: Jehohanan, Hananiah, Zabbai, Athlai;
and of the sons of Bani: Meshullam, Malluch, Adaiah, Jashub, Sheal, and Jeremoth;
and of the sons of Pahath-moab: Adna, Chelal, Benaiah, Maaseiah, Mattaniah, Bezalel, Binnui, and Manasseh;
and the sons of Harim: Eliezer, Isshijah, Malchijah, Shemaiah, Shimeon,
Benjamin, Malluch, Shemariah;
of the sons of Hashum: Mattenai, Mattattah, Zabad, Eliphelet, Jeremai, Manasseh, Shimei;
of the sons of Bani: Maadai, Amram, and Uel,
Benaiah, Bedeiah, Cheluhi,
Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib,
Mattaniah, Mattenai, and Jaasu,
and Bani and Binnui, Shimei,
and Shelemiah and Nathan and Adaiah,
Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai,
Azarel and Shelemiah, Shemariah,
Shallum, Amariah, Joseph;
of the sons of Nebo: Jeiel, Mattithiah, Zabad, Zebina, Jaddai, and Joel, Benaiah.
All these had taken foreign wives — and some of the wives had borne children.

neh.1.text: The words of Nehemiah son of Hacaliah. And it came to pass in the month of Chislev, in the twentieth year, while I was in Susa the citadel,
that Hanani, one of my brothers, came with men from Judah; and I asked them about the Jews — the escaped remnant left from the captivity — and about Jerusalem.

And they said to me: "The survivors left from the captivity, there in the province, are in great trouble and disgrace; and the wall of Jerusalem is broken through, and her gates have been burned with fire."
And when I heard these words, I sat down and wept, and mourned for days; and I kept fasting and praying before the God of the heavens.
And I said: "Please, YHWH, God of the heavens — the great and awesome God, keeping covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments —

let your ear be attentive and your eyes open, to hear the prayer of your servant that I am praying before you today, day and night, for the children of Israel your servants — confessing the sins of the children of Israel that we have sinned against you. I and my father's house have sinned.
We have dealt corruptly, corruptly with you: we have not kept the commandments and the statutes and the judgments that you commanded Moses your servant.
Remember, please, the word you commanded Moses your servant: 'If you are faithless, I will scatter you among the peoples;

but if you return to me and keep my commandments and do them — though your outcasts be at the edge of the heavens, from there I will gather them and bring them to the place I have chosen to make my name dwell.'
And they are your servants and your people, whom you redeemed by your great power and your strong hand.

Please, my Lord — let your ear be attentive to the prayer of your servant, and to the prayer of your servants who delight to fear your name; and give your servant success today, and grant him compassion before this man." Now I was cupbearer to the king.

neh.2.text: And it came to pass in the month of Nisan, in the twentieth year of Artaxerxes the king: wine was before him, and I lifted the wine and gave it to the king. Now I had never been downcast in his presence;
and the king said to me, "Why is your face downcast, when you are not ill? This is nothing but sadness of heart." And I was very greatly afraid.
And I said to the king: "May the king live forever! Why should my face not be downcast, when the city — the house of my fathers' graves — lies in ruins, and her gates have been eaten by fire?"
And the king said to me, "What is it you are asking?" So I prayed to the God of the heavens,
and said to the king: "If it please the king, and if your servant has found favor before you — that you would send me to Judah, to the city of my fathers' graves, that I may rebuild it."
And the king said to me — with the queen sitting beside him — "How long will your journey be, and when will you return?" So it pleased the king to send me, and I set him a time.
And I said to the king: "If it please the king, let letters be given me to the governors of Beyond-the-River, that they may let me pass through until I come to Judah;

and a letter to Asaph, keeper of the king's forest, that he may give me timber to beam the gates of the citadel by the temple, and for the wall of the city, and for the house I shall enter." And the king granted it to me — for the good hand of my God was upon me.
And I came to the governors of Beyond-the-River and gave them the king's letters. And the king had sent with me officers of the army and horsemen.
And when Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite servant heard of it, it displeased them with a great displeasure that a man had come to seek the good of the children of Israel.
So I came to Jerusalem, and was there three days.
And I rose in the night, I and a few men with me — and I told no one what my God was putting into my heart to do for Jerusalem; and there was no animal with me except the animal I was riding.
And I went out by night through the Valley Gate, toward the Dragon Spring and to the Dung Gate; and I was inspecting the walls of Jerusalem, which were broken through, and her gates, eaten by fire.
And I passed on to the Fountain Gate and to the King's Pool — and there was no room for the animal under me to pass.
So I went up the ravine by night, inspecting the wall; then I turned back and came in through the Valley Gate, and so returned.
And the officials did not know where I had gone or what I was doing — for I had not yet told the Jews, the priests, the nobles, the officials, or the rest who would do the work.
Then I said to them: "You see the trouble we are in — that Jerusalem lies in ruins and her gates are burned with fire. Come, let us build the wall of Jerusalem, that we may no longer be a disgrace."

And I told them of the hand of my God, which was good upon me, and also the king's words that he had spoken to me. And they said, "Let us rise and build!" — and they strengthened their hands for the good work.

But when Sanballat the Horonite and Tobiah the Ammonite servant and Geshem the Arab heard of it, they mocked us and despised us, and said, "What is this thing you are doing? Are you rebelling against the king?"

And I returned them an answer and said to them: "The God of the heavens — he will give us success, and we his servants will rise and build. But you have no portion, no right, and no memorial in Jerusalem."

neh.3.text: Then Eliashib the high priest rose with his brothers the priests, and they built the Sheep Gate: they consecrated it and set up its doors; as far as the Tower of the Hundred they consecrated it, up to the Tower of Hananel.
And next to him the men of Jericho built; and next to him Zaccur son of Imri built.
And the Fish Gate the sons of Hassenaah built: they laid its beams and set up its doors, its bolts, and its bars.
And next to them Meremoth son of Uriah, son of Hakkoz, made repairs; and next to them Meshullam son of Berechiah, son of Meshezabel, made repairs; and next to them Zadok son of Baana made repairs.
And next to them the Tekoites made repairs — but their nobles would not bend their necks to the service of their Lord.
And the Old Gate Joiada son of Paseah and Meshullam son of Besodeiah repaired: they laid its beams and set up its doors, its bolts, and its bars.
And next to them Melatiah the Gibeonite and Jadon the Meronothite made repairs — men of Gibeon and Mizpah, under the seat of the governor of Beyond-the-River.
Next to him Uzziel son of Harhaiah, of the goldsmiths, made repairs; and next to him Hananiah, of the perfumers, made repairs — and they restored Jerusalem as far as the Broad Wall.
And next to them Rephaiah son of Hur, chief of half the district of Jerusalem, made repairs.
And next to them Jedaiah son of Harumaph made repairs, opposite his own house; and next to him Hattush son of Hashabneiah made repairs.
A second section Malchijah son of Harim and Hasshub son of Pahath-moab repaired — and the Tower of the Ovens.
And next to him Shallum son of Hallohesh, chief of half the district of Jerusalem, made repairs — he and his daughters.
The Valley Gate Hanun repaired, with the inhabitants of Zanoah: they built it and set up its doors, its bolts, and its bars — and a thousand cubits of the wall, as far as the Dung Gate.
And the Dung Gate Malchijah son of Rechab, chief of the district of Beth-haccherem, repaired: he built it and set up its doors, its bolts, and its bars.

And the Fountain Gate Shallun son of Col-hozeh, chief of the district of Mizpah, repaired: he built it and roofed it and set up its doors, its bolts, and its bars — and the wall of the Pool of Shelah by the king's garden, as far as the steps going down from the City of David.
After him Nehemiah son of Azbuk, chief of half the district of Beth-zur, made repairs — to a point opposite the graves of David, and to the artificial pool, and to the House of the Mighty Men.
After him the Levites made repairs: Rehum son of Bani; next to him Hashabiah, chief of half the district of Keilah, made repairs for his district.
After him their brothers made repairs: Bavvai son of Henadad, chief of half the district of Keilah.
And next to him Ezer son of Jeshua, chief of Mizpah, repaired a second section, opposite the ascent to the armory at the Angle.
After him Baruch son of Zabbai burned with zeal and repaired a second section, from the Angle to the door of the house of Eliashib the high priest.
After him Meremoth son of Uriah, son of Hakkoz, repaired a second section, from the door of Eliashib's house to the end of Eliashib's house.
And after him the priests, the men of the Plain, made repairs.
After them Benjamin and Hasshub made repairs opposite their house; after them Azariah son of Maaseiah, son of Ananiah, made repairs beside his house.
After him Binnui son of Henadad repaired a second section, from the house of Azariah to the Angle and to the Corner.
Palal son of Uzai worked opposite the Angle and the tower projecting from the upper king's house, by the court of the guard; after him Pedaiah son of Parosh —
and the temple servants were living on the Ophel — to a point opposite the Water Gate on the east, and the projecting tower.
After him the Tekoites repaired a second section, from opposite the great projecting tower to the wall of the Ophel.
Above the Horse Gate the priests made repairs, each opposite his own house.
After them Zadok son of Immer made repairs opposite his house; and after him Shemaiah son of Shecaniah, keeper of the East Gate, made repairs.
After him Hananiah son of Shelemiah and Hanun, sixth son of Zalaph, repaired a second section; after him Meshullam son of Berechiah made repairs opposite his chamber.
After him Malchijah, one of the goldsmiths, made repairs as far as the house of the temple servants and the merchants, opposite the Muster Gate, and to the upper chamber of the corner;
and between the upper chamber of the corner and the Sheep Gate, the goldsmiths and the merchants made repairs.
And it came to pass, when Sanballat heard that we were building the wall, that it burned within him; he was greatly angered, and he mocked the Jews.

And he said before his brothers and the army of Samaria: "What are these feeble Jews doing? Will they leave it to themselves? Will they sacrifice? Will they finish in a day? Will they bring the stones back to life out of the heaps of rubble — burned as they are?"
And Tobiah the Ammonite was beside him, and he said, "Even what they are building — if a fox climbed up on it, he would break their stone wall down!"
Hear, O our God, for we are despised! Turn their taunt back on their own heads, and give them up as plunder in a land of captivity.
Do not cover their iniquity, and let not their sin be blotted out from before you — for they have provoked you to anger before the builders.
So we built the wall; and the whole wall was joined together to half its height — for the people had a heart to work.

neh.4.text: And it came to pass, when Sanballat and Tobiah and the Arabs and the Ammonites and the Ashdodites heard that healing had come up on the walls of Jerusalem — that the breaches were beginning to be closed — that it burned in them exceedingly;
and they all conspired together to come and fight against Jerusalem, and to make confusion in it.
But we prayed to our God, and set a guard against them day and night because of them.
And Judah said: "The strength of the burden-bearer is failing, and there is so much rubble — we cannot build the wall."
And our adversaries said: "They will not know and will not see until we come among them and kill them and stop the work."
And it came to pass, when the Jews living near them came, they said to us ten times, "From every place — turn back to us!"
So I stationed men in the lowest places behind the wall, in the exposed spots; I stationed the people by families, with their swords, their spears, and their bows.

And I looked, and rose, and said to the nobles and the officials and the rest of the people: "Do not fear them! Remember the Lord, great and awesome — and fight for your brothers, your sons and your daughters, your wives and your houses!"
And it came to pass, when our enemies heard that it was known to us, and that God had frustrated their plan, that we all returned to the wall, each to his work.

And from that day on, half of my young men labored at the work, and half of them held the spears, the shields, the bows, and the coats of mail — and the officers stood behind the whole house of Judah.
Those building the wall and those carrying the loads were also armed: with one hand each did the work, and with the other he grasped the weapon;
and the builders each had his sword strapped at his waist as he built — and the trumpeter of the shofar stood beside me.
And I said to the nobles and the officials and the rest of the people: "The work is great and spread out, and we are separated on the wall, far from one another.
At whatever place you hear the sound of the shofar — gather there to us. Our God will fight for us!"
So we labored at the work, with half of them holding the spears, from the rising of the dawn until the coming out of the stars.
At that time I also said to the people: "Let every man and his servant spend the night inside Jerusalem, so that they may be a guard for us by night and a workforce by day."
And neither I nor my brothers nor my young men nor the men of the guard behind me — none of us took off our clothes; each kept his weapon even at the water.

neh.5.text: And there arose a great outcry of the people and their wives against their brothers the Jews.
There were those who said: "Our sons and our daughters — we are many; let us get grain, that we may eat and live!"
And there were those who said: "Our fields and our vineyards and our houses we are pledging, to get grain in the famine."
And there were those who said: "We have borrowed silver against our fields and our vineyards for the king's tribute —

and now, our flesh is as the flesh of our brothers, our children as their children; and behold, we are forcing our sons and daughters into slavery — some of our daughters are enslaved already — and it is not in the power of our hands, for our fields and vineyards belong to others."
And it burned within me exceedingly when I heard their outcry and these words.

And my heart took counsel within me, and I contended with the nobles and the officials, and said to them: "You are exacting interest, each from his own brother!" And I set a great assembly against them,

and said to them: "We, as far as we were able, have bought back our brothers the Jews who were sold to the nations — and now you would sell your own brothers, that they may be sold back to us!" And they were silent, and found not a word.
And I said: "The thing you are doing is not good. Should you not walk in the fear of our God, to escape the taunting of the nations, our enemies?
I too — my brothers and my young men — are lending them silver and grain. Let us abandon this exacting of debt!

Return to them this very day their fields, their vineyards, their olive groves, and their houses — and the hundredth part of the silver and the grain, the new wine and the oil, that you have been exacting from them."
And they said: "We will return it, and we will require nothing from them; we will do just as you say." Then I called the priests and made them swear to do according to this word.

I also shook out the fold of my garment and said: "So may God shake out from his house and from his labor every man who does not keep this word — so may he be shaken out and emptied!" And all the assembly said, "Amen!" and praised YHWH. And the people did according to this word.

Moreover, from the day I was appointed to be their governor in the land of Judah — from the twentieth year to the thirty-second year of Artaxerxes the king, twelve years — neither I nor my brothers ate the governor's food-allowance.

But the former governors who were before me laid heavy burdens on the people, and took from them bread and wine besides forty shekels of silver; even their servants lorded it over the people. But I did not do so, because of the fear of God.
And I also held fast to the work on this wall; we bought no field, and all my young men were gathered there for the work.
And the Jews and the officials — a hundred and fifty men — and those who came to us from the nations around us were at my table;

and what was prepared for one day was one ox, six choice sheep, and birds were prepared for me, and every ten days all kinds of wine in abundance. Yet with all this I did not demand the governor's food-allowance — because the service lay heavy on this people.
Remember for my good, O my God, all that I have done for this people.

neh.6.text: And it came to pass, when it was reported to Sanballat and Tobiah and to Geshem the Arab and to the rest of our enemies that I had built the wall and that no breach was left in it — though up to that time I had not yet set up the doors in the gates —
