I have sent him to you for this very purpose: that you may know how we are, and that he may comfort your hearts.
Peace to the brothers, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
Grace be with all who love our Lord Jesus Christ — in imperishability.

dan.1.text: In the third year of the reign of Jehoiakim king of Judah, Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came to Jerusalem and besieged it.

And the Lord gave Jehoiakim king of Judah into his hand, with some of the vessels of the house of God; and he brought them to the land of Shinar, to the house of his god — and the vessels he brought into the treasure-house of his god.
And the king told Ashpenaz, the chief of his court officials, to bring some of the children of Israel — of the royal seed and of the nobles:

youths in whom was no blemish, good-looking, skilled in all wisdom, knowing knowledge and understanding learning, with strength in them to stand in the king's palace — and to teach them the writing and the tongue of the Chaldeans.
And the king assigned them a daily portion from the king's fine food and from the wine he drank, and that they be raised three years — and at their end, they would stand before the king.
Among them, from the sons of Judah, were Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah;
and the chief of the officials gave them names: to Daniel he gave Belteshazzar; to Hananiah, Shadrach; to Mishael, Meshach; and to Azariah, Abed-nego.
But Daniel set it on his heart that he would not defile himself with the king's fine food or with the wine he drank; and he sought from the chief of the officials leave not to defile himself.
And God gave Daniel steadfast love and compassion before the chief of the officials;

but the chief of the officials said to Daniel: "I fear my lord the king, who assigned your food and your drink. Why should he see your faces more haggard than the youths of your own age — and you would make my head guilty before the king!"
Then Daniel said to the steward whom the chief of the officials had appointed over Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah:
"Test your servants, please, for ten days: let them give us some of the seed-plants, and we will eat — and water, and we will drink;
then let our appearance and the appearance of the youths who eat the king's fine food be seen before you — and deal with your servants according to what you see."
And he listened to them in this matter, and tested them ten days;
and at the end of ten days their appearance looked better and fuller in flesh than all the youths who ate the king's fine food.
So the steward kept carrying away their fine food and the wine they were to drink — and gave them seed-plants.
And these four youths — God gave them learning and skill in every writing and wisdom; and Daniel understood every vision and dreams.
And at the end of the days the king had said to bring them in, the chief of the officials brought them before Nebuchadnezzar;
and the king spoke with them — and among them all none was found like Daniel, Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah; so they stood before the king.
And in every matter of wisdom and understanding that the king sought from them, he found them ten hands better than all the magicians and enchanters in all his kingdom.
And Daniel remained until the first year of King Cyrus.

dan.2.text: And in the second year of the reign of Nebuchadnezzar, Nebuchadnezzar dreamed dreams; and his spirit was pounded, and his sleep was finished for him.
And the king said to call the magicians and the enchanters and the sorcerers and the Chaldeans, to tell the king his dreams; and they came and stood before the king.
And the king said to them: "I have dreamed a dream, and my spirit is pounded to know the dream."
And the Chaldeans spoke to the king in Aramaic: "O king, live forever! Tell the dream to your servants, and we will declare the interpretation."

The king answered and said to the Chaldeans: "The word from me is final: if you do not make known to me the dream and its interpretation, you shall be cut in pieces, and your houses shall be made dunghills;
but if you declare the dream and its interpretation, you shall receive from before me gifts and rewards and great honor. Only — declare to me the dream and its interpretation!"
They answered a second time and said: "Let the king tell the dream to his servants, and we will declare the interpretation."
The king answered and said: "I know for certain that you are buying time — because you have seen that the word from me is final:

that if you do not make the dream known to me, there is but one sentence for you. You have conspired to speak a lying and corrupt word before me until the time changes. Therefore tell me the dream — and I shall know that you can declare me its interpretation."

The Chaldeans answered before the king and said: "There is no man on the dry land who can declare the king's matter — because no great and ruling king has ever asked a thing like this of any magician or enchanter or Chaldean.
The thing the king asks is too weighty, and there is no other who can declare it before the king — except gods, whose dwelling is not with flesh."
Because of this the king raged and was furious exceedingly, and said to destroy all the wise men of Babylon;
and the decree went out, and the wise men were being killed — and they sought Daniel and his companions, to be killed.
Then Daniel returned counsel and discretion to Arioch, the chief of the king's executioners, who had gone out to kill the wise men of Babylon;
he answered and said to Arioch, the king's officer: "Why is the decree from before the king so harsh?" Then Arioch made the matter known to Daniel;
and Daniel went in and asked of the king that he give him time — and he would declare the interpretation to the king.
Then Daniel went to his house and made the matter known to Hananiah, Mishael, and Azariah, his companions —
to seek compassion from before the God of the heavens concerning this mystery, so that Daniel and his companions might not be destroyed with the rest of the wise men of Babylon.
Then the mystery was unveiled to Daniel in a vision of the night; then Daniel blessed the God of the heavens.
Daniel answered and said: "May the name of God be blessed from age to age — for the wisdom and the might are his.
And it is he who changes the times and the seasons; he removes kings and sets up kings; he gives wisdom to the wise, and knowledge to those who know understanding.
It is he who unveils the deep things and the hidden; he knows what is in the darkness — and the light dwells with him.

To you, God of my fathers, I give thanks and praise: for you have given me the wisdom and the might — and now you have made known to me what we sought from you, for you have made known to us the king's matter."

Because of this Daniel went in to Arioch, whom the king had appointed to destroy the wise men of Babylon; he went and said this to him: "Do not destroy the wise men of Babylon! Bring me in before the king, and I will declare the interpretation to the king."
Then Arioch in haste brought Daniel in before the king, and said this to him: "I have found a man among the sons of the exile of Judah who will make the interpretation known to the king!"
The king answered and said to Daniel, whose name was Belteshazzar: "Are you able to make known to me the dream I saw — and its interpretation?"
Daniel answered before the king and said: "The mystery the king asks — no wise men, enchanters, magicians, or diviners can declare it to the king.

But there is a God in the heavens who unveils mysteries, and he has made known to King Nebuchadnezzar what shall be in the after-part of the days. Your dream, and the visions of your head upon your bed — this is it:
You, O king — on your bed your thoughts came up: what shall be after this? And the Unveiler of Mysteries has made known to you what shall be.

And as for me — not by any wisdom in me beyond all the living has this mystery been unveiled to me, but so that the interpretation might be made known to the king, and that you might know the thoughts of your heart.
You, O king, were watching — and behold: one great image. That image was huge, and its brightness surpassing; it stood before you, and its appearance was dreadful.
That image: its head of fine gold, its chest and its arms of silver, its belly and its thighs of bronze,
its legs of iron, its feet partly of iron and partly of clay.
You were watching — until a stone was cut out, not by hands, and it struck the image on its feet of iron and clay, and crushed them.

Then the iron, the clay, the bronze, the silver, and the gold were crushed together, and became like chaff from the summer threshing floors; and the wind carried them away, and no place was found for them. And the stone that struck the image became a great mountain — and filled the whole earth.
This is the dream — and its interpretation we shall speak before the king.
You, O king — king of kings, to whom the God of the heavens has given the kingdom, the power, the might, and the honor;
and wherever the children of men dwell, the beasts of the field and the birds of the heavens he has given into your hand, and made you ruler over them all — you are the head of gold.
And after you shall arise another kingdom, lower than yours; and a third kingdom, another, of bronze, which shall rule over all the earth.
And a fourth kingdom shall be strong as iron — because iron crushes and hammers everything; and like iron that shatters, it shall crush and shatter all these.

And as you saw the feet and toes partly of potter's clay and partly of iron: it shall be a divided kingdom, yet some of the firmness of iron shall be in it — just as you saw the iron mixed with the miry clay;
and as the toes of the feet were partly iron and partly clay: part of the kingdom shall be strong, and part of it brittle.
And as you saw the iron mixed with miry clay: they shall mix themselves with the seed of men, but they shall not hold together, this with that — just as iron does not mix with clay.

And in the days of those kings the God of the heavens shall set up a kingdom that shall never be destroyed, and its kingship shall not be left to another people. It shall crush and bring to an end all these kingdoms — and it shall stand forever;

just as you saw that from the mountain a stone was cut, not by hands, and it crushed the iron, the bronze, the clay, the silver, and the gold. A great God has made known to the king what shall be after this. And the dream is certain, and its interpretation trustworthy."
Then King Nebuchadnezzar fell on his face and paid homage to Daniel, and commanded that an offering and sweet incense be poured out to him.
The king answered Daniel and said: "In truth, your God is the God of gods and the Lord of kings, and an Unveiler of Mysteries — for you were able to unveil this mystery."
Then the king made Daniel great and gave him many great gifts, and made him ruler over the whole province of Babylon, and chief prefect over all the wise men of Babylon.
And Daniel asked of the king, and he appointed Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego over the administration of the province of Babylon — and Daniel remained at the king's gate.

dan.3.text: Nebuchadnezzar the king made an image of gold: its height sixty cubits, its width six cubits. He set it up in the plain of Dura, in the province of Babylon.

And Nebuchadnezzar the king sent to gather the satraps, the prefects and the governors, the counselors, the treasurers, the judges, the magistrates, and all the officials of the provinces, to come to the dedication of the image that Nebuchadnezzar the king had set up.

Then the satraps, the prefects and the governors, the counselors, the treasurers, the judges, the magistrates, and all the officials of the provinces gathered for the dedication of the image that Nebuchadnezzar the king had set up; and they stood before the image Nebuchadnezzar had set up.
And the herald cried aloud: "To you it is commanded, O peoples, nations, and tongues:

at the moment you hear the sound of the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the trigon, the harp, the bagpipe, and every kind of music — you shall fall down and pay homage to the image of gold that Nebuchadnezzar the king has set up;
and whoever does not fall down and pay homage shall in that same hour be thrown into the midst of the furnace of blazing fire."

Therefore at that moment, when all the peoples heard the sound of the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the trigon, the harp, and every kind of music, all the peoples, nations, and tongues fell down, paying homage to the image of gold that Nebuchadnezzar the king had set up.
Therefore at that moment certain Chaldean men came forward and ate the pieces of the Jews — they denounced them.
They answered and said to Nebuchadnezzar the king: "O king, live forever!

You, O king, issued a decree that every man who hears the sound of the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the trigon, the harp, and the bagpipe, and every kind of music, shall fall down and pay homage to the image of gold;
and whoever does not fall down and pay homage shall be thrown into the midst of the furnace of blazing fire.

There are Jewish men whom you appointed over the administration of the province of Babylon — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego. These men, O king, have paid no heed to you: your gods they do not serve, and to the image of gold you set up they do not pay homage."
Then Nebuchadnezzar, in rage and fury, said to bring Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego; then these men were brought before the king.
Nebuchadnezzar answered and said to them: "Is it true, Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego, that you do not serve my gods, and to the image of gold I set up you do not pay homage?

Now — if you are ready, at the moment you hear the sound of the horn, the pipe, the lyre, the trigon, the harp, and the bagpipe, and every kind of music, to fall down and pay homage to the image I made, good. But if you do not pay homage, that same hour you shall be thrown into the midst of the furnace of blazing fire — and who is the god who will rescue you out of my hands?"
Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego answered and said to the king: "O Nebuchadnezzar, we have no need to return you an answer about this.
If our God whom we serve exists, he is able to rescue us from the furnace of blazing fire — and from your hand, O king, he will rescue.
But if not — be it known to you, O king: your gods we will not serve, and to the image of gold you have set up we will not pay homage."

Then Nebuchadnezzar was filled with fury, and the image of his face changed against Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego. He answered and commanded to heat the furnace seven times beyond what was fitting to heat it;
and he commanded mighty men of valor in his army to bind Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego, to throw them into the furnace of blazing fire.
Then these men were bound in their cloaks, their trousers, their turbans, and their garments, and thrown into the midst of the furnace of blazing fire.
Therefore — because the king's word was so harsh and the furnace exceedingly heated — the flame of the fire killed those men who carried up Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego;
and these three men — Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego — fell, bound, into the midst of the furnace of blazing fire.

Then Nebuchadnezzar the king was astonished; he rose in haste, and answered and said to his counselors: "Did we not throw three men, bound, into the midst of the fire?" They answered and said to the king: "Certainly, O king."
He answered and said: "Look! — I see four men, unbound, walking in the midst of the fire, and there is no harm on them; and the appearance of the fourth is like a son of the gods."

Then Nebuchadnezzar drew near to the door of the furnace of blazing fire; he answered and said: "Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego, servants of the Most High God — come out, and come here!" Then Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego came out from the midst of the fire.

And the satraps, the prefects, and the governors, and the king's counselors gathered; they saw these men — that the fire had no power over their bodies: the hair of their heads was not singed, their cloaks were not changed, and the smell of fire had not passed onto them.

Nebuchadnezzar answered and said: "Blessed be the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego, who sent his angel and rescued his servants who trusted in him — who changed the king's word and gave up their bodies rather than serve or pay homage to any god except their own God.

And by me a decree is issued: any people, nation, or tongue that speaks slander against the God of Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego shall be cut in pieces, and his house made a dunghill — because there is no other god who can rescue like this."
Then the king made Shadrach, Meshach, and Abed-nego prosper in the province of Babylon.
Nebuchadnezzar the king — to all the peoples, nations, and tongues dwelling in all the earth: may your peace grow great!
The signs and wonders that the Most High God has worked with me — it has seemed good before me to declare them.
His signs — how great! His wonders — how mighty! His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and his dominion from generation to generation.

dan.4.text: I, Nebuchadnezzar, was at ease in my house, flourishing in my palace.
I saw a dream, and it frightened me; and imaginings on my bed, and the visions of my head alarmed me.
So by me a decree was issued: to bring in before me all the wise men of Babylon, that they might make the interpretation of the dream known to me.
Then the magicians, the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the diviners came in; and I told the dream before them — but its interpretation they could not make known to me.
And at the last there came in before me Daniel, whose name is Belteshazzar, after the name of my god, and in whom is the spirit of the holy gods; and I told the dream before him:

"Belteshazzar, chief of the magicians — because I know that the spirit of the holy gods is in you, and no mystery is too hard for you: the visions of my dream that I saw, and its interpretation — tell them!
The visions of my head upon my bed: I was watching — and behold: a tree in the midst of the earth, and its height was huge.
The tree grew great and strong; its height reached to the heavens, and it was visible to the end of all the earth.

Its foliage was beautiful and its fruit abundant, and in it was food for all; under it the beasts of the field found shade, and in its branches dwelt the birds of the heavens — and from it all flesh was fed.
I was watching, in the visions of my head upon my bed — and behold: a Watcher, a holy one, coming down from the heavens.
He cried aloud and said this: 'Cut down the tree and lop off its branches; strip its foliage and scatter its fruit. Let the beasts flee from under it, and the birds from its branches.

But leave the stump of its roots in the earth — in a band of iron and bronze, in the tender grass of the field; and let it be drenched with the dew of the heavens, and let its portion be with the beasts, in the grass of the earth.
Let its heart be changed from a man's, and let a beast's heart be given it — and let seven seasons pass over it.

By decree of the Watchers is the sentence, and by word of the holy ones the demand — to the end that the living may know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men, and gives it to whom he will, and sets up over it the lowliest of men.'

This is the dream I, King Nebuchadnezzar, saw. And you, Belteshazzar — say the interpretation, since all the wise men of my kingdom cannot make the interpretation known to me. But you can — for the spirit of the holy gods is in you."

Then Daniel, whose name is Belteshazzar, was appalled for about an hour, and his thoughts alarmed him. The king answered and said: "Belteshazzar, let not the dream or the interpretation alarm you." Belteshazzar answered and said: "My lord — the dream be for those who hate you, and its interpretation for your adversaries!
The tree you saw, which grew great and strong, whose height reached to the heavens and which was visible to all the earth,
whose foliage was beautiful and fruit abundant, in which was food for all, under which the beasts of the field dwelt, and in whose branches the birds of the heavens made their home —
it is you, O king: you have grown great and strong; your greatness has grown and reached to the heavens, and your dominion to the end of the earth.

And as the king saw a Watcher, a holy one, coming down from the heavens and saying, 'Cut down the tree and destroy it, but leave the stump of its roots in the earth — in a band of iron and bronze, in the tender grass of the field; let it be drenched with the dew of the heavens, and let its portion be with the beasts of the field, until seven seasons pass over it' —
this is the interpretation, O king, and it is a decree of the Most High that has reached my lord the king:

you shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with the beasts of the field; you shall be fed grass like oxen, and be drenched with the dew of the heavens; and seven seasons shall pass over you — until you know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men, and gives it to whom he will.
And as they said to leave the stump of the tree's roots: your kingdom shall stand for you — from the time you know that the Heavens rule.
Therefore, O king, let my counsel be pleasing to you: break off your sins by righteousness, and your iniquities by showing mercy to the afflicted — if there may be a lengthening of your ease."
All of it came upon Nebuchadnezzar the king.
At the end of twelve months he was walking on the roof of the royal palace of Babylon;
the king answered and said: "Is this not Babylon the great, which I have built as a royal house by the might of my power and for the honor of my majesty?"
The word was still in the king's mouth when a voice fell from the heavens: "To you it is spoken, King Nebuchadnezzar: the kingdom has passed from you!

You shall be driven from among men, and your dwelling shall be with the beasts of the field; you shall be fed grass like oxen, and seven seasons shall pass over you — until you know that the Most High rules in the kingdom of men, and gives it to whom he will."

That same hour the word was fulfilled on Nebuchadnezzar: he was driven from among men, and ate grass like oxen, and his body was drenched with the dew of the heavens — until his hair grew like eagles' feathers, and his nails like birds' claws.

And at the end of the days, I, Nebuchadnezzar, lifted my eyes to the heavens, and my understanding returned to me; and I blessed the Most High, and praised and honored the one who lives forever — whose dominion is an everlasting dominion, and whose kingdom endures from generation to generation.

And all the dwellers of the earth are counted as nothing; he does as he wills with the host of the heavens and the dwellers of the earth — and there is none who can strike his hand and say to him, "What have you done?"

At that time my understanding returned to me; and for the honor of my kingdom, my majesty and my brightness returned to me. My counselors and my nobles sought me out, and I was established over my kingdom — and surpassing greatness was added to me.
Now I, Nebuchadnezzar, praise and exalt and honor the King of the Heavens — for all his works are truth and his ways justice, and those who walk in pride he is able to bring low.

dan.5.text: Belshazzar the king made a great feast for a thousand of his nobles — and before the thousand he drank wine.

Belshazzar, under the taste of the wine, commanded to bring the vessels of gold and silver that Nebuchadnezzar his father had taken from the temple in Jerusalem, that the king and his nobles, his consorts and his concubines, might drink from them.
Then they brought the vessels of gold that had been taken from the temple, the house of God in Jerusalem; and the king and his nobles, his consorts and his concubines, drank from them.
They drank the wine — and praised the gods of gold and silver, bronze, iron, wood, and stone.
In that same hour the fingers of a human hand came out and wrote, opposite the lampstand, on the plaster of the wall of the king's palace — and the king watched the palm of the hand that was writing.
Then the king's brightness changed, and his thoughts alarmed him; the joints of his hips came loose, and his knees knocked one against the other.

The king cried aloud to bring in the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the diviners. The king answered and said to the wise men of Babylon: "Any man who reads this writing and declares to me its interpretation shall be clothed in purple, with a chain of gold around his neck — and he shall rule as third in the kingdom."
Then all the king's wise men came in — but they could not read the writing, nor make the interpretation known to the king.
Then King Belshazzar was greatly alarmed, and his brightness changed upon him — and his nobles were thrown into confusion.

The queen, because of the words of the king and his nobles, came into the banquet house. The queen answered and said: "O king, live forever! Let not your thoughts alarm you, and let not your brightness change.

There is a man in your kingdom in whom is the spirit of the holy gods; and in the days of your father, light and insight and wisdom like the wisdom of the gods were found in him. And King Nebuchadnezzar your father — your father, O king — set him up as chief of the magicians, the enchanters, the Chaldeans, and the diviners,

because a surpassing spirit, and knowledge, and insight — interpreting dreams, declaring riddles, and loosening knots — were found in him: in Daniel, whom the king named Belteshazzar. Now let Daniel be called — and he will declare the interpretation."
Then Daniel was brought in before the king. The king answered and said to Daniel: "Are you that Daniel, of the sons of the exile of Judah, whom the king my father brought from Judah?
I have heard of you: that the spirit of the gods is in you, and that light and insight and surpassing wisdom are found in you.
And now the wise men, the enchanters, were brought in before me, to read this writing and make its interpretation known to me — and they could not declare the interpretation of the matter.

But I have heard of you, that you can interpret interpretations and loosen knots. Now — if you can read the writing and make its interpretation known to me, you shall be clothed in purple, with a chain of gold around your neck, and rule as third in the kingdom."

Then Daniel answered and said before the king: "Let your gifts stay yours, and give your rewards to another. Nevertheless I will read the writing to the king, and make the interpretation known to him.
You, O king — the Most High God gave Nebuchadnezzar your father the kingdom, and greatness, and honor, and majesty;

and because of the greatness he gave him, all the peoples, nations, and tongues trembled and feared before him: whom he would, he killed, and whom he would, he kept alive; whom he would, he raised up, and whom he would, he brought low.
But when his heart lifted itself high and his spirit hardened into arrogance, he was brought down from the throne of his kingdom, and the honor passed from him;

and he was driven from the sons of men, and his heart was made like a beast's; his dwelling was with the wild donkeys, they fed him grass like oxen, and his body was drenched with the dew of the heavens — until he knew that the Most High God rules in the kingdom of men, and sets up over it whom he will.
And you, his son Belshazzar — you have not humbled your heart, though you knew all this;

but you have lifted yourself against the Lord of the Heavens: the vessels of his house were brought before you, and you and your nobles, your consorts and your concubines, drank wine from them; and you praised gods of silver and gold, bronze, iron, wood, and stone — which do not see and do not hear and do not know. But the God in whose hand is your breath, and to whom all your ways belong — him you have not honored.
Then from before him the palm of the hand was sent, and this writing inscribed.
And this is the writing that was inscribed: MENE, MENE, TEKEL, and PARSIN.
This is the interpretation of the matter: MENE — God has numbered your kingdom and finished it;
TEKEL — you have been weighed in the balances and found lacking;
PERES — your kingdom has been divided, and given to Media and Persia."
Then Belshazzar commanded, and they clothed Daniel in purple, with a chain of gold around his neck; and they proclaimed concerning him that he should be third ruler in the kingdom.
That very night Belshazzar, king of the Chaldeans, was killed.

dan.6.text: And Darius the Mede received the kingdom, being about sixty-two years old.
It pleased Darius to set over the kingdom a hundred and twenty satraps, to be throughout the whole kingdom,
and over them three chief ministers — of whom Daniel was one — to whom these satraps would give account, so that the king should suffer no loss.
Then this Daniel kept distinguishing himself above the ministers and the satraps, because a surpassing spirit was in him; and the king planned to set him over the whole kingdom.

Then the ministers and the satraps kept seeking a ground of accusation against Daniel from the side of the kingdom; but they could find no ground and no corruption — because he was faithful, and no negligence or corruption was found in him.
Then these men said: "We shall find no ground against this Daniel — unless we find it against him in the law of his God."
Then these ministers and satraps thronged in to the king and said this to him: "King Darius — live forever!

All the ministers of the kingdom, the prefects and the satraps, the counselors and the governors, have taken counsel together that the king should establish a statute and enforce a ban: that whoever petitions any god or man for thirty days — except from you, O king — shall be thrown into the den of lions.
Now, O king, establish the ban and sign the writing, so that it cannot be changed — according to the law of Media and Persia, which does not pass away."
Therefore King Darius signed the writing and the ban.

And Daniel — when he knew that the writing was signed — went into his house, where the windows of his upper room stood open toward Jerusalem; and three times a day he knelt on his knees and prayed and gave thanks before his God, just as he had done before this.
Then these men thronged in and found Daniel petitioning and pleading before his God.

Then they approached and spoke before the king about the king's ban: "Did you not sign a ban, that any man who petitions any god or man within thirty days — except from you, O king — shall be thrown into the den of lions?" The king answered and said: "The word is certain, according to the law of Media and Persia, which does not pass away."
Then they answered and said before the king: "Daniel, of the sons of the exile of Judah, has paid no heed to you, O king, or to the ban you signed — three times a day he makes his petition!"
Then the king, when he heard the word, was deeply grieved, and set his heart on Daniel, to deliver him; and until the going down of the sun he kept striving to rescue him.
Then these men thronged in to the king and said to the king: "Know, O king, that it is a law of Media and Persia that no ban or statute the king establishes may be changed."
Then the king commanded, and they brought Daniel and threw him into the den of lions. The king answered and said to Daniel: "Your God, whom you serve continually — he himself will rescue you."
And a stone was brought and set over the mouth of the den; and the king sealed it with his own signet and with the signets of his nobles, so that nothing might be changed concerning Daniel.
Then the king went to his palace and spent the night fasting; no diversions were brought before him — and his sleep fled from him.
Then the king rose at dawn, at first light, and went in haste to the den of lions;

and as he drew near the den, he cried out to Daniel in a pained voice. The king answered and said to Daniel: "Daniel, servant of the living God — your God, whom you serve continually: was he able to rescue you from the lions?"
Then Daniel spoke with the king: "O king, live forever!
My God sent his angel and shut the mouth of the lions, and they have not harmed me — because before him innocence was found in me; and also before you, O king, I have done no harm."
Then the king was exceedingly glad, and commanded that Daniel be lifted up out of the den. So Daniel was lifted out of the den — and no harm was found on him, because he had trusted in his God.

And the king commanded, and they brought those men who had eaten Daniel's pieces — who had denounced him — and threw them into the den of lions, them, their children, and their wives; and they had not reached the floor of the den before the lions overpowered them and crushed all their bones.
Then King Darius wrote to all the peoples, nations, and tongues dwelling in all the earth: "May your peace grow great!

From before me a decree is issued that in every dominion of my kingdom men are to tremble and fear before the God of Daniel — for he is the living God, enduring forever; his kingdom shall not be destroyed, and his dominion is unto the end.
He rescues and delivers, and works signs and wonders in the heavens and on the earth — he who rescued Daniel from the paw of the lions."
So this Daniel prospered in the reign of Darius and in the reign of Cyrus the Persian.

dan.7.text: In the first year of Belshazzar king of Babylon, Daniel saw a dream, and visions of his head upon his bed. Then he wrote down the dream; the sum of the matters he told.
Daniel answered and said: "I was watching in my vision with the night — and behold: the four winds of the heavens churning up the Great Sea;
and four great beasts coming up from the sea, each different from the other.

The first was like a lion, and it had eagle's wings. I was watching until its wings were plucked, and it was lifted from the earth and made to stand on two feet like a man — and a man's heart was given it.
And behold: another beast, a second, like a bear. It was raised up on one side, and three ribs were in its mouth between its teeth; and they said this to it: 'Rise — devour much flesh!'
After this I was watching, and behold: another, like a leopard, with four wings of a bird on its back; and the beast had four heads — and dominion was given to it.

After this I was watching in the visions of the night, and behold: a fourth beast — dreadful and terrifying and exceedingly strong. It had great teeth of iron; it devoured and crushed, and what remained it trampled with its feet. And it was different from all the beasts before it — and it had ten horns.

I was considering the horns — and behold, another horn, a little one, came up among them; and three of the first horns were uprooted before it. And behold: eyes like the eyes of a man in this horn — and a mouth speaking great things.

I was watching until thrones were set in place, and an Ancient of Days took his seat. His clothing was white as snow, and the hair of his head like pure wool; his throne was flames of fire, its wheels burning fire.
A river of fire was flowing and going out from before him; a thousand thousands were serving him, and ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him. The court sat — and books were opened.
I kept watching then, because of the sound of the great words the horn was speaking — I was watching until the beast was killed and its body destroyed and given to the burning of fire.
And the rest of the beasts — their dominion was taken away, but a lengthening of life was given them for a season and a time.
I was watching in the visions of the night — and behold: with the clouds of the heavens one like a son of man was coming; and he came to the Ancient of Days, and they brought him near before him.

And to him was given dominion and honor and kingship, that all the peoples, nations, and tongues should serve him. His dominion is an everlasting dominion that shall not pass away, and his kingdom one that shall not be destroyed.
As for me, Daniel — my spirit was distressed within its sheath, and the visions of my head alarmed me.
I drew near to one of those standing there, and asked him the certainty concerning all this; and he told me, and made known to me the interpretation of the matters:
'These great beasts, which are four — four kings shall arise from the earth;
but the holy ones of the Most High shall receive the kingdom, and possess the kingdom forever — even forever and ever.'

Then I desired certainty about the fourth beast, which was different from all of them — exceedingly dreadful, its teeth of iron and its claws of bronze, devouring, crushing, trampling the remainder with its feet;
and about the ten horns on its head, and the other that came up, before which three fell — that horn with eyes and a mouth speaking great things, whose look was greater than its fellows.
I was watching — and that horn made war with the holy ones, and prevailed over them,
until the Ancient of Days came; and judgment was given for the holy ones of the Most High, and the time arrived — and the holy ones took possession of the kingdom.
Thus he said: 'The fourth beast — a fourth kingdom shall be on the earth, different from all the kingdoms; it shall devour the whole earth and thresh it and crush it.
And the ten horns: out of this kingdom ten kings shall arise; and another shall arise after them — and he shall be different from the former ones, and shall bring low three kings.

And he shall speak words against the Most High, and shall wear out the holy ones of the Most High, and shall intend to change the seasons and the law; and they shall be given into his hand for a season, seasons, and half a season.
But the court shall sit, and they shall take away his dominion — to annihilate and destroy it to the end;

and the kingdom and the dominion and the greatness of the kingdoms under all the heavens shall be given to the people of the holy ones of the Most High. Their kingdom is an everlasting kingdom, and all dominions shall serve and obey them.'
Here is the end of the matter. As for me, Daniel — my thoughts alarmed me greatly, and my brightness changed upon me; but I kept the matter in my heart.

dan.8.text: In the third year of the reign of King Belshazzar, a vision appeared to me — to me, Daniel — after the one that had appeared to me at the first.
And I saw in the vision — and it was, as I watched, that I was in Susa the citadel, in the province of Elam; and I saw in the vision that I was beside the Ulai canal.
And I lifted my eyes and saw — and behold: one ram standing before the canal, and it had two horns. The horns were high, but one was higher than the second — and the higher one came up last.
I saw the ram butting westward and northward and southward, and no beast could stand before it, and there was no one to rescue from its hand; it did as it pleased, and grew great.
And as I was considering — behold: a he-goat of the goats coming from the west over the face of the whole earth, not touching the ground; and the goat had a horn of vision between its eyes.
And it came to the ram with the two horns, which I had seen standing before the canal, and ran at it in the fury of its strength.

And I saw it reach the side of the ram; it was embittered against it, and struck the ram and shattered its two horns — and there was no strength in the ram to stand before it. It threw it to the ground and trampled it, and there was no one to rescue the ram from its hand.
And the he-goat grew exceedingly great; but at the height of its strength the great horn was shattered — and four horns of vision came up in its place, toward the four winds of the heavens.
And out of one of them came a single horn, from littleness — and it grew exceedingly great toward the south and toward the east and toward the Beautiful Land.
And it grew great, up to the host of the heavens; and it made some of the host and some of the stars fall to the earth — and trampled them.
Even up to the Prince of the host it made itself great; and from him the regular offering was taken away, and the place of his sanctuary was thrown down.
And a host was given over, along with the regular offering, through transgression; and it threw truth to the ground — and it acted, and prospered.

Then I heard a holy one speaking; and another holy one said to the certain one who spoke: "Until when is the vision — the regular offering, and the desolating transgression, the giving over of both sanctuary and host to be trampled?"
And he said to me: "Until two thousand three hundred evenings-mornings; then the sanctuary shall be set right."
And it came to pass, as I — I, Daniel — was seeing the vision, that I sought understanding; and behold, standing before me was one with the appearance of a man,
and I heard a human voice between the banks of the Ulai; and it called and said: "Gabriel — make this man understand the sight."
So he came beside where I stood; and when he came, I was terrified and fell on my face. And he said to me: "Understand, son of man — for the vision belongs to the time of the end."
And as he spoke with me I fell into deep sleep, my face to the ground; but he touched me and set me up where I had stood,
and said: "Behold, I am making known to you what shall be in the after-part of the indignation — for it belongs to the appointed time of the end.
The ram you saw, with the two horns: the kings of Media and Persia.
And the shaggy he-goat: the king of Greece; and the great horn between its eyes — that is the first king.
And the shattered one, in whose place four arose: four kingdoms shall arise from the nation — but not with its strength.
And in the after-part of their kingdom, when the transgressors have finished their measure, there shall arise a king hard of face, and understanding riddles;
and his power shall grow mighty — but not by his own power. He shall destroy in wondrous ways, and prosper, and act; and he shall destroy the mighty, and the people of the holy ones.

And by his cunning he shall make deceit prosper in his hand; and in his heart he shall grow great, and in the midst of ease he shall destroy many. He shall even stand against the Prince of princes — and he shall be broken, but not by a hand.
And the sight of the evenings and the mornings that was told — it is truth. But you — seal up the vision, for it belongs to many days from now."
And I, Daniel, was spent, and lay sick for days; then I rose and did the king's business — but I was appalled at the sight, and there was no one who understood.

dan.9.text: In the first year of Darius son of Ahasuerus — of the seed of Media, who was made king over the kingdom of the Chaldeans —

in the first year of his reign, I, Daniel, discerned in the books the number of the years of which the word of YHWH had come to Jeremiah the prophet: for the fulfilling of the ruins of Jerusalem — seventy years.
And I set my face toward the Lord God, to seek prayer and pleadings — with fasting, and sackcloth, and ashes.
And I prayed to YHWH my God and confessed, and said: "Please, Lord — the great and awesome God, keeping the covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments:
we have sinned and done wrong; we have acted wickedly and rebelled — turning aside from your commandments and your judgments.
And we have not listened to your servants the prophets, who spoke in your name to our kings, our princes, and our fathers, and to all the people of the land.

To you, Lord, belongs the righteousness — and to us the shame of face, as at this day: to the men of Judah, to the inhabitants of Jerusalem, and to all Israel, those near and those far, in all the lands where you have driven them for the faithlessness they committed against you.
YHWH, to us belongs the shame of face — to our kings, our princes, and our fathers — because we have sinned against you.
To the Lord our God belong the compassions and the forgivenesses — for we have rebelled against him,
and have not listened to the voice of YHWH our God, to walk in his laws, which he set before us by the hand of his servants the prophets.

And all Israel has transgressed your law, turning aside so as not to listen to your voice; so the curse and the oath written in the law of Moses, the servant of God, have been poured out on us — because we sinned against him.

And he has confirmed his words, which he spoke against us and against our judges who judged us, bringing on us a great evil — for under all the heavens nothing has been done like what was done to Jerusalem.
As written in the law of Moses, all this evil has come upon us; yet we have not sought the favor of YHWH our God, turning from our iniquities and gaining insight in your truth.
So YHWH kept watch over the evil and brought it upon us — for YHWH our God is righteous in all his works that he has done, and we have not listened to his voice.
And now, Lord our God — you who brought your people out of the land of Egypt with a strong hand, and made yourself a name, as at this day: we have sinned, we have been wicked.

Lord, according to all your righteous acts, let your anger and your fury turn back, please, from your city Jerusalem, your holy mountain — for because of our sins and the iniquities of our fathers, Jerusalem and your people have become a taunt to all around us.
And now, our God — hear the prayer of your servant and his pleadings, and let your face shine on your desolated sanctuary — for the Lord's own sake.

Incline your ear, my God, and hear! Open your eyes and see our desolations, and the city over which your name is called — for not on our righteous acts do we cast our pleadings before you, but on your abundant compassion.
Lord, hear! Lord, forgive! Lord, attend and act — do not delay! For your own sake, my God — for your name is called over your city and over your people."
And while I was still speaking and praying, confessing my sin and the sin of my people Israel, and casting my plea before YHWH my God for the holy mountain of my God —
while I was still speaking in the prayer, the man Gabriel, whom I had seen in the vision at the first, flying in swift flight, touched me — about the time of the evening offering.
And he gave understanding, and spoke with me, and said: "Daniel — now I have come out to give you insight and understanding.
At the beginning of your pleadings a word went forth, and I have come to declare it — for you are treasured. So understand the word, and gain understanding in the sight:

Seventy sevens are cut off and decreed for your people and for your holy city — to shut up the transgression and seal up sins, to atone for iniquity and bring in everlasting righteousness, to seal vision and prophet, and to anoint a holy of holies.

Know, then, and gain insight: from the going forth of the word to restore and rebuild Jerusalem, until an anointed one, a prince — seven sevens; then for sixty-two sevens it shall be rebuilt, square and moat — but in the distress of the times.

And after the sixty-two sevens an anointed one shall be cut off, and have nothing; and the people of a coming prince shall destroy the city and the sanctuary. Its end shall come with the flood; and to the end — war; desolations are decreed.

And he shall make a covenant prevail with the many for one seven; and for half the seven he shall put a stop to sacrifice and offering. And on the wing of abominations — one who desolates, until the decreed end is poured out on the desolator."

dan.10.text: In the third year of Cyrus king of Persia a word was unveiled to Daniel, whose name was called Belteshazzar; and the word was truth — a great warfare. And he understood the word, and had understanding of the sight.
In those days I, Daniel, was mourning three whole weeks:
I ate no treasured bread; meat and wine did not enter my mouth; and I did not anoint myself at all — until three whole weeks were fulfilled.
And on the twenty-fourth day of the first month, as I was on the bank of the great river — that is, the Tigris —
I lifted my eyes and saw: and behold, one man clothed in linen, his waist girded with fine gold of Uphaz;

his body like beryl, his face like the appearance of lightning, his eyes like torches of fire, his arms and his feet like the gleam of burnished bronze — and the sound of his words like the sound of a multitude.
And I, Daniel, alone saw the vision; the men who were with me did not see the vision — but a great trembling fell on them, and they fled to hide themselves.
So I was left alone, and saw this great vision. No strength remained in me; my dignity was overturned upon me into ruin, and I retained no strength.
And I heard the sound of his words — and as I heard the sound of his words, I fell into deep sleep on my face, my face to the ground.
And behold — a hand touched me, and set me trembling on my knees and the palms of my hands.

And he said to me: "Daniel, man of treasures — understand the words I am speaking to you, and stand where you stood, for now I have been sent to you." And as he spoke this word with me, I stood up, trembling.

And he said to me: "Do not fear, Daniel — for from the first day that you set your heart to understand and to humble yourself before your God, your words were heard; and I have come because of your words.
But the prince of the kingdom of Persia stood against me twenty-one days — and behold, Michael, one of the first princes, came to help me; and I was left there beside the kings of Persia.
And I have come to give you understanding of what shall happen to your people in the after-part of the days — for the vision is yet for those days."
And as he spoke with me according to these words, I set my face to the ground and was struck dumb.

And behold — one in the likeness of the sons of men touched my lips; and I opened my mouth and spoke, and said to the one standing before me: "My lord — at the vision my pangs have overturned upon me, and I retain no strength.
How can this servant of my lord speak with this my lord? As for me — no strength stands in me now, and no breath is left in me."
Then the one with the appearance of a man touched me again and strengthened me,
and said: "Do not fear, man of treasures. Peace to you! Be strong — yes, be strong!" And as he spoke with me I was strengthened, and I said: "Let my lord speak — for you have strengthened me."
And he said: "Do you know why I have come to you? Now I return to fight with the prince of Persia; and when I go out — behold, the prince of Greece comes.
But I will declare to you what is inscribed in the writing of truth. And there is not one who holds strong with me against these — except Michael, your prince.

dan.11.text: And I — in the first year of Darius the Mede, I stood up to strengthen and fortify him.

And now I will declare truth to you: Behold, three more kings shall arise for Persia; and the fourth shall grow richer than all — and when he is strong through his riches, he shall stir up everything against the kingdom of Greece.
Then a mighty king shall arise, and rule a great dominion, and do as he pleases;

but as he arises, his kingdom shall be broken, and divided toward the four winds of the heavens — not to his posterity, and not according to the dominion he ruled; for his kingdom shall be uprooted — for others besides these.
And the king of the South shall grow strong — but one of his princes shall grow stronger than he, and rule; his dominion shall be a great dominion.

And at the end of years they shall ally themselves: the daughter of the king of the South shall come to the king of the North to make an equitable agreement. But she shall not retain the strength of her arm, and neither he nor his arm shall stand; and she shall be given up — she and those who brought her, and he who fathered her, and he who strengthened her in those times.
But from a shoot of her roots one shall arise in his place; and he shall come against the army and enter the fortress of the king of the North, and deal with them, and prevail.
Even their gods, with their cast images and their treasured vessels of silver and gold, he shall carry captive to Egypt; and for some years he shall stand off from the king of the North.
Then the latter shall come into the kingdom of the king of the South — but return to his own soil.
And his sons shall stir themselves for war and gather a multitude of great forces; and one shall surely come, and flood over, and pass through — and return, and war up to his fortress.

Then the king of the South shall be embittered, and go out and fight with him — with the king of the North; and he shall raise a great multitude, but the multitude shall be given into the other's hand.
And when the multitude is carried off, his heart shall be lifted high; he shall bring down tens of thousands — but he shall not prevail.
For the king of the North shall return and raise a multitude greater than the first; and at the end of the times — years — he shall surely come with a great army and abundant supplies.
And in those times many shall stand up against the king of the South; and the sons of the violent among your own people shall lift themselves up, to establish a vision — and they shall stumble.

And the king of the North shall come and pour out a siege-ramp and capture a fortified city; and the arms of the South shall not stand — neither its choicest troops, for there shall be no strength to stand.
And the one who comes against him shall do as he pleases, and none shall stand before him; and he shall stand in the Beautiful Land — with destruction in his hand.

And he shall set his face to come with the strength of his whole kingdom — and equitable terms with him — and he shall act: he shall give him the daughter of women, to destroy it; but it shall not stand, and shall not be his.
Then he shall turn his face to the coastlands and capture many; but a commander shall put a stop to his taunting — indeed, shall return his taunt upon him.
So he shall turn his face back to the fortresses of his own land — and stumble and fall, and be found no more.
And in his place shall arise one who sends an exactor through the glory of the kingdom; but within a few days he shall be broken — not in anger and not in battle.
And in his place shall arise a despised man, on whom the majesty of kingship was not conferred; he shall come in the midst of ease, and seize the kingdom by smooth slipperiness.
And the arms of the flood shall be flooded away from before him and broken — and also a prince of the covenant.
And from the time an alliance is made with him he shall work deceit; he shall rise and grow mighty with a small nation.

In the midst of ease he shall come into the richest parts of the province, and do what his fathers and his fathers' fathers never did: plunder and spoil and goods he shall scatter among them; and against fortresses he shall devise his devisings — but only until a time.

And he shall stir up his power and his heart against the king of the South with a great army; and the king of the South shall stir himself for battle with an exceedingly great and mighty army — but he shall not stand, for they shall devise devisings against him:
those who eat his fine food shall break him; his army shall be flooded away — and many shall fall slain.
And the two kings — their hearts bent on evil — shall speak lies at one table; but it shall not prosper, for the end is still for the appointed time.
Then he shall return to his land with great goods, his heart set against the holy covenant; and he shall act — and return to his own land.
At the appointed time he shall return and come into the South — but the latter time shall not be like the former;

for ships of Kittim shall come against him, and he shall lose heart; and he shall turn back and rage against the holy covenant, and act — and turn back and give heed to those who forsake the holy covenant.
And arms from him shall stand and profane the sanctuary — the fortress; and they shall remove the regular offering and set up the desolating abomination.
And those who act wickedly against the covenant he shall corrupt with smooth words; but the people who know their God shall hold strong and act.
And the insightful among the people shall give understanding to the many; yet they shall stumble by sword and by flame, by captivity and by plunder — for days.
And when they stumble, they shall be helped with a little help — and many shall join them with slippery words;
and some of the insightful shall stumble — to refine among them, and to purify, and to make white, until the time of the end; for it is still for the appointed time.

And the king shall do as he pleases; he shall exalt himself and magnify himself above every god, and against the God of gods he shall speak astonishing things. And he shall prosper until the indignation is finished — for what is decreed shall be done.
And to the gods of his fathers he shall give no heed, nor to the treasure of women, nor to any god — for above all he shall magnify himself.
But to a god of fortresses, in his place, he shall give honor; a god his fathers never knew he shall honor with gold and silver and precious stone and treasured things.

And he shall deal with the strongest fortresses with the help of a foreign god; whoever acknowledges him he shall load with honor — he shall make them rule over the many, and shall parcel out land for a price.

And at the time of the end the king of the South shall lock horns with him; and the king of the North shall storm against him with chariots and horsemen and many ships — and he shall come into the lands and flood over and pass through.
And he shall come into the Beautiful Land, and many shall stumble; but these shall escape from his hand: Edom and Moab and the chief part of the sons of Ammon.
And he shall stretch out his hand against the lands — and the land of Egypt shall not escape:
he shall rule over the hidden hoards of gold and silver and over all the treasures of Egypt; and Libyans and Cushites shall follow at his steps.
But reports from the east and from the north shall alarm him; and he shall go out in great fury to annihilate and devote many to destruction.
And he shall pitch his palace-tents between the seas and the beautiful holy mountain — and he shall come to his end, with no one to help him.

dan.12.text: And at that time Michael shall stand up — the great prince who stands over the sons of your people; and there shall be a time of distress such as has never been since there was a nation until that time. And at that time your people shall escape — everyone found written in the book.
And many of those who sleep in the soil of dust shall awake: these to everlasting life, and these to reproaches — to everlasting abhorrence.
And the insightful shall shine like the shining of the sky-vault — and those who bring the many to righteousness, like the stars, forever and ever.
And you, Daniel — shut up the words and seal the book until the time of the end. Many shall run to and fro, and knowledge shall multiply.
Then I, Daniel, looked — and behold: two others standing, one here on this bank of the river and one there on that bank of the river.
And one said to the man clothed in linen, who was above the waters of the river: "Until when — the end of the wonders?"

And I heard the man clothed in linen, who was above the waters of the river: he raised his right hand and his left to the heavens and swore by the one who lives forever — that it is for an appointed time, appointed times, and a half; and when the shattering of the hand of the holy people is finished, all these things shall be finished.
And I heard, but I did not understand; and I said: "My lord — what is the after-part of these things?"
And he said: "Go, Daniel — for the words are shut up and sealed until the time of the end.
Many shall be purified and made white and refined; and the wicked shall act wickedly, and none of the wicked shall understand — but the insightful shall understand.
And from the time the regular offering is removed and the desolating abomination set up: one thousand two hundred and ninety days.
Happy is the one who waits, and arrives at one thousand three hundred and thirty-five days.
But you — go on to the end. And you shall rest, and you shall stand in your allotted portion at the end of the days."

mrk.4.text: And again he began to teach beside the sea. And a very great crowd gathers to him, so that he got into a boat and sat in it, on the sea — and the whole crowd was toward the sea, on the land.
And he taught them many things in parables, and said to them in his teaching:
"Listen! Behold — the sower went out to sow.
And it came to pass in the sowing: some fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it.
And other seed fell on the rocky ground, where it had not much earth; and immediately it sprang up, because it had no depth of earth —
and when the sun rose it was scorched, and because it had no root it withered.
And other seed fell into the thorns, and the thorns came up and choked it together — and it gave no fruit.
And other seeds fell into the beautiful earth, and gave fruit, coming up and growing — and bore thirtyfold, and sixtyfold, and a hundredfold."
And he said: "Whoever has ears to hear — let him hear."
And when he was alone, those around him with the twelve asked him about the parables.
And he said to them: "To you the mystery of the kingdom of God has been given; but to those outside, everything comes in parables —
so that seeing they may see and not perceive, and hearing they may hear and not understand; lest they turn, and it be forgiven them."
And he says to them: "You do not grasp this parable? Then how will you know all the parables?
The sower sows the word.
And these are the ones along the path where the word is sown: when they hear, immediately Satan comes and carries off the word sown into them.
And likewise these are the ones sown on the rocky ground: when they hear the word, immediately they receive it with joy —
and they have no root in themselves; they last for a season. Then, when affliction or persecution comes because of the word, immediately they fall away.
And others are the ones sown into the thorns: these are the ones who heard the word,
and the worries of the age and the deceit of wealth and the cravings for the rest of things come in and choke the word together — and it becomes fruitless.
And those are the ones sown on the beautiful earth: they hear the word and welcome it, and bear fruit — thirtyfold, and sixtyfold, and a hundredfold."
And he said to them: "Does the lamp come in order to be put under the measuring-basket, or under the bed? Not in order to be set on the lampstand?
For nothing is hidden except in order to be made visible; nothing became a secret except that it should come into the open.
If anyone has ears to hear — let him hear."
And he said to them: "Watch what you hear. With the measure you measure, it will be measured to you — and more will be added to you;
for whoever has, to him will be given; and whoever has not — even what he has will be taken from him."
And he said: "The kingdom of God is like this: like a man who throws the seed on the earth,
and sleeps and rises, night and day — and the seed sprouts and lengthens, he himself does not know how.
Of itself the earth bears fruit: first a blade, then a head, then full grain in the head.
And when the fruit yields itself, immediately he sends out the sickle — because the harvest has come to stand ready."
And he said: "How shall we liken the kingdom of God? Or in what parable shall we set it?
Like a grain of mustard — which, when it is sown on the earth, is smaller than all the seeds on the earth;
and when it is sown, it comes up and becomes greater than all the garden plants, and makes great branches — so that under its shadow the birds of the heaven can nest."
And with many such parables he kept speaking the word to them, according as they were able to hear;
and without a parable he did not speak to them — but privately, to his own disciples, he untied everything.
And he says to them on that day, when evening had come: "Let us cross over to the other side."
And leaving the crowd, they take him along, just as he was, in the boat; and other boats were with him.
And a great windstorm arises, and the waves kept breaking into the boat, so that the boat was already filling.
And he himself was in the stern, sleeping on the cushion. And they wake him and say to him: "Teacher — does it not matter to you that we are perishing?"
And he woke and rebuked the wind, and said to the sea: "Silence! Be muzzled!" And the wind sank down, and there came a great calm.
And he said to them: "Why are you so afraid? Do you not yet have faith?"
And they feared a great fear, and kept saying to one another: "Who then is this — that even the wind and the sea obey him?"

mrk.5.text: And they came to the other side of the sea, into the country of the Gerasenes.
And as he got out of the boat, immediately there met him, out of the tombs, a man in an unclean spirit,
who had his dwelling among the tombs; and no one could bind him any longer, not even with a chain —
because he had often been bound with shackles and chains, and the chains had been torn apart by him and the shackles smashed; and no one had the strength to tame him.
And through it all, night and day, among the tombs and in the mountains, he was crying out and cutting himself with stones.
And seeing Jesus from far off, he ran and fell down before him,
and crying out with a great voice he says: "What have I to do with you, Jesus, Son of the Most High God? I put you under oath by God — do not torment me!"
For he was saying to him: "Come out of the man, unclean spirit!"
And he questioned him: "What is your name?" And he says to him: "Legion is my name — for we are many."
And he kept begging him earnestly not to send them out of the country.
Now there, on the mountainside, a great herd of pigs was feeding;
and they begged him, saying: "Send us into the pigs, that we may enter into them."
And he permitted them. And the unclean spirits came out and entered into the pigs; and the herd rushed down the cliff into the sea — about two thousand — and they were drowned in the sea.
And their herdsmen fled and reported it in the city and in the fields; and people came to see what it was that had happened.
And they come to Jesus and gaze at the demon-possessed man — sitting, clothed, and in his right mind, the man who had had the Legion — and they were afraid.
And those who had seen it recounted to them what had happened to the demon-possessed man, and about the pigs.
And they began to beg him to depart from their borders.
And as he was getting into the boat, the man who had been demon-possessed begged him that he might be with him.
And he did not let him — but says to him: "Go home to your own people, and report to them all that the Lord has done for you, and how he showed you mercy."
And he went off and began to proclaim in the Decapolis all that Jesus had done for him — and everyone marveled.
And when Jesus had crossed over in the boat again to the other side, a great crowd gathered to him; and he was beside the sea.
And one of the synagogue rulers comes — Jairus by name — and seeing him, he falls at his feet,
and begs him earnestly, saying: "My little daughter is at her last. Come — lay your hands on her, that she may be saved and live."
And he went with him. And a great crowd was following him, and they pressed in on him.
And a woman who had had a flow of blood twelve years,
and had suffered much under many physicians, and had spent everything she had and been helped not at all — rather had come to worse —
having heard about Jesus, came up in the crowd behind and touched his garment;
for she kept saying: "If I touch even his garments, I shall be saved."
And immediately the spring of her blood was dried up, and she knew in her body that she was healed of the scourge.
And immediately Jesus, knowing in himself that power had gone out from him, turned around in the crowd and said: "Who touched my garments?"
And his disciples said to him: "You see the crowd pressing in on you — and you say, 'Who touched me?'"
And he kept looking around to see the woman who had done it.
And the woman — fearing and trembling, knowing what had happened to her — came and fell down before him and told him the whole truth.
And he said to her: "Daughter, your faith has saved you. Go into peace — and be whole from your scourge."
While he was still speaking, they come from the synagogue ruler's house, saying: "Your daughter has died. Why trouble the Teacher any longer?"
But Jesus, overhearing the word being spoken, says to the synagogue ruler: "Do not fear — only believe."
And he let no one follow along with him except Peter and James and John, the brother of James.
And they come into the house of the synagogue ruler, and he sees an uproar — people weeping and wailing loudly;
and going in he says to them: "Why this uproar and weeping? The child has not died — she is sleeping."
And they laughed him down. But he, putting them all out, takes along the father of the child and the mother and those with him, and goes in where the child was;
and grasping the child's hand he says to her: "Talitha koum" — which, translated, is: "Little girl, I say to you — arise."
And immediately the little girl stood up and walked around — for she was twelve years old. And immediately they were beside themselves with great amazement.
And he charged them strictly that no one should know this — and said that something be given her to eat.

mrk.6.text: And he went out from there and comes into his hometown; and his disciples follow him.

And when the sabbath came, he began to teach in the synagogue; and the many who heard were astounded, saying: "From where does this man get these things? And what is the wisdom given to him — and such deeds of power coming about through his hands?
Is this not the carpenter, the son of Mary and brother of James and Joses and Judas and Simon? And are not his sisters here with us?" And they stumbled over him.
And Jesus said to them: "A prophet is not without honor — except in his hometown and among his relatives and in his own house."
And he could do no deed of power there — except that he laid hands on a few sick people and healed them;
and he marveled at their unbelief. And he went around the villages in a circuit, teaching.
And he calls the twelve to himself, and began to send them out two by two — and he kept giving them authority over the unclean spirits.
And he commanded them to take nothing for the road except a staff only — no bread, no bag, no copper in the belt —
but to wear sandals; "and do not put on two tunics."
And he said to them: "Wherever you enter a house, stay there until you go out from that place;
and whatever place does not receive you and they do not hear you — going out from there, shake off the dust under your feet, as a testimony to them."
And going out, they proclaimed that people should turn their lives around;
and they kept casting out many demons, and anointing many sick people with oil, and healing them.
And King Herod heard — for his name had become known — and people were saying: "John the Baptizer has been raised from the dead; that is why the powers are at work in him."
But others said, "He is Elijah"; and others said, "A prophet — like one of the prophets."
But Herod, when he heard, kept saying: "The one I beheaded — John — he has been raised."
For Herod himself had sent and seized John and bound him in prison — because of Herodias, the wife of Philip his brother, whom he had married;
for John kept saying to Herod: "It is not lawful for you to have your brother's wife."
And Herodias held it against him and wanted to kill him — and she could not;
for Herod feared John, knowing him to be a righteous and holy man, and kept him safe. And hearing him, he was at a great loss — and yet he heard him gladly.
And an opportune day came, when Herod on his birthday made a banquet for his great men and the commanders and the first men of Galilee;
and when the daughter of Herodias herself came in and danced, she pleased Herod and those reclining with him. The king said to the girl: "Ask me whatever you wish, and I will give it to you";
and he swore to her: "Whatever you ask me, I will give you — up to half my kingdom."
And going out, she said to her mother: "What shall I ask for?" And she said: "The head of John the Baptizer."
And coming in immediately, with haste, to the king, she asked, saying: "I want you to give me at once, on a platter, the head of John the Baptist."
And the king, though deeply grieved — because of the oaths and those reclining at table — did not want to refuse her.
And immediately the king sent an executioner and ordered him to bring his head. And he went off and beheaded him in the prison,
and brought his head on a platter and gave it to the girl — and the girl gave it to her mother.
And when his disciples heard, they came and took his corpse and laid it in a tomb.
And the apostles gather to Jesus, and reported to him everything — all they had done and all they had taught.
And he says to them: "Come, you yourselves, privately, to a desert place — and rest a little." For those coming and going were many, and they had no good moment even to eat.
And they went away in the boat to a desert place, privately.
And many saw them going and recognized them, and ran together there on foot from all the cities — and got there ahead of them.
And getting out, he saw a great crowd; and his inmost being was moved for them, because they were like sheep without a shepherd — and he began to teach them many things.
And when the hour was already late, his disciples came to him and said: "The place is desert, and the hour is already late;
send them away, so that they may go into the surrounding farms and villages and buy themselves something to eat."
But he answered and said to them: "You give them something to eat." And they say to him: "Shall we go off and buy two hundred denarii worth of bread and give it to them to eat?"
And he says to them: "How many loaves do you have? Go — see." And finding out, they say: "Five — and two fish."
And he directed them to have everyone recline, party by party, on the green grass;
and they lay down in plots — garden-bed by garden-bed — by hundreds and by fifties.
And taking the five loaves and the two fish, looking up to heaven, he blessed and broke the loaves, and kept giving them to his disciples to set before them; and the two fish he divided among all.
And they all ate and were filled;
and they took up broken pieces — the fillings of twelve baskets — and some of the fish.
And those who ate the loaves were five thousand men.
And immediately he compelled his disciples to get into the boat and go ahead to the other side, toward Bethsaida, while he himself dismissed the crowd.
And having taken leave of them, he went off to the mountain to pray.
And when evening came, the boat was in the middle of the sea, and he was alone on the land.
And seeing them tormented in the rowing — for the wind was against them — around the fourth watch of the night he comes toward them, walking on the sea; and he intended to pass by them.
But they, seeing him walking on the sea, thought it was a phantom, and cried out —
for they all saw him, and were shaken. But immediately he spoke with them, and says to them: "Take courage. I AM — do not fear."
And he went up to them into the boat — and the wind sank down. And they were utterly, beyond measure, beside themselves within;
for they had not understood about the loaves — rather, their heart had been hardened.
And crossing over to the land, they came to Gennesaret and moored there.
And as they got out of the boat, immediately recognizing him,
people ran through that whole country and began to carry those who were ill around on their mats, to wherever they heard he was;

and wherever he entered — into villages or cities or farms — they laid the sick in the marketplaces, and begged him that they might touch even the fringe of his garment. And as many as touched him were saved.

mrk.7.text: And the Pharisees gather to him, with some of the scribes who had come from Jerusalem;
and they saw that some of his disciples were eating the loaves with common hands — that is, unwashed.
(For the Pharisees, and all the Judeans, do not eat unless they wash their hands with the fist, holding fast the tradition of the elders;

and coming from the marketplace they do not eat unless they immerse themselves; and there are many other things they have received and hold fast: immersings of cups and jugs and bronze vessels and dining couches.)
And the Pharisees and the scribes question him: "Why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders, but eat their bread with common hands?"
And he said to them: "Beautifully did Isaiah prophesy about you hypocrites — as it is written: 'This people honors me with the lips, but their heart holds far off from me;
in emptiness they revere me, teaching as teachings the commandments of men.'
Abandoning the commandment of God, you hold fast the tradition of men."
And he said to them: "How beautifully you set aside the commandment of God — so that you may keep your own tradition!
For Moses said: 'Honor your father and your mother,' and: 'Whoever speaks evil of father or mother — let him end in death.'
But you say: if a man says to his father or mother, 'Korban' — that is, a gift — 'is whatever of mine might have benefited you,'
you no longer let him do anything for his father or mother —
voiding the word of God by your tradition, which you have handed down. And many such things you do."
And calling the crowd to himself again, he said to them: "Hear me, all of you — and understand:
there is nothing outside the human being that, entering into him, can make him common; rather, the things coming out of the human being are what make the human being common."
And when he had entered a house, away from the crowd, his disciples questioned him about the parable.
And he says to them: "Are you also so without understanding? Do you not perceive that everything entering the human being from outside cannot make him common —
because it does not enter into his heart but into the belly, and goes out into the latrine?" — cleansing all foods.
And he said: "What comes out of the human being — that makes the human being common;
for from inside, out of the heart of human beings, come the evil reasonings: sexual immoralities, thefts, murders,
adulteries, greeds, wickednesses, deceit, shamelessness, an evil eye, slander, arrogance, senselessness.
All these evil things come out from inside — and they make the human being common."
And rising up from there, he went away into the borders of Tyre. And entering a house, he wanted no one to know — and he could not stay hidden;
but immediately a woman heard about him — a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit — and coming, she fell down at his feet.
Now the woman was a Greek, Syrophoenician by birth; and she kept asking him to cast the demon out of her daughter.
And he said to her: "Let the children be filled first — for it is not beautiful to take the children's bread and throw it to the little dogs."
But she answered and says to him: "Lord — even the little dogs under the table eat from the children's crumbs."
And he said to her: "Because of this word — go. The demon has gone out of your daughter."
And going away to her house, she found the child laid on the bed — and the demon gone out.
