And again, going out from the borders of Tyre, he came through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee, up through the middle of the borders of the Decapolis.
And they bring him a deaf man who could barely speak, and they beg him to lay his hand on him.
And taking him aside from the crowd, privately, he put his fingers into his ears; and spitting, he touched his tongue;
and looking up to heaven he sighed, and says to him: "Ephphatha" — that is, "Be opened wide."
And his ears were opened, and the bond of his tongue was loosed — and he spoke straight.
And he charged them to tell no one; but the more he charged them, the more abundantly they kept proclaiming it.
And they were astounded beyond all bounds, saying: "He has done all things beautifully — he even makes the deaf hear and the speechless speak!"

mrk.8.text: In those days, when again there was a great crowd and they had nothing to eat, calling the disciples to himself he says to them:
"My inmost being is moved for the crowd — for already three days they have stayed with me, and they have nothing to eat;
and if I send them home fasting, they will give out on the road — and some of them have come from far away."
And his disciples answered him: "From where will anyone be able to fill these people with bread, here in a desolate place?"
And he asked them: "How many loaves do you have?" And they said: "Seven."

And he directs the crowd to recline on the ground; and taking the seven loaves, giving thanks, he broke them and kept giving them to his disciples to set before them — and they set them before the crowd.
And they had a few small fish; and blessing them, he said to set these before them also.
And they ate and were filled; and they took up what was left over of the broken pieces — seven hampers.
Now they were about four thousand. And he sent them away.
And immediately, getting into the boat with his disciples, he came into the district of Dalmanutha.
And the Pharisees came out and began to dispute with him, seeking from him a sign from heaven — testing him.
And groaning deep in his spirit, he says: "Why does this generation seek a sign? Amen, I say to you — no sign shall be given to this generation."
And leaving them, getting in again, he went away to the other side.
And they had forgotten to take loaves — and except for one loaf, they had nothing with them in the boat.
And he kept charging them, saying: "Watch — look out for the leaven of the Pharisees and the leaven of Herod."
And they kept reasoning with one another that they had no loaves.
And knowing it, he says to them: "Why do you reason that you have no loaves? Do you not yet perceive or understand? Has your heart been hardened?
Having eyes, do you not see? And having ears, do you not hear? And do you not remember —
when I broke the five loaves for the five thousand, how many baskets full of broken pieces did you take up?" They say to him: "Twelve."
"And when the seven for the four thousand — the fillings of how many hampers of broken pieces did you take up?" And they say to him: "Seven."
And he said to them: "Do you not yet understand?"
And they come to Bethsaida. And they bring him a blind man, and beg him to touch him.
And taking the blind man's hand, he led him outside the village; and spitting into his eyes, laying his hands on him, he asked him: "Do you see anything?"
And looking up he said: "I see the people — for I see them like trees, walking."
Then again he laid his hands on his eyes — and he saw through clearly, and was restored, and gazed on everything far and bright.
And he sent him to his house, saying: "Do not even enter the village."
And Jesus went out, with his disciples, into the villages of Caesarea Philippi; and on the road he questioned his disciples, saying to them: "Who do people say that I am?"
And they told him, saying: "John the Baptist; and others, Elijah; but others, one of the prophets."
And he questioned them: "But you — who do you say that I am?" Answering, Peter says to him: "You are the Christ."
And he warned them to tell no one about him.
And he began to teach them that the Son of Man must suffer many things, and be rejected by the elders and the chief priests and the scribes, and be killed — and after three days rise.
And he spoke the word with open boldness. And Peter, taking him aside, began to rebuke him.
But he, turning around and seeing his disciples, rebuked Peter — and says: "Get behind me, Satan! For your mind is not on the things of God but on the things of men."
And calling the crowd to himself, with his disciples, he said to them: "If anyone wants to come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross and follow me.
For whoever wants to save his soul will lose it; but whoever loses his soul for my sake and the good news' sake will save it.
For what does it profit a man to gain the whole world — and forfeit his soul?
For what could a man give in exchange for his soul?
For whoever is ashamed of me and of my words in this adulterous and sinful generation — the Son of Man will also be ashamed of him, when he comes in the glory of his Father with the holy angels."

mrk.10.text: And rising up from there, he comes into the borders of Judea and beyond the Jordan; and again crowds journey together to him — and as was his custom, again he taught them.
And they questioned him whether it is lawful for a husband to send away a wife — testing him.
And he answered and said to them: "What did Moses command you?"
And they said: "Moses permitted writing a scroll of divorce and sending her away."
And Jesus said to them: "For your hardness of heart he wrote you this commandment;
but from the beginning of creation, male and female he made them.
For this cause a man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife,
and the two shall become one flesh — so that they are no longer two, but one flesh.
What God, then, has yoked together — let man not separate."
And in the house the disciples questioned him about this again;
and he says to them: "Whoever sends away his wife and marries another commits adultery against her;
and if she, sending away her husband, marries another — she commits adultery."
And they kept bringing him little children, that he might touch them — and the disciples rebuked them.
But Jesus, seeing it, was indignant, and said to them: "Let the little children come to me. Do not hinder them — for of such is the kingdom of God.
Amen, I say to you: whoever does not receive the kingdom of God like a little child shall by no means enter into it."
And folding them in his arms, he kept blessing them richly, laying his hands on them.
And as he was going out onto the road, one man ran up and knelt before him and asked him: "Good Teacher — what shall I do to inherit everlasting life?"
And Jesus said to him: "Why do you call me good? No one is good but one — God.
You know the commandments: Do not murder, Do not commit adultery, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Do not defraud, Honor your father and mother."
And he said to him: "Teacher — all these I have kept from my youth."
And Jesus, gazing at him, loved him, and said to him: "One thing you lack: go, sell all you have and give to the poor — and you will have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me."
But his face fell at the word, and he went away grieving — for he had many possessions.
And looking around, Jesus says to his disciples: "How hard it will be for those who have riches to enter the kingdom of God!"
And the disciples were amazed at his words. But Jesus, answering again, says to them: "Children — how hard it is to enter the kingdom of God!
It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of the needle than for a rich man to enter the kingdom of God."
And they were astounded beyond measure, saying to themselves: "Then who can be saved?"
Gazing at them, Jesus says: "With men, impossible — but not with God; for all things are possible with God."
Peter began to say to him: "Behold — we have left everything and followed you."
Jesus said: "Amen, I say to you: there is no one who has left house or brothers or sisters or mother or father or children or fields for my sake and the good news' sake,
who will not receive a hundredfold now, in this season — houses and brothers and sisters and mothers and children and fields, with persecutions — and in the coming age, everlasting life.
But many first shall be last — and the last, first."

Now they were on the road, going up to Jerusalem, and Jesus was going ahead of them; and they were amazed — and those following were afraid. And taking the twelve aside again, he began to tell them the things about to happen to him:
"Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be handed over to the chief priests and the scribes; and they will condemn him to death and hand him over to the nations,
and they will mock him and spit on him and flog him and kill him — and after three days he will rise."
And James and John, the sons of Zebedee, come up to him, saying to him: "Teacher — we want you to do for us whatever we ask you."
And he said to them: "What do you want me to do for you?"
And they said to him: "Grant us to sit, one at your right and one at your left, in your glory."
But Jesus said to them: "You do not know what you are asking. Can you drink the cup that I drink, or be baptized with the baptism I am baptized with?"
And they said to him: "We can." And Jesus said to them: "The cup that I drink you shall drink, and with the baptism I am baptized with you shall be baptized;
but to sit at my right or at my left is not mine to give — it is for those for whom it has been prepared."
And hearing it, the ten began to be indignant about James and John.
And calling them to himself, Jesus says to them: "You know that those who are counted rulers of the nations lord it over them, and their great ones wield authority down upon them.
But it is not so among you. Rather, whoever wants to become great among you shall be your servant,
and whoever wants to be first among you shall be slave of all;
for even the Son of Man did not come to be served but to serve — and to give his soul as a ransom in the place of many."
And they come to Jericho. And as he was going out from Jericho with his disciples and a considerable crowd, the son of Timaeus — Bartimaeus, a blind beggar — was sitting beside the road.
And hearing that it was Jesus the Nazarene, he began to cry out and say: "Son of David — Jesus — have mercy on me!"
And many rebuked him, that he should be silent; but he cried out all the more: "Son of David, have mercy on me!"
And Jesus stood still and said: "Call him." And they call the blind man, saying to him: "Take courage — rise, he is calling you."
And throwing off his garment, leaping up, he came to Jesus.
And answering him, Jesus said: "What do you want me to do for you?" And the blind man said to him: "Rabbouni — that I may see again."
And Jesus said to him: "Go — your faith has saved you." And immediately he saw again, and he followed him on the road.

mrk.11.text: And when they draw near to Jerusalem, to Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, he sends two of his disciples
and says to them: "Go into the village opposite you, and immediately as you enter it you will find a colt tied, on which no human being has yet sat. Untie it and bring it.
And if anyone says to you, 'Why are you doing this?' — say: 'The Lord has need of it, and he is sending it back here immediately.'"
And they went away and found a colt tied at a door, outside on the street — and they untie it.
And some of those standing there said to them: "What are you doing, untying the colt?"
And they told them just as Jesus had said — and they let them go.
And they bring the colt to Jesus, and they throw their garments over it — and he sat on it.
And many spread their garments on the road, and others spread leafy branches, cut from the fields.
And those going ahead and those following kept crying out: "Hosanna! Blessed is the one coming in the name of the Lord!
Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David! Hosanna in the highest!"
And he entered into Jerusalem, into the temple; and looking around at everything — the hour being already late — he went out to Bethany with the twelve.
And on the next day, as they went out from Bethany, he was hungry;
and seeing from far off a fig tree with leaves, he came to see if perhaps he would find something on it — and coming to it he found nothing but leaves, for it was not the season of figs.
And answering, he said to it: "May no one ever eat fruit from you again — to the age." And his disciples were listening.

And they come to Jerusalem. And entering the temple, he began to throw out those selling and those buying in the temple; and he overturned the tables of the money-changers and the seats of those selling the doves,
and he would not allow anyone to carry a vessel through the temple.
And he taught and said to them: "Is it not written: 'My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations'? But you have made it a robbers' cave."
And the chief priests and the scribes heard, and kept seeking how to destroy him — for they feared him, because the whole crowd was astounded at his teaching.
And whenever evening came, they would go out of the city.
And passing by early in the morning, they saw the fig tree withered from the roots.
And Peter, remembering, says to him: "Rabbi — look, the fig tree you cursed has withered."
And answering, Jesus says to them: "Have the faith of God.
Amen, I say to you: whoever says to this mountain, 'Be lifted up and thrown into the sea,' and does not divide in his heart but believes that what he speaks comes to pass — it shall be his.
Therefore I say to you: everything you pray and ask for — believe that you have received it, and it shall be yours.
And whenever you stand praying, forgive — if you have anything against anyone — so that your Father in the heavens may also forgive you your trespasses."
And they come again to Jerusalem. And as he was walking in the temple, the chief priests and the scribes and the elders come to him,
and they said to him: "By what kind of authority do you do these things? Or who gave you this authority, that you should do them?"
But Jesus said to them: "I will question you about one thing — answer me, and I will tell you by what authority I do these things:
the baptism of John — was it from heaven, or from men? Answer me."
And they kept reasoning with themselves, saying: "What shall we say? If we say, 'From heaven,' he will say, 'Why then did you not believe him?'
But shall we say, 'From men'?" — they feared the crowd, for everyone held that John really was a prophet.
And answering Jesus, they say: "We do not know." And Jesus says to them: "Neither do I tell you by what authority I do these things."

mrk.12.text: And he began to speak to them in parables: "A man planted a vineyard, and put a fence around it, and dug a wine-vat, and built a tower; and he leased it to tenant farmers, and went abroad.
And at the season he sent a slave to the farmers, to receive from the farmers some of the fruits of the vineyard;
and taking him, they beat him and sent him away empty.
And again he sent them another slave — and that one they struck on the head and dishonored.
And he sent another — and that one they killed; and many others: some beating, some killing.
He had one still: a beloved son. He sent him to them last, saying, 'They will respect my son.'
But those farmers said to themselves: 'This is the heir. Come — let us kill him, and the inheritance will be ours.'
And taking him, they killed him — and threw him out of the vineyard.
What will the lord of the vineyard do? He will come and destroy the farmers — and give the vineyard to others.
Have you not read even this scripture: 'The stone the builders rejected — this one became the head of the corner;
from the Lord this came to be, and it is marvelous in our eyes'?"
And they kept seeking to seize him — and they feared the crowd; for they knew he had spoken the parable against them. And leaving him, they went away.
And they send to him some of the Pharisees and the Herodians, to snare him in a word.

And coming, they say to him: "Teacher, we know that you are true and that you care about no one's opinion — for you do not look at the face of men, but teach the way of God upon truth. Is it lawful to give the poll-tax to Caesar, or not? Shall we give, or shall we not give?"
But he, knowing their hypocrisy, said to them: "Why do you test me? Bring me a denarius, that I may see it."
And they brought one. And he says to them: "Whose is this image — and the inscription?" And they said to him: "Caesar's."
And Jesus said to them: "The things of Caesar, give back to Caesar — and the things of God, to God." And they marveled greatly at him.
And Sadducees come to him — those who say there is no resurrection — and they questioned him, saying:
"Teacher, Moses wrote for us that if a man's brother dies and leaves a wife and leaves no child, his brother should take the wife and raise up seed for his brother.
There were seven brothers. And the first took a wife, and dying, left no seed;
and the second took her, and died leaving no seed behind — and the third likewise;
and the seven left no seed. Last of all, the woman also died.
In the resurrection, when they rise — whose wife will she be? For the seven had her as wife."
Jesus said to them: "Is this not why you go astray — knowing neither the scriptures nor the power of God?
For when they rise from the dead, they neither marry nor are given in marriage; rather, they are like angels in the heavens.

But concerning the dead — that they are raised — have you not read in the book of Moses, at the bush, how God spoke to him, saying: 'I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'?
He is not the God of the dead but of the living. You go far astray."
And one of the scribes, coming up and hearing them disputing, seeing that he had answered them beautifully, questioned him: "Which commandment is first of all?"
Jesus answered: "The first is: 'Hear, O Israel — the Lord our God, the Lord is one;
and you shall love the Lord your God out of your whole heart and out of your whole soul and out of your whole mind and out of your whole strength.'
The second is this: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.' There is no other commandment greater than these."
And the scribe said to him: "Beautifully said, Teacher — you have spoken upon truth: he is one, and there is no other besides him;

and to love him out of the whole heart and out of the whole understanding and out of the whole strength, and to love the neighbor as oneself, is far more than all the whole burnt offerings and sacrifices."
And Jesus, seeing that he answered with sense, said to him: "You are not far from the kingdom of God." And no one dared question him any longer.
And answering, Jesus said, teaching in the temple: "How can the scribes say that the Christ is the son of David?
David himself said, in the Holy Spirit: 'The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet.'
David himself calls him Lord — so from where is he his son?" And the great crowd heard him gladly.
And in his teaching he said: "Watch out for the scribes — who want to walk around in long robes, and want greetings in the marketplaces,
and the first seats in the synagogues, and the first couches at the banquets;
who devour the houses of the widows, and pray at length for a pretext. These will receive the heavier judgment."
And sitting down opposite the treasury, he watched how the crowd throws copper into the treasury; and many rich people were throwing in much.
And one poor widow came and threw in two lepta — that is, a quadrans.
And calling his disciples to himself, he said to them: "Amen, I say to you: this poor widow has thrown in more than all those throwing into the treasury;
for they all threw in out of their abundance — but she, out of her lack, threw in everything she had: her whole living."

mrk.13.text: And as he was going out of the temple, one of his disciples says to him: "Teacher — look! What stones, and what buildings!"
And Jesus said to him: "You see these great buildings? There shall by no means be left here stone upon stone that shall not be torn down."
And as he sat on the Mount of Olives, opposite the temple, Peter and James and John and Andrew questioned him privately:
"Tell us — when will these things be? And what is the sign when all these things are about to be brought to completion?"
And Jesus began to say to them: "Watch that no one leads you astray.
Many will come in my name, saying, 'I am he' — and they will lead many astray.
And when you hear of wars and reports of wars, do not be alarmed. It must come to pass — but the end is not yet;
for nation will rise against nation and kingdom against kingdom; there will be earthquakes place by place; there will be famines. These things are the beginning of the birth-pangs.
But you — watch yourselves. They will hand you over to councils, and in synagogues you will be beaten, and before governors and kings you will be made to stand, for my sake — as a testimony to them.
And to all the nations, first, the good news must be proclaimed.

And when they lead you off, handing you over, do not worry beforehand what you will say; rather, whatever is given you in that hour — speak that. For it is not you who are the speakers, but the Holy Spirit.
And brother will hand over brother to death, and a father a child; and children will rise up against parents and put them to death;
and you will be hated by all because of my name. But the one who endures to the end — this one will be saved.
But when you see the desolating abomination standing where it must not — let the reader perceive — then let those in Judea flee to the mountains;
let the one on the housetop not come down, nor go inside to take anything out of his house;
and let the one in the field not turn back to what is behind, to take his garment.
But woe to those with child in the womb, and to those nursing, in those days!
And pray that it may not happen in winter;
for those days will be affliction such as has not happened from the beginning of the creation that God created until now — and shall by no means happen again.
And if the Lord had not cut short the days, no flesh would be saved; but for the sake of the chosen, whom he chose, he cut short the days.
And then, if anyone says to you, 'Look — here is the Christ!' 'Look — there!' — do not believe it;
for false christs and false prophets will arise and will give signs and wonders, to lead astray, if possible, the chosen.
But you — watch. I have told you everything beforehand.
But in those days, after that affliction, the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give her glow,
and the stars will be falling out of the heaven, and the powers in the heavens will be shaken.
And then they will see the Son of Man coming in clouds, with great power and glory;
and then he will send out the angels and gather together the chosen from the four winds — from the edge of earth to the edge of heaven.
From the fig tree learn the parable: when her branch has already become tender and puts out the leaves, you know that summer is near.
So also you: when you see these things coming to pass, know that he is near — at the doors.
Amen, I say to you: this generation shall by no means pass away until all these things come to pass.
The heaven and the earth will pass away — but my words shall by no means pass away.
But concerning that day or hour, no one knows — not the angels in heaven, not even the Son — except the Father.
Watch! Stay wakeful! For you do not know when the season is.
It is like a man gone abroad, who left his house and gave his slaves the authority — to each his work — and commanded the doorkeeper to stay awake.
Stay awake, then — for you do not know when the lord of the house is coming: whether at evening, or at midnight, or at cockcrow, or at dawn —
lest, coming suddenly, he find you sleeping.
And what I say to you, I say to all: stay awake!"

mrk.14.text: Now the Passover and the Unleavened Bread were two days away. And the chief priests and the scribes kept seeking how to seize him by deceit and kill him;
for they said: "Not at the feast — lest there be an uproar of the people."

And while he was in Bethany, in the house of Simon the leper, as he reclined at table, a woman came with an alabaster jar of perfumed oil — pure nard, very costly; shattering the alabaster jar, she poured it down over his head.
But there were some who were indignant among themselves: "To what end has this destruction of the perfume happened?
For this perfume could have been sold for more than three hundred denarii and given to the poor." And they snorted with anger at her.
But Jesus said: "Leave her alone. Why do you make trouble for her? She has worked a beautiful work in me.
For the poor you always have with you, and whenever you want you can do them good — but me you do not always have.
What she had, she did: she took beforehand the anointing of my body for the burial.
And amen, I say to you: wherever the good news is proclaimed, into the whole world, what this woman did will also be spoken — as her memorial."
And Judas Iscariot, the one of the twelve, went off to the chief priests, to hand him over to them.
And hearing it, they rejoiced and promised to give him silver. And he kept seeking how he might hand him over at a good moment.
And on the first day of Unleavened Bread, when they were sacrificing the Passover lamb, his disciples say to him: "Where do you want us to go and prepare, that you may eat the Passover?"
And he sends two of his disciples and says to them: "Go into the city, and a man carrying a jar of water will meet you. Follow him;
and wherever he enters, say to the master of the house: 'The Teacher says: Where is my guest-room, where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?'
And he himself will show you a great upper room, spread and ready — and there prepare for us."
And the disciples went out and came into the city and found it just as he had told them — and they prepared the Passover.
And when evening came, he comes with the twelve.
And as they reclined and were eating, Jesus said: "Amen, I say to you: one of you will hand me over — one who is eating with me."
They began to grieve and to say to him, one by one: "Surely not I?"
And he said to them: "One of the twelve — the one dipping with me into the one bowl.
For the Son of Man goes just as it is written about him — but woe to that man through whom the Son of Man is handed over! Better for him if that man had not been born."
And as they were eating, taking bread, blessing, he broke it and gave it to them and said: "Take — this is my body."
And taking a cup, giving thanks, he gave it to them — and they all drank from it.
And he said to them: "This is my blood of the covenant, poured out for many.
Amen, I say to you: I shall by no means drink again from the fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new in the kingdom of God."
And having sung the hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives.
And Jesus says to them: "You will all fall away — for it is written: 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered.'
But after I am raised, I will go ahead of you into Galilee."
But Peter said to him: "Even if all fall away — yet not I."
And Jesus says to him: "Amen, I say to you: today — this very night — before a rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times."
But he kept saying, all the more vehemently: "If I must die with you, I shall by no means deny you!" And they all said the same.
And they come to a place whose name is Gethsemane; and he says to his disciples: "Sit here while I pray."
And he takes along Peter and James and John with him — and he began to be gripped with awe-struck dread and deep distress,
and he says to them: "My soul is grieved all around, to the point of death. Stay here — and stay awake."
And going forward a little, he kept falling on the ground, and prayed that if it were possible the hour might pass from him;
and he said: "Abba — Father — all things are possible for you. Carry this cup away from me. Yet not what I will — but what you will."
And he comes and finds them sleeping, and says to Peter: "Simon — are you sleeping? Did you not have the strength to stay awake one hour?
Stay awake, and pray that you may not come into testing. The spirit indeed is eager — but the flesh is weak."
And again, going away, he prayed, saying the same word.
And coming again, he found them sleeping — for their eyes were being weighed down; and they did not know what to answer him.
And he comes the third time and says to them: "Sleep on now, and rest. It is enough — the hour has come. Behold, the Son of Man is handed over into the hands of the sinners.
Rise — let us go. Behold, the one handing me over has drawn near."
And immediately, while he was still speaking, Judas arrives — one of the twelve — and with him a crowd with swords and clubs, from the chief priests and the scribes and the elders.
Now the one handing him over had given them a signal, saying: "Whomever I kiss — he is the one. Seize him, and lead him away under guard."
And coming, immediately approaching him, he says: "Rabbi!" — and kissed him warmly.
And they laid hands on him and seized him.
But a certain one of those standing by, drawing his sword, struck the slave of the high priest and took off his ear.
And answering, Jesus said to them: "As against a robber you came out, with swords and clubs, to take me?
Day after day I was with you in the temple, teaching — and you did not seize me. But — that the scriptures might be fulfilled."
And leaving him, they all fled.
And a certain young man was following along with him, wearing a linen cloth over his naked body; and they seize him —
but he, leaving the linen cloth behind, fled naked.
And they led Jesus away to the high priest; and all the chief priests and the elders and the scribes come together.
And Peter followed him from far off, right inside, into the courtyard of the high priest; and he was sitting with the attendants, warming himself at the fire-light.
Now the chief priests and the whole council kept seeking testimony against Jesus, to put him to death — and they found none;
for many bore false witness against him, and the testimonies did not agree.
And some stood up and bore false witness against him, saying:
"We heard him saying: 'I will tear down this temple made by hands, and through three days I will build another, not made by hands.'"
And not even so did their testimony agree.
And the high priest, standing up in the midst, questioned Jesus: "Do you answer nothing? What are these men testifying against you?"
But he kept silent and answered nothing. Again the high priest questioned him, and says to him: "Are you the Christ, the Son of the Blessed One?"
And Jesus said: "I AM. And you will see the Son of Man sitting at the right hand of the Power, and coming with the clouds of heaven."
And the high priest, tearing his tunics, says: "What further need do we have of witnesses?
You heard the blasphemy! How does it appear to you?" And they all condemned him to be deserving of death.
And some began to spit on him, and to cover his face and strike him with fists and say to him: "Prophesy!" And the attendants took him with slaps.
And while Peter was below in the courtyard, one of the servant girls of the high priest comes;
and seeing Peter warming himself, looking straight at him, she says: "You too were with the Nazarene — with Jesus."
But he denied it, saying: "I neither know nor understand what you are saying." And he went outside into the forecourt — and a rooster crowed.
And the servant girl, seeing him, began again to say to those standing by: "This man is one of them."
But again he kept denying it. And after a little while, again those standing by said to Peter: "Truly you are one of them — for you are a Galilean, and your speech shows it."
But he began to call down curses and to swear: "I do not know this man of whom you speak!"
And immediately, a second time, a rooster crowed. And Peter remembered the word — how Jesus had said to him: "Before a rooster crows twice, you will deny me three times." And breaking down, he wept.

mrk.15.text: And immediately, early in the morning, the chief priests with the elders and scribes and the whole council, having formed their plan, bound Jesus and carried him off and handed him over to Pilate.
And Pilate questioned him: "Are you the king of the Jews?" And answering him he says: "You say it."
And the chief priests kept accusing him of many things.
And Pilate again questioned him: "Do you answer nothing? Look how many things they accuse you of."
But Jesus answered nothing further — so that Pilate marveled.
Now at each feast he would release to them one prisoner, whom they requested.
And there was the man called Barabbas, bound with the insurrectionists — men who had committed murder in the uprising.
And the crowd, coming up, began to ask him to do as he used to do for them.
And Pilate answered them, saying: "Do you want me to release to you the king of the Jews?" —
for he knew that the chief priests had handed him over out of envy.
But the chief priests stirred up the crowd, that he should rather release Barabbas to them.
And Pilate, answering again, said to them: "What then shall I do with the one you call the king of the Jews?"
And they cried out again: "Crucify him!"
And Pilate said to them: "Why — what evil has he done?" But they cried out all the more: "Crucify him!"
And Pilate, wanting to satisfy the crowd, released Barabbas to them; and he handed Jesus over — having had him flogged — to be crucified.
And the soldiers led him away, inside the courtyard — that is, the praetorium — and they call together the whole cohort.
And they dress him in purple, and having woven a crown of thorns they set it around him;
and they began to salute him: "Hail, king of the Jews!"
And they kept striking his head with a reed and spitting on him; and bending their knees, they kept bowing down to him.
And when they had mocked him, they stripped the purple off him and put his own garments on him. And they lead him out to crucify him.
And they press into service a passerby — a certain Simon of Cyrene, coming from the field, the father of Alexander and Rufus — to carry his cross.
And they bring him to the place Golgotha — which is, translated, Place of a Skull.
And they offered him wine mixed with myrrh — but he did not take it.
And they crucify him — and they divide his garments, throwing lots over them: who should take what.
Now it was the third hour — and they crucified him.
And the inscription of the charge against him was written up: THE KING OF THE JEWS.
And with him they crucify two robbers — one at his right and one at his left.
And those passing by kept blaspheming him, shaking their heads and saying: "Aha! You who tear down the temple and build it in three days —
save yourself: come down from the cross!"
Likewise also the chief priests, mocking among themselves with the scribes, kept saying: "Others he saved; himself he cannot save.
The Christ, the king of Israel — let him come down now from the cross, that we may see and believe!" And those crucified together with him kept reviling him.
And when the sixth hour came, darkness came over the whole land, until the ninth hour.
And at the ninth hour Jesus cried with a great voice: "Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?" — which is, translated: "My God, my God — for what have you abandoned me?"
And some of those standing by, hearing it, said: "Look — he is calling Elijah."
And someone ran, filled a sponge with sour wine, put it on a reed, and gave him to drink, saying: "Leave him — let us see if Elijah comes to take him down."
But Jesus, letting out a great voice, breathed out his last.
And the curtain of the temple was torn in two — from top to bottom.
And the centurion standing there, opposite him, seeing that he breathed his last this way, said: "Truly this man was Son of God."
Now there were also women watching from far off — among them Mary the Magdalene, and Mary the mother of James the small and of Joses, and Salome,
who, when he was in Galilee, used to follow him and serve him — and many others who had come up with him to Jerusalem.
And when evening had already come — since it was Preparation Day, that is, the day before the sabbath —
Joseph of Arimathea came, a respected council member, who was himself also awaiting the kingdom of God; and taking courage, he went in to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.
And Pilate marveled that he was already dead; and calling the centurion to him, he questioned him whether he had died some time ago;
and learning it from the centurion, he granted the corpse to Joseph.
And having bought a linen cloth, taking him down, he wrapped him in the linen and laid him in a tomb that had been quarried out of rock — and he rolled a stone against the door of the tomb.
And Mary the Magdalene and Mary the mother of Joses watched where he had been laid.

mrk.16.text: And when the sabbath had passed, Mary the Magdalene and Mary the mother of James, and Salome, bought spices, so that they might come and anoint him.
And very early on the first day of the week they come to the tomb — the sun having risen.
And they kept saying to one another: "Who will roll away the stone for us from the door of the tomb?"
And looking up, they gaze: the stone has been rolled away — for it was exceedingly great.
And entering into the tomb, they saw a young man sitting on the right, clothed in a white robe — and they were gripped with awe-struck dread.
But he says to them: "Do not be terrified. You are seeking Jesus the Nazarene, the crucified one. He has been raised. He is not here. Look — the place where they laid him.
But go, tell his disciples — and Peter — that he is going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you."

And going out, they fled from the tomb — for trembling and amazement held them; and they said nothing to anyone, for they were afraid. ⟦And all that had been commanded they reported briefly to those around Peter. And after these things Jesus himself also, from the east even to the west, sent out through them the holy and imperishable proclamation of everlasting salvation. Amen.⟧
⟦And having risen early on the first day of the week, he appeared first to Mary the Magdalene, from whom he had cast out seven demons.
She went and reported to those who had been with him, as they mourned and wept;
and they, hearing that he lives and had been seen by her, did not believe.
And after these things he was made visible in another form to two of them as they walked, going into the country;
and they, going away, reported to the rest — and they did not believe them either.
And later he was made visible to the eleven themselves as they reclined at table; and he reproached their unbelief and hardness of heart — because they had not believed those who had seen him raised.
And he said to them: "Go into all the world and proclaim the good news to the whole creation.
The one who believes and is baptized will be saved; but the one who does not believe will be condemned.
And these signs will follow alongside those who believe: in my name they will cast out demons; they will speak in new tongues;
they will pick up snakes; and if they drink anything deadly, it shall by no means harm them; on the sick they will lay hands — and they will be well."
So then the Lord Jesus, after speaking to them, was taken up into heaven and sat at the right hand of God;
and they, going out, proclaimed everywhere — the Lord working with them and confirming the word through the accompanying signs.⟧

rom.1.text: Paul, a slave of Christ Jesus, called as an apostle, set apart for the good news of God —
which he promised beforehand through his prophets in holy writings —
concerning his Son, who came from the seed of David according to the flesh,
who was marked out Son of God in power, according to the Spirit of holiness, out of the resurrection of the dead: Jesus Christ our Lord —
through whom we received grace and apostleship, for the obedience of faith among all the nations, on behalf of his name —
among whom you also are called of Jesus Christ —
to all who are in Rome, beloved of God, called holy ones: grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
First, I give thanks to my God, through Jesus Christ, for all of you — because your faith is being announced in the whole world.
For God is my witness — whom I serve in my spirit in the good news of his Son — how unceasingly I make mention of you,
always in my prayers, begging that somehow, now at last, by the will of God, my way to come to you may be opened.
For I long to see you, that I may share with you some spiritual gift, so that you may be strengthened —
that is, that we may be encouraged together among you, through the faith in one another, both yours and mine.

And I do not want you to be unknowing, brothers, that I often purposed to come to you — and was hindered until now — that I might have some fruit among you also, just as among the rest of the nations.
To Greeks and to barbarians, to the wise and to the senseless, I am a debtor;
hence my eagerness to bring the good news to you also who are in Rome.
For I am not ashamed of the good news — for it is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes: to the Jew first, and to the Greek.
For in it the righteousness of God is unveiled — out of faith, into faith — just as it is written: "But the righteous one shall live out of faith."
For the wrath of God is unveiled from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of human beings who hold the truth down in unrighteousness —
because what can be known of God is visible among them; for God made it visible to them.
For his invisible things — his everlasting power and divinity — have been clearly seen since the creation of the world, perceived in the things made; so that they are without defense,
because, knowing God, they did not glorify him as God or give thanks; rather they became empty in their reasonings, and their uncomprehending heart was darkened.
Claiming to be wise, they became fools,
and exchanged the glory of the imperishable God for the likeness of an image of perishable man — and of birds and four-footed beasts and creeping things.
Therefore God handed them over, in the cravings of their hearts, to impurity — to the dishonoring of their bodies among themselves:
they who exchanged the truth of God for the lie, and revered and served the creature rather than the Creator — who is blessed to the ages. Amen.
For this reason God handed them over to passions of dishonor: their females exchanged the natural use for what is against nature,

and likewise the males also, abandoning the natural use of the female, were burned out in their appetite for one another — males with males working what is shameless, and receiving back in themselves the due repayment of their wandering.
And just as they did not see fit to hold God in full knowledge, God handed them over to an unfit mind, to do what is not fitting —
filled with all unrighteousness, wickedness, greed, malice; stuffed with envy, murder, strife, deceit, mean-spiritedness; whisperers,
slanderers, God-haters, insolent, arrogant, boasters, inventors of evils, disobedient to parents,
without understanding, without faithfulness, without natural affection, without mercy —
who, knowing full well the just decree of God — that those who practice such things are deserving of death — not only do them, but also applaud those who practice them.

rom.2.text: Therefore you are without defense, O man — every one of you who judges. For in what you judge the other, you condemn yourself; for you who judge practice the same things.
And we know that the judgment of God is according to truth, upon those who practice such things.
And do you reckon this, O man — you who judge those who practice such things and do them yourself — that you will escape the judgment of God?
Or do you despise the wealth of his kindness and forbearance and patience — not knowing that the kindness of God is leading you to a turning of the mind?
But by your hardness and unturning heart you are storing up wrath for yourself, in a day of wrath and of the unveiling of the righteous judgment of God,
who will repay each one according to his works:
to those who by endurance in good work seek glory and honor and imperishability — everlasting life;
but to those of self-seeking ambition, who disobey the truth and obey unrighteousness — wrath and fury.
Affliction and hemmed-in distress upon every human soul that works out the evil — the Jew first, and the Greek;
but glory and honor and peace to everyone who works the good — the Jew first, and the Greek.
For there is no favoritism toward faces with God.
For as many as sinned without the law will also perish without the law; and as many as sinned in the law will be judged through the law —
for it is not the hearers of the law who are righteous with God; rather, the doers of the law will be counted righteous.
For whenever nations that do not have the law do by nature the things of the law, these — not having the law — are a law to themselves:
they display the work of the law written in their hearts, their conscience bearing witness together, and their reasonings among one another accusing or even defending —
in the day when God judges the hidden things of human beings, according to my good news, through Christ Jesus.
But if you are named a Jew, and rest upon the law, and boast in God,
and know the will, and test what truly matters — being instructed out of the law —
and are persuaded that you yourself are a guide of the blind, a light of those in darkness,
an instructor of the senseless, a teacher of infants, having the embodiment of knowledge and truth in the law —
you, then, who teach the other: do you not teach yourself? You who proclaim not to steal: do you steal?
You who say not to commit adultery: do you commit adultery? You who abhor the idols: do you rob temples?
You who boast in the law: through the transgression of the law do you dishonor God?
For "the name of God is blasphemed among the nations because of you" — just as it is written.
For circumcision indeed profits, if you practice the law; but if you are a transgressor of the law, your circumcision has become foreskin.
If then the foreskin keeps the just decrees of the law, will not his foreskin be counted as circumcision?
And the foreskin-by-nature that fulfills the law will judge you — the transgressor of the law through letter and circumcision.
For the Jew is not the one in the open, nor is circumcision the one in the open, in flesh;
rather, the Jew is the one in the hidden place — and circumcision is of the heart, in spirit, not in letter; whose praise is not from human beings but from God.

rom.3.text: What then is the advantage of the Jew? Or what the profit of the circumcision?
Much, in every way. First of all: they were entrusted with the spoken oracles of God.
For what then? If some were unfaithful — will their unfaithfulness nullify the faithfulness of God?
Never may it be! Rather, let God be true, and every human being a liar — just as it is written: "That you may be counted righteous in your words, and prevail when you are judged."
But if our unrighteousness makes the righteousness of God stand out, what shall we say? Is God unjust when he brings the wrath? (I speak in human terms.)
Never may it be! Otherwise, how shall God judge the world?
But if by my lie the truth of God overflowed to his glory, why am I still judged as a sinner?
And why not — as we are slandered, and as some claim that we say — "Let us do the evil things so that the good things may come"? Their condemnation is just.
What then? Are we better off? Not at all — for we have already charged that Jews and Greeks alike are all under sin,
just as it is written: "There is no righteous one — not even one;
there is none who understands; there is none who seeks God out.
All have turned aside; together they have become worthless; there is none who does kindness — there is not so much as one.
Their throat is an opened grave; with their tongues they kept deceiving; the venom of asps is under their lips;
their mouth is full of cursing and bitterness;
their feet are swift to pour out blood;
shattering and misery are in their ways,
and the way of peace they have not known.
There is no fear of God before their eyes."
Now we know that whatever the law says, it speaks to those in the law — so that every mouth may be stopped, and the whole world become answerable to God;
because out of works of the law no flesh will be counted righteous before him — for through the law comes the full knowledge of sin.
But now, apart from the law, the righteousness of God has been made visible — witnessed to by the law and the prophets:
the righteousness of God, through the faithfulness of Jesus Christ, unto all who believe. For there is no distinction:
all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God —
being counted righteous freely, by his grace, through the ransom-release that is in Christ Jesus,
whom God set forth as the atoning mercy-place, through faith, in his own blood — as a display of his righteousness, because of the passing over of the sins previously committed

in the forbearance of God; toward the display of his righteousness in the present season: that he might be righteous himself, and the one who counts righteous the person who lives out of the faithfulness of Jesus.
Where then is the boasting? It has been shut out. Through what kind of law — of works? No: through the law of faith.
For we reckon that a human being is counted righteous by faith, apart from works of the law.
Or is God the God of Jews only? Not of the nations also? Yes — of the nations also,
since God is one: he will count the circumcision righteous out of faith, and the foreskin through the faith.
Do we then nullify the law through the faith? Never may it be — rather, we make the law stand.

rom.4.text: What then shall we say that Abraham, our forefather according to the flesh, has found?
For if Abraham was counted righteous out of works, he has a boast — but not toward God.
For what does the scripture say? "And Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness."
Now to the one who works, the wage is not counted according to grace but according to debt;
but to the one who does not work, but believes on the one who counts the ungodly righteous — his faith is counted as righteousness.
Just as David also speaks the blessing of the person to whom God counts righteousness apart from works:
"Happy are those whose lawless deeds have been forgiven, and whose sins have been covered over;
happy the man whose sin the Lord shall by no means count."
Is this blessing, then, on the circumcision — or also on the foreskin? For we say: "Faith was counted to Abraham as righteousness."
How then was it counted? When he was in circumcision, or in foreskin? Not in circumcision — in foreskin!

And he received the sign of circumcision as a seal of the righteousness of the faith he had while in foreskin — that he might be the father of all who believe through foreskin, so that righteousness might be counted to them,
and father of circumcision to those who are not of the circumcision only, but who also walk in line with the footprints of the in-foreskin faith of our father Abraham.
For the promise to Abraham, or to his seed — that he should be heir of the world — did not come through the law, but through the righteousness of faith.
For if those of the law are heirs, faith has been emptied and the promise nullified;
for the law works out wrath — but where there is no law, neither is there transgression.

For this reason it is out of faith — so that it may be according to grace, so that the promise may stand firm for all the seed: not only for those of the law but also for those of the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all —
as it is written: "A father of many nations I have made you" — before the God in whom he believed, who makes the dead alive and calls the things that are not as though they are.
Against hope, upon hope, he believed — that he should become a father of many nations, according to what had been spoken: "So shall your seed be."
And not weakening in faith, he considered his own body, already dead — being about a hundred years old — and the deadness of Sarah's womb;
yet at the promise of God he did not waver in unbelief, but was empowered by faith, giving glory to God,
fully convinced that what he has promised, he is also able to do.
Therefore it was counted to him as righteousness.
Now it was not written for his sake only — that it was counted to him —
but also for ours, to whom it is about to be counted: to us who believe on the one who raised Jesus our Lord from the dead —
who was handed over for our trespasses, and raised for our being-counted-righteous.

rom.5.text: Having been counted righteous, then, out of faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ,
through whom we have also gained the way of approach, by faith, into this grace in which we stand — and we boast upon the hope of the glory of God.
And not only that — we also boast in the afflictions, knowing that the affliction works out endurance,
and the endurance, tested character; and the tested character, hope;
and the hope does not put to shame — because the love of God has been poured out in our hearts through the Holy Spirit given to us.
For while we were still weak, Christ — at the right season — died for the ungodly.
For scarcely will anyone die for a righteous man — though for the good man perhaps someone even dares to die.
But God makes his own love toward us stand out in this: that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.
By much more, then, having now been counted righteous in his blood, we shall be saved through him from the wrath;
for if, being enemies, we were reconciled to God through the death of his Son — by much more, having been reconciled, we shall be saved in his life.
And not only that — we also boast in God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom we have now received the reconciliation.
Therefore: just as through one man sin entered into the world, and through sin death — and so death spread through to all human beings, in that all sinned —
for until the law, sin was in the world; but sin is not put on the account where there is no law;
yet death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over those who had not sinned in the likeness of Adam's transgression — Adam, who is a pattern of the coming one.

But not as the trespass, so also the grace-gift; for if by the trespass of the one the many died, by much more the grace of God — and the gift in the grace of the one man Jesus Christ — overflowed to the many.
And the gift is not like what came through the one who sinned: the judgment, out of one, led to condemnation — but the grace-gift, out of many trespasses, to a decree of righteousness.

For if by the trespass of the one, death reigned through the one — by much more those who receive the overflow of grace and of the gift of righteousness shall reign in life through the one, Jesus Christ.
So then: as through one trespass came condemnation to all human beings, so also through one act of righteousness came the making-righteous of life to all human beings;
for just as through the disobedience of the one man the many were set down as sinners, so also through the obedience of the one the many shall be set down as righteous.
And the law slipped in alongside, so that the trespass might increase; but where sin increased, grace overflowed beyond all measure —
so that, just as sin reigned in death, so also grace might reign through righteousness, into everlasting life, through Jesus Christ our Lord.

rom.6.text: What then shall we say? Shall we go on remaining in sin, so that grace may increase?
Never may it be! We who died to sin — how shall we still live in it?
Or do you not know that as many of us as were baptized into Christ Jesus were baptized into his death?
We were buried, then, together with him through the baptism into death — so that just as Christ was raised from the dead through the glory of the Father, so also we might walk in newness of life.
For if we have become grown-together with the likeness of his death, we shall certainly also be of the resurrection —
knowing this: that our old humanity was crucified with him, so that the body of sin might be made powerless, that we should no longer be slaves to sin;
for the one who has died has been set right, free from sin.
And if we died with Christ, we believe that we shall also live together with him —
knowing that Christ, raised from the dead, dies no more; death is lord over him no longer.
For the death he died, he died to sin — once for all; but the life he lives, he lives to God.
So also you: count yourselves dead to sin, but living to God, in Christ Jesus.
Let sin, then, not reign in your mortal body, so that you obey its cravings;
and do not go on presenting your members to sin as weapons of unrighteousness — rather, present yourselves to God as those living from the dead, and your members to God as weapons of righteousness.
For sin shall not be lord over you — for you are not under law, but under grace.
What then? Shall we sin, because we are not under law but under grace? Never may it be!
Do you not know that to whomever you present yourselves as slaves for obedience, you are slaves of the one you obey — whether of sin, unto death, or of obedience, unto righteousness?
But thanks be to God: you were slaves of sin, but you obeyed from the heart the pattern of teaching to which you were handed over;
and having been set free from sin, you were made slaves to righteousness.

(I speak in human terms, because of the weakness of your flesh.) For just as you presented your members as slaves to impurity and to lawlessness, unto lawlessness — so now present your members as slaves to righteousness, unto sanctification.
For when you were slaves of sin, you were free with respect to righteousness.
What fruit, then, did you have at that time from the things you are now ashamed of? For the end of those things is death.
But now, set free from sin and enslaved to God, you have your fruit unto sanctification — and the end: everlasting life.
For the wages of sin is death — but the grace-gift of God is everlasting life, in Christ Jesus our Lord.

rom.7.text: Or do you not know, brothers — for I speak to those who know the law — that the law is lord over a person for as long a time as he lives?
For the married woman is bound by law to the living husband; but if the husband dies, she is released from the law of the husband.

So then, while the husband lives, she will bear the name adulteress if she becomes another man's; but if the husband dies, she is free from the law — so that she is no adulteress, though she become another man's.
So, my brothers, you also were put to death to the law through the body of the Christ — that you might become another's: his who was raised from the dead, so that we might bear fruit to God.
For when we were in the flesh, the passions of sins, aroused through the law, kept working in our members, to bear fruit to death;
but now we have been released from the law — having died to what held us fast — so that we serve in newness of Spirit, and not in oldness of letter.
What then shall we say — is the law sin? Never may it be! But I would not have known sin except through the law; for I would not have known craving if the law had not said, "You shall not crave."
But sin, taking its opening through the commandment, worked out in me every craving — for apart from the law, sin is dead.
And I was once alive apart from the law; but when the commandment came, sin came back to life —
and I died. And the commandment that was meant for life — this very one was found, for me, to be for death;
for sin, taking its opening through the commandment, deceived me — and through it, killed me.
So the law is holy, and the commandment holy and righteous and good.

Did the good thing, then, become death for me? Never may it be! But sin — so that it might be shown as sin — was working death for me through the good thing; so that sin, through the commandment, might become sinful beyond all measure.
For we know that the law is spiritual; but I am fleshly — sold under sin.
For what I work out, I do not comprehend; for what I want — this I do not practice; but what I hate — this I do.
And if I do what I do not want, I agree with the law — that it is beautiful.
But as it is, it is no longer I who work it out — it is the sin dwelling in me.
For I know that good does not dwell in me — that is, in my flesh; for the wanting lies ready beside me, but the working out of the beautiful — no.
For I do not do the good I want; rather, the evil I do not want — this I practice.
But if I do what I do not want, it is no longer I who work it out — it is the sin dwelling in me.
I find, then, this law: when I want to do the beautiful, the evil lies ready beside me.
For I delight in the law of God, in the inner human being;
but I see another law in my members — waging war against the law of my mind, and taking me captive in the law of sin that is in my members.
Miserable man that I am! Who will rescue me out of this body of death?
Thanks be to God — through Jesus Christ our Lord! So then, I myself with the mind serve the law of God — but with the flesh, the law of sin.

rom.8.text: There is, then, now — no condemnation for those in Christ Jesus;
for the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus has set you free from the law of sin and of death.
For what the law could not do — where it was weak through the flesh — God did: sending his own Son in the likeness of sin's flesh, and concerning sin, he condemned sin in the flesh,
so that the just decree of the law might be fulfilled in us who walk not according to flesh but according to Spirit.
For those who are according to flesh set their minds on the things of the flesh; but those according to Spirit, on the things of the Spirit.
For the mindset of the flesh is death — but the mindset of the Spirit, life and peace;
because the mindset of the flesh is enmity toward God: it does not submit itself to the law of God — indeed it cannot;
and those who are in flesh cannot please God.
But you are not in flesh — you are in Spirit, if indeed the Spirit of God dwells in you. And if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, this one is not his.
But if Christ is in you: the body indeed is dead because of sin — but the Spirit is life because of righteousness.
And if the Spirit of the one who raised Jesus from the dead dwells in you, the one who raised Christ Jesus from the dead will also make your mortal bodies alive, through his Spirit indwelling in you.
So then, brothers, we are debtors — but not to the flesh, to live according to flesh;
for if you live according to flesh, you are going to die; but if by the Spirit you put to death the practices of the body, you will live.
For as many as are led by the Spirit of God — these are sons of God.
For you did not receive a spirit of slavery, back into fear; you received the Spirit of adoption as sons — in whom we cry: "Abba! Father!"
The Spirit himself bears witness together with our spirit that we are children of God;
and if children, then heirs: heirs of God, and co-heirs with Christ — if indeed we suffer together with him, so that we may also be glorified together with him.
For I reckon that the sufferings of the present season are not worth setting against the coming glory to be unveiled unto us.
For the craning-forward expectation of the creation is eagerly awaiting the unveiling of the sons of God;
for the creation was made subject to emptiness — not willingly, but because of the one who subjected it — upon hope:
that the creation itself also will be set free from the slavery of decay, into the freedom of the glory of the children of God.
For we know that the whole creation groans together and labors in birth-pangs together, up to now;
and not only that — we ourselves, holding the firstfruits of the Spirit, we too groan within ourselves, eagerly awaiting the adoption: the ransom-release of our body.
For in this hope we were saved. But hope that is seen is not hope — for what one sees, why does he hope for it?
But if we hope for what we do not see, we await it eagerly, through endurance.
And likewise the Spirit also takes hold together with us in our weakness; for we do not know what to pray as we ought — but the Spirit himself intercedes beyond words, with groanings unutterable;
and the one who searches the hearts knows what the mindset of the Spirit is — because he intercedes for the holy ones according to God.
And we know that for those who love God, all things work together toward good — for those who are called according to purpose.
Because those he foreknew, he also marked out beforehand, shaped-together with the image of his Son — that he might be firstborn among many brothers;
and those he marked out beforehand, these he also called; and those he called, these he also counted righteous; and those he counted righteous, these he also glorified.
What then shall we say toward these things? If God is for us — who is against us?
He who did not spare his own Son, but handed him over for us all — how shall he not also, with him, freely grace us with all things?
Who shall bring a charge against God's chosen? God is the one who counts righteous.
Who is the one who condemns? Christ is the one who died — rather, who was raised — who is also at the right hand of God, who also intercedes for us.
Who shall separate us from the love of the Christ? Affliction, or hemmed-in distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword?
Just as it is written: "For your sake we are being put to death the whole day long; we were counted as sheep for slaughter."
But in all these things we more-than-conquer, through the one who loved us.
For I am persuaded that neither death nor life, nor angels nor rulers, nor things standing present nor things to come, nor powers,
nor height nor depth, nor any other created thing, will be able to separate us from the love of God that is in Christ Jesus our Lord.

rom.9.text: I speak truth in Christ — I do not lie, my conscience bearing witness with me in the Holy Spirit —
that I have great grief, and unceasing anguish in my heart;
for I could wish that I myself were accursed, cut off from the Christ, for the sake of my brothers, my kin according to the flesh —
who are Israelites: theirs the adoption as sons, and the glory, and the covenants, and the giving of the law, and the worship, and the promises;
theirs the fathers — and from them, according to the flesh, the Christ, who is over all, God blessed to the ages. Amen.
But it is not as though the word of God has fallen out. For not all who are out of Israel — these are Israel;
nor, because they are Abraham's seed, are they all children. Rather: "In Isaac shall your seed be called" —
that is: not the children of the flesh are the children of God; rather, the children of the promise are counted as seed.
For this is the word of promise: "At this season I will come — and Sarah shall have a son."
And not only that — there is also Rebecca, having conceived from one man, Isaac our father:
for when they had not yet been born, and had practiced nothing good or worthless — so that God's purpose-by-choosing might remain,
not out of works but out of the one who calls — it was said to her: "The greater shall serve the lesser";
just as it is written: "Jacob I loved — but Esau I hated."
What then shall we say? Is there unrighteousness with God? Never may it be!
For to Moses he says: "I will show mercy to whomever I show mercy, and I will have compassion on whomever I have compassion."
So then, it is not of the one who wants, nor of the one who runs — but of God who shows mercy.
For the scripture says to Pharaoh: "For this very thing I raised you up: that I might display my power in you, and that my name might be announced throughout all the earth."
So then, to whom he wills, he shows mercy — and whom he wills, he hardens.
You will say to me, then: "Why does he still find fault? For who has stood against his purpose?"
O man — who then are you, answering back to God? Shall the thing molded say to the one who molded it: "Why did you make me this way?"
Or does the potter not have authority over the clay — out of the same lump to make one vessel for honor, and another for dishonor?
And what if God, willing to display the wrath and make known his power, bore with much patience vessels of wrath, fitted for destruction —
and so that he might make known the wealth of his glory upon vessels of mercy, which he prepared beforehand for glory —
us, whom he also called, not only out of Jews but also out of nations?
As he says also in Hosea: "I will call the not-my-people 'my people,' and the not-beloved 'beloved';
and it shall be: in the place where it was said to them, 'You are not my people' — there they shall be called sons of the living God."
And Isaiah cries out over Israel: "Though the number of the sons of Israel be like the sand of the sea, the remnant shall be saved;
for the Lord will accomplish his word on the earth — completing it and cutting it short."
And just as Isaiah has said before: "If the Lord of Hosts had not left us seed, we would have become like Sodom, and been made like Gomorrah."
What then shall we say? That nations who were not pursuing righteousness seized righteousness — the righteousness that is out of faith;
but Israel, pursuing a law of righteousness, did not arrive at that law.
Why? Because it was not out of faith, but as if out of works. They struck their foot against the stone of stumbling —
just as it is written: "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense; and the one who believes upon him shall not be put to shame."

rom.10.text: Brothers, the good pleasure of my heart, and my petition to God for them, is for their salvation.
For I bear them witness that they have a zeal for God — but not according to full knowledge;
for not knowing the righteousness of God, and seeking to establish their own, they did not submit themselves to the righteousness of God.
For Christ is the end-goal of the law — for righteousness, to everyone who believes.
For Moses writes of the righteousness that is out of the law: "The person who does these things shall live in it."
But the righteousness out of faith speaks this way: "Do not say in your heart, 'Who will go up into heaven?'" — that is, to bring Christ down —
"or, 'Who will go down into the abyss?'" — that is, to bring Christ up from the dead.
But what does it say? "The spoken word is near you — in your mouth and in your heart": that is, the spoken word of the faith that we proclaim.
Because if you confess with your mouth "Jesus is Lord," and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead — you will be saved;
for with the heart one believes unto righteousness, and with the mouth one confesses unto salvation.
For the scripture says: "Everyone who believes upon him shall not be put to shame."
For there is no distinction between Jew and Greek — for the same one is Lord of all, rich toward all who call upon him;
for "everyone who calls upon the name of the Lord shall be saved."
How then shall they call on one in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in one they have not heard? And how shall they hear without one proclaiming?
And how shall they proclaim, unless they are sent? Just as it is written: "How beautiful the feet of those who bring the good news of good things!"
But not all obeyed the good news; for Isaiah says: "Lord, who has believed what he heard from us?"
So faith comes out of hearing — and the hearing, through the spoken word of Christ.
But I say: did they not hear? Indeed they did: "Into all the earth their sound went out, and to the ends of the inhabited world their words."
But I say: did Israel not know? First, Moses says: "I will make you jealous over a non-nation; over a nation without understanding I will provoke you to anger."
And Isaiah dares fully, and says: "I was found by those not seeking me; I became visible to those not asking for me."
But to Israel he says: "The whole day long I stretched out my hands to a disobeying and contradicting people."

rom.11.text: I say then: has God pushed away his people? Never may it be! For I too am an Israelite — from the seed of Abraham, the tribe of Benjamin.
God has not pushed away his people, whom he foreknew. Or do you not know what the scripture says in the Elijah passage — how he pleads with God against Israel:
"Lord, they have killed your prophets, they have torn down your altars — and I alone am left, and they seek my soul"?
But what does the divine answer say to him? "I have left for myself seven thousand men who have not bent a knee to Baal."
So then, in the present season also there has come to be a remnant, according to the choosing of grace;
and if by grace, then no longer out of works — otherwise grace is no longer grace.
What then? What Israel is seeking, this it did not obtain; but the chosen obtained it — and the rest were hardened,
just as it is written: "God gave them a spirit of stupor — eyes not to see and ears not to hear — until this very day."
And David says: "Let their table become a snare and a trap, a stumbling-block and a repayment to them;
let their eyes be darkened so as not to see — and bend their back continually."
I say then: did they stumble so as to fall? Never may it be! Rather, by their trespass salvation has come to the nations — to make them jealous.
And if their trespass is the wealth of the world, and their loss the wealth of the nations — how much more their fullness!
Now I am speaking to you, the nations. Inasmuch as I am an apostle of the nations, I glorify my service —
if somehow I may make my own flesh jealous, and save some of them.
For if their being cast off is the reconciliation of the world, what will their being received be — but life from the dead?
And if the firstfruits are holy, so is the whole lump; and if the root is holy, so are the branches.
But if some of the branches were broken off, and you — being a wild olive — were grafted in among them and became a co-sharer of the rich root of the olive tree,
do not boast over the branches. And if you boast: it is not you who carry the root — the root carries you.
You will say, then: "Branches were broken off so that I might be grafted in."
True. They were broken off by unbelief — and you stand by faith. Do not set your mind on high things; rather, fear.
For if God did not spare the branches according to nature, neither will he spare you.
See, then, the kindness and the severity of God: on those who fell — severity; but on you — God's kindness, if you remain in the kindness; otherwise you too will be cut off.
And they also, if they do not remain in unbelief, will be grafted in — for God is able to graft them in again.

For if you were cut out of the wild olive according to nature, and against nature were grafted into the cultivated olive — how much more will these, according to nature, be grafted into their own olive tree!
For I do not want you to be unknowing, brothers, of this mystery — lest you be wise in your own eyes: a hardening in part has come upon Israel, until the fullness of the nations comes in;
and so all Israel shall be saved — just as it is written: "Out of Zion shall come the Rescuer; he will turn ungodliness away from Jacob;
and this is the covenant from me with them — when I take away their sins."
According to the good news, they are enemies for your sake; but according to the choosing, beloved because of the fathers —
for the grace-gifts and the calling of God are without regret.
For just as you once disobeyed God, but have now been shown mercy through their disobedience,
so also they have now disobeyed — for the sake of the mercy shown you — so that they also may now be shown mercy;
for God has shut all together into disobedience, so that he may show mercy to all.
O depth of the wealth and wisdom and knowledge of God! How unsearchable his judgments, and untraceable his ways!
For who has known the mind of the Lord? Or who has become his counselor?
Or who has first given to him — and it shall be repaid to him?
Because out of him and through him and unto him are all things. To him be the glory, to the ages. Amen.

rom.12.text: I urge you therefore, brothers, through the compassions of God: present your bodies as a living sacrifice — holy, well-pleasing to God — your reasonable worship;
and do not be shaped after the pattern of this age, but be transformed by the renewing of the mind — that you may test what the will of God is: the good and well-pleasing and complete.

For through the grace given me I say to everyone among you: do not think higher of yourself than you ought to think; rather, think so as to think soundly — as God has apportioned to each a measure of faith.
For just as in one body we have many members, and the members do not all have the same function,
so we, the many, are one body in Christ — and each one members of one another.
And having grace-gifts differing according to the grace given us: if prophecy — according to the proportion of the faith;
if service — in the serving; the one who teaches — in the teaching;
the one who encourages — in the encouragement; the one who shares — with singleness; the one who leads — with diligence; the one who shows mercy — with cheerfulness.
Let love be unhypocritical. Abhorring the evil; glued to the good;
in brotherly love, warmly devoted to one another; in honor, going ahead of one another;
in diligence, not sluggish; in the spirit, boiling; serving the Lord;
in hope, rejoicing; in affliction, enduring; in prayer, persevering;
sharing in the needs of the holy ones; pursuing hospitality to strangers.
Bless those who persecute — bless, and do not curse.
Rejoice with those rejoicing; weep with those weeping.
Set your minds the same toward one another — not minding the high things, but carried along with the lowly. Do not become wise in your own eyes.
Repaying no one evil for evil; taking forethought for what is beautiful before all human beings;
if possible, so far as it depends on you, being at peace with all human beings;
not avenging yourselves, beloved — rather, give place to the wrath; for it is written: "Vengeance is mine; I will repay, says the Lord."
But "if your enemy hungers, feed him; if he thirsts, give him drink — for by doing this you will heap coals of fire on his head."
Do not be conquered by the evil — conquer the evil with the good.

rom.13.text: Let every soul submit itself to the higher authorities; for there is no authority except by God, and those that exist have been ordered by God.
So the one who sets himself against the authority has stood against the ordering of God — and those who have stood against will receive judgment on themselves.
For the rulers are not a terror to the good work, but to the evil. Do you want not to fear the authority? Do the good — and you will have praise from it;
for it is God's servant to you, for the good. But if you do the evil — fear; for it does not carry the sword for nothing. It is God's servant: an avenger, for wrath, to the one practicing the evil.
Therefore it is necessary to submit — not only because of the wrath, but also because of the conscience.
For this is also why you pay taxes: they are God's public ministers, persevering at this very thing.
