And having the same spirit of the faith — according to what is written: "I believed; therefore I spoke" — we also believe, therefore we also speak,
knowing that the one who raised Jesus will raise us also, with Jesus, and will present us with you.
For all things are for your sake — so that the grace, having multiplied through the many, may make the thanksgiving overflow, to the glory of God.
Therefore we do not lose heart. Even if our outer human being is wasting away, yet our inner one is being made new day by day;
for the momentary lightness of our affliction is working out for us — beyond measure, into beyond-measure — an everlasting weight of glory,
as we fix our eyes not on the things seen but on the things not seen; for the things seen are for a season, but the things not seen are everlasting.

2co.5.text: For we know that if our earthly house — this tent — is torn down, we have a building from God: a house not made by hands, everlasting, in the heavens.
For indeed in this tent we groan, longing to be clothed over with our dwelling that is from heaven —
if indeed, having been clothed, we shall not be found naked.
For we who are in the tent groan, being weighed down — because we do not want to be unclothed but clothed over, so that the mortal may be swallowed down by the life.
And the one who worked us out for this very thing is God — who gave us the down-payment of the Spirit.
Being always of good courage, then, and knowing that while at home in the body we are away from home, away from the Lord —
for we walk through faith, not through sight —
we are of good courage, and would rather be away from home, out of the body, and at home with the Lord.
Therefore we also make it our ambition — whether at home or away from home — to be well-pleasing to him.
For we must all be made visible before the judgment seat of the Christ, so that each may receive back the things done through the body, according to what he practiced — whether good or worthless.
Knowing, then, the fear of the Lord, we persuade human beings — and to God we have been made visible; and I hope to have been made visible in your consciences also.
We are not commending ourselves to you again; rather, we are giving you an opening for a boast on our behalf — so that you may have an answer for those who boast in face and not in heart.
For if we were out of our minds — it was for God; if we are sound-minded — it is for you.
For the love of the Christ holds us together — having judged this: that one died for all; therefore all died;
and he died for all so that the living should no longer live to themselves, but to the one who died for them and was raised.
So from now on we know no one according to flesh. Even if we have known Christ according to flesh — yet now we know him so no longer.
So then: if anyone is in Christ — new creation! The ancient things have passed away; behold, they have become new.
And all things are out of God, who reconciled us to himself through Christ — and gave us the service of the reconciliation:
namely, that God was in Christ, reconciling the world to himself — not counting their trespasses against them — and he has placed in us the word of the reconciliation.
On behalf of Christ, then, we are ambassadors — as though God were making his appeal through us. We beg, on behalf of Christ: be reconciled to God!
The one who knew no sin — for us he made him sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.

2co.6.text: And working together with him, we also urge you not to receive the grace of God into emptiness —
for he says: "At an acceptable season I listened to you, and in a day of salvation I helped you." Behold — now is the well-acceptable season; behold — now is the day of salvation.
We give no cause for stumbling in anything, so that the service may not be faulted;
rather, in everything we commend ourselves as servants of God: in much endurance, in afflictions, in hardships, in hemmed-in distresses,
in beatings, in prisons, in riots, in labors, in sleepless nights, in fastings;
in purity, in knowledge, in patience, in kindness, in the Holy Spirit, in unhypocritical love,
in the word of truth, in the power of God; through the weapons of righteousness for the right hand and the left;
through glory and dishonor, through slander and good report; as deceivers — and true;
as unknown — and fully known; as dying — and behold, we live; as disciplined — and not put to death;
as grieving — yet always rejoicing; as poor — yet making many rich; as having nothing — and possessing everything.
Our mouth stands open toward you, Corinthians; our heart has been widened.
You are not cramped in us — you are cramped in your own inward affections.
So — the same in return (I speak as to children): be widened, you also.
Do not become unevenly yoked with unbelievers. For what partnership have righteousness and lawlessness? Or what fellowship has light with darkness?
And what harmony has Christ with Beliar? Or what portion has a believer with an unbeliever?

And what agreement has the temple of God with idols? For we are the temple of the living God — just as God said: "I will dwell in them and walk among them; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
Therefore come out from their midst and be set apart, says the Lord; and touch no unclean thing — and I will receive you in;
and I will be a Father to you, and you shall be sons and daughters to me — says the Lord, the All-Ruler."

2co.7.text: Having, then, these promises, beloved — let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of flesh and spirit, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God.
Make room for us! We wronged no one, we corrupted no one, we took advantage of no one.
I do not say this for condemnation — for I have said before that you are in our hearts, to die together and to live together.
Great is my open boldness toward you; great is my boasting on your behalf. I have been filled with the comfort; I am overflowing beyond measure with the joy, in all our affliction.
For even when we came into Macedonia, our flesh had no rest — in everything afflicted: battles outside, fears within.
But the one who comforts the lowly — God — comforted us by the arrival of Titus;
and not only by his arrival, but also by the comfort with which he had been comforted over you — reporting to us your longing, your mourning, your zeal for me — so that I rejoiced all the more.
Because even if I grieved you with the letter, I do not regret it. Even if I was regretting it — I see that that letter grieved you, though for an hour —
now I rejoice: not that you were grieved, but that you were grieved into a turning of the mind. For you were grieved according to God, so that you suffered loss from us in nothing;
for grief according to God works a turning of the mind unto salvation, without regret — but the grief of the world works out death.

For behold — this very thing, being grieved according to God: how much diligence it worked out in you! What defense, what indignation, what fear, what longing, what zeal, what setting-right! In everything you have shown yourselves pure in the matter.

So then, even if I wrote to you, it was not for the sake of the one who did the wrong, nor for the sake of the one wronged — but so that your diligence on our behalf might be made visible to you, before God.
For this reason we have been comforted. And beyond our own comfort, we rejoiced still more abundantly at the joy of Titus — because his spirit has been refreshed by you all;
because if I have boasted to him about you at all, I was not put to shame; rather, as we spoke everything to you in truth, so also our boasting before Titus became truth.
And his inward affection toward you is all the more abundant as he remembers the obedience of you all — how you received him with fear and trembling.
I rejoice that in everything I have good courage in you.

2co.8.text: And we make known to you, brothers, the grace of God given in the assemblies of Macedonia:
that in a great testing of affliction, the overflow of their joy and their down-to-the-depths poverty overflowed into the wealth of their singlehearted generosity —
because according to their power, I bear witness, and beyond their power, of their own free choice,
with much appeal they begged us for the grace of sharing in the service to the holy ones —
and not as we had hoped: they gave themselves first to the Lord, and to us, through the will of God,
so that we urged Titus that, just as he had begun before, so he should also bring to completion among you this grace as well.
But just as you overflow in everything — in faith and word and knowledge and all diligence, and in the love from us in you — so may you overflow in this grace also.
I do not say this as a command; rather, through the diligence of others, I am testing the genuineness of your love as well.
For you know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ: that for your sake he became poor, being rich — so that you, by his poverty, might become rich.
And in this I give a judgment: this is fitting for you — you who last year began before others, not only in the doing but also in the wanting.
Now, then, bring the doing also to completion — so that just as there was the eagerness of the wanting, so also the completing may come out of what you have.
For if the eagerness lies ready, it is well-acceptable according to what one has — not according to what one does not have.
For it is not that others should have relief and you affliction; rather, out of equality:
in the present season your abundance goes to their lack — so that their abundance may also come to your lack; so that there may be equality,
just as it is written: "The one with much did not have too much, and the one with little did not have too little."
And thanks be to God, who gives the same diligence for you in the heart of Titus —
because he received our appeal; and being more diligent still, of his own free choice he went out to you.
And we sent together with him the brother whose praise in the good news is through all the assemblies —
and not only that: he was also elected by the assemblies as our traveling companion with this grace being served by us — toward the glory of the Lord himself, and our eagerness —
taking this precaution: that no one should fault us in this abundance being served by us;
for we take forethought for what is beautiful, not only before the Lord but also before human beings.
And we sent together with them our brother, whom we have tested and found diligent many times in many things — and now much more diligent, through his great confidence in you.
As for Titus: he is my partner and co-worker toward you. As for our brothers: they are apostles of assemblies — the glory of Christ.
So display to them, before the face of the assemblies, the proof of your love — and of our boasting about you.

2co.9.text: For concerning the service to the holy ones, it is superfluous for me to write to you;
for I know your eagerness, of which I boast about you to the Macedonians — that Achaia has stood ready since last year; and your zeal has stirred up the majority.
But I sent the brothers so that our boast about you should not be emptied in this respect — so that, as I kept saying, you may stand ready;
lest somehow, if Macedonians come with me and find you unready, we — not to say you — should be put to shame in this confidence.
So I considered it necessary to urge the brothers to go ahead to you and arrange in advance your previously promised blessing — that it may be ready as a blessing, and not as an extortion.
And this: the one who sows sparingly will also reap sparingly; and the one who sows upon blessings will also reap upon blessings.
Each one as he has chosen beforehand in his heart — not out of grief or out of compulsion; for God loves a cheerful giver.
And God is able to make all grace overflow into you, so that — having in everything, always, all sufficiency — you may overflow into every good work;
as it is written: "He scattered; he gave to the needy; his righteousness remains to the age."
And the one who supplies seed to the sower and bread for eating will supply and multiply your seed, and will grow the harvests of your righteousness —
you being made rich in everything, into all singlehearted generosity, which works out through us thanksgiving to God;
because the service of this public ministry is not only filling up the lacks of the holy ones — it is also overflowing through many thanksgivings to God.
Through the tested proof of this service they glorify God for the submission of your confession to the good news of the Christ, and for the generosity of your sharing with them and with all;
and in their petition for you they long for you, because of the surpassing grace of God upon you.
Thanks be to God for his indescribable gift!

2co.10.text: Now I myself, Paul, appeal to you through the meekness and considerateness of the Christ — I who am lowly face-to-face among you, but bold toward you when absent —
and I beg that when present I may not have to be bold with the confidence with which I reckon to dare against certain people who reckon us as walking according to flesh.
For though we walk in flesh, we do not wage war according to flesh —
for the weapons of our warfare are not fleshly, but powerful to God for tearing down strongholds — tearing down reasonings
and every high thing lifting itself up against the knowledge of God, and taking every thought captive into the obedience of the Christ;
and we hold ourselves in readiness to avenge every disobedience — once your obedience is filled full.
You look at the things before your face. If anyone is confident in himself that he is Christ's, let him reckon this again within himself: just as he is Christ's, so also are we.
For even if I should boast somewhat more abundantly about our authority — which the Lord gave for building you up and not for tearing you down — I shall not be put to shame;
that I may not seem, as it were, to be frightening you through the letters.
For "his letters," someone says, "are weighty and strong — but the presence of his body is weak, and his speech worth nothing."
Let such a person reckon this: what we are in word, through letters, when absent — such we are also in deed when present.
For we do not dare to class or compare ourselves with certain ones who commend themselves. But they, measuring themselves by themselves and comparing themselves with themselves, do not understand.
We, however, will not boast into the unmeasured — but according to the measure of the measuring-line that God apportioned to us as a measure: to reach even as far as you.
For we are not overstretching ourselves, as though not reaching to you — for we did come all the way to you with the good news of the Christ —
not boasting into the unmeasured in other men's labors; but holding a hope that, as your faith grows, we shall be enlarged among you according to our measuring-line, into abundance:
to bring the good news to the regions beyond you — not to boast in another's measuring-line over things already prepared.
"But let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord";
for it is not the one commending himself who is tested-and-approved, but the one whom the Lord commends.

2co.11.text: Would that you would bear with me in a little foolishness! But indeed — you do bear with me.
For I am jealous over you with God's own jealousy — for I betrothed you to one husband, to present a pure virgin to the Christ;
but I fear that somehow — as the snake deceived Eve by his craftiness — your thoughts may be corrupted away from the singleness and the purity that is toward the Christ.

For if the one who comes proclaims another Jesus, whom we did not proclaim — or you receive a different spirit, which you did not receive, or a different good news, which you did not accept — you bear it beautifully!
For I reckon that I fall short in nothing of these super-super apostles.
And even if I am unskilled in speech — yet not in knowledge; rather, in every way we have made this visible to you, in all things.
Or did I commit a sin — lowering myself so that you might be raised high — because I brought you the good news of God free of charge?
I plundered other assemblies, taking wages from them for the service of you;

and when I was present with you and in lack, I deadweighted no one — for the brothers coming from Macedonia filled up my lack; and in everything I kept myself unburdensome to you — and will keep myself so.
The truth of Christ is in me: this boast shall not be fenced off from me in the regions of Achaia.
Why? Because I do not love you? God knows!
But what I do, I will also keep doing — to cut off the opening of those who want an opening, so that in what they boast about, they may be found just as we are.
For such men are false apostles — deceitful workers, refashioning themselves into apostles of Christ.
And no marvel: Satan himself refashions himself into an angel of light;
so it is nothing great if his servants also refashion themselves as servants of righteousness — whose end shall be according to their works.
Again I say: let no one think me a fool. But if you do — then receive me even as a fool, so that I too may boast a little.
What I am saying, I am not saying according to the Lord, but as in foolishness — in this confidence of boasting.
Since many boast according to flesh — I too will boast.
For gladly you bear with the fools — being so sensible yourselves!
For you bear it if someone enslaves you, if someone devours you, if someone takes from you, if someone lifts himself up, if someone strikes you in the face.
To my dishonor I say it: we have been weak! Yet in whatever anyone dares — I speak in foolishness — I dare too.
Are they Hebrews? So am I. Are they Israelites? So am I. Are they Abraham's seed? So am I.
Are they servants of Christ? — I speak as one out of his mind — I, beyond them: in labors more abundantly, in prisons more abundantly, in beatings beyond measure, in deaths often.
From Jews, five times, I received the forty lashes less one;
three times I was beaten with rods; once I was stoned; three times I was shipwrecked; a night and a day I have spent in the deep;

in journeys often — in dangers of rivers, dangers of robbers, dangers from my own race, dangers from the nations, dangers in the city, dangers in the wilderness, dangers on the sea, dangers among false brothers;
in labor and toil, in sleepless nights often, in hunger and thirst, in fastings often, in cold and nakedness —
and apart from the things outside: the daily pressure on me, the anxiety for all the assemblies.
Who is weak — and I am not weak? Who is made to stumble — and I do not burn?
If I must boast, I will boast of the things of my weakness.
The God and Father of the Lord Jesus — the one who is blessed to the ages — knows that I am not lying.
In Damascus, the governor under King Aretas was guarding the city of the Damascenes to seize me —
and through a window, in a basket, I was let down through the wall, and escaped his hands.

2co.12.text: Boasting is necessary — not profitable, indeed — but I will come to visions and unveilings of the Lord.
I know a man in Christ who, fourteen years ago — whether in the body I do not know, or out of the body I do not know; God knows — such a man was snatched up to the third heaven.
And I know such a man — whether in the body or apart from the body I do not know; God knows —
that he was snatched up into paradise and heard unspeakable spoken words, which it is not permitted a human being to speak.
On behalf of such a man I will boast; but on behalf of myself I will not boast — except in the weaknesses.
For if I should want to boast, I shall not be a fool — for I will speak truth. But I refrain, lest anyone credit to me more than what he sees in me or hears from me —

even with the surpassing greatness of the unveilings. Therefore, so that I should not lift myself too high, there was given me a thorn in the flesh — an angel of Satan, to strike me with fists — so that I should not lift myself too high.
Concerning this I appealed to the Lord three times, that it might depart from me;

and he has said to me: "My grace is sufficient for you — for the power is brought to its completion in weakness." Most gladly, then, will I boast rather in my weaknesses, so that the power of the Christ may pitch its tent over me.
Therefore I am well pleased in weaknesses, in insults, in hardships, in persecutions and hemmed-in distresses, for Christ's sake — for whenever I am weak, then I am powerful.
I have become a fool — you compelled me! For I ought to have been commended by you; for I fell short in nothing of the super-super apostles — even if I am nothing.
The signs of the apostle were indeed worked out among you, in all endurance — with signs and wonders and deeds of power.
For in what were you made less than the rest of the assemblies — except that I myself did not deadweight you? Freely forgive me this wrong!

Behold, this third time I stand ready to come to you — and I will not deadweight you; for I do not seek what is yours, but you. For the children ought not to store up treasure for the parents — but the parents for the children.
And I will most gladly spend, and be utterly spent out, for your souls. If I love you more abundantly — am I loved the less?
But let it be: I did not weigh you down. Yet — being crafty — I took you by deceit!
Did I take advantage of you through anyone I have sent to you?
I urged Titus, and sent the brother with him. Did Titus take advantage of you? Did we not walk in the same spirit — in the same footprints?
Have you been thinking all along that we are defending ourselves to you? Before the face of God, in Christ, we speak — and all of it, beloved, for your building up.

For I fear that somehow, coming, I may find you not as I want — and I be found by you such as you do not want; that somehow there be strife, jealousy, rages, self-seeking ambitions, slanders, whisperings, puffings-up, disorders;
that when I come again my God may humble me before you, and I mourn over many who sinned before and have not turned from the impurity and immorality and shamelessness they practiced.

2co.13.text: This third time I am coming to you. "On the mouth of two witnesses and three shall every spoken word be made to stand."
I have said before, and I say beforehand — as when present the second time, and now absent — to those who sinned before, and to all the rest: if I come again, I will not spare;
since you seek a tested proof of the Christ speaking in me — who is not weak toward you, but powerful among you.
For indeed he was crucified out of weakness — but he lives out of the power of God. For we also are weak in him — but we shall live with him, out of the power of God, toward you.
Test yourselves — whether you are in the faith. Prove yourselves! Or do you not recognize about yourselves that Jesus Christ is in you? — unless you are unproved.
And I hope you will know that we are not unproved.
And we pray to God that you do no evil — not so that we may appear proved, but so that you may do the beautiful, and we be as unproved.
For we have no power against the truth — only for the truth.
For we rejoice whenever we are weak and you are powerful. This we even pray for: your complete mending.
For this reason I write these things while absent — so that when present I may not deal sharply, according to the authority the Lord gave me: for building up, and not for tearing down.
For the rest, brothers: rejoice; be mended into completeness; be comforted; set your minds the same; be at peace — and the God of love and peace will be with you.
Greet one another with a holy kiss. All the holy ones greet you.
The grace of the Lord Jesus Christ, and the love of God, and the sharing of the Holy Spirit be with you all.

heb.1.text: In many parts and in many ways, long ago, God spoke to the fathers in the prophets;
at the last of these days he has spoken to us in a Son — whom he appointed heir of all things, through whom he also made the ages;

who — being the out-shining of his glory and the exact imprint of his very being, and carrying all things by the spoken word of his power — having made purification of sins, sat down at the right hand of the Majesty in the high places,
having become as much better than the angels as the name he has inherited is more excellent than theirs.
For to which of the angels did he ever say: "You are my Son; today I have begotten you"? And again: "I will be a Father to him, and he shall be a Son to me"?
And again, when he brings the Firstborn into the inhabited world, he says: "And let all God's angels worship him."
And of the angels he says: "He makes his angels winds, and his public ministers a flame of fire";
but of the Son: "Your throne, O God, is to the age of the age; and the rod of uprightness is the rod of your kingdom.
You loved righteousness and hated lawlessness; therefore God, your God, anointed you with the oil of exultation beyond your companions."
And: "You, Lord, at the beginnings founded the earth, and the heavens are the works of your hands;
they will perish — but you remain through; and they will all grow old like a garment,
and like a cloak you will roll them up — like a garment — and they will be changed. But you are the same, and your years will not run out."
And to which of the angels has he ever said: "Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies as a footstool for your feet"?
Are they not all ministering spirits, sent out for service on account of those who are about to inherit salvation?

heb.2.text: For this reason we must attend all the more abundantly to the things heard — lest we drift past them.
For if the word spoken through angels proved firm, and every transgression and disobedience received a just wage-repayment,
how shall we escape if we neglect so great a salvation? — which, having taken its beginning in being spoken through the Lord, was made firm to us by those who heard,
God bearing witness together with them: with signs and wonders and many-colored deeds of power, and with distributions of the Holy Spirit according to his will.
For it was not to angels that he subjected the coming inhabited world, of which we speak;
but someone testified somewhere, saying: "What is a human being, that you remember him? Or a son of man, that you visit him?
You made him a little lower than angels; with glory and honor you crowned him;
you subjected all things under his feet." For in subjecting all things to him, he left nothing unsubjected to him. But as it is, we do not yet see all things subjected to him —
but we do see the one made a little lower than angels: Jesus — crowned with glory and honor because of the suffering of death, so that by the grace of God he might taste death for everyone.
For it was fitting for him — for whom are all things and through whom are all things — in leading many sons to glory, to bring the pioneer of their salvation to completion through sufferings.
For both the one who makes holy and those being made holy are all out of one; for which cause he is not ashamed to call them brothers,
saying: "I will announce your name to my brothers; in the midst of the assembly I will hymn you";
and again: "I will put my trust in him"; and again: "Behold — I, and the children God gave me."

Since, then, the children have shared blood and flesh, he himself likewise partook of the same — so that through death he might bring to nothing the one holding the might of death, that is, the devil,
and set free all those who, by fear of death, were held in slavery through the whole of their living.
For surely it is not angels he takes hold of — he takes hold of the seed of Abraham.
Hence he had to be made like his brothers in every way, so that he might become a merciful and faithful high priest in the things toward God — to make atonement for the sins of the people;
for in what he himself has suffered, being tested, he is able to help those being tested.

heb.3.text: Therefore, holy brothers, sharers of a heavenly calling: consider the Apostle and High Priest of our confession — Jesus,
who is faithful to the one who appointed him, as Moses also was, in his house.
For this one has been counted worthy of more glory than Moses — by as much as the builder of a house has more honor than the house;
for every house is built by someone — but the builder of all things is God.
And Moses indeed was faithful in his whole house, as an attendant — for a testimony to the things that would be spoken;
but Christ, as a Son over his house. And his house are we — if we hold fast the open boldness and the boast of the hope.
Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says: "Today, if you hear his voice,
do not harden your hearts as in the Embittering, according to the day of the testing in the wilderness,
where your fathers tested me by proving me — and saw my works
forty years. Therefore I was provoked at this generation, and said: They are always wandering in their heart, and they have not known my ways;
as I swore in my wrath: They shall never enter into my rest."
Watch, brothers — lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief, in falling away from the living God;
rather, encourage each other every single day — as long as it is called "Today" — so that none of you be hardened by the deceit of sin.
For we have become sharers of the Christ — if indeed we hold the beginning of our confidence firm to the end —
while it is said: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as in the Embittering."
For who were they who heard and embittered him? Was it not all those who came out of Egypt through Moses?
And with whom was he provoked forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose limbs fell in the wilderness?
And to whom did he swear that they should not enter into his rest — if not to those who disobeyed?
And we see that they were not able to enter, because of unbelief.

heb.4.text: Let us fear, then — lest, while a promise of entering into his rest still remains, any of you should seem to have fallen short of it.
For we too have had good news brought to us, just as they did; but the word heard did not profit them — not having been mixed together with faith in those who heard.
For we who have believed do enter into the rest — just as he has said: "As I swore in my wrath: They shall never enter into my rest" — although the works were finished from the founding of the world;
for he has said somewhere about the seventh day, thus: "And God rested on the seventh day from all his works";
and in this place again: "They shall never enter into my rest."
Since, then, it remains for some to enter into it, and those who formerly had the good news brought to them did not enter because of disobedience,
he again marks out a certain day — "Today" — saying in David, after so long a time, just as it has been said before: "Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts."
For if Joshua had given them rest, he would not have spoken of another day after these things.
So then, a sabbath-keeping remains for the people of God;
for the one who has entered into his rest has himself also rested from his works — just as God did from his own.
Let us be diligent, then, to enter into that rest — so that no one may fall in the same pattern of disobedience.

For the word of God is living and at work, and sharper beyond every two-mouthed sword — piercing through even to the dividing of soul and spirit, of joints and marrow — and able to judge the ponderings and intentions of the heart;
and there is no created thing invisible before him: all things are naked and laid bare, throat exposed, to the eyes of him with whom we have to do.
Having, then, a great high priest who has passed through the heavens — Jesus, the Son of God — let us hold fast the confession;
for we do not have a high priest unable to suffer along with our weaknesses, but one who has been tested in every way, in our likeness — apart from sin.
Let us come forward, then, with open boldness to the throne of grace — so that we may receive mercy, and find grace for well-timed help.

heb.5.text: For every high priest, taken from among human beings, is appointed on behalf of human beings in the things toward God — to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins;
able to feel with measure for the ignorant and the wandering — since he himself also is wrapped around with weakness,
and because of it he must offer for sins — as for the people, so also for himself.
And no one takes the honor for himself — only when called by God, just as Aaron also was.
So also the Christ did not glorify himself to become high priest — but the one who spoke to him did: "You are my Son; today I have begotten you";
just as he says in another place also: "You are a priest to the age, according to the order of Melchizedek" —
who, in the days of his flesh, having offered petitions and pleadings — with strong crying and tears — to the one able to save him out of death, and having been heard because of his reverent awe,
although being a Son, learned the obedience from the things he suffered;
and having been brought to completion, he became, to all who obey him, the source of everlasting salvation —
addressed by God as high priest according to the order of Melchizedek.
Concerning him we have much to say — and hard to interpret in the telling, since you have become sluggish in your hearing.

For though by this time you ought to be teachers, you again have need of someone to teach you the elementary letters of the beginning of the spoken oracles of God — and you have come to need milk, not solid food.
For everyone partaking of milk is unversed in the word of righteousness — for he is an infant;
but solid food is for the complete — those who through practiced habit have their senses trained for the telling-apart of beautiful and evil.

heb.6.text: Therefore, leaving behind the word of the beginning of the Christ, let us be carried on toward completeness — not laying down again a foundation of turning from dead works, and of faith in God,
of teaching about washings and laying on of hands, and of resurrection of the dead and everlasting judgment.
And this we shall do — if indeed God permits.
For it is impossible, for those once enlightened — who tasted the heavenly gift, and became sharers of the Holy Spirit,
and tasted the beautiful spoken word of God and the powers of the coming age —
and then fell alongside, to renew them again unto a turning of the mind: they are crucifying the Son of God again for themselves, and holding him up to open shame.
For land that drinks the rain coming often upon it, and brings forth vegetation useful to those for whom it is farmed, receives blessing from God;
but bearing out thorns and thistles, it is unproved, and near a curse — its end is for burning.
But we are persuaded of better things concerning you, beloved — things belonging to salvation — even though we speak this way;
for God is not unjust, to forget your work and the love you displayed for his name — having served the holy ones, and serving still.
And we long for each of you to display the same diligence, toward the full conviction of the hope, up to the end —
so that you may not become sluggish, but imitators of those who through faith and patience inherit the promises.
For when God promised to Abraham — since he had no one greater to swear by — he swore by himself,
saying: "Surely, blessing I will bless you, and multiplying I will multiply you";
and so, having waited patiently, he obtained the promise.
For men swear by what is greater, and for them the oath, for confirmation, is an end of every dispute;
in which God — willing all the more abundantly to show the heirs of the promise the unchangeableness of his counsel — mediated with an oath,
so that through two unchangeable things, in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled for refuge might have strong encouragement to grasp the hope set before us —
which we have as an anchor of the soul, both safe and firm, and entering into the inner side of the curtain,
where a forerunner has entered for us — Jesus, having become high priest to the age, according to the order of Melchizedek.

heb.7.text: For this Melchizedek — king of Salem, priest of God Most High, who met Abraham returning from the cutting-down of the kings and blessed him,
to whom Abraham also apportioned a tenth of everything — is first, by interpretation, King of Righteousness; and then also King of Salem, which is King of Peace;
fatherless, motherless, without genealogy; having neither beginning of days nor end of life — but made like the Son of God — he remains a priest without interruption.
Now consider how great this man was, to whom Abraham the patriarch gave a tenth out of the choicest spoils.
And those of the sons of Levi who receive the priesthood have a commandment to tithe the people according to the law — that is, their brothers, though they too have come out of the loins of Abraham;
but the one not counted in their genealogy has tithed Abraham — and has blessed the holder of the promises.
And beyond all dispute: the lesser is blessed by the greater.
And here, dying men receive tithes — but there, one of whom it is testified that he lives.
And, so to speak, through Abraham even Levi, the receiver of tithes, has been tithed —
for he was still in the loins of his father when Melchizedek met him.

If, then, completion had come through the Levitical priesthood — for on the basis of it the people received the law — what need was there still for another priest to arise according to the order of Melchizedek, and not be named according to the order of Aaron?
For when the priesthood is changed, out of necessity a change of law also comes about.
For the one about whom these things are said has partaken of a different tribe, from which no one has attended the altar;
for it is fully evident that our Lord has risen out of Judah — a tribe about which Moses said nothing concerning priests.
And it is still more abundantly obvious if another priest arises according to the likeness of Melchizedek,
who has become so not according to a law of fleshly commandment, but according to the power of an indestructible life;
for it is testified: "You are a priest to the age, according to the order of Melchizedek."
For there comes a setting-aside of the preceding commandment, because of its weakness and uselessness —
for the law brought nothing to completion — and the bringing-in-besides of a better hope, through which we draw near to God.
And inasmuch as it was not without the swearing of an oath — for the others have become priests without oath-swearing,
but he with oath-swearing, through the one who says to him: "The Lord swore, and will not change his mind: You are a priest to the age" —
by so much has Jesus become the guarantee of a better covenant.
And the others have become priests in greater numbers, because by death they are prevented from continuing;
but he — because he remains to the age — holds the priesthood untransferable;
hence he is also able to save, to the very uttermost, those who come to God through him — always living to intercede for them.
For such a high priest was indeed fitting for us: holy, without evil, undefiled, separated from the sinners, and having become higher than the heavens;
who has no daily need, like the high priests, first to offer up sacrifices for his own sins and then for those of the people — for this he did once for all, when he offered up himself.
For the law appoints as high priests men who have weakness; but the word of the oath-swearing, which came after the law, appoints a Son — brought to completion, to the age.

heb.8.text: Now the crowning point of what is being said: we have such a high priest, who sat down at the right hand of the throne of the Majesty in the heavens —
a public minister of the holy places and of the true tent, which the Lord pitched, not man.
For every high priest is appointed to offer gifts and sacrifices; hence it was necessary that this one also have something to offer.
Now if he were on earth, he would not even be a priest — since there are those who offer the gifts according to law;

(who serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things — just as Moses was divinely warned when about to complete the tent: "See," he says, "that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain") —
but as it is, he has obtained a more excellent public ministry, by as much as he is also mediator of a better covenant — one enacted on better promises.
For if that first covenant had been blameless, no place would have been sought for a second;
for finding fault with them, he says: "Behold, days are coming, says the Lord, when I will complete upon the house of Israel and upon the house of Judah a new covenant —

not according to the covenant I made with their fathers, in the day I took them by the hand to lead them out of the land of Egypt; because they did not remain in my covenant — and I disregarded them, says the Lord.

Because this is the covenant I will covenant with the house of Israel after those days, says the Lord: giving my laws into their understanding, I will also write them upon their hearts; and I will be their God, and they shall be my people.
And they shall never teach — each his fellow citizen and each his brother — saying, 'Know the Lord'; because all shall know me, from the small to the great of them.
Because I will be merciful toward their unrighteousnesses — and their sins I shall never remember again."
In saying "new," he has made the first old; and what is growing old and aging is near vanishing.

heb.9.text: Now the first covenant did have just decrees of worship, and its holy place — an earthly one.
For a tent was built — the first — in which were the lampstand and the table and the setting-out of the loaves; it is called the Holy Place.
And behind the second curtain, a tent called the Holy of Holies,

holding a golden incense-altar, and the ark of the covenant covered all around with gold — in which were a golden jar holding the manna, and Aaron's staff that budded, and the tablets of the covenant;
and high above it, cherubim of glory overshadowing the mercy-place — about which things it is not now the time to speak part by part.
Now with these things thus built, the priests continually enter into the first tent, completing the acts of worship;
but into the second, once a year, the high priest alone — and not without blood, which he offers for himself and for the unknowing-sins of the people —
the Holy Spirit making this plain: that the way into the holy places has not yet been made visible while the first tent still has standing.
This is a parable for the present season — according to which gifts and sacrifices are offered that cannot, as regards conscience, bring the worshiper to completion;
they rest only on foods and drinks and various washings — just decrees of the flesh, imposed until a season of setting-things-straight.
But Christ, having arrived as high priest of the good things that have come — through the greater and more complete tent, not made by hands, that is, not of this creation —
and not through the blood of goats and calves, but through his own blood — entered once for all into the holy places, having found an everlasting ransom-release.
For if the blood of goats and bulls, and the ash of a heifer sprinkling the defiled, makes holy toward the cleanness of the flesh,
by how much more shall the blood of the Christ — who through an everlasting Spirit offered himself blameless to God — cleanse our conscience from dead works, to worship the living God!

And for this reason he is mediator of a new covenant: so that — a death having come to pass for the ransom-release of the transgressions under the first covenant — those who are called may receive the promise of the everlasting inheritance.
For where there is a covenant-will, the death of the one who made it must be brought forward;
for a covenant-will is firm over the dead — since it never has force while the one who made it lives.
Hence not even the first covenant has been inaugurated without blood;

for when every commandment according to the law had been spoken by Moses to all the people, taking the blood of the calves, with water and scarlet wool and hyssop, he sprinkled both the scroll itself and all the people,
saying: "This is the blood of the covenant which God commanded toward you";
and the tent also, and all the vessels of the public ministry, he sprinkled likewise with the blood.
And with blood almost everything is cleansed, according to the law — and without the pouring out of blood there comes no forgiveness.
It was necessary, then, for the copies of the things in the heavens to be cleansed with these — but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these.
For Christ did not enter holy places made by hands — counterparts of the true ones — but into heaven itself, now to appear before the face of God on our behalf;
and not so that he should offer himself many times, as the high priest enters the holy places year by year with blood not his own —

since then he would have had to suffer many times from the founding of the world. But as it is, once, at the completion of the ages, he has been made visible — for the setting-aside of sin through his sacrifice.
And inasmuch as it is laid up for human beings to die once — and after this, judgment —
so also the Christ, having been offered once to carry up the sins of many, shall be seen a second time — apart from sin — by those eagerly awaiting him, for salvation.

heb.10.text: For the law — holding a shadow of the coming good things, not the very image of the realities — can never, with the same sacrifices they offer year by year without interruption, bring to completion those who come forward;
since would they not have ceased being offered — because the worshipers, once cleansed, would no longer have any consciousness of sins?
But in these sacrifices there is a reminding of sins, year by year —
for it is impossible for the blood of bulls and goats to carry sins away.
Therefore, entering into the world, he says: "Sacrifice and offering you did not want — but a body you mended and made ready for me;
in whole burnt offerings and offerings for sin you took no pleasure.
Then I said: Behold, I have come — in the scroll's heading it is written about me — to do, O God, your will."
Saying above, "Sacrifices and offerings and whole burnt offerings and offerings for sin you did not want, nor took pleasure in them" — which are offered according to law —
then he has said: "Behold, I have come to do your will." He takes away the first, in order to make the second stand.
In that will we have been made holy — through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ, once for all.
And every priest stands day by day, ministering and offering many times the same sacrifices — which can never take sins away all around;
but this one, having offered one sacrifice for sins, without interruption sat down at the right hand of God —
for the rest, awaiting until his enemies are set as a footstool for his feet;
for by one offering he has brought to completion, without interruption, those being made holy.
And the Holy Spirit also bears witness to us; for after having said:
"This is the covenant I will covenant with them after those days, says the Lord: giving my laws upon their hearts, I will also write them upon their understanding" —
then: "and their sins and their lawless deeds I shall never remember again."
And where there is forgiveness of these — there is no longer an offering for sin.
Having, then, brothers, open boldness for the entrance into the holy places, in the blood of Jesus —
a way he inaugurated for us, freshly slain and living, through the curtain — that is, his flesh —
and a great priest over the house of God:
let us come forward with a true heart, in full conviction of faith — our hearts sprinkled clean from an evil conscience, and our body washed with clean water;
let us hold fast the confession of the hope without bending — for faithful is the one who promised;
and let us consider one another, toward a sharpening-spur of love and beautiful works —
not abandoning our own gathering-together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another — and all the more, by as much as you see the Day drawing near.
For if we go on sinning willingly after receiving the full knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins —
only a fearful expectation of judgment, and a zeal of fire about to devour the adversaries.
Anyone who has set aside the law of Moses dies without compassions, on two or three witnesses;

of how much worse punishment, do you think, will he be counted worthy who has trampled down the Son of God, and counted common the blood of the covenant by which he was made holy, and insulted the Spirit of grace?
For we know the one who said: "Vengeance is mine; I will repay"; and again: "The Lord will judge his people."
Fearful it is — to fall into the hands of the living God.
But remember the former days in which, having been enlightened, you endured a great contest of sufferings:
partly being made a public theater by taunts and afflictions, partly having become sharers with those so treated.
For you suffered together with the prisoners, and you accepted the plundering of your possessions with joy — knowing that you yourselves have a better possession, and one that remains.
Do not throw away, then, your open boldness — which holds a great wage-repayment;
for you have need of endurance, so that — having done the will of God — you may carry off the promise.
For "yet a very, very little while — the Coming One will arrive, and will not delay;
and my righteous one shall live out of faith — and if he shrinks back, my soul takes no pleasure in him."
But we are not of the shrinking-back, unto destruction — we are of faith, unto the possession of the soul.

heb.11.text: Now faith is the standing-ground of things hoped for — the proving of realities not seen;
for in this the elders received their testimony.
By faith we perceive that the ages were mended into order by the spoken word of God — so that what is seen has not come to be out of things that appear.
By faith Abel offered God a greater sacrifice than Cain — through which he was testified to be righteous, God bearing witness over his gifts; and through it, though he died, he still speaks.
By faith Enoch was transferred, so as not to see death — and he was not found, because God transferred him; for before the transfer he had received testimony that he had been well-pleasing to God.
And without faith it is impossible to please him — for the one coming to God must believe that he is, and that he becomes a wage-repayer to those who seek him out.

By faith Noah — divinely warned about things not yet seen — in reverent awe built an ark for the salvation of his house; through it he condemned the world, and became heir of the righteousness that is according to faith.
By faith Abraham, being called, obeyed — going out to a place he was about to receive as an inheritance; and he went out not knowing where he was going.
By faith he sojourned in the land of the promise as in a foreign land — living in tents with Isaac and Jacob, the co-heirs of the same promise;
for he was awaiting the city that has the foundations — whose craftsman and builder is God.
By faith even Sarah herself received power for the founding of seed — and that beyond the season of her age — since she counted faithful the one who had promised;
therefore also from one man — and him as good as dead — were born descendants like the stars of the heaven in multitude, and like the sand along the lip of the sea, beyond counting.
In faith all these died — not having received the promises, but having seen them from far off and greeted them, and having confessed that they were strangers and passing sojourners on the earth.
For those who say such things make it plain that they are seeking a homeland;
and if they had been remembering that land from which they went out, they would have had opportunity to bend back —
but as it is, they reach out for a better one: that is, a heavenly one. Therefore God is not ashamed of them — to be called their God; for he has made ready for them a city.
By faith Abraham, being tested, has offered up Isaac — and he who had welcomed the promises was offering up his only-begotten,
the one to whom it had been spoken: "In Isaac shall your seed be called" —
having reckoned that God is able to raise even from the dead; from which, in a parable, he did receive him back.
By faith, even concerning things to come, Isaac blessed Jacob and Esau.
By faith Jacob, dying, blessed each of the sons of Joseph — and worshiped, leaning on the top of his staff.
By faith Joseph, at his end, made mention of the exodus of the sons of Israel — and gave orders concerning his bones.
By faith Moses, having been born, was hidden three months by his parents — because they saw the child was beautiful; and they did not fear the king's edict.
By faith Moses, having become great, refused to be called son of Pharaoh's daughter —
choosing rather to be mistreated together with the people of God than to hold the enjoyment of sin for a season;
counting the taunt of the Christ greater wealth than the treasures of Egypt — for he kept looking away toward the wage-repayment.
By faith he left Egypt behind, not fearing the fury of the king — for he held firm, as seeing the Invisible One.
By faith he has kept the Passover and the pouring on of the blood — so that the destroyer of the firstborn should not touch them.
By faith they crossed through the Red Sea as through dry land — which the Egyptians, making the attempt, were swallowed down.
By faith the walls of Jericho fell, having been encircled for seven days.
By faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish together with those who disobeyed — having received the scouts with peace.
And what more shall I say? For the time will fail me, telling of Gideon, Barak, Samson, Jephthah, of David and also Samuel, and the prophets —
who through faith fought down kingdoms, worked righteousness, obtained promises, fenced shut the mouths of lions,
quenched the power of fire, escaped the mouths of the sword, were empowered out of weakness, became strong in war, turned back the camps of foreigners.
Women received back their dead — out of resurrection. But others were beaten on the rack, not accepting the ransom-release — so that they might obtain a better resurrection;
and others received the trial of mockings and floggings — and beyond that, of chains and prison.
They were stoned; they were sawn in two; they died in the murder of the sword; they went around in sheepskins, in goats' hides — lacking, afflicted, mistreated —
of whom the world was not worthy — wandering over deserts and mountains and caves and the holes of the earth.
And all these, having received testimony through the faith, did not carry off the promise —
God having foreseen something better concerning us: that not without us should they be brought to completion.

heb.12.text: For that very reason — we also, having so great a cloud of witnesses lying around us — putting off every weight, and the sin that stands so easily around us, let us run with endurance the contest lying before us,
looking away to the pioneer and completer of the faith: Jesus — who, for the joy lying before him, endured a cross, despising shame — and has sat down at the right hand of the throne of God.
For consider closely the one who has endured such contradiction from the sinners against himself — so that you do not grow weary, coming loose in your souls.
Not yet, in your struggle against sin, have you stood in resistance to the point of blood;
and you have quite forgotten the encouragement that reasons with you as with sons: "My son, do not make light of the Lord's discipline, and do not come loose when exposed by him;
for whom the Lord loves, he disciplines — and he whips every son he receives."
It is for discipline that you endure: God is dealing with you as with sons. For what son is there whom a father does not discipline?
But if you are without discipline, of which all have become sharers — then you are illegitimate, and not sons.
Furthermore, we had the fathers of our flesh as discipliners, and we respected them; shall we not much rather submit ourselves to the Father of spirits — and live?
For they disciplined for a few days, according to what seemed right to them; but he, for our profit — that we may partake of his holiness.
And all discipline, for the present, seems to be not of joy but of grief; but afterward it gives back a peaceable fruit of righteousness to those trained through it.
Therefore straighten up the drooping hands and the paralyzed knees,
and make straight tracks for your feet — so that what is lame may not be turned out of the way, but rather be healed.
Pursue peace with all — and the holiness without which no one will see the Lord;
watching over one another lest anyone fall short of the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness, growing up, cause trouble — and through it many be defiled;
lest there be anyone immoral, or profane like Esau — who for one meal sold off his own firstborn rights;
for you know that afterward, wanting to inherit the blessing, he was rejected — for he found no place for a turning of the mind, though he sought the blessing out with tears.
For you have not come to something touchable — a burning fire, and thick darkness, and gloom, and whirlwind,
and a trumpet's blast, and a voice of spoken words — whose hearers begged off, that no further word be added to them;
for they could not bear the command: "Even if a beast touches the mountain, it shall be stoned";
and — so fearful was the sight — Moses said: "I am terrified, and trembling."
No — you have come to Mount Zion, and to the city of the living God, heavenly Jerusalem; and to myriads of angels in festal gathering;
and to the assembly of the firstborn, enrolled in the heavens; and to God, judge of all; and to the spirits of the righteous brought to completion;
and to the mediator of a new covenant — Jesus; and to the blood of sprinkling, speaking better than Abel's.
Watch that you do not beg off from the one speaking. For if those did not escape who begged off from the one warning them on earth — much more we, who are turning away from the one from the heavens;
whose voice shook the earth then; but now he has promised, saying: "Yet once more I will shake not only the earth, but also the heaven."
And the "Yet once more" makes plain the removal of the things shaken — as things that have been made — so that the things not shaken may remain.
Therefore, receiving an unshakable kingdom, let us hold grace — through which let us serve God well-pleasingly, with reverent awe and dread;
for indeed our God is a devouring fire.

heb.13.text: Let brotherly love remain.
Do not forget hospitality to strangers — for through it some have hosted angels without knowing it.
Remember the prisoners, as bound together with them; those mistreated, as being yourselves also in a body.
Let marriage be honored among all, and the marriage bed undefiled — for the immoral and adulterers God will judge.
Let your way of life be free of silver-love — content with what is at hand; for he himself has said: "I will never let you go, and never, never will I abandon you";
so that we say with good courage: "The Lord is my helper; I will not fear. What shall a human being do to me?"
Remember your leaders, who spoke to you the word of God; observing closely the outcome of their way of life, imitate their faith.
Jesus Christ — yesterday and today the same, and to the ages.
Do not be carried off by many-colored and strange teachings; for it is beautiful for the heart to be made firm by grace — not by foods, in which those who walked by them were not profited.
We have an altar from which those serving the tent have no right to eat;
for the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest, for sin, are burned down outside the camp.
Therefore Jesus also — that he might make the people holy through his own blood — suffered outside the gate.
So then, let us go out to him, outside the camp — carrying his taunt;
for here we have no remaining city — we are seeking out the coming one.
Through him, then, let us offer up a sacrifice of praise to God through everything — that is, the fruit of lips confessing his name.
And do not forget the doing of good and the sharing — for with such sacrifices God is well pleased.

Trust your leaders and yield to them — for they keep watch, sleepless, over your souls, as those who will give an account; that they may do this with joy and not groaning, for that would be unprofitable for you.
Pray for us — for we are persuaded that we have a beautiful conscience, wanting to conduct ourselves beautifully in everything;
and I urge you all the more abundantly to do this, so that I may be restored to you more quickly.
Now may the God of peace — who led up out of the dead the great Shepherd of the sheep, in the blood of an everlasting covenant: our Lord Jesus —
mend you into completeness in every good thing, to do his will; working in us what is well-pleasing before him, through Jesus Christ — to whom be the glory, to the ages. Amen.
And I urge you, brothers: bear with the word of encouragement — for indeed I have written to you briefly.
Know that our brother Timothy has been released; with him, if he comes quickly, I will see you.
Greet all your leaders, and all the holy ones. Those from Italy greet you.
Grace be with you all.

1co.1.text: Paul, called as an apostle of Christ Jesus through the will of God, and Sosthenes the brother —
to the assembly of God that is in Corinth, to those made holy in Christ Jesus, called holy ones — with all who call on the name of our Lord Jesus Christ in every place, theirs and ours:
grace to you and peace from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
I give thanks to my God always concerning you, for the grace of God given you in Christ Jesus —
that in everything you were made rich in him: in every word and all knowledge,
just as the testimony of the Christ was made firm in you;
so that you lack no grace-gift, eagerly awaiting the unveiling of our Lord Jesus Christ —
who will also make you firm to the end: uncharged, in the day of our Lord Jesus Christ.
Faithful is God, through whom you were called into the sharing of his Son, Jesus Christ our Lord.

And I appeal to you, brothers, through the name of our Lord Jesus Christ: that you all say the same thing, and that there be no tears in your fabric — that you be mended together in the same mind and in the same judgment.
For it was made plain to me about you, my brothers, by Chloe's people, that there are strifes among you.
And I mean this: each of you says, "I am of Paul," or "I of Apollos," or "I of Cephas," or "I of Christ."
Has the Christ been parceled out? Was Paul crucified for you? Or were you baptized into the name of Paul?
I give thanks that I baptized none of you — except Crispus and Gaius —
so that no one can say you were baptized into my name.
And I did baptize the household of Stephanas also; beyond that, I do not know whether I baptized anyone else.
For Christ did not send me to baptize but to bring the good news — not in wisdom of word, so that the cross of the Christ should not be emptied.
For the word of the cross is foolishness to those who are perishing — but to us who are being saved, it is the power of God.
For it is written: "I will destroy the wisdom of the wise, and the understanding of the understanding I will set aside."
Where is the wise man? Where the scribe? Where the debater of this age? Did God not make foolish the wisdom of the world?
For since, in the wisdom of God, the world through its wisdom did not know God, God was well pleased — through the foolishness of the proclamation — to save those who believe.
Since Jews ask for signs and Greeks seek wisdom,
we proclaim Christ crucified: to Jews a stumbling-block, and to nations foolishness —
but to the called themselves, Jews and Greeks alike: Christ, the power of God and the wisdom of God;
because the foolish thing of God is wiser than human beings, and the weak thing of God stronger than human beings.
For look at your calling, brothers: not many wise according to flesh, not many powerful, not many well-born;
but God chose the foolish things of the world, so that he might put the wise to shame; and God chose the weak things of the world, so that he might put the strong things to shame;
and the low-born things of the world and the despised things God chose — the things that are not — so that he might bring to nothing the things that are;
so that no flesh might boast before God.
But out of him you are in Christ Jesus — who became wisdom for us from God: righteousness and sanctification and ransom-release —
so that, just as it is written: "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord."

1co.2.text: And I, coming to you, brothers, did not come announcing to you the testimony of God with any towering superiority of word or wisdom;
for I judged to know nothing among you except Jesus Christ — and him crucified.
And I came to you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling;
and my word and my proclamation were not in persuasive words of wisdom, but in demonstration of Spirit and of power —
so that your faith should not stand in the wisdom of human beings, but in the power of God.
Yet we do speak wisdom among the complete — a wisdom, though, not of this age, nor of the rulers of this age, who are being brought to nothing;
rather we speak God's wisdom in a mystery — the hidden wisdom, which God marked out beforehand, before the ages, for our glory;
which none of the rulers of this age has known — for if they had known, they would not have crucified the Lord of glory.
But just as it is written: "What eye did not see, and ear did not hear, and what did not rise onto the human heart — all that God made ready for those who love him."
For to us God unveiled it through the Spirit; for the Spirit searches all things — even the depths of God.
For who among human beings knows the things of the human being, except the spirit of the human being that is in him? So also the things of God no one has known — except the Spirit of God.
And we did not receive the spirit of the world, but the Spirit that is out of God — so that we might know the things freely graced to us by God;
which things we also speak — not in words taught by human wisdom, but in words taught by the Spirit: fitting spiritual things to spiritual.
But the soulish human being does not receive the things of the Spirit of God — for they are foolishness to him, and he cannot know them, because they are discerned spiritually;
but the spiritual person discerns all things — and is himself discerned by no one.
For "who has known the mind of the Lord, who will instruct him?" But we hold the mind of Christ.

1co.3.text: And I, brothers, could not speak to you as to spiritual people — only as to fleshly ones: as to infants in Christ.
I gave you milk to drink, not solid food — for you were not yet able. And even now you are not yet able —
for you are still fleshly. For where there is jealousy and strife among you, are you not fleshly, and walking according to man?
For whenever someone says, "I am of Paul," and another, "I of Apollos" — are you not merely human?
What then is Apollos? And what is Paul? Servants — through whom you believed, and to each as the Lord gave.
I planted, Apollos watered — but God kept making it grow;
so that neither the one who plants is anything, nor the one who waters — only God, who makes it grow.
And the one who plants and the one who waters are one — and each shall receive his own wage according to his own labor;
for we are God's co-workers; you are God's field, God's building.
According to the grace of God given me, like a wise master-builder I laid a foundation — and another builds on it. But let each one watch how he builds on it;
for no one can lay another foundation beside the one lying there — which is Jesus Christ.
And if anyone builds on the foundation with gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, straw —
each one's work will become visible; for the Day will make it plain, because it is unveiled in fire — and the fire itself will test each one's work, what kind it is.
If anyone's work remains — what he built upon it — he will receive a wage;
if anyone's work is burned down, he will suffer loss — but he himself will be saved, yet so: as through fire.
Do you not know that you are God's temple, and the Spirit of God dwells in you?
If anyone destroys the temple of God, God will destroy him; for the temple of God is holy — which you are.
Let no one deceive himself: if anyone among you thinks he is wise in this age, let him become a fool — so that he may become wise;
for the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God. For it is written: "He catches the wise in their craftiness";
and again: "The Lord knows the reasonings of the wise — that they are empty."
So let no one boast in human beings. For all things are yours:
whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas; whether world or life or death; whether things standing present or things to come — all are yours,
and you are Christ's — and Christ is God's.

1co.4.text: So let a person count us this way: as under-rowers of Christ, and house-stewards of the mysteries of God.
Here, moreover, it is sought in the house-stewards: that one be found faithful.
But to me it is the smallest of matters that I be examined by you, or by any human day of judgment — indeed, I do not even examine myself;
for I am conscious of nothing against myself — yet not by this have I been counted righteous. The one who examines me is the Lord.

So judge nothing before its season — until the Lord comes, who will both light up the hidden things of the darkness and make visible the counsels of the hearts; and then the praise will come to each one from God.

Now these things, brothers, I have transferred in figure to myself and Apollos for your sake — so that in us you may learn the rule: "Not beyond the things that are written" — so that none of you be puffed up, one over the one and against the other.
For who marks you out as different? And what do you have that you did not receive? And if you did receive it — why do you boast as if you had not received it?
Already you are stuffed full! Already you have grown rich! Without us, you have become kings! And would that you had become kings — so that we might reign together with you.
For I think God has displayed us, the apostles, last of all — like men sentenced to death; because we have become a theater to the world, both to angels and to human beings.
We are fools for Christ's sake — but you are sensible in Christ; we weak — but you strong; you glorious — but we without honor.
Up to this very hour we both hunger and thirst, and go poorly clothed, and are struck with fists, and are homeless,
and we labor, working with our own hands. Reviled — we bless; persecuted — we bear up;
slandered — we appeal gently. We have become like the world's sweepings — the scraping-off of all things, until now.
I write these things not to shame you, but admonishing you as my beloved children;
for though you may have ten thousand child-guides in Christ, yet not many fathers — for in Christ Jesus, through the good news, I fathered you.
I appeal to you, then: become imitators of me.
For this reason I sent you Timothy, who is my beloved and faithful child in the Lord; he will remind you of my ways in Christ Jesus — just as I teach everywhere, in every assembly.
Now some have been puffed up — as though I were not coming to you;
but I will come to you quickly, if the Lord wills — and I will find out not the word of the puffed-up ones, but the power;
for the kingdom of God is not in word — but in power.
What do you want? Shall I come to you with a rod — or in love, and a spirit of meekness?

1co.5.text: It is actually reported that there is immorality among you — and such immorality as is not even among the nations: that a man has his father's wife.
And you are puffed up! And you did not rather mourn — so that the one who did this deed might be removed from your midst?
For I — absent in the body but present in the spirit — have already judged, as though present, the one who worked this out this way:
in the name of our Lord Jesus, when you are gathered together, and my spirit with you, with the power of our Lord Jesus —
hand such a man over to Satan, for the destruction of the flesh, so that the spirit may be saved in the day of the Lord.
Your boast is not beautiful. Do you not know that a little leaven leavens the whole lump?
Clean out the old leaven, so that you may be a new lump — just as you are unleavened. For indeed our Passover has been sacrificed: Christ.
So let us keep the feast — not with old leaven, nor with the leaven of malice and wickedness, but with the unleavened bread of unmixed sincerity and truth.
I wrote to you in the letter not to mix together with the immoral —
not meaning entirely the immoral of this world, or the greedy and swindlers or idolaters; since then you would have had to go out of the world!
But now I write to you not to mix together with anyone named a brother if he is immoral or greedy or an idolater or a reviler or a drunkard or a swindler — with such a one not even to eat.
For what business of mine is it to judge those outside? Is it not those inside whom you judge —
while those outside, God judges? "Remove the evil one from among yourselves."

1co.6.text: Does any of you dare, having a case against the other, to go to law before the unrighteous — and not before the holy ones?
Or do you not know that the holy ones will judge the world? And if the world is judged among you, are you unworthy of the smallest tribunals?
Do you not know that we will judge angels — let alone matters of daily life?
If, then, you have tribunals over matters of daily life — do you seat as judges those who are despised in the assembly?
I say this to your shame. Is it so, that there is not one wise person among you who will be able to decide between his brothers —
but brother goes to law with brother, and this before unbelievers?
Already, then, it is altogether a defeat for you that you have lawsuits with one another. Why not rather be wronged? Why not rather be defrauded?
But you do the wronging and the defrauding — and this to brothers!
Or do you not know that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be led astray: neither the immoral, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor the soft-passive, nor men who bed men,
nor thieves, nor the greedy, not drunkards, not revilers, not swindlers will inherit the kingdom of God.
And such were some of you. But you were washed off; but you were made holy; but you were counted righteous — in the name of the Lord Jesus and in the Spirit of our God.
"All things are lawful for me" — but not all things are profitable. "All things are lawful for me" — but I will not be put under the authority of anything.
"The foods for the belly, and the belly for the foods" — and God will bring to nothing both the one and the other. But the body is not for immorality — it is for the Lord, and the Lord for the body;
and God both raised the Lord and will raise us up, through his power.
Do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ? Shall I then take the members of the Christ and make them members of a prostitute? Never may it be!
Or do you not know that the one gluing himself to the prostitute is one body with her? For "the two," it says, "shall become one flesh";
but the one gluing himself to the Lord is one spirit with him.
Flee immorality! Every sin a person may commit is outside the body — but the one committing immorality sins into his own body.
Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit in you, whom you have from God — and you are not your own?
For you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body.

1co.7.text: Now concerning the things you wrote about: "It is beautiful for a man not to touch a woman."
But because of the immoralities, let each man have his own wife, and let each woman have her own husband.
Let the husband give back to the wife what is owed — and likewise the wife also to the husband.
The wife does not hold authority over her own body — the husband does; and likewise the husband also does not hold authority over his own body — the wife does.

Do not deprive one another — except perhaps by mutual agreement for a season, so that you may have leisure for prayer; and then be together again, so that Satan may not test you through your lack of self-mastery.
But I say this by way of concession, not by way of command.
And I want all human beings to be as I myself am; but each has his own grace-gift from God — one this way, and one that way.
Now to the unmarried and the widows I say: it is beautiful for them if they remain as I am;
but if they do not have self-mastery, let them marry — for it is better to marry than to burn.
And to those who have married I command — not I, but the Lord: a wife is not to be separated from her husband
(but if indeed she is separated, let her remain unmarried, or be reconciled to her husband) — and a husband is not to send away his wife.
But to the rest I say — I, not the Lord: if any brother has an unbelieving wife, and she is content to live with him, let him not send her away;
and if any woman has an unbelieving husband, and he is content to live with her, let her not send away her husband.
For the unbelieving husband has been made holy in the wife, and the unbelieving wife has been made holy in the brother — since otherwise your children would be unclean; but as it is, they are holy.
But if the unbeliever separates — let him separate; the brother or the sister is not enslaved in such cases. God has called us in peace.
For how do you know, wife, whether you will save your husband? Or how do you know, husband, whether you will save your wife?
Only — as the Lord apportioned to each, as God has called each, so let him walk. And so I order in all the assemblies.
Was anyone called circumcised? Let him not undo it. Has anyone been called in foreskin? Let him not be circumcised.
Circumcision is nothing, and the foreskin is nothing — but keeping God's commandments.
Let each one remain in the calling in which he was called.
Were you called as a slave? Let it not weigh on you. But if you can indeed become free — rather, use it.
For the one called in the Lord as a slave is the Lord's freedman; likewise the one called as a free man is Christ's slave.
You were bought with a price — do not become slaves of human beings.
In whatever state each was called, brothers — in that let him remain, with God.
Now concerning the virgins I have no command of the Lord; but I give a judgment, as one shown mercy by the Lord to be faithful.
I consider, then, that this is beautiful because of the present pressure: that it is beautiful for a person to be as he is.
Are you bound to a wife? Do not seek release. Are you released from a wife? Do not seek a wife.
But if you do marry, you have not sinned; and if the virgin marries, she has not sinned. Yet such people will have affliction in the flesh — and I am sparing you.
And this I declare, brothers: the season has been drawn in tight. For the rest, let even those who have wives be as though not having,
and those who weep as though not weeping, and those who rejoice as though not rejoicing, and those who buy as though not possessing,
and those who use the world as though not using it up — for the form of this world is passing by.
