And I want you to be free of anxiety. The unmarried man is anxious for the things of the Lord — how he may please the Lord;
but the married man is anxious for the things of the world — how he may please his wife —

and he is divided. And the unmarried woman, and the virgin, is anxious for the things of the Lord, that she may be holy both in body and in spirit; but the married woman is anxious for the things of the world — how she may please her husband.
And I say this for your own profit — not to throw a noose over you, but for what is seemly, and for undistracted, devoted attendance on the Lord.
But if anyone thinks he is behaving unseemly toward his virgin — if she is past her prime, and so it ought to be — let him do what he wants: he does not sin; let them marry.
But he who stands settled in his heart, having no compulsion, and holds authority over his own will, and has judged this in his own heart — to keep his virgin as she is — he will do beautifully.
So then: the one who marries his virgin does beautifully — and the one who does not marry will do better.
A wife is bound for as long a time as her husband lives; but if the husband falls asleep, she is free to be married to whom she wishes — only in the Lord.
But she is happier if she remains as she is — according to my judgment; and I think that I too have the Spirit of God.

1co.8.text: Now concerning the idol-sacrifices: we know that "we all have knowledge." Knowledge puffs up — but love builds up.
If anyone thinks he has come to know something, he does not yet know as he must know;
but if anyone loves God — this one has been known by him.
Concerning, then, the eating of the idol-sacrifices: we know that an idol is nothing in the world, and that there is no God but one.
For even if there are so-called gods, whether in heaven or on earth — as indeed there are many gods and many lords —
yet for us there is one God, the Father — out of whom are all things, and we unto him; and one Lord, Jesus Christ — through whom are all things, and we through him.
But the knowledge is not in everyone. Some, by their habituation to the idol until now, eat the food as an idol-sacrifice — and their conscience, being weak, is defiled.
But food will not present us to God: neither, if we do not eat, do we fall short; nor, if we eat, do we overflow.
But watch that this authority of yours does not somehow become a stumbling-stone to the weak.
For if someone sees you — the one who has knowledge — reclining at table in an idol's temple, will not his conscience, weak as he is, be "built up" to eat the idol-sacrifices?
For the weak one is being destroyed by your knowledge — the brother for whose sake Christ died.
And sinning this way against the brothers, and striking their weak conscience, you sin against Christ.
For that very reason: if food snares my brother, I will never eat meat again, to the age — so that I may not snare my brother.

1co.9.text: Am I not free? Am I not an apostle? Have I not seen Jesus our Lord? Are you not my work in the Lord?
If to others I am not an apostle — surely I am to you; for you are the seal of my apostleship in the Lord.
My defense to those who examine me is this:
Do we not have the right to eat and to drink?
Do we not have the right to take along a sister as wife — like the rest of the apostles, and the brothers of the Lord, and Cephas?
Or is it only I and Barnabas who have no right not to work?
Who ever serves as a soldier at his own wages? Who plants a vineyard and does not eat its fruit? Who shepherds a flock and does not eat from the milk of the flock?
Am I saying these things merely on human authority — or does the law not say them too?
For in the law of Moses it is written: "You shall not muzzle an ox while it is threshing." Is it for the oxen that God cares —
or does he say it entirely for our sake? For our sake it was written: that the plowman ought to plow upon hope, and the thresher, upon hope of sharing.
If we sowed the spiritual things among you, is it a great thing if we shall reap your fleshly things?
If others share this right over you, do not we all the more? But we did not use this right — we bear up under everything, so that we may give no cutting-in to the good news of the Christ.
Do you not know that those who work the temple things eat from the temple — that those attending devotedly at the altar share together with the altar?
So also the Lord ordered that those announcing the good news should live from the good news.

But I have used none of these things. And I have not written these things so that it should be done this way with me — for it would be beautiful for me rather to die than — no one shall empty my boast!
For if I bring the good news, that is no boast for me — for necessity lies upon me; for woe to me if I do not bring the good news!
For if I do this willingly, I have a wage; but if unwillingly — I have been entrusted with a stewardship.
What then is my wage? That in bringing the good news I may set the good news out free of cost — so as not to use up my right in the good news.
For being free from all, I enslaved myself to all — so that I might gain the more:
and to the Jews I became as a Jew, so that I might gain Jews; to those under law, as under law — not being myself under law — so that I might gain those under law;
to those without law, as without law — not being without God's law, but in-lawed to Christ — so that I might gain those without law.
To the weak I became weak, so that I might gain the weak. To all people I have become all things — so that by all means I might save some.
And I do everything for the sake of the good news — so that I may become a co-sharer of it.
Do you not know that the runners in the stadium all run — but one receives the prize? So run — that you may seize it.
And every contestant exercises self-mastery in everything: they, indeed, to receive a perishable crown — but we, an imperishable one.
So then I run this way: not as without aim; I box this way: not as beating air;
rather, I strike my body under the eye and lead it as a slave — lest somehow, having proclaimed to others, I myself should become unproved.

1co.10.text: For I do not want you to be unknowing, brothers: our fathers were all under the cloud, and all passed through the sea,
and all were baptized into Moses, in the cloud and in the sea,
and all ate the same spiritual food,
and all drank the same spiritual drink — for they kept drinking from a spiritual rock that followed them; and the rock was the Christ.
But with most of them God was not well pleased — for they were strewn down dead in the wilderness.
Now these things became patterns for us — so that we should not be cravers of evil things, as those men craved.
And do not become idolaters, as some of them did — as it is written: "The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play."
Nor let us commit immorality, as some of them committed immorality — and in one day twenty-three thousand fell.
Nor let us test the Christ to the limit, as some of them tested him — and were being destroyed by the snakes.
Nor grumble, just as some of them grumbled — and perished by the Destroyer.
Now these things kept happening to them as patterns; and they were written for our admonition — we upon whom the ends of the ages have arrived.
So then: let the one who thinks he stands watch that he does not fall.

No testing has taken hold of you except what is human. And God is faithful — who will not let you be tested beyond what you are able; rather, with the testing he will also make the way out, so that you can bear up under it.
For that very reason, my beloved: flee from idolatry.
I speak as to sensible people: you judge what I declare.
The cup of blessing that we bless — is it not a sharing in the blood of the Christ? The bread that we break — is it not a sharing in the body of the Christ?
Because there is one bread, we the many are one body — for we all partake out of the one bread.
Look at Israel according to the flesh: are not those who eat the sacrifices sharers of the altar?
What then do I declare — that an idol-sacrifice is anything, or that an idol is anything?
No — but that what they sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons and not to God; and I do not want you to become sharers of the demons.
You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons; you cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons.
Or shall we provoke the Lord to jealousy? Are we stronger than he?
"All things are lawful" — but not all things are profitable. "All things are lawful" — but not all things build up.
Let no one seek his own good — but the good of the other.
Everything sold in the meat market, eat — examining nothing, for the sake of conscience;
for "the earth is the Lord's, and her fullness."
If one of the unbelievers invites you, and you want to go — everything set before you, eat, examining nothing for the sake of conscience.
But if someone says to you, "This is temple-sacrificed" — do not eat it, for the sake of the one who informed you, and for conscience —
and by conscience I mean not your own, but the other's. For why should my freedom be judged by another's conscience?
If I partake with thanksgiving, why am I slandered over that for which I give thanks?
So then: whether you eat or drink or do anything at all — do everything to the glory of God.
Be without stumbling-cause — to Jews and to Greeks and to the assembly of God;
just as I also please everyone in everything — not seeking my own profit, but that of the many, so that they may be saved.

1co.11.text: Become imitators of me — just as I also am of Christ.
And I praise you, that you remember me in everything and hold fast the traditions just as I handed them over to you.
But I want you to know that the head of every man is the Christ; and the head of a woman, the man; and the head of the Christ, God.
Every man praying or prophesying with something down over his head shames his head;
but every woman praying or prophesying with her head uncovered shames her head — for it is one and the same as the shaven woman.
For if a woman is not covered, let her also be shorn; but if it is shameful for a woman to be shorn or shaven — let her be covered.
For a man ought not to have his head covered, being the image and glory of God; but the woman is the glory of man.
For man is not out of woman — but woman out of man;
and indeed man was not created for the woman's sake — but woman for the man's sake.
For this reason the woman ought to have authority upon her head — because of the angels.
Nevertheless: neither is woman apart from man, nor man apart from woman, in the Lord;
for just as the woman is out of the man, so also the man is through the woman — and all things are out of God.
Judge among your own selves: is it fitting for a woman to pray to God uncovered?
Does not nature herself teach you that if a man wears long hair, it is a dishonor to him —
but if a woman wears long hair, it is a glory to her? Because the long hair has been given her in place of a covering.
But if anyone is inclined to be fond of quarrels — we have no such custom, nor do the assemblies of God.
Now in giving this order I do not praise you — because you come together not for the better but for the worse.
For first of all, when you come together in assembly, I hear that there are tears in your fabric — and in some part I believe it;
for there must be factions among you also, so that the tested-and-approved may become visible among you.
When you come together, then, in one place, it is not the Lord's supper you eat —
for in the eating, each one takes his own supper first; and one goes hungry, while another is drunk.
Do you not have houses for eating and drinking? Or do you despise the assembly of God, and shame those who have nothing? What shall I say to you? Shall I praise you? In this I do not praise you.
For I received from the Lord what I also handed over to you: that the Lord Jesus, in the night in which he was being handed over, took bread,
and having given thanks, he broke it and said: "This is my body, which is for you. Do this — for my remembrance."
Likewise also the cup, after the supper, saying: "This cup is the new covenant in my blood. Do this, as often as you drink it — for my remembrance."
For as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you announce the death of the Lord — until he comes.
So then, whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord unworthily will be answerable for the body and the blood of the Lord.
But let a person prove himself — and so let him eat of the bread and drink of the cup;
for the one who eats and drinks without discerning the body eats and drinks judgment on himself.
For this reason many among you are weak and sick — and a good number sleep.
But if we discerned ourselves, we would not be judged;
and when we are judged by the Lord, we are being disciplined — so that we may not be condemned together with the world.
So then, my brothers: when you come together to eat, wait for one another.
If anyone is hungry, let him eat at home — so that you do not come together into judgment. And the remaining matters I will set in order whenever I come.

1co.12.text: Now concerning the spiritual things, brothers, I do not want you to be unknowing.
You know that when you were nations, you were led away to the voiceless idols — however you were led.
Therefore I make known to you: no one speaking in the Spirit of God says, "Jesus is accursed"; and no one can say, "Jesus is Lord," except in the Holy Spirit.
Now there are distributions of grace-gifts — but the same Spirit;
and there are distributions of services — and the same Lord;
and there are distributions of workings — but the same God, who works all things in all.
And to each is given the making-visible of the Spirit, toward what profits all:
for to one, through the Spirit, is given a word of wisdom; to another, a word of knowledge, according to the same Spirit;
to a different one, faith, in the same Spirit; to another, grace-gifts of healings, in the one Spirit;
to another, workings of deeds of power; to another, prophecy; to another, discernings of spirits; to a different one, kinds of tongues; to another, interpretation of tongues.
And all these things one and the same Spirit works — distributing individually to each just as he wills.
For just as the body is one and has many members, and all the members of the body — being many — are one body: so also the Christ.
For indeed in one Spirit we were all baptized into one body — whether Jews or Greeks, whether slaves or free — and we were all given one Spirit to drink.
For the body also is not one member, but many.
If the foot says, "Because I am not a hand, I am not of the body" — is it not, for all that, of the body?
And if the ear says, "Because I am not an eye, I am not of the body" — is it not, for all that, of the body?
If the whole body were an eye — where would the hearing be? If the whole were hearing — where the smelling?
But as it is, God has placed the members — each single one of them — in the body just as he willed.
And if all were one member — where would the body be?
But as it is: many members — and one body.
And the eye cannot say to the hand, "I have no need of you" — nor again the head to the feet, "I have no need of you."
On the contrary — much rather, the members of the body that seem to be weaker are necessary;
and the parts of the body we think less honorable — around these we set the more abundant honor; and our unseemly parts have the more abundant seemliness,
while our seemly parts have no need. But God has blended the body together — giving the more abundant honor to the part that lacked —
so that there be no tear in the fabric of the body, but the members have the same anxious care for one another;
and if one member suffers, all the members suffer together; if a member is glorified, all the members rejoice together.
Now you are the body of Christ — and members, each in part.
And God placed some in the assembly: first apostles, second prophets, third teachers; then deeds of power; then grace-gifts of healings, helpings, steerings, kinds of tongues.
Are all apostles? Are all prophets? Are all teachers? Are all deeds of power?
Do all have grace-gifts of healings? Do all speak in tongues? Do all interpret?
But be zealous for the greater grace-gifts. And still — I show you a way beyond all measure.

1co.13.text: If I speak in the tongues of human beings and of angels, but do not have love — I have become sounding bronze or a clashing cymbal.
And if I have prophecy, and know all the mysteries and all the knowledge; and if I have all the faith, so as to move mountains — but do not have love, I am nothing.
And if I feed out all my possessions morsel by morsel, and if I hand over my body so that I may boast — but do not have love, I am profited nothing.
Love is patient; love is kind; love is not jealous; love does not parade itself, is not puffed up,
does not behave unseemly, does not seek its own, is not sharpened to anger, does not keep count of the evil,
does not rejoice over the unrighteousness — but rejoices together with the truth;
it bears up under all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things.
Love never falls. But whether prophecies — they will be brought to nothing; whether tongues — they will cease; whether knowledge — it will be brought to nothing.
For we know in part, and we prophesy in part;
but when the complete comes, the in-part will be brought to nothing.
When I was an infant, I spoke like an infant, I thought like an infant, I reasoned like an infant; when I became a man, I brought to nothing the things of the infant.
For now we see through a mirror, in a riddle — but then, face to face. Now I know in part — but then I shall know fully, just as I also have been fully known.
And now remain faith, hope, love — these three; and the greatest of these is love.

1co.14.text: Pursue love; and be zealous for the spiritual things — but rather, that you may prophesy.
For the one speaking in a tongue speaks not to human beings but to God — for no one hears with understanding; but in the Spirit he speaks mysteries.
But the one prophesying speaks to human beings: building-up and encouragement and consolation.
The one speaking in a tongue builds up himself; but the one prophesying builds up an assembly.

Now I want you all to speak in tongues — but rather, that you may prophesy; and greater is the one prophesying than the one speaking in tongues — unless he interprets, so that the assembly may receive building-up.
But as it is, brothers: if I come to you speaking in tongues, what shall I profit you — unless I speak to you either in unveiling or in knowledge or in prophecy or in teaching?
Even the soulless things that give a voice — whether flute or harp — if they give no distinction to the notes, how will what is played on flute or harp be known?
For indeed, if a trumpet gives an unclear voice — who will make ready for war?
So also you: unless with the tongue you give an easily-marked word, how will what is spoken be known? For you will be speaking into air.
There are, it may be, so many kinds of voices in the world — and none is voiceless;
if then I do not know the force of the voice, I shall be a barbarian to the speaker — and the speaker a barbarian to me.
So also you: since you are zealots for spirits, seek that you may overflow toward the building-up of the assembly.
Therefore let the one speaking in a tongue pray that he may interpret.
For if I pray in a tongue, my spirit prays — but my mind is fruitless.
What then is it? I will pray with the spirit — and I will pray with the mind also; I will make music with the spirit — and I will make music with the mind also.
Otherwise, if you bless with the spirit, how shall the one filling the place of the outsider say the "Amen" at your thanksgiving — since he does not know what you are saying?
For you indeed give thanks beautifully — but the other is not built up.
I give thanks to God — I speak in tongues more than all of you;
but in an assembly I want to speak five words with my mind, so that I may also instruct others, rather than ten thousand words in a tongue.
Brothers, do not become children in your thinking — in malice be infants, but in your thinking become complete.
In the law it is written: "By people of other tongues and by lips of others I will speak to this people — and not even so will they listen to me, says the Lord."
So then, the tongues are for a sign — not to the believers but to the unbelieving; but prophecy is not for the unbelieving but for the believers.
If, then, the whole assembly comes together in one place and all speak in tongues, and outsiders or unbelievers come in — will they not say that you are raving mad?
But if all prophesy, and some unbeliever or outsider comes in — he is exposed by all, examined by all;
the hidden things of his heart become visible; and so, falling on his face, he will worship God, announcing: "God really is among you!"
What then is it, brothers? Whenever you come together, each one has a psalm, has a teaching, has an unveiling, has a tongue, has an interpretation. Let all things be done toward building-up.
If anyone speaks in a tongue — two, or at most three, and each in turn; and let one interpret;
but if there is no interpreter, let him be silent in assembly — and let him speak to himself, and to God.
And let two or three prophets speak — and let the others discern;
but if something is unveiled to another who is seated, let the first be silent.
For you can all prophesy, one by one — so that all may learn and all be encouraged;
(and the spirits of prophets submit themselves to prophets —
for God is not a God of disorder, but of peace), as in all the assemblies of the holy ones.
Let the women be silent in the assemblies; for it is not permitted them to speak — rather, let them submit themselves, just as the law also says.
And if they want to learn something, let them ask their own husbands at home; for it is shameful for a woman to speak in assembly.
Or did the word of God go out from you? Or did it arrive at you alone?
If anyone thinks he is a prophet or spiritual, let him fully recognize that the things I write to you are the Lord's;
but if anyone does not know it — he is not known.
So then, my brothers: be zealous for the prophesying — and do not forbid the speaking in tongues;
but let everything come to pass becomingly, and according to order.

1co.15.text: Now I make known to you, brothers, the good news that I brought you — which you also received, in which you also stand,
through which you are also being saved — if you hold fast the word with which I brought you the good news; unless you believed in vain.
For I handed over to you among the first things what I also received: that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures,
and that he was buried, and that he has been raised on the third day according to the scriptures,
and that he was seen by Cephas — then by the twelve;
then he was seen by more than five hundred brothers at once — of whom the greater number remain until now, though some have fallen asleep;
then he was seen by James — then by all the apostles;
and last of all — as to the one untimely born — he was seen by me also.
For I am the least of the apostles — not sufficient to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the assembly of God;
but by the grace of God I am what I am — and his grace toward me did not prove empty; rather, I labored more abundantly than all of them — yet not I, but the grace of God with me.
Whether then it was I or they — so we proclaim, and so you believed.
Now if Christ is proclaimed as raised from the dead, how can some among you say that there is no resurrection of the dead?
But if there is no resurrection of the dead — neither has Christ been raised;
and if Christ has not been raised, then empty is our proclamation — and empty also your faith;
and we are found to be even false witnesses of God — because we testified against God that he raised the Christ, whom he did not raise, if indeed the dead are not raised.
For if the dead are not raised — neither has Christ been raised;
and if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile — you are still in your sins;
then also those who fell asleep in Christ have perished.
If in this life only we have set our hope in Christ — we are, of all human beings, the most to be pitied.
But as it is: Christ has been raised from the dead — the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep.
For since through a man came death, through a man also comes the resurrection of the dead;
for just as in Adam all die, so also in the Christ all shall be made alive —
but each in his own rank: Christ the firstfruits; then those who are Christ's, at his coming;
then the end — when he hands over the kingdom to the God and Father; when he brings to nothing every rulership and every authority and power.
For he must reign until he has put all the enemies under his feet.
The last enemy brought to nothing is death;
for "he subjected all things under his feet." But when it says that all things have been subjected, it is plain that this excepts the one who subjected all things to him.
And when all things are subjected to him, then the Son himself will also be subjected to the one who subjected all things to him — so that God may be all in all.
Otherwise, what will they do — those baptized on behalf of the dead? If the dead are not raised at all, why indeed are people baptized on their behalf?
And why do we ourselves stand in danger every hour?
Day by day I die — yes, by the boast about you, which I hold in Christ Jesus our Lord!
If, humanly speaking, I fought wild beasts in Ephesus — what is the profit to me? If the dead are not raised: "Let us eat and drink, for tomorrow we die."
Do not be led astray: "Bad companionships corrupt kind ways."
Sober up rightly, and stop sinning — for some hold an ignorance of God; I speak to your shame.
But someone will say: "How are the dead raised? And with what kind of body do they come?"
Fool! What you yourself sow is not made alive unless it dies;
and what you sow — you do not sow the body that shall come to be, but a naked kernel: perhaps of wheat, or one of the rest;
and God gives it a body just as he willed — and to each of the seeds its own body.
Not all flesh is the same flesh: there is one flesh of human beings, another flesh of livestock, another flesh of birds, another of fish.
And there are heavenly bodies and earthly bodies — but the glory of the heavenly is one kind, and that of the earthly another:
one glory of the sun, and another glory of the moon, and another glory of the stars — for star differs from star in glory.
So also is the resurrection of the dead: it is sown in decay — it is raised in imperishability;
it is sown in dishonor — it is raised in glory; it is sown in weakness — it is raised in power;
it is sown a soulish body — it is raised a spiritual body. If there is a soulish body, there is also a spiritual one.
So also it is written: "The first man, Adam, became a living soul" — the last Adam, a life-making Spirit.
But the spiritual is not first; the soulish is — then the spiritual.
The first man is out of earth — of dust; the second man is out of heaven.
As is the man of dust, so also are the people of dust; and as is the heavenly one, so also are the heavenly ones;
and just as we have worn the image of the man of dust, we shall wear also the image of the heavenly one.
But this I declare, brothers: flesh and blood cannot inherit the kingdom of God — nor does decay inherit imperishability.
Behold, I tell you a mystery: we shall not all sleep — but we shall all be changed:
in an indivisible instant, in the flick of an eye, at the last trumpet. For the trumpet will sound — and the dead will be raised imperishable, and we shall be changed.
For this perishable thing must put on imperishability, and this mortal thing put on deathlessness.

And when this perishable thing has put on imperishability, and this mortal thing has put on deathlessness — then shall come to pass the word that is written: "Death has been swallowed down into victory."
"Where, O Death, is your victory? Where, O Death, is your sting?"
Now the sting of death is sin — and the power of sin, the law;
but thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ!
So then, my beloved brothers: become settled, unmovable, overflowing in the work of the Lord always — knowing that your labor is not empty, in the Lord.

1co.16.text: Now concerning the collection for the holy ones: just as I ordered for the assemblies of Galatia, so also you do.
On the first day of every week, let each of you set something aside by himself, storing up whatever he may prosper in — so that there be no collections when I come.
And when I arrive, whomever you approve, I will send them with letters to carry your grace-gift to Jerusalem;
and if it is fitting for me to travel also, they shall travel with me.
And I will come to you when I pass through Macedonia — for I am passing through Macedonia —
and with you, perhaps, I will stay on, or even spend the winter, so that you may send me onward wherever I go.
For I do not want to see you now just in passing; for I hope to remain some time with you, if the Lord permits.
But I will stay on in Ephesus until Pentecost;
for a door has opened to me — great, and full of working — and the adversaries are many.
Now if Timothy comes, see that he is with you without fear — for he works the work of the Lord, as I do;
so let no one despise him. Send him onward in peace, that he may come to me — for I am expecting him with the brothers.
Now concerning Apollos the brother: I strongly urged him to come to you with the brothers; and it was not at all his will to come now — but he will come whenever he has a good season.
Stay awake. Stand firm in the faith. Be courageous. Be strong.
Let everything you do come to pass in love.
And I urge you, brothers — you know the household of Stephanas: that it is the firstfruits of Achaia, and they set themselves in order for service to the holy ones —
that you also submit yourselves to such people, and to everyone working together and laboring.
And I rejoice at the arrival of Stephanas and Fortunatus and Achaicus — because these men filled up what was lacking from you;
for they refreshed my spirit — and yours. So give full recognition to such people.
The assemblies of Asia greet you. Aquila and Prisca, with the assembly at their house, greet you warmly in the Lord.
All the brothers greet you. Greet one another with a holy kiss.
The greeting in my own hand — Paul's.
If anyone does not love the Lord — let him be accursed. Marana tha — Our Lord, come!
The grace of the Lord Jesus be with you.
My love be with you all, in Christ Jesus.

jdg.4.text: And the children of Israel again did the evil in the eyes of YHWH — and Ehud was dead.
And YHWH sold them into the hand of Jabin king of Canaan, who reigned in Hazor; and the commander of his army was Sisera, who lived in Harosheth-of-the-Nations.
And the children of Israel cried out to YHWH — for he had nine hundred chariots of iron, and he had crushed the children of Israel with force twenty years.
Now Deborah — a woman, a prophetess, wife of Lappidoth — she was judging Israel at that time;
and she used to sit under the Palm of Deborah, between Ramah and Bethel in the hill country of Ephraim; and the children of Israel went up to her for judgment.

And she sent and called Barak son of Abinoam from Kedesh in Naphtali, and said to him: "Has not YHWH, the God of Israel, commanded: Go, draw toward Mount Tabor, and take with you ten thousand men from the sons of Naphtali and the sons of Zebulun?
And I will draw out to you, to the wadi Kishon, Sisera the commander of Jabin's army, with his chariots and his multitude — and I will give him into your hand."
And Barak said to her: "If you go with me, I will go; but if you will not go with me — I will not go."

And she said: "I will surely go with you. Only — there will be no glory for you on the road you are walking, for into the hand of a woman YHWH will sell Sisera." And Deborah rose and went with Barak to Kedesh.
And Barak summoned Zebulun and Naphtali to Kedesh, and ten thousand men went up at his feet — and Deborah went up with him.
Now Heber the Kenite had separated from Kain — from the sons of Hobab, Moses' father-in-law — and had pitched his tent as far as the oak in Zaanannim, which is near Kedesh.
And they told Sisera that Barak son of Abinoam had gone up Mount Tabor;
and Sisera summoned all his chariots — nine hundred chariots of iron — and all the people who were with him, from Harosheth-of-the-Nations to the wadi Kishon.
And Deborah said to Barak: "Rise! For this is the day YHWH has given Sisera into your hand. Has not YHWH gone out before you?" And Barak went down from Mount Tabor, ten thousand men behind him;
and YHWH threw Sisera and all the chariots and all the camp into panic, by the mouth of the sword, before Barak. And Sisera got down from the chariot and fled on foot,
while Barak pursued the chariots and the camp as far as Harosheth-of-the-Nations; and all the camp of Sisera fell by the mouth of the sword — not so much as one was left.
And Sisera fled on foot to the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite — for there was peace between Jabin king of Hazor and the house of Heber the Kenite.
And Jael came out to meet Sisera and said to him: "Turn aside, my lord — turn aside to me. Do not fear." And he turned aside to her, into the tent, and she covered him with the rug.
And he said to her: "Give me, please, a little water to drink, for I am thirsty." And she opened the skin of milk and gave him drink — and covered him.
And he said to her: "Stand at the opening of the tent; and if any man comes and asks you, 'Is there a man here?' — say, 'There is not.'"

And Jael, wife of Heber, took the tent peg, and set the hammer in her hand, and came to him softly — and drove the peg into his temple, and it went down into the ground; for he was fast asleep and worn out. And he died.

And behold — Barak, pursuing Sisera. And Jael came out to meet him and said to him: "Come, and I will show you the man you are seeking." And he came in to her — and behold: Sisera, fallen, dead, with the peg in his temple.
So God subdued, on that day, Jabin king of Canaan before the children of Israel;
and the hand of the children of Israel pressed harder and harder on Jabin king of Canaan — until they had cut off Jabin king of Canaan.

jdg.5.text: And Deborah sang — with Barak son of Abinoam — on that day:
"When locks of hair flew loose in Israel, when the people offered themselves freely — bless YHWH!
Hear, O kings! Give ear, O rulers! I — to YHWH — I will sing; I will make music to YHWH, the God of Israel.
YHWH, when you went out from Seir, when you strode from the field of Edom, the earth quaked; the heavens also dripped — the clouds also dripped water;
the mountains flowed away before YHWH — this Sinai — before YHWH, the God of Israel.
In the days of Shamgar son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the roads ceased; and travelers on the paths walked twisting byways.
The unwalled villages ceased in Israel — they ceased — until I arose, Deborah; I arose, a mother in Israel.
They chose new gods — then war was at the gates. Was shield seen, or spear, among forty thousand in Israel?
My heart goes to the commanders of Israel — those who offered themselves freely among the people. Bless YHWH!
You riders on tawny donkeys, you who sit on saddle-cloths, you who walk on the road — tell of it!

At the voice of those dividing spoil among the watering troughs — there they recount the righteous acts of YHWH, the righteous acts of his unwalled villagers in Israel. Then the people of YHWH went down to the gates.
Awake, awake, Deborah! Awake, awake — speak a song! Rise, Barak — and lead away your captives, son of Abinoam!
Then a remnant went down to the nobles — a people; YHWH went down for me among the mighty.
Out of Ephraim came those whose root is in Amalek — after you, Benjamin, among your peoples; out of Machir came down commanders, and out of Zebulun those who wield the scribe's staff;
and my princes in Issachar were with Deborah — and Issachar, so too Barak: into the valley he was sent at his feet. Among the divisions of Reuben — great searchings of heart!
Why did you sit among the sheepfolds, listening to the pipings for the flocks? Among the divisions of Reuben — great searchings of heart!
Gilead stayed put beyond the Jordan; and Dan — why did he sojourn by the ships? Asher sat at the shore of the seas, and stayed put at his landings.
Zebulun — a people that taunted its own soul to the death; and Naphtali — on the high places of the field.
Kings came; they fought. Then fought the kings of Canaan, at Taanach by the waters of Megiddo — they took no gain of silver.
From the heavens the stars fought; from their courses they fought with Sisera.
The wadi Kishon swept them away — the ancient wadi, the wadi Kishon. March on, my soul, in strength!
Then the horses' hooves hammered — from the galloping, galloping of his stallions.
'Curse Meroz,' said the angel of YHWH — 'curse, curse her inhabitants: for they did not come to the help of YHWH, to the help of YHWH among the mighty.'
Blessed above women be Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite — above women in the tent, blessed.
Water he asked; milk she gave — in a bowl fit for nobles she brought curds.
Her hand she stretched to the peg, her right hand to the laborers' hammer; and she hammered Sisera — she crushed his head; she shattered and pierced through his temple.
Between her feet he bowed, he fell, he lay: between her feet he bowed, he fell — where he bowed, there he fell: destroyed.
Through the window she leaned and looked, wailing — the mother of Sisera, through the lattice: 'Why does his chariot delay in coming? Why do the hoofbeats of his chariots linger?'
The wisest of her ladies answer her — indeed, she returns her own words to herself:

'Are they not finding, dividing the spoil? — a womb, two wombs, for the head of each man; spoil of dyed cloth for Sisera, spoil of dyed cloth, embroidered — dyed cloth doubly embroidered for the necks of the spoilers.'
So may all your enemies perish, O YHWH! But those who love him — like the going out of the sun in its strength." And the land was quiet forty years.

jdg.6.text: And the children of Israel did the evil in the eyes of YHWH; and YHWH gave them into the hand of Midian seven years.
And the hand of Midian was strong over Israel. Because of Midian the children of Israel made themselves the dens in the mountains, and the caves, and the strongholds.
And it came to pass, whenever Israel sowed, that Midian would come up, and Amalek, and the sons of the east — they would come up against them,
and camp against them and destroy the produce of the land as far as the approach to Gaza; and they would leave no sustenance in Israel — not a sheep or an ox or a donkey.
For they would come up with their livestock and their tents — they came like locusts in multitude; they and their camels were without number — and they came into the land to destroy it.
And Israel was brought very low because of Midian; and the children of Israel cried out to YHWH.
And it came to pass, when the children of Israel cried out to YHWH on account of Midian,
that YHWH sent a man, a prophet, to the children of Israel; and he said to them: "Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: I brought you up from Egypt, and I brought you out of the house of slaves;
and I rescued you from the hand of Egypt and from the hand of all your oppressors, and I drove them out before you, and I gave you their land;
and I said to you: I am YHWH your God — do not fear the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are living. But you have not listened to my voice."
And the angel of YHWH came and sat under the terebinth in Ophrah, which belonged to Joash the Abiezrite — while Gideon his son was beating out wheat in the winepress, to hide it from Midian.
And the angel of YHWH appeared to him and said to him: "YHWH is with you, mighty man of valor!"

And Gideon said to him: "Please, my lord — if YHWH is with us, why has all this found us? And where are all his wonders that our fathers recounted to us, saying, 'Did not YHWH bring us up from Egypt?' But now YHWH has cast us off and given us into the palm of Midian."
And YHWH turned to him and said: "Go in this strength of yours, and save Israel from the palm of Midian. Have I not sent you?"
And he said to him: "Please, my Lord — with what shall I save Israel? Behold, my clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the youngest in my father's house."
And YHWH said to him: "Because I will be with you — and you shall strike Midian as one man."
And he said to him: "If now I have found favor in your eyes, then make me a sign that it is you speaking with me.
Do not move from here, please, until I come back to you and bring out my offering and set it before you." And he said: "I will stay until you return."

And Gideon went in and prepared a young goat, and unleavened cakes from an ephah of flour; the meat he put in the basket and the broth he put in the pot, and he brought it out to him under the terebinth — and presented it.
And the angel of God said to him: "Take the meat and the unleavened cakes, and set them on this rock — and pour out the broth." And he did so.

And the angel of YHWH stretched out the tip of the staff in his hand and touched the meat and the unleavened cakes; and fire came up from the rock and devoured the meat and the unleavened cakes — and the angel of YHWH went from his eyes.
And Gideon saw that he was the angel of YHWH; and Gideon said: "Alas, Lord YHWH! For I have seen the angel of YHWH face to face!"
And YHWH said to him: "Peace to you. Do not fear — you shall not die."
And Gideon built there an altar to YHWH and called it YHWH-Shalom — YHWH-Is-Peace; to this day it still stands in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

And it came to pass that night that YHWH said to him: "Take your father's bull — and a second bull, seven years old — and tear down the altar of Baal that belongs to your father, and cut down the Asherah pole beside it;

and build an altar to YHWH your God on the top of this stronghold, in the ordered arrangement; and take the second bull and offer it up as a burnt offering, with the wood of the Asherah you cut down."
And Gideon took ten men of his servants and did just as YHWH had spoken to him; but because he feared his father's household and the men of the city too much to do it by day — he did it by night.
And the men of the city rose early in the morning — and behold: the altar of Baal torn down, and the Asherah beside it cut down, and the second bull offered up on the newly built altar.
And they said to one another: "Who has done this thing?" And they inquired and searched, and they said: "Gideon son of Joash has done this thing."
And the men of the city said to Joash: "Bring out your son, and let him die — for he has torn down the altar of Baal, and cut down the Asherah beside it!"

And Joash said to all who stood against him: "Will you contend for Baal? Will you save him? Whoever contends for him shall be put to death by morning! If he is a god, let him contend for himself — for someone has torn down his altar."
So on that day they called him Jerubbaal — Let-Baal-Contend — saying: "Let Baal contend with him, for he has torn down his altar."
And all Midian and Amalek and the sons of the east gathered together, and crossed over, and camped in the Valley of Jezreel.
And the Spirit of YHWH clothed itself with Gideon; and he blew the shofar — and Abiezer was summoned after him.
And he sent messengers through all Manasseh, and it too was summoned after him; and he sent messengers through Asher and Zebulun and Naphtali — and they came up to meet them.
And Gideon said to God: "If you are going to save Israel by my hand, as you have spoken —

behold, I am setting the fleece of wool on the threshing floor: if there is dew on the fleece alone, and dryness on all the ground, then I shall know that you will save Israel by my hand, as you have spoken."
And it was so. He rose early the next morning and squeezed the fleece — and wrung dew out of the fleece: a bowl full of water.

And Gideon said to God: "Let not your anger burn against me — let me speak just this once more. Let me test, please, just this once with the fleece: let there be dryness on the fleece alone, and on all the ground let there be dew."
And God did so that night: there was dryness on the fleece alone — and on all the ground there was dew.

jdg.7.text: And Jerubbaal — that is, Gideon — rose early, and all the people with him, and they camped beside the spring of Harod; and the camp of Midian was north of him, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley.
And YHWH said to Gideon: "The people with you are too many for me to give Midian into their hand — lest Israel glorify itself over me, saying, 'My own hand has saved me.'

So now, call out in the ears of the people: 'Whoever is afraid and trembling — let him turn back and depart from Mount Gilead.'" And twenty-two thousand of the people turned back — and ten thousand were left.

And YHWH said to Gideon: "The people are still too many. Bring them down to the water, and I will refine them for you there. And whoever I say to you, 'This one shall go with you' — he shall go with you; and everyone of whom I say to you, 'This one shall not go with you' — he shall not go."
So he brought the people down to the water. And YHWH said to Gideon: "Everyone who laps the water with his tongue as a dog laps — set him apart; likewise everyone who kneels on his knees to drink."
And the number of those who lapped, hand to mouth, was three hundred men; and all the rest of the people knelt on their knees to drink water.
And YHWH said to Gideon: "With the three hundred men who lapped I will save you, and I will give Midian into your hand; and all the rest of the people — let them go, each to his place."

So they took the people's provisions in their hands, and their shofars; and every man of Israel he sent away, each to his tents — but the three hundred men he kept hold of. And the camp of Midian was below him in the valley.
And it came to pass that night that YHWH said to him: "Rise — go down against the camp, for I have given it into your hand.
But if you are afraid to go down — go down with Purah your servant to the camp,

and hear what they are saying; and afterward your hands will be strengthened, and you will go down against the camp." So he went down, with Purah his servant, to the edge of the armed men in the camp.
And Midian and Amalek and all the sons of the east lay along the valley like locusts in multitude; and their camels were without number — like the sand on the seashore in multitude.

And Gideon came — and behold: a man recounting a dream to his fellow. And he said: "Behold, I dreamed a dream — and behold: a round loaf of barley bread tumbling into the camp of Midian; and it came to the tent and struck it, and it fell; it turned it upside down — and the tent fell flat."
And his fellow answered and said: "This is nothing other than the sword of Gideon son of Joash, a man of Israel. God has given Midian and the whole camp into his hand."

And when Gideon heard the telling of the dream and its breaking-open, he bowed in worship; and he returned to the camp of Israel and said: "Rise — for YHWH has given the camp of Midian into your hand!"
And he divided the three hundred men into three companies; and he put shofars into the hands of them all — and empty jars, with torches inside the jars.
And he said to them: "Watch me, and do the same. Behold — when I come to the edge of the camp, then as I do, so shall you do.
When I blow the shofar — I and all who are with me — then you also blow the shofars, all around the whole camp, and say: 'For YHWH and for Gideon!'"

And Gideon came, with the hundred men who were with him, to the edge of the camp at the head of the middle watch — they had just posted the sentries — and they blew the shofars and smashed the jars in their hands.

And the three companies blew the shofars and broke the jars; they gripped the torches in their left hands and the shofars in their right hands for blowing — and they cried: "A sword for YHWH and for Gideon!"
And they stood, each man in his place, all around the camp — and the whole camp ran; they shouted, and fled.

And the three hundred blew the shofars — and YHWH set each man's sword against his fellow, throughout the whole camp; and the camp fled as far as Beth-shittah toward Zererah, up to the edge of Abel-meholah, by Tabbath.
And the men of Israel were called out from Naphtali and from Asher and from all Manasseh — and they pursued after Midian.

And Gideon sent messengers through all the hill country of Ephraim, saying: "Come down against Midian, and seize the waters ahead of them, as far as Beth-barah, and the Jordan." And every man of Ephraim was called out, and they seized the waters as far as Beth-barah, and the Jordan;

and they captured the two princes of Midian — Oreb and Zeeb. They killed Oreb at the Rock of Oreb, and Zeeb they killed at the Winepress of Zeeb; and they pursued Midian. And the heads of Oreb and Zeeb they brought to Gideon from beyond the Jordan.

jdg.8.text: And the men of Ephraim said to him: "What is this thing you have done to us — not calling us when you went to fight against Midian?" And they contended with him fiercely.
And he said to them: "What have I done now, compared with you? Are not the gleanings of Ephraim better than the grape harvest of Abiezer?
Into your hand God gave the princes of Midian — Oreb and Zeeb. What was I able to do, compared with you?" Then their spirit toward him slackened, when he spoke this word.
And Gideon came to the Jordan, crossing over — he and the three hundred men with him — worn out, yet pursuing.
And he said to the men of Succoth: "Give, please, rounds of bread to the people at my feet — for they are worn out, and I am pursuing Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian."
And the princes of Succoth said: "Is the palm of Zebah and Zalmunna already in your hand — that we should give bread to your army?"
And Gideon said: "For that — when YHWH gives Zebah and Zalmunna into my hand, I will thresh your flesh with wilderness thorns and with briers!"
And he went up from there to Penuel and spoke to them the same way; and the men of Penuel answered him as the men of Succoth had answered.
And he said also to the men of Penuel: "When I return in peace, I will tear down this tower."

Now Zebah and Zalmunna were in Karkor, and their camps with them — about fifteen thousand, all who were left of the whole camp of the sons of the east; and the fallen were a hundred and twenty thousand men who drew the sword.
And Gideon went up by the road of the tent-dwellers, east of Nobah and Jogbehah — and he struck the camp while the camp felt secure.
And Zebah and Zalmunna fled, and he pursued after them; and he captured the two kings of Midian — Zebah and Zalmunna — and threw the whole camp into trembling.
And Gideon son of Joash returned from the battle, by the Ascent of Heres;
and he caught a young man of the men of Succoth and questioned him; and the young man wrote down for him the princes of Succoth and its elders — seventy-seven men.

And he came to the men of Succoth and said: "Behold Zebah and Zalmunna — about whom you taunted me, saying: 'Is the palm of Zebah and Zalmunna already in your hand, that we should give bread to your worn-out men?'"
And he took the elders of the city — and wilderness thorns and briers — and with them he taught the men of Succoth a lesson.
And the tower of Penuel he tore down; and he killed the men of the city.
And he said to Zebah and Zalmunna: "Where are the men you killed at Tabor?" And they said: "They were like you — each one with the look of a king's sons."
And he said: "They were my brothers — the sons of my mother! As YHWH lives, if you had let them live, I would not kill you."
And he said to Jether his firstborn: "Rise — kill them!" But the young man did not draw his sword, for he was afraid — for he was still a youth.

And Zebah and Zalmunna said: "Rise yourself, and fall upon us — for as the man is, so is his strength." And Gideon rose and killed Zebah and Zalmunna; and he took the crescent ornaments that were on the necks of their camels.
And the men of Israel said to Gideon: "Rule over us — you, and your son, and your son's son also — for you have saved us from the hand of Midian."
And Gideon said to them: "I will not rule over you, and my son will not rule over you. YHWH shall rule over you."
And Gideon said to them: "Let me make one request of you: give me, each man, an earring from his spoil." (For they had gold earrings — because they were Ishmaelites.)
And they said: "We will surely give them." And they spread out a garment, and each man threw into it an earring from his spoil.

And the weight of the gold earrings he had requested was one thousand seven hundred shekels of gold — apart from the crescent ornaments and the pendants and the purple garments on the kings of Midian, and apart from the chains on the necks of their camels.
And Gideon made it into an ephod, and set it up in his city, in Ophrah; and all Israel went whoring after it there — and it became a snare to Gideon and to his house.
So Midian was subdued before the children of Israel, and they lifted their head no more; and the land was quiet forty years in the days of Gideon.
And Jerubbaal son of Joash went and lived in his own house.
And Gideon had seventy sons, come from his own body — for he had many wives;
and his concubine who was in Shechem — she also bore him a son, and he gave him the name Abimelech.
And Gideon son of Joash died in a good old age, and was buried in the tomb of Joash his father, in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.
And it came to pass, when Gideon was dead, that the children of Israel turned back and went whoring after the Baals — and they made Baal-berith their god;
and the children of Israel did not remember YHWH their God, who had rescued them from the hand of all their enemies on every side;
and they showed no steadfast love to the house of Jerubbaal — Gideon — for all the good he had done for Israel.

jdg.9.text: And Abimelech son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem, to his mother's brothers, and spoke to them and to the whole clan of his mother's father's house:

"Speak, please, in the ears of all the lords of Shechem: Which is better for you — that seventy men, all the sons of Jerubbaal, rule over you, or that one man rule over you? And remember that I am your bone and your flesh."
And his mother's brothers spoke all these words about him in the ears of all the lords of Shechem; and their heart leaned after Abimelech — for they said, "He is our brother."
And they gave him seventy pieces of silver from the house of Baal-berith; and with them Abimelech hired empty and reckless men — and they followed him.
And he came to his father's house at Ophrah and killed his brothers, the sons of Jerubbaal — seventy men on one stone. But Jotham, the youngest son of Jerubbaal, was left — for he hid himself.
And all the lords of Shechem gathered, and all Beth-millo; and they went and made Abimelech king, by the oak of the standing-stone that is in Shechem.
And they told Jotham; and he went and stood on the top of Mount Gerizim, and lifted his voice and called, and said to them: "Listen to me, lords of Shechem — and may God listen to you!
Once the trees surely went to anoint a king over themselves. And they said to the olive tree: 'Reign over us!'
And the olive tree said to them: 'Shall I leave my rich oil — by which gods and men are honored — and go sway over the trees?'
And the trees said to the fig tree: 'Come, you — reign over us!'
And the fig tree said to them: 'Shall I leave my sweetness and my good yield, and go sway over the trees?'
And the trees said to the vine: 'Come, you — reign over us!'
And the vine said to them: 'Shall I leave my new wine — which gladdens gods and men — and go sway over the trees?'
Then all the trees said to the thornbush: 'Come, you — reign over us!'

And the thornbush said to the trees: 'If in truth you are anointing me king over you — come, take refuge in my shade. But if not — let fire go out from the thornbush and devour the cedars of Lebanon!'
So now — if you have acted in truth and integrity in making Abimelech king, and if you have done good to Jerubbaal and his house, and if you have done to him as his hands deserved —
for my father fought for you, and threw his own life out before him, and rescued you from the hand of Midian;

but you have risen against my father's house today and killed his sons — seventy men on one stone — and made Abimelech, the son of his slave woman, king over the lords of Shechem, because he is your brother —
if, then, you have acted in truth and integrity toward Jerubbaal and his house this day: rejoice in Abimelech, and let him also rejoice in you!
But if not — let fire go out from Abimelech and devour the lords of Shechem and Beth-millo; and let fire go out from the lords of Shechem and from Beth-millo, and devour Abimelech!"
And Jotham fled — he ran away and went to Beer, and lived there, away from the face of Abimelech his brother.
And Abimelech ruled over Israel three years.
And God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the lords of Shechem — and the lords of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech;

so that the violence done to the seventy sons of Jerubbaal might come home, and their blood be laid on Abimelech their brother, who killed them — and on the lords of Shechem, who strengthened his hands to kill his brothers.
And the lords of Shechem set ambushers against him on the tops of the mountains, and they robbed everyone who passed by them on the road; and it was told to Abimelech.
And Gaal son of Ebed came with his brothers, and they crossed over into Shechem — and the lords of Shechem put their trust in him.
And they went out into the field and gathered their vineyards and trod the grapes and held a festival; and they went into the house of their god, and ate and drank — and cursed Abimelech.

And Gaal son of Ebed said: "Who is Abimelech — and who is Shechem — that we should serve him? Is he not the son of Jerubbaal, and Zebul his officer? Serve the men of Hamor, the father of Shechem! Why should we serve him?
If only this people were in my hand — I would remove Abimelech!" And he said of Abimelech: "Multiply your army — and come out!"
And Zebul, the ruler of the city, heard the words of Gaal son of Ebed — and his anger burned.
And he sent messengers to Abimelech secretly, saying: "Behold, Gaal son of Ebed and his brothers have come to Shechem — and behold, they are stirring the city up against you.
So now, rise by night — you and the people with you — and lie in ambush in the field;
and in the morning, at sunrise, rise early and rush upon the city; and behold — when he and the people with him come out against you, do to him whatever your hand finds."
And Abimelech rose by night, and all the people with him, and they set ambushes against Shechem in four companies.
And Gaal son of Ebed went out and stood at the opening of the city gate; and Abimelech rose from the ambush, and the people with him.
And Gaal saw the people and said to Zebul: "Behold — people coming down from the tops of the mountains!" And Zebul said to him: "You are seeing the shadow of the mountains as men."
But Gaal spoke yet again and said: "Behold — people coming down from the Navel of the Land; and one company is coming by way of the Diviners' Oak!"
And Zebul said to him: "Where now is your mouth — you who said, 'Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him?' Is this not the people you despised? Go out now, please — and fight them!"
And Gaal went out at the head of the lords of Shechem — and fought with Abimelech;
and Abimelech chased him, and he fled from before him — and many fell slain, all the way to the opening of the gate.
And Abimelech stayed at Arumah; and Zebul drove out Gaal and his brothers from living in Shechem.
And it came to pass the next day that the people went out into the field — and it was told to Abimelech.
And he took the people and divided them into three companies and lay in ambush in the field; and he looked — and behold, the people coming out of the city — and he rose against them and struck them.
And Abimelech, with the companies that were with him, rushed forward and stood at the opening of the city gate; and the two other companies rushed on all who were in the field, and struck them.
And Abimelech fought against the city all that day; and he captured the city, and the people in it he killed; and he tore down the city — and sowed it with salt.
And all the lords of the Tower of Shechem heard, and they went into the vault of the house of El-berith.
And it was told to Abimelech that all the lords of the Tower of Shechem had gathered together;

and Abimelech went up Mount Zalmon — he and all the people with him. And Abimelech took the axes in his hand and cut a branch of the trees, and lifted it and set it on his shoulder; and he said to the people with him: "What you have seen me do — hurry, do as I have done!"

And all the people also cut, each man his branch, and they followed Abimelech and set them against the vault, and set the vault on fire over them — and all the people of the Tower of Shechem died as well: about a thousand men and women.
And Abimelech went to Thebez; and he camped against Thebez and captured it.
But there was a strong tower in the midst of the city, and all the men and women — all the lords of the city — fled there and shut themselves in; and they went up onto the roof of the tower.
And Abimelech came to the tower and fought against it; and he drew near to the opening of the tower, to burn it with fire —
and one woman threw an upper millstone on Abimelech's head, and cracked his skull.
And he called quickly to the young man, his armor-bearer, and said to him: "Draw your sword and finish me — lest they say of me: A woman killed him." And his young man ran him through, and he died.
And when the men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they went, each man, to his place.
So God returned upon Abimelech the evil he had done to his father in killing his seventy brothers;
and all the evil of the men of Shechem God returned upon their own heads — and the curse of Jotham son of Jerubbaal came upon them.

jdg.10.text: And after Abimelech there arose, to save Israel, Tola son of Puah, son of Dodo, a man of Issachar; and he lived in Shamir, in the hill country of Ephraim.
And he judged Israel twenty-three years; and he died and was buried in Shamir.
And after him arose Jair the Gileadite; and he judged Israel twenty-two years.
And he had thirty sons, riding on thirty donkeys — and they had thirty towns; they are called Havvoth-jair to this day, which are in the land of Gilead.
And Jair died and was buried in Kamon.

And the children of Israel again did the evil in the eyes of YHWH: they served the Baals and the Ashtaroth, and the gods of Aram and the gods of Sidon and the gods of Moab and the gods of the sons of Ammon and the gods of the Philistines — and they abandoned YHWH and did not serve him.
And the anger of YHWH burned against Israel, and he sold them into the hand of the Philistines and into the hand of the sons of Ammon;
and they shattered and crushed the children of Israel in that year — eighteen years, all the children of Israel beyond the Jordan, in the land of the Amorites, in Gilead.
And the sons of Ammon crossed the Jordan to fight also against Judah and against Benjamin and against the house of Ephraim — and Israel was pressed exceedingly hard.
And the children of Israel cried out to YHWH: "We have sinned against you — for we have abandoned our God and served the Baals."
And YHWH said to the children of Israel: "Did I not save you from Egypt, and from the Amorites, and from the sons of Ammon, and from the Philistines?
And the Sidonians and Amalek and Maon oppressed you — and you cried to me, and I saved you from their hand.
But you have abandoned me and served other gods. Therefore I will save you no more.
Go and cry to the gods you have chosen — let them save you in your time of distress!"
And the children of Israel said to YHWH: "We have sinned. Do to us whatever is good in your eyes — only rescue us, please, this day!"
And they removed the foreign gods from among them and served YHWH — and his soul grew short with the misery of Israel.
And the sons of Ammon were called out and camped in Gilead; and the children of Israel gathered and camped at Mizpah.
And the people — the princes of Gilead — said to one another: "Who is the man who will begin to fight the sons of Ammon? He shall be head over all the inhabitants of Gilead."

jdg.11.text: Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a mighty man of valor — and he was the son of a prostitute; and Gilead fathered Jephthah.
And Gilead's wife bore him sons; and when the wife's sons grew up, they drove out Jephthah and said to him: "You shall not inherit in our father's house — for you are the son of another woman."
And Jephthah fled from before his brothers and lived in the land of Tob; and empty men gathered themselves around Jephthah and went out with him.
And it came to pass, after some days, that the sons of Ammon made war with Israel;
and when the sons of Ammon made war with Israel, the elders of Gilead went to take Jephthah from the land of Tob.
And they said to Jephthah: "Come and be our commander, and let us fight the sons of Ammon."
And Jephthah said to the elders of Gilead: "Did you not hate me and drive me out of my father's house? Why have you come to me now, when you are in distress?"
And the elders of Gilead said to Jephthah: "That is why we have come back to you now. Go with us and fight the sons of Ammon — and you shall be head over us, over all the inhabitants of Gilead."
And Jephthah said to the elders of Gilead: "If you bring me back to fight the sons of Ammon and YHWH gives them over before me — I shall be your head."
And the elders of Gilead said to Jephthah: "YHWH shall be the hearer between us — if we do not do just as you have said."
So Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people set him over them as head and commander; and Jephthah spoke all his words before YHWH at Mizpah.
And Jephthah sent messengers to the king of the sons of Ammon: "What is there between me and you, that you have come against me to fight in my land?"

And the king of the sons of Ammon said to Jephthah's messengers: "Because Israel took my land when it came up from Egypt — from the Arnon to the Jabbok, and to the Jordan. So now, return it in peace."
And Jephthah again sent messengers to the king of the sons of Ammon,
and said to him: "Thus says Jephthah: Israel did not take the land of Moab or the land of the sons of Ammon;
for when they came up from Egypt, Israel went through the wilderness to the Sea of Reeds, and came to Kadesh.

And Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, 'Let me pass, please, through your land' — but the king of Edom did not listen; and also to the king of Moab he sent — and he was not willing. So Israel stayed in Kadesh.

Then he went through the wilderness and circled around the land of Edom and the land of Moab, and came from the sunrise side of the land of Moab; and they camped beyond the Arnon — and did not enter the border of Moab, for the Arnon is the border of Moab.
And Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites, king of Heshbon; and Israel said to him: 'Let us pass, please, through your land to my place.'
But Sihon did not trust Israel to pass through his border; and Sihon gathered all his people, and they camped at Jahaz — and he fought with Israel.
And YHWH, the God of Israel, gave Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel, and they struck them; and Israel took possession of all the land of the Amorites who lived in that land.
And they possessed all the territory of the Amorites — from the Arnon to the Jabbok, and from the wilderness to the Jordan.
So now — YHWH, the God of Israel, has dispossessed the Amorites before his people Israel; and you would possess it?
Do you not possess what Chemosh your god gives you to possess? So all that YHWH our God has dispossessed before us — that we will possess.
And now — are you truly better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever strive with Israel, or ever fight against them?
While Israel lived in Heshbon and its daughter-towns, in Aroer and its daughter-towns, and in all the cities along the Arnon — three hundred years — why did you not recover them in that time?
So I have not sinned against you — but you are doing me wrong by making war on me. Let YHWH the Judge judge this day between the children of Israel and the sons of Ammon!"
But the king of the sons of Ammon did not listen to the words of Jephthah, which he had sent him.
And the Spirit of YHWH came upon Jephthah; and he crossed Gilead and Manasseh, and crossed Mizpeh of Gilead — and from Mizpeh of Gilead he crossed over to the sons of Ammon.
And Jephthah vowed a vow to YHWH, and said: "If you will surely give the sons of Ammon into my hand,
then whatever comes out — whatever comes out of the doors of my house to meet me when I return in peace from the sons of Ammon — it shall belong to YHWH, and I will offer it up as a burnt offering."
And Jephthah crossed over to the sons of Ammon to fight them — and YHWH gave them into his hand;
and he struck them from Aroer to the approach of Minnith — twenty cities — and as far as Abel-keramim: an exceedingly great blow. And the sons of Ammon were subdued before the children of Israel.

And Jephthah came to Mizpah, to his house — and behold: his daughter coming out to meet him, with tambourines and with dances. And she was his one and only; besides her he had neither son nor daughter.

And it came to pass, when he saw her, that he tore his garments and said: "Ah, my daughter! You have brought me to my knees — you have become my undoing! For I have opened my mouth to YHWH, and I cannot turn back."

And she said to him: "My father — you have opened your mouth to YHWH; do to me according to what has gone out of your mouth, now that YHWH has worked vengeance for you on your enemies, the sons of Ammon."
And she said to her father: "Let this thing be done for me: let me alone two months, that I may go and go down upon the mountains and weep over my maidenhood — I and my companions."
And he said: "Go." And he sent her away for two months; and she went — she and her companions — and wept over her maidenhood on the mountains.
And it came to pass at the end of two months that she returned to her father, and he did to her his vow that he had vowed. And she had never known a man. And it became a fixed custom in Israel:
from year to year the daughters of Israel go to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite — four days in the year.

jdg.12.text: And the men of Ephraim were called out, and crossed over northward, and said to Jephthah: "Why did you cross over to fight the sons of Ammon and did not call us to go with you? We will burn your house down over you with fire!"
And Jephthah said to them: "I was a man in a hard quarrel — I and my people — with the sons of Ammon; and I called you, and you did not save me from their hand.

And when I saw that you were not saving, I put my life in my own palm and crossed over to the sons of Ammon — and YHWH gave them into my hand. So why have you come up against me this day, to fight me?"

And Jephthah gathered all the men of Gilead and fought Ephraim; and the men of Gilead struck Ephraim — because they had said: "You are fugitives of Ephraim — Gilead, in the midst of Ephraim, in the midst of Manasseh!"

And Gilead seized the fords of the Jordan against Ephraim; and it came to pass, when the fugitives of Ephraim said, "Let me cross," that the men of Gilead would say to him, "Are you an Ephraimite?" — and if he said, "No,"

they would say to him: "Say now: Shibboleth." And he would say "Sibboleth" — for he could not manage to speak it right; and they would seize him and slaughter him at the fords of the Jordan. And there fell at that time, of Ephraim, forty-two thousand.
And Jephthah judged Israel six years; and Jephthah the Gileadite died, and was buried in the cities of Gilead.
And after him Ibzan of Bethlehem judged Israel;
and he had thirty sons; and thirty daughters he sent outside, and thirty daughters he brought in for his sons from outside. And he judged Israel seven years;
and Ibzan died, and was buried in Bethlehem.
And after him Elon the Zebulunite judged Israel; and he judged Israel ten years;
and Elon the Zebulunite died, and was buried in Aijalon, in the land of Zebulun.
And after him Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite judged Israel;
and he had forty sons and thirty grandsons, riding on seventy donkeys. And he judged Israel eight years;
and Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite died, and was buried in Pirathon, in the land of Ephraim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.

jdg.13.text: And the children of Israel again did the evil in the eyes of YHWH; and YHWH gave them into the hand of the Philistines forty years.
And there was one man from Zorah, from the clan of the Danites, and his name was Manoah; and his wife was barren — she had not given birth.
And the angel of YHWH appeared to the woman and said to her: "Behold now — you are barren and have not given birth; but you shall conceive and bear a son.
So now, guard yourself, please: drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean.

For behold — you are conceiving, and shall bear a son; and no razor shall come up on his head, for the boy shall be a Nazirite of God from the womb — and he shall begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines."

And the woman came and told her husband: "A man of God came to me, and his appearance was like the appearance of the angel of God — exceedingly awesome. And I did not ask him where he was from, and he did not tell me his name;

but he said to me: 'Behold, you are conceiving, and shall bear a son. So now: drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean — for the boy shall be a Nazirite of God from the womb to the day of his death.'"
And Manoah entreated YHWH and said: "Please, my Lord — let the man of God whom you sent come again to us and teach us what we shall do for the boy who is to be born."
And God listened to the voice of Manoah; and the angel of God came again to the woman as she sat in the field — and Manoah her husband was not with her.
And the woman hurried and ran and told her husband; and she said to him: "Behold — the man who came to me the other day has appeared to me!"
And Manoah rose and followed his wife, and came to the man and said to him: "Are you the man who spoke to the woman?" And he said: "I am."
And Manoah said: "Now — when your words come to pass, what shall be the rule for the boy, and his work?"
And the angel of YHWH said to Manoah: "Of all that I said to the woman, let her keep guard:
of anything that comes from the vine of wine she shall not eat; wine and strong drink she shall not drink, and nothing unclean shall she eat. All that I commanded her, let her keep."
And Manoah said to the angel of YHWH: "Let us detain you, please, and prepare a young goat before you."

And the angel of YHWH said to Manoah: "Though you detain me, I will not eat your bread; but if you prepare a burnt offering — offer it up to YHWH." For Manoah did not know that he was the angel of YHWH.
And Manoah said to the angel of YHWH: "What is your name — so that when your words come to pass, we may honor you?"
And the angel of YHWH said to him: "Why is it that you ask my name? It is Wonder-Beyond-Telling."
And Manoah took the young goat and the grain offering and offered them up on the rock to YHWH — to the One who works wonders — while Manoah and his wife watched.

And it came to pass, as the flame went up from the altar toward the heavens, that the angel of YHWH went up in the flame of the altar — while Manoah and his wife watched; and they fell on their faces to the ground.
And the angel of YHWH did not appear again to Manoah and his wife. Then Manoah knew that he was the angel of YHWH;
and Manoah said to his wife: "We shall surely die — for we have seen God!"

But his wife said to him: "If YHWH had wanted to kill us, he would not have taken a burnt offering and a grain offering from our hand, and would not have shown us all these things — nor at this time let us hear such a thing."
And the woman bore a son and called his name Samson; and the boy grew, and YHWH blessed him.
And the Spirit of YHWH began to stir him in Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol.

jdg.14.text: And Samson went down to Timnah; and he saw a woman in Timnah, of the daughters of the Philistines.
And he came up and told his father and his mother, and said: "I have seen a woman in Timnah, of the daughters of the Philistines. Now — take her for me as a wife."

And his father and his mother said to him: "Is there no woman among the daughters of your brothers, or among all my people, that you go to take a wife from the uncircumcised Philistines?" And Samson said to his father: "Take her for me — for she is right in my eyes."
Now his father and his mother did not know that it was from YHWH — that he was seeking an opening against the Philistines; for at that time the Philistines were ruling over Israel.
And Samson went down to Timnah, with his father and his mother; and they came as far as the vineyards of Timnah — and behold: a young lion, roaring to meet him.
And the Spirit of YHWH rushed upon him, and he tore it apart as one tears apart a young goat — with nothing at all in his hand; and he did not tell his father and his mother what he had done.
And he went down and spoke to the woman — and she was right in Samson's eyes.
And he returned after some days to take her, and he turned aside to see the carcass of the lion — and behold: a swarm of bees in the body of the lion, and honey.

And he scraped it into his palms and walked on, eating as he went; and he went to his father and his mother and gave them some, and they ate — and he did not tell them that he had scraped the honey from the body of the lion.
And his father went down to the woman; and Samson made a feast there — for so the young men used to do.
And it came to pass, when they saw him, that they took thirty companions — and they were with him.

And Samson said to them: "Let me pose you a riddle. If you can surely tell it to me within the seven days of the feast, and solve it, I will give you thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing;
but if you cannot tell me — then you shall give me thirty linen garments and thirty changes of clothing." And they said to him: "Pose your riddle — let us hear it."
And he said to them: "Out of the eater came something to eat; and out of the fierce came something sweet." And they could not tell the riddle for three days.

And it came to pass on the seventh day that they said to Samson's wife: "Coax your husband, that he may tell us the riddle — or we will burn you and your father's house with fire! Did you invite us here to impoverish us?"

And Samson's wife wept over him and said: "You only hate me — you do not love me! You have posed the riddle to the sons of my people, and you have not told it to me." And he said to her: "Behold — I have not told my father or my mother; shall I tell you?"
