And the woman came to Saul and saw that he was utterly shaken; and she said to him: "Behold — your servant has listened to your voice, and I put my life in my palm, and I listened to your words that you spoke to me.
So now — you also listen, please, to the voice of your servant, and let me set before you a piece of bread; and eat, that there may be strength in you when you go on the road."
But he refused and said: "I will not eat." And his servants urged him — and the woman too — and he listened to their voice; and he rose from the ground and sat on the bed.
And the woman had a fattened calf in the house, and she hurried and slaughtered it; and she took flour and kneaded it, and baked it into unleavened bread;
and she brought it before Saul and before his servants, and they ate. And they rose and went away that same night.

1sa.29.text: And the Philistines gathered all their camps to Aphek; and Israel was camping by the spring that is in Jezreel.
And the lords of the Philistines were passing by in hundreds and in thousands, with David and his men passing at the rear with Achish.

And the commanders of the Philistines said: "What are these Hebrews?" And Achish said to the commanders of the Philistines: "Is this not David, the servant of Saul king of Israel, who has been with me these days — or rather these years? And I have found nothing wrong in him from the day he came over until this day."

But the commanders of the Philistines were furious with him; and the commanders of the Philistines said to him: "Send the man back — let him return to his place where you stationed him. He shall not go down with us into the battle, and he shall not become an adversary to us in the battle. For with what could this fellow make himself acceptable to his master — if not with the heads of these men of ours?
Is this not David, of whom they sang in the dances: 'Saul has struck down his thousands — and David his ten thousands'?"

And Achish called David and said to him: "As YHWH lives — you are upright, and your going out and coming in with me in the camp has been good in my eyes; for I have found no evil in you from the day you came to me until this day. But in the eyes of the lords you are not good.
So now — return, and go in peace; and do nothing evil in the eyes of the lords of the Philistines."

And David said to Achish: "But what have I done? And what have you found in your servant from the day I came before you until this day — that I may not come and fight against the enemies of my lord the king?"
And Achish answered and said to David: "I know that you are good in my eyes, like an angel of God. Only — the commanders of the Philistines have said: 'He shall not go up with us into the battle.'
So now — rise early in the morning, with the servants of your lord who came with you; rise early in the morning, and as soon as it is light for you — go."
And David rose early — he and his men — to go in the morning, to return to the land of the Philistines; and the Philistines went up to Jezreel.

1sa.30.text: And it came to pass, when David and his men came to Ziklag on the third day, that the Amalekites had raided the Negev and Ziklag; and they had struck Ziklag and burned it with fire,
and taken captive the women who were in it, from small to great. They had killed no one — but had driven them off and gone their way.
And David and his men came to the city — and behold: burned with fire, and their wives and their sons and their daughters taken captive.
And David lifted his voice and wept — and the people with him — until there was no strength in them to weep.
And David's two wives had been taken captive: Ahinoam the Jezreelite and Abigail, Nabal's widow, the Carmelite.

And David was in great distress — for the people spoke of stoning him, because the soul of all the people was bitter, each man over his sons and his daughters. But David strengthened himself in YHWH his God.
And David said to Abiathar the priest, son of Ahimelech: "Bring the ephod near to me, please." And Abiathar brought the ephod near to David.
And David inquired of YHWH: "Shall I pursue after this raiding band? Shall I overtake it?" And he said to him: "Pursue — for you shall surely overtake, and surely rescue."
And David went — he and the six hundred men with him — and they came to the wadi Besor, where those left behind stayed.
And David pursued — he and four hundred men; and two hundred men stayed behind, who were too exhausted to cross the wadi Besor.
And they found an Egyptian man in the field and took him to David; and they gave him bread, and he ate, and they gave him water to drink;
and they gave him a slice of pressed figs and two clusters of raisins, and he ate — and his spirit returned to him; for he had eaten no bread and drunk no water for three days and three nights.

And David said to him: "To whom do you belong — and where are you from?" And he said: "I am an Egyptian boy, servant to an Amalekite man; and my master abandoned me because I fell sick three days ago.
We raided the Negev of the Cherethites, and what belongs to Judah, and the Negev of Caleb — and Ziklag we burned with fire."

And David said to him: "Will you bring me down to this raiding band?" And he said: "Swear to me by God that you will not put me to death and will not shut me into my master's hand — and I will bring you down to this band."

And he brought him down — and behold: they were sprawled over the face of the whole land, eating and drinking and celebrating over all the great spoil they had taken from the land of the Philistines and from the land of Judah.
And David struck them from twilight until the evening of the next day; and not a man of them escaped, except four hundred young men who rode on camels and fled.
And David rescued all that Amalek had taken — and his two wives David rescued.
And nothing of theirs was missing — from small to great, sons or daughters, spoil, or anything they had taken for themselves. Everything David brought back.
And David took all the flocks and the herds; they drove them before that other livestock, and said: "This is David's spoil."

And David came to the two hundred men who had been too exhausted to follow David, whom they had left at the wadi Besor; and they came out to meet David and to meet the people with him. And David drew near to the people and asked them of their welfare.

But every evil and worthless man among those who had gone with David answered and said: "Because they did not go with me, we will not give them any of the spoil we rescued — except each man his wife and his sons; let them lead them off and go."
But David said: "You shall not do so, my brothers, with what YHWH has given us — for he has kept us, and given the raiding band that came against us into our hand.
And who would listen to you in this matter? For as the share of the one who goes down into battle — so is the share of the one who stays with the baggage. Together they shall share."
And so it has been from that day onward: he set it as a statute and a judgment for Israel — to this day.
And David came to Ziklag, and sent some of the spoil to the elders of Judah, to his friends: "Behold — a blessing-gift for you from the spoil of the enemies of YHWH" —
to those in Bethel, and to those in Ramoth of the Negev, and to those in Jattir,
and to those in Aroer, and to those in Siphmoth, and to those in Eshtemoa,
and to those in Racal, and to those in the cities of the Jerahmeelites, and to those in the cities of the Kenites,
and to those in Hormah, and to those in Bor-ashan, and to those in Athach,
and to those in Hebron — and to all the places where David had gone about, he and his men.

1sa.31.text: Now the Philistines were fighting against Israel; and the men of Israel fled from before the Philistines, and fell slain on Mount Gilboa.
And the Philistines clung close after Saul and his sons; and the Philistines struck down Jonathan and Abinadab and Malchi-shua, the sons of Saul.
And the battle grew heavy against Saul; and the archers — men with the bow — found him, and he was badly wounded by the archers.

And Saul said to his armor-bearer: "Draw your sword and run me through with it — lest these uncircumcised come and run me through and abuse me." But his armor-bearer was not willing, for he was very afraid. So Saul took the sword — and fell on it.
And his armor-bearer saw that Saul was dead — and he too fell on his sword, and died with him.
So Saul died — and his three sons, and his armor-bearer, and all his men — on that day, together.

And the men of Israel who were across the valley, and those across the Jordan, saw that the men of Israel had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead; and they abandoned the cities and fled — and the Philistines came and settled in them.
And it came to pass the next day, when the Philistines came to strip the slain, that they found Saul and his three sons fallen on Mount Gilboa.
And they cut off his head and stripped off his armor, and sent word through the land of the Philistines all around, to carry the news to the house of their idols — and to the people.
And they put his armor in the house of the Ashtaroth; and his body they nailed to the wall of Beth-shan.
And the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead heard about him — what the Philistines had done to Saul;
and every man of valor rose, and they walked all night, and took the body of Saul and the bodies of his sons from the wall of Beth-shan; and they came to Jabesh and burned them there.
And they took their bones and buried them under the tamarisk at Jabesh — and they fasted seven days.

2sa.1.text: And it came to pass, after the death of Saul — David having returned from striking Amalek — that David stayed in Ziklag two days.
And it came to pass on the third day — behold: a man coming from the camp, from Saul, his garments torn and soil on his head; and when he came to David, he fell to the ground and prostrated himself.
And David said to him: "Where do you come from?" And he said to him: "From the camp of Israel I have escaped."
And David said to him: "What was the word? Tell me, please." And he said: "The people fled from the battle, and many of the people also fell and died; and Saul and Jonathan his son also died."
And David said to the young man telling him: "How do you know that Saul and Jonathan his son are dead?"
And the young man telling him said: "I happened by chance to be on Mount Gilboa — and behold: Saul, leaning on his spear; and behold: the chariots and the horsemen closing in on him.
And he turned behind him and saw me, and called to me; and I said: 'Here I am.'
And he said to me: 'Who are you?' And I said to him: 'I am an Amalekite.'
And he said to me: 'Stand over me, please, and finish me — for the dizziness of death has seized me, yet my life is still whole in me.'
So I stood over him and finished him — for I knew he could not live after his fall; and I took the crown that was on his head, and the band on his arm, and I have brought them here to my lord."
And David took hold of his garments and tore them — and so did all the men with him;
and they mourned and wept and fasted until the evening — over Saul and over Jonathan his son, and over the people of YHWH and over the house of Israel, because they had fallen by the sword.
And David said to the young man telling him: "Where are you from?" And he said: "I am the son of a sojourner — an Amalekite."
And David said to him: "How were you not afraid to stretch out your hand to destroy the anointed of YHWH?"
And David called one of the young men and said: "Come near — fall on him." And he struck him, and he died.
And David said to him: "Your blood is on your own head — for your own mouth testified against you: 'I finished the anointed of YHWH.'"
And David keened this lament over Saul and over Jonathan his son —
and he said it was to teach the sons of Judah "The Bow"; behold, it is written in the Scroll of the Upright:
"The gazelle, O Israel — slain on your high places! How the mighty have fallen!
Do not tell it in Gath; do not carry the news through the streets of Ashkelon — lest the daughters of the Philistines rejoice, lest the daughters of the uncircumcised exult.
Mountains of Gilboa — no dew and no rain upon you, nor fields of offerings! For there the shield of the mighty was defiled: the shield of Saul, no longer rubbed with oil.
From the blood of the slain, from the fat of the mighty, the bow of Jonathan never turned back — and the sword of Saul never returned empty.
Saul and Jonathan — beloved and delightful in their lives, and in their death not divided: swifter than eagles they were; stronger than lions.
Daughters of Israel — weep over Saul, who clothed you in scarlet with delights, who set ornaments of gold on your clothing.
How the mighty have fallen in the midst of battle — Jonathan, slain on your high places!
I am in anguish over you, my brother Jonathan; you were exceedingly delightful to me. Your love was more wonderful to me than the love of women.
How the mighty have fallen — and the weapons of war have perished!"

2sa.2.text: And it came to pass afterward that David inquired of YHWH: "Shall I go up into one of the cities of Judah?" And YHWH said to him: "Go up." And David said: "Where shall I go up?" And he said: "To Hebron."
And David went up there — and also his two wives: Ahinoam the Jezreelite and Abigail, Nabal's widow, the Carmelite;
and his men who were with him David brought up, each man with his household; and they settled in the towns of Hebron.
And the men of Judah came, and there they anointed David king over the house of Judah. And they told David: "It was the men of Jabesh-gilead who buried Saul."
And David sent messengers to the men of Jabesh-gilead and said to them: "Blessed are you to YHWH, for showing this steadfast love to your lord — to Saul — in burying him.
So now — may YHWH show steadfast love and truth to you; and I also will do you this same good, because you have done this thing.
So now — let your hands be strong, and be sons of valor; for your lord Saul is dead, and the house of Judah has anointed me king over them."
But Abner son of Ner, commander of Saul's army, had taken Ish-bosheth son of Saul and brought him across to Mahanaim;
and he made him king over Gilead and over the Ashurites and over Jezreel, and over Ephraim and over Benjamin — over all Israel.
Ish-bosheth son of Saul was forty years old when he became king over Israel, and he reigned two years. Only the house of Judah followed after David.
And the number of the days David was king in Hebron over the house of Judah was seven years and six months.
And Abner son of Ner, and the servants of Ish-bosheth son of Saul, went out from Mahanaim to Gibeon;

and Joab son of Zeruiah, and the servants of David, went out; and they met them together at the pool of Gibeon — and they sat down, these on one side of the pool and those on the other side of the pool.
And Abner said to Joab: "Let the young men rise, please, and put on a contest before us." And Joab said: "Let them rise."
And they rose and crossed over by number: twelve for Benjamin and for Ish-bosheth son of Saul, and twelve of the servants of David.
And each seized his opponent by the head, with his sword into his opponent's side — and they fell together. And that place was called the Field of the Flints, which is at Gibeon.
And the battle was exceedingly hard on that day; and Abner and the men of Israel were struck down before the servants of David.
And the three sons of Zeruiah were there: Joab and Abishai and Asahel. And Asahel was swift on his feet, like one of the gazelles of the field;
and Asahel pursued after Abner, and did not swerve right or left from following Abner.
And Abner turned behind him and said: "Is that you, Asahel?" And he said: "It is I."
And Abner said to him: "Swerve to your right or to your left; seize one of the young men and take his gear for yourself." But Asahel was not willing to turn aside from following him.
And Abner said again to Asahel: "Turn aside from following me! Why should I strike you to the ground? And how could I lift my face to Joab your brother?"

But he refused to turn aside; so Abner struck him with the butt of the spear into the belly, and the spear came out behind him — and he fell there, and died on the spot. And it came to pass that everyone who came to the place where Asahel fell and died — stood still.
And Joab and Abishai pursued after Abner; and the sun was setting as they came to the hill of Ammah, which faces Giah, on the road to the wilderness of Gibeon.
And the sons of Benjamin gathered themselves behind Abner and became one band, and took their stand on the top of a hill.
And Abner called to Joab and said: "Shall the sword devour forever? Do you not know that it will be bitter in the end? How long before you tell the people to turn back from following their brothers?"
And Joab said: "As God lives — had you not spoken, then only in the morning would the people have gone up, each from following his brother."
And Joab blew the shofar, and all the people stood still; and they pursued Israel no more, and they fought no more.
And Abner and his men walked through the Arabah all that night, and crossed the Jordan, and walked the whole Bithron, and came to Mahanaim.
And Joab turned back from following Abner and gathered all the people; and of David's servants nineteen men were missing — and Asahel.
But the servants of David had struck down, of Benjamin and among Abner's men, three hundred and sixty men who died.
And they lifted Asahel and buried him in his father's tomb, which is at Bethlehem; and Joab and his men walked all night — and day broke on them at Hebron.

2sa.3.text: And the war was long between the house of Saul and the house of David; and David went on growing stronger — and the house of Saul went on growing weaker.
And sons were born to David at Hebron: his firstborn was Amnon, by Ahinoam the Jezreelite;
and his second Chileab, by Abigail, Nabal's widow, the Carmelite; and the third Absalom, son of Maacah, daughter of Talmai king of Geshur;
and the fourth Adonijah, son of Haggith; and the fifth Shephatiah, son of Abital;
and the sixth Ithream, by Eglah, David's wife. These were born to David at Hebron.
And it came to pass, while the war went on between the house of Saul and the house of David, that Abner was strengthening his own hold on the house of Saul.
Now Saul had a concubine, and her name was Rizpah, daughter of Aiah. And Ish-bosheth said to Abner: "Why have you gone in to my father's concubine?"

And it burned in Abner exceedingly at the words of Ish-bosheth, and he said: "Am I a dog's head — for Judah? Today I show steadfast love to the house of Saul your father, to his brothers and to his friends, and I have not let you fall into the hand of David — and you charge me today with guilt over the woman!
So may God do to Abner, and so may he add — if I do not do for David just as YHWH swore to him:
to carry the kingdom over from the house of Saul, and to raise up the throne of David over Israel and over Judah, from Dan to Beersheba."
And he could not answer Abner another word — for fear of him.
And Abner sent messengers to David where he was, saying: "Whose is the land?" — saying: "Cut your covenant with me; and behold, my hand is with you, to bring all Israel around to you."
And he said: "Good — I will cut a covenant with you. Only one thing I ask of you: you shall not see my face unless you first bring Michal, Saul's daughter, when you come to see my face."
And David sent messengers to Ish-bosheth son of Saul: "Give up my wife Michal, whom I betrothed to myself for a hundred foreskins of the Philistines."
And Ish-bosheth sent and took her from a husband — from Paltiel son of Laish;
and her husband walked with her, walking and weeping behind her, as far as Bahurim. And Abner said to him: "Go — return." And he returned.
Now Abner's word had gone to the elders of Israel: "For a long time now you have been seeking David as king over you.
So now — act! For YHWH has spoken concerning David: 'By the hand of David my servant I will save my people Israel from the hand of the Philistines, and from the hand of all their enemies.'"
And Abner also spoke in the ears of Benjamin; and Abner also went to speak in the ears of David at Hebron all that was good in the eyes of Israel and in the eyes of the whole house of Benjamin.
And Abner came to David at Hebron, and twenty men with him; and David made a feast for Abner and the men with him.

And Abner said to David: "Let me rise and go and gather all Israel to my lord the king, that they may cut a covenant with you — and you shall reign over all that your soul desires." And David sent Abner off, and he went in peace.
And behold — the servants of David, and Joab, coming from a raid, bringing much spoil with them; and Abner was no longer with David at Hebron, for he had sent him off, and he had gone in peace.
And Joab and all the army with him arrived; and they told Joab: "Abner son of Ner came to the king — and he sent him off, and he went in peace."
And Joab came to the king and said: "What have you done? Behold — Abner came to you. Why did you send him off, so that he is gone, clean away?
You know Abner son of Ner: he came to deceive you — to learn your going out and your coming in, and to learn all that you are doing."
And Joab went out from David and sent messengers after Abner, and they brought him back from the cistern of Sirah — and David did not know.
And when Abner returned to Hebron, Joab drew him aside inside the gate, to speak with him quietly; and there he struck him in the belly — and he died, for the blood of Asahel his brother.
And David heard afterward, and said: "I and my kingdom are innocent before YHWH, to the age, of the blood of Abner son of Ner.

May it whirl down on the head of Joab and on all his father's house; and may there never be cut off from the house of Joab one with a discharge, or a leper, or one who leans on a spindle, or one who falls by the sword, or one who lacks bread!"
So Joab and Abishai his brother killed Abner — because he had put to death Asahel their brother at Gibeon, in the battle.
And David said to Joab and to all the people with him: "Tear your garments, and gird on sackcloth, and mourn before Abner." And King David walked behind the bier.
And they buried Abner at Hebron; and the king lifted his voice and wept at the grave of Abner — and all the people wept.
And the king keened over Abner and said: "Should Abner die as a fool dies?
Your hands were not bound; your feet were not put in bronze fetters. As one falls before sons of injustice — you fell." And all the people wept over him again.
And all the people came to press bread on David while it was still day; but David swore: "So may God do to me, and so may he add — if before the sun sets I taste bread or anything at all."
And all the people took note, and it was good in their eyes — as everything the king did was good in the eyes of all the people;
and all the people, and all Israel, knew on that day that it had not been from the king to put Abner son of Ner to death.
And the king said to his servants: "Do you not know that a commander — a great man — has fallen this day in Israel?
And I today am soft, though anointed king; and these men, the sons of Zeruiah, are too hard for me. May YHWH repay the doer of the evil according to his evil."

2sa.4.text: And the son of Saul heard that Abner had died at Hebron — and his hands went slack, and all Israel was terrified.

Now the son of Saul had two men, commanders of raiding bands: the name of one was Baanah and the name of the second Rechab — sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, of the sons of Benjamin (for Beeroth also is counted to Benjamin;
and the Beerothites fled to Gittaim and have been sojourners there to this day).

And Jonathan son of Saul had a son crippled in his feet. He was five years old when the report of Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel, and his nurse lifted him and fled; and it came to pass, in her hurrying to flee, that he fell and was made lame. And his name was Mephibosheth.
And the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite — Rechab and Baanah — went, and came at the heat of the day to the house of Ish-bosheth, as he was lying down for the noon rest.
And they came right into the middle of the house, as though fetching wheat, and struck him in the belly; and Rechab and Baanah his brother slipped away.

For they had come into the house while he lay on his bed in his bedroom, and they struck him and put him to death, and removed his head; and they took his head and walked the road of the Arabah all night.

And they brought the head of Ish-bosheth to David at Hebron, and said to the king: "Behold — the head of Ish-bosheth son of Saul, your enemy, who sought your life. YHWH has given my lord the king vengeance this day on Saul and on his seed."
And David answered Rechab and Baanah his brother, the sons of Rimmon the Beerothite, and said to them: "As YHWH lives, who has ransomed my life from every distress:
the one who told me, 'Behold — Saul is dead,' and was in his own eyes a bringer of good news — I seized him and killed him at Ziklag; that was the reward I gave him for his news.
How much more, when wicked men have killed a righteous man in his own house, on his own bed! So now — shall I not seek his blood from your hand, and burn you away from the earth?"

And David commanded the young men, and they killed them, and cut off their hands and their feet, and hanged them by the pool at Hebron; but the head of Ish-bosheth they took and buried in the grave of Abner at Hebron.

2sa.5.text: And all the tribes of Israel came to David at Hebron and said: "Behold — we are your bone and your flesh.
Already long ago, when Saul was king over us, it was you who led Israel out and brought it in; and YHWH said to you: 'You shall shepherd my people Israel, and you shall be leader over Israel.'"
And all the elders of Israel came to the king at Hebron; and King David cut a covenant with them at Hebron, before YHWH — and they anointed David king over Israel.
David was thirty years old when he became king; forty years he reigned:
at Hebron he reigned over Judah seven years and six months, and at Jerusalem he reigned thirty-three years over all Israel and Judah.

And the king and his men went to Jerusalem, against the Jebusites, the inhabitants of the land; and they said to David: "You shall not come in here — even the blind and the lame will turn you back" (thinking: "David cannot come in here").
But David captured the stronghold of Zion — that is, the City of David.

And David said on that day: "Whoever strikes the Jebusites, let him reach the water shaft — and those lame and blind ones, hated by David's soul." Therefore they say: "The blind and the lame shall not come into the house."
And David lived in the stronghold and called it the City of David; and David built all around, from the Millo inward.
And David went on and on growing great — and YHWH, the God of Armies, was with him.
And Hiram king of Tyre sent messengers to David, and cedar timbers, and craftsmen in wood and craftsmen in stone for walls — and they built a house for David.
And David knew that YHWH had established him as king over Israel, and that he had lifted his kingdom high for the sake of his people Israel.
And David took more concubines and wives from Jerusalem, after he came from Hebron; and more sons and daughters were born to David.
And these are the names of those born to him in Jerusalem: Shammua and Shobab and Nathan and Solomon,
and Ibhar and Elishua and Nepheg and Japhia,
and Elishama and Eliada and Eliphelet.
And the Philistines heard that David had been anointed king over Israel, and all the Philistines came up to seek David; and David heard, and went down to the stronghold.
And the Philistines came and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
And David inquired of YHWH: "Shall I go up against the Philistines? Will you give them into my hand?" And YHWH said to David: "Go up — for I will surely give the Philistines into your hand."

And David came to Baal-perazim, and David struck them there; and he said: "YHWH has burst through my enemies before me like a bursting of waters." Therefore he called the name of that place Baal-perazim — Lord of Burstings.
And they abandoned their idols there — and David and his men carried them off.
And the Philistines came up yet again, and spread out in the Valley of Rephaim.
And David inquired of YHWH; and he said: "Do not go up. Circle around behind them, and come at them opposite the balsam trees.
And it shall be, when you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees — then move sharply; for then YHWH will have gone out before you, to strike the camp of the Philistines."
And David did so, as YHWH commanded him; and he struck the Philistines from Geba as far as the approach to Gezer.

2sa.6.text: And David again gathered every chosen man in Israel: thirty thousand.

And David rose and went — and all the people with him — from Baale-judah, to bring up from there the ark of God, over which the Name is called: the name of YHWH of Armies, who sits enthroned on the cherubim.
And they mounted the ark of God on a new cart and carried it from the house of Abinadab, which was on the hill; and Uzzah and Ahio, sons of Abinadab, were driving the new cart.
And they carried it from the house of Abinadab, which was on the hill, with the ark of God — and Ahio walking in front of the ark.
And David and all the house of Israel were making merry before YHWH with all manner of cypress-wood instruments — with lyres and with harps and with tambourines and with rattles and with cymbals.
And they came to the threshing floor of Nacon; and Uzzah reached out to the ark of God and took hold of it — for the oxen had stumbled.
And the anger of YHWH burned against Uzzah, and God struck him there for the offense; and he died there, beside the ark of God.
And it burned in David, because YHWH had burst out against Uzzah with a bursting; and that place has been called Perez-uzzah to this day.
And David was afraid of YHWH on that day, and said: "How can the ark of YHWH come to me?"
And David was not willing to move the ark of YHWH to himself, to the City of David; so David diverted it to the house of Obed-edom the Gittite.
And the ark of YHWH stayed at the house of Obed-edom the Gittite three months; and YHWH blessed Obed-edom and all his house.

And it was told to King David: "YHWH has blessed the house of Obed-edom and all that is his, because of the ark of God." And David went and brought up the ark of God from the house of Obed-edom to the City of David — with joy.
And it came to pass, when the carriers of the ark of YHWH had stepped six steps, that he sacrificed an ox and a fatling.
And David was whirling with all his strength before YHWH — and David was girded with a linen ephod.
And David and all the house of Israel were bringing up the ark of YHWH with shouting, and with the sound of the shofar.

And it came to pass, as the ark of YHWH entered the City of David, that Michal daughter of Saul looked down through the window and saw King David leaping and whirling before YHWH — and she despised him in her heart.
And they brought in the ark of YHWH and set it up in its place, inside the tent David had pitched for it; and David offered up burnt offerings before YHWH, and peace offerings.
And when David had finished offering up the burnt offering and the peace offerings, he blessed the people in the name of YHWH of Armies;

and he divided out to all the people — to the whole multitude of Israel, man and woman alike — to each one round loaf of bread, and one date cake, and one raisin cake. And all the people went, each to his house.

And David returned to bless his own house; and Michal daughter of Saul came out to meet David and said: "How honored today was the king of Israel — uncovering himself today before the eyes of his servants' maidservants, as one of the empty fellows shamelessly uncovers himself!"
And David said to Michal: "Before YHWH — who chose me over your father and over all his house, to appoint me leader over the people of YHWH, over Israel — before YHWH I will make merry!
And I will make myself lighter yet than this, and be lowly in my own eyes; but with the maidservants you spoke of — with them I shall be honored."
And Michal daughter of Saul had no child to the day of her death.

2sa.7.text: And it came to pass, when the king was settled in his house, and YHWH had given him rest all around from all his enemies,
that the king said to Nathan the prophet: "See now — I am living in a house of cedar, and the ark of God is living inside a curtain."
And Nathan said to the king: "All that is in your heart — go, do; for YHWH is with you."
But it came to pass that night that the word of YHWH came to Nathan:
"Go, and say to my servant, to David: Thus says YHWH: Shall you build me a house for me to live in?
For I have not lived in a house from the day I brought the children of Israel up from Egypt to this day — I have been walking about in a tent, in a dwelling.

In all my walking about among all the children of Israel, did I speak a word with any one of the tribes of Israel whom I commanded to shepherd my people Israel: 'Why have you not built me a house of cedar?'
So now — thus shall you say to my servant, to David: Thus says YHWH of Armies: I took you from the pasture, from behind the flock, to be leader over my people, over Israel;
and I have been with you everywhere you went, and I have cut off all your enemies from before you; and I will make you a great name — like the name of the great ones of the earth.
And I will set a place for my people, for Israel, and plant them, and they shall settle in their own place and tremble no more; and the sons of injustice shall not afflict them again as at the first,
since the day I commanded judges over my people Israel. And I will give you rest from all your enemies. And YHWH declares to you: YHWH himself will make you a house.
When your days are full and you lie down with your fathers, I will raise up your seed after you — one who comes out of your own body — and I will establish his kingdom.
He shall build a house for my name; and I will establish the throne of his kingdom to the age.
I will be a father to him, and he shall be a son to me. When he goes crooked, I will correct him with the rod of men, and with the blows of the sons of humankind;
but my steadfast love shall not turn aside from him, as I turned it aside from Saul, whom I turned aside from before you.
And your house and your kingdom shall stand faithful to the age before you; your throne shall be established to the age."
According to all these words, and according to all this vision — so Nathan spoke to David.
And King David came in and sat before YHWH, and said: "Who am I, Lord YHWH — and what is my house — that you have brought me this far?
And even this was small in your eyes, Lord YHWH — and you have spoken also of your servant's house far into the distance. And is this the teaching for humankind, Lord YHWH?
And what more can David add, to speak to you? You yourself know your servant, Lord YHWH.
For the sake of your word, and according to your own heart, you have done all this greatness — to make it known to your servant.
Therefore you are great, Lord YHWH — for there is none like you, and there is no God besides you, according to all we have heard with our ears.

And who is like your people, like Israel — one nation on earth whom God went to ransom for himself as a people, and to make himself a name, and to do for you the greatness, and awesome things for your land — before your people, whom you ransomed for yourself from Egypt, from nations and their gods?
And you established for yourself your people Israel as your own people, to the age; and you, YHWH, became their God.
So now, YHWH God — the word you have spoken concerning your servant and concerning his house: raise it up, to the age; and do just as you have spoken.
And may your name be great to the age, so that it is said: 'YHWH of Armies is God over Israel' — and may the house of your servant David stand established before you.
For you, YHWH of Armies, God of Israel, have uncovered the ear of your servant: 'I will build you a house.' Therefore your servant has found his heart, to pray this prayer to you.
And now, Lord YHWH — you are God, and your words shall be truth; and you have spoken this good thing to your servant.
So now — be willing, and bless the house of your servant, to be before you to the age; for you, Lord YHWH, have spoken. And from your blessing, may the house of your servant be blessed to the age."

2sa.8.text: And it came to pass afterward that David struck the Philistines and subdued them; and David took Metheg-ammah out of the hand of the Philistines.

And he struck Moab, and measured them off with a line, making them lie down on the ground: he measured two lines to be put to death, and one full line to be kept alive. And Moab became servants to David, carriers of tribute.
And David struck Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah, as he went to restore his hand at the River;
and David captured from him one thousand seven hundred horsemen and twenty thousand foot soldiers; and David hamstrung all the chariot horses, keeping back of them a hundred chariot teams.
And Aram of Damascus came to help Hadadezer king of Zobah; and David struck down among Aram twenty-two thousand men.
And David set garrisons in Aram of Damascus, and Aram became servants to David, carriers of tribute. And YHWH saved David everywhere he went.
And David took the shields of gold that had belonged to the servants of Hadadezer and brought them to Jerusalem;
and from Betah and from Berothai, cities of Hadadezer, King David took bronze in great abundance.
And Toi king of Hamath heard that David had struck the whole army of Hadadezer;

and Toi sent Joram his son to King David, to ask him of his welfare and to bless him, because he had fought against Hadadezer and struck him — for Hadadezer had been a man of wars against Toi. And in his hand were objects of silver and objects of gold and objects of bronze;
these too King David consecrated to YHWH, with the silver and the gold he had consecrated from all the nations he subdued:
from Aram and from Moab and from the sons of Ammon and from the Philistines and from Amalek — and from the spoil of Hadadezer son of Rehob, king of Zobah.
And David made a name for himself when he returned from striking Aram in the Valley of Salt — eighteen thousand.
And he set garrisons in Edom — in all Edom he set garrisons — and all Edom became servants to David. And YHWH saved David everywhere he went.
And David reigned over all Israel; and David kept doing justice and righteousness for all his people.
And Joab son of Zeruiah was over the army; and Jehoshaphat son of Ahilud was recorder;
and Zadok son of Ahitub, and Ahimelech son of Abiathar, were priests; and Seraiah was scribe;
and Benaiah son of Jehoiada was over the Cherethites and the Pelethites; and David's sons were priests.

2sa.9.text: And David said: "Is there still anyone left of the house of Saul, that I may show him steadfast love for Jonathan's sake?"
Now the house of Saul had a servant whose name was Ziba; and they called him to David, and the king said to him: "Are you Ziba?" And he said: "Your servant."
And the king said: "Is there no one still of the house of Saul, that I may show him the steadfast love of God?" And Ziba said to the king: "There is still a son of Jonathan — crippled in his feet."
And the king said to him: "Where is he?" And Ziba said to the king: "Behold — he is in the house of Machir son of Ammiel, at Lo-debar."
And King David sent and took him from the house of Machir son of Ammiel, from Lo-debar.
And Mephibosheth son of Jonathan, son of Saul, came to David, and fell on his face and prostrated himself. And David said: "Mephibosheth!" And he said: "Behold your servant."

And David said to him: "Do not fear — for I will surely show you steadfast love for the sake of Jonathan your father; and I will restore to you all the fields of Saul your father, and you shall eat bread at my table always."
And he prostrated himself and said: "What is your servant — that you have turned toward a dead dog like me?"
And the king called Ziba, Saul's steward, and said to him: "All that belonged to Saul and to all his house I have given to your master's son.

And you shall work the ground for him — you and your sons and your servants — and bring in the harvest, that your master's son may have bread to eat; but Mephibosheth, your master's son, shall eat bread at my table always." Now Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants.

And Ziba said to the king: "According to all my lord the king commands his servant — so shall your servant do." "And Mephibosheth" — said the king — "shall eat at my table like one of the king's sons."
And Mephibosheth had a young son whose name was Mica; and all who lived in Ziba's house were servants to Mephibosheth.
And Mephibosheth lived in Jerusalem — for he ate always at the king's table; and he was lame in both his feet.

2sa.10.text: And it came to pass afterward that the king of the sons of Ammon died, and Hanun his son became king in his place.

And David said: "I will show steadfast love to Hanun son of Nahash, as his father showed steadfast love to me." So David sent by the hand of his servants to console him concerning his father; and David's servants came into the land of the sons of Ammon.

But the commanders of the sons of Ammon said to Hanun their lord: "Is David honoring your father in your eyes, that he has sent you consolers? Is it not in order to search out the city, and to spy it out, and to overthrow it, that David has sent his servants to you?"
So Hanun took David's servants and shaved off half of each man's beard, and cut off their garments at the middle, up to their buttocks — and sent them away.
And they told David, and he sent to meet them — for the men were deeply humiliated; and the king said: "Stay at Jericho until your beards grow — then return."

And the sons of Ammon saw that they had made themselves a stench to David; and the sons of Ammon sent and hired Aram of Beth-rehob and Aram of Zobah — twenty thousand foot soldiers — and the king of Maacah with a thousand men, and the men of Tob, twelve thousand men.
And David heard — and he sent Joab, and the whole army: the mighty men.
And the sons of Ammon came out and drew up battle order at the opening of the gate — with Aram of Zobah and Rehob, and the men of Tob and Maacah, by themselves in the open field.
And Joab saw that the face of battle was against him from front and rear; and he chose from all the choicest of Israel and drew up to meet Aram,
and the rest of the people he gave into the hand of Abishai his brother, who drew up to meet the sons of Ammon.
And he said: "If Aram is too strong for me — you shall be my deliverance; and if the sons of Ammon are too strong for you — I will come to deliver you.
Be strong! And let us make ourselves strong for our people and for the cities of our God — and may YHWH do what is good in his eyes."
And Joab drew near, with the people who were with him, to battle against Aram — and they fled before him;
and the sons of Ammon saw that Aram had fled, and they fled before Abishai and went into the city. So Joab turned back from the sons of Ammon and came to Jerusalem.
And Aram saw that it was struck down before Israel — and they gathered together;
and Hadadezer sent and brought out the Aram that was beyond the River, and they came to Helam — with Shobach, commander of Hadadezer's army, at their head.
And it was told to David; and he gathered all Israel and crossed the Jordan and came to Helam; and Aram drew up to meet David — and fought with him.
And Aram fled before Israel; and David killed of Aram seven hundred chariot teams and forty thousand horsemen; and Shobach the commander of their army he struck — and he died there.
And all the kings, servants of Hadadezer, saw that they were struck down before Israel; and they made peace with Israel and served them. And Aram was afraid to save the sons of Ammon any more.

2sa.11.text: And it came to pass at the turn of the year, at the time when kings go out, that David sent Joab, and his servants with him, and all Israel; and they ravaged the sons of Ammon and besieged Rabbah. But David stayed in Jerusalem.

And it came to pass at the time of evening that David rose from his bed and walked about on the roof of the king's house; and from the roof he saw a woman bathing — and the woman was very good to look at.
And David sent and inquired about the woman; and one said: "Is this not Bathsheba daughter of Eliam — the wife of Uriah the Hittite?"
And David sent messengers and took her; and she came to him, and he lay with her — she having just purified herself from her uncleanness — and she returned to her house.
And the woman conceived; and she sent and told David, and said: "I am pregnant."
And David sent to Joab: "Send me Uriah the Hittite." And Joab sent Uriah to David.
And Uriah came to him; and David asked how Joab was, and how the people were, and how the war was going.
And David said to Uriah: "Go down to your house — and wash your feet." And Uriah went out of the king's house, and a gift from the king went out after him.
But Uriah lay down at the opening of the king's house, with all the servants of his lord — and did not go down to his house.
And they told David: "Uriah did not go down to his house." And David said to Uriah: "Have you not come from a journey? Why did you not go down to your house?"

And Uriah said to David: "The ark, and Israel, and Judah are staying in shelters; and my lord Joab, and the servants of my lord, are camping in the open field — and shall I go to my house to eat and to drink and to lie with my wife? By your life, and by the life of your soul — I will not do this thing!"
And David said to Uriah: "Stay here today also — and tomorrow I will send you off." So Uriah stayed in Jerusalem that day and the next;
and David invited him, and he ate before him and drank — and he made him drunk. And he went out in the evening to lie on his bed with the servants of his lord; and to his house he did not go down.
And it came to pass in the morning that David wrote a letter to Joab, and sent it by the hand of Uriah.
And he wrote in the letter: "Set Uriah at the front, facing the hardest fighting; then draw back from behind him — that he may be struck down, and die."
And it came to pass, as Joab kept watch on the city, that he put Uriah at the place where he knew there were men of valor;
and the men of the city came out and fought with Joab, and some of the people — of David's servants — fell; and Uriah the Hittite died too.
And Joab sent and told David all the details of the battle;
and he commanded the messenger: "When you have finished telling the king all the details of the battle,
then, if the king's rage rises and he says to you: 'Why did you go so near the city to fight? Did you not know they would shoot from the wall?

Who struck down Abimelech son of Jerubbesheth? Did not a woman throw an upper millstone on him from the wall, so that he died at Thebez? Why did you go so near the wall?' — then say: 'Your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead too.'"
And the messenger went, and came and told David all that Joab had sent him to say.
And the messenger said to David: "The men overpowered us and came out against us into the open field; and we drove them back to the opening of the gate.
And the archers shot at your servants from the wall, and some of the king's servants died; and your servant Uriah the Hittite is dead too."

And David said to the messenger: "Thus shall you say to Joab: 'Do not let this thing be evil in your eyes — for the sword devours now one, now another. Strengthen your battle against the city, and tear it down.' And so encourage him."
And the wife of Uriah heard that Uriah her husband was dead; and she lamented over her husband.
And when the mourning was past, David sent and gathered her into his house, and she became his wife, and bore him a son. But the thing David had done was evil in the eyes of YHWH.

2sa.12.text: And YHWH sent Nathan to David. And he came to him and said to him: "There were two men in one city — one rich, and one poor.
The rich man had flocks and herds in great abundance;

but the poor man had nothing at all — except one small ewe lamb he had bought. And he kept her alive, and she grew up with him and with his sons, together: she would eat from his morsel and drink from his cup and lie in his lap — and she was like a daughter to him.

And a traveler came to the rich man; and he spared taking from his own flock or his own herd to prepare for the wayfarer who had come to him — and he took the poor man's ewe lamb, and prepared her for the man who had come to him."
And David's anger burned exceedingly against the man, and he said to Nathan: "As YHWH lives — the man who did this is a son of death!
And the lamb he shall repay fourfold — because he did this thing, and because he had no pity."
And Nathan said to David: "You are the man! Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: I anointed you king over Israel, and I rescued you from the hand of Saul;

and I gave you your lord's house, and your lord's wives into your lap; and I gave you the house of Israel and Judah — and if that were too little, I would add for you as much again, and as much again.

Why have you despised the word of YHWH, doing the evil in his eyes? Uriah the Hittite you struck with the sword, and his wife you took for yourself as a wife — him you killed with the sword of the sons of Ammon.
So now — the sword shall not turn aside from your house, to the age; because you despised me and took the wife of Uriah the Hittite to be your wife.

Thus says YHWH: Behold — I am raising up evil against you out of your own house; and I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to your fellow, and he shall lie with your wives in the sight of this sun.
For you acted in secret — but I will do this thing before all Israel, and before the sun."
And David said to Nathan: "I have sinned against YHWH." And Nathan said to David: "YHWH also has carried your sin over — you shall not die.
Only — because in this thing you have utterly scorned the enemies of YHWH, the son born to you shall surely die."
And Nathan went to his house. And YHWH struck the child that Uriah's wife had borne to David — and he fell gravely ill.
And David sought God for the boy; and David fasted a fast, and went in and spent the night lying on the ground.
And the elders of his house rose over him, to raise him from the ground; but he was not willing, and he would not eat food with them.

And it came to pass on the seventh day that the child died. And David's servants were afraid to tell him that the child was dead — for they said: "Behold, while the child was alive we spoke to him and he did not listen to our voice; how then can we say to him, 'The child is dead'? He may do harm."
And David saw that his servants were whispering, and David understood that the child was dead; and David said to his servants: "Is the child dead?" And they said: "He is dead."

And David rose from the ground, and washed and anointed himself and changed his garments, and came into the house of YHWH and worshiped; then he came to his own house, and asked, and they set food before him, and he ate.
And his servants said to him: "What is this thing you have done? For the living child you fasted and wept — and when the child died, you rose and ate food."
And he said: "While the child was still alive, I fasted and wept — for I said: Who knows? YHWH may be gracious to me, and the child may live.
But now he is dead — why should I fast? Can I bring him back again? I am going to him — but he will not return to me."
And David comforted Bathsheba his wife; and he went in to her and lay with her, and she bore a son, and he called his name Solomon. And YHWH loved him,
and sent word by the hand of Nathan the prophet; and he called his name Jedidiah — Beloved-of-YHWH — for YHWH's sake.
And Joab fought against Rabbah of the sons of Ammon, and captured the royal city.
And Joab sent messengers to David and said: "I have fought against Rabbah; I have also captured the city of waters.
So now — gather the rest of the people and camp against the city and capture it; lest I capture the city myself, and my name be called over it."
And David gathered all the people and went to Rabbah, and fought against it and captured it.
And he took the crown of their king from his head — its weight a talent of gold, with a precious stone — and it was set on David's head; and he brought out the spoil of the city in great abundance.

And the people who were in it he brought out, and set them to the saws and the iron picks and the iron axes, and made them cross over to the brick-kilns; and so he did to all the cities of the sons of Ammon. And David and all the people returned to Jerusalem.

2sa.14.text: And Joab son of Zeruiah knew that the king's heart was toward Absalom.

And Joab sent to Tekoa and took from there a wise woman; and he said to her: "Put on mourning, please, and dress, please, in garments of mourning, and do not anoint yourself with oil; and be like a woman who has been mourning many days over the dead.
And go to the king, and speak to him according to this word." And Joab put the words in her mouth.
And the woman of Tekoa spoke to the king: she fell on her face to the ground and prostrated herself, and said: "Save, O king!"
And the king said to her: "What is the matter with you?" And she said: "Truly — I am a widow woman; my husband is dead."
And your servant had two sons; and the two of them fought in the field, with no one to part them — and the one struck the other, and killed him.

And behold — the whole clan has risen against your servant, and they say: 'Hand over the one who struck his brother, that we may put him to death for the life of his brother whom he killed — and destroy the heir as well.' So they would quench my last coal that is left, leaving my husband neither name nor remnant on the face of the ground."
And the king said to the woman: "Go to your house — and I myself will give command concerning you."
And the woman of Tekoa said to the king: "On me, my lord the king, be the guilt — and on my father's house; and the king and his throne be innocent."
And the king said: "Whoever speaks against you — bring him to me, and he shall not touch you again."

And she said: "Let the king remember, please, YHWH your God, so that the redeemer of blood not multiply destruction, and they not destroy my son." And he said: "As YHWH lives — not a hair of your son shall fall to the ground."
And the woman said: "Let your servant speak, please, a word to my lord the king." And he said: "Speak."

And the woman said: "Then why have you devised such a thing against the people of God? For in speaking this word the king is like one who is guilty — since the king does not bring back his own banished one.

For we surely die, and are like water poured out on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again; and God does not carry off a life — but he devises devisings, so that a banished one not stay banished from him.

And now — the reason I came to speak this word to the king my lord is that the people made me afraid; and your servant said: 'Let me speak, please, to the king. Perhaps the king will act on the word of his maidservant;
for the king will listen, to rescue his maidservant from the palm of the man who would destroy me and my son together, out of the inheritance of God.'
And your servant said: 'May the word of my lord the king bring rest' — for like an angel of God, so is my lord the king, to hear the good and the evil. And may YHWH your God be with you."
And the king answered and said to the woman: "Do not hide from me, please, anything I ask you." And the woman said: "Let my lord the king speak."

And the king said: "Is the hand of Joab with you in all this?" And the woman answered and said: "By the life of your soul, my lord the king — no one can turn right or left from anything my lord the king has spoken. Yes — it was your servant Joab who commanded me, and it was he who put all these words in the mouth of your servant.
In order to turn the face of the matter around, your servant Joab did this thing. But my lord is wise, with the wisdom of an angel of God — knowing all that is in the land."
And the king said to Joab: "Behold now — I have done this thing. Go, bring back the young man Absalom."

And Joab fell on his face to the ground and prostrated himself and blessed the king; and Joab said: "Today your servant knows that I have found favor in your eyes, my lord the king — for the king has acted on the word of his servant."
And Joab rose and went to Geshur — and brought Absalom to Jerusalem.
And the king said: "Let him turn aside to his own house; my face he shall not see." So Absalom turned aside to his own house — and the face of the king he did not see.
Now like Absalom there was no man so beautiful in all Israel, so greatly to be praised: from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him.

And when he shaved his head — for it was at the end of every year that he shaved it, because it was heavy on him, so he shaved it — he would weigh the hair of his head: two hundred shekels by the king's weight.
And to Absalom were born three sons, and one daughter whose name was Tamar; she was a woman beautiful to look at.
And Absalom lived in Jerusalem two full years — and the face of the king he did not see.
And Absalom sent for Joab, to send him to the king; but he was not willing to come to him. And he sent again, a second time — and he was not willing to come.
So he said to his servants: "See Joab's plot of ground beside mine — he has barley there. Go, set it on fire." And Absalom's servants set the plot on fire.
And Joab rose and came to Absalom, to his house, and said to him: "Why have your servants set my plot on fire?"

And Absalom said to Joab: "Behold — I sent to you, saying: 'Come here, that I may send you to the king to say: Why did I come from Geshur? It would be better for me if I were still there.' So now — let me see the king's face; and if there is guilt in me, let him put me to death."
And Joab came to the king and told him. And he called Absalom, and he came to the king and prostrated himself, face to the ground, before the king — and the king kissed Absalom.

2sa.15.text: And it came to pass after this that Absalom got himself a chariot and horses, and fifty men to run before him.

And Absalom would rise early and stand beside the road to the gate; and whenever any man had a dispute to bring to the king for judgment, Absalom would call to him and say: "From which city are you?" And he would say: "From one of the tribes of Israel is your servant."
And Absalom would say to him: "See — your claims are good and right; but there is no one from the king to hear you."
And Absalom would say: "If only someone would set me as judge in the land — then every man with a dispute or a case could come to me, and I would give him justice."
And whenever a man drew near to bow down to him, he would stretch out his hand and take hold of him and kiss him.
And Absalom acted this way toward all Israel who came to the king for judgment — and Absalom stole the heart of the men of Israel.
And it came to pass at the end of forty years that Absalom said to the king: "Let me go, please, and pay my vow that I vowed to YHWH, at Hebron.
For your servant vowed a vow while I stayed at Geshur in Aram: 'If YHWH indeed brings me back to Jerusalem — then I will serve YHWH.'"
And the king said to him: "Go in peace." And he rose and went to Hebron.
But Absalom sent agents through all the tribes of Israel: "When you hear the sound of the shofar, then say: 'Absalom has become king at Hebron!'"
And with Absalom went two hundred men from Jerusalem — invited guests, going in their innocence; they knew nothing at all.

And Absalom sent for Ahithophel the Gilonite, David's counselor, from his city, from Giloh, while he was offering the sacrifices. And the conspiracy grew strong — and the people with Absalom kept increasing.
And the informer came to David: "The heart of the men of Israel has gone after Absalom."

And David said to all his servants who were with him in Jerusalem: "Rise — let us flee, for there will be no escape for us from Absalom. Hurry and go — lest he hurry and overtake us, and bring the evil down on us, and strike the city with the mouth of the sword."
And the king's servants said to the king: "According to all that my lord the king chooses — behold, your servants."
And the king went out, and all his house at his feet; and the king left ten women — concubines — to keep the house.
And the king went out, and all the people at his feet; and they halted at the Far House.

And all his servants were crossing at his side — all the Cherethites and all the Pelethites; and all the Gittites, six hundred men who had come at his feet from Gath, crossing before the face of the king.
And the king said to Ittai the Gittite: "Why should you go with us — you too? Return and stay with the king; for you are a foreigner, and an exile besides, far from your own place.
Only yesterday you came — and today shall I make you wander with us in our going, when I am going wherever I am going? Return, and take your brothers back with you — in steadfast love and truth."
And Ittai answered the king and said: "As YHWH lives, and as my lord the king lives — in whatever place my lord the king is, whether for death or for life, there your servant will be."
And David said to Ittai: "Go — and cross over." And Ittai the Gittite crossed over, with all his men and all the little ones with him.
And the whole land was weeping with a great voice as all the people crossed; and the king crossed the wadi Kidron, and all the people crossed, on the road toward the wilderness.

And behold — Zadok too, and all the Levites with him, carrying the ark of the covenant of God; and they set down the ark of God, and Abiathar went up, until all the people had finished crossing out of the city.
And the king said to Zadok: "Return the ark of God to the city. If I find favor in the eyes of YHWH, he will bring me back and let me see it — and its dwelling place.
But if he says this: 'I take no delight in you' — here I am; let him do to me what is good in his eyes."
And the king said to Zadok the priest: "Are you a seer? Return to the city in peace — with Ahimaaz your son, and Jonathan son of Abiathar: your two sons with you.
See — I will linger at the fords of the wilderness until word comes from you to inform me."
So Zadok and Abiathar returned the ark of God to Jerusalem — and they stayed there.
And David was going up the Ascent of the Olives — going up weeping, his head covered, and walking barefoot; and all the people with him covered each his head, and went up, going up and weeping.
And David was told: "Ahithophel is among the conspirators with Absalom." And David said: "Turn the counsel of Ahithophel to foolishness, please, YHWH!"
And it came to pass, as David came to the summit where one bows down to God — behold: Hushai the Archite coming to meet him, his tunic torn and soil on his head.
And David said to him: "If you cross over with me, you will be a burden on me;

but if you return to the city and say to Absalom: 'Your servant I will be, O king — your father's servant I was before, and now I am your servant' — then you can break the counsel of Ahithophel for me.
And are not Zadok and Abiathar the priests there with you? And it shall be: every word you hear from the king's house, you shall tell to Zadok and Abiathar the priests.
Behold — their two sons are there with them: Ahimaaz for Zadok, and Jonathan for Abiathar; and by their hand you shall send me every word you hear."
So Hushai, David's friend, came into the city — as Absalom was entering Jerusalem.

2sa.16.text: And David had passed a little beyond the summit — and behold: Ziba, the servant of Mephibosheth, to meet him, with a pair of saddled donkeys, and on them two hundred loaves of bread and a hundred clusters of raisins and a hundred summer fruits and a skin of wine.

And the king said to Ziba: "What are these for?" And Ziba said: "The donkeys are for the king's household to ride; and the bread and the summer fruit for the young men to eat; and the wine for the weary to drink in the wilderness."
And the king said: "And where is your master's son?" And Ziba said to the king: "Behold — he is staying in Jerusalem; for he said: 'Today the house of Israel will give me back my father's kingdom.'"
And the king said to Ziba: "Behold — all that belongs to Mephibosheth is yours." And Ziba said: "I bow down. May I find favor in your eyes, my lord the king."
And King David came as far as Bahurim — and behold: from there a man coming out, of the clan of the house of Saul, and his name was Shimei son of Gera — coming out, and cursing as he came;
and he pelted David with stones, and all the servants of King David — though all the people and all the mighty men were at his right and at his left.
And thus said Shimei as he cursed: "Out! Out! Man of blood! Man of worthlessness!

YHWH has returned on you all the blood of the house of Saul, in whose place you became king; and YHWH has given the kingship into the hand of Absalom your son. And behold — you are caught in your own evil, for you are a man of blood!"
And Abishai son of Zeruiah said to the king: "Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me cross over, please, and take off his head."
But the king said: "What is there between me and you, sons of Zeruiah? If he curses — and if YHWH has said to him, 'Curse David' — then who shall say, 'Why have you done so?'"

And David said to Abishai and to all his servants: "Behold — my own son, who came out of my body, is seeking my life; how much more now this Benjaminite! Leave him alone, and let him curse — for YHWH has told him to.
Perhaps YHWH will look on my affliction, and YHWH will return me good in place of his cursing this day."
And David and his men walked on the road, with Shimei walking along the flank of the hill opposite him — walking and cursing, and pelting stones opposite him, and flinging dust.
And the king, and all the people with him, arrived weary — and he refreshed himself there.
And Absalom, and all the people — the men of Israel — came to Jerusalem; and Ahithophel with him.
And it came to pass, when Hushai the Archite, David's friend, came to Absalom, that Hushai said to Absalom: "Long live the king! Long live the king!"
And Absalom said to Hushai: "Is this your steadfast love for your friend? Why did you not go with your friend?"
And Hushai said to Absalom: "No — for the one whom YHWH has chosen, and this people, and all the men of Israel — his I will be, and with him I will stay.
And second: whom should I serve? Is it not before his son? As I served before your father, so will I be before you."
And Absalom said to Ahithophel: "Give your counsel: what shall we do?"

And Ahithophel said to Absalom: "Go in to your father's concubines, whom he left to keep the house; and all Israel will hear that you have made yourself a stench to your father — and the hands of all who are with you will be strengthened."
So they pitched the tent for Absalom on the roof; and Absalom went in to his father's concubines before the eyes of all Israel.
Now the counsel of Ahithophel, which he counseled in those days, was as if one inquired of the word of God — such was all the counsel of Ahithophel, both to David and to Absalom.

2sa.17.text: And Ahithophel said to Absalom: "Let me choose, please, twelve thousand men, and let me rise and pursue after David tonight;
and I will come upon him while he is weary and slack of hands, and I will terrify him — and all the people with him will flee; and I will strike down the king alone.
And I will bring all the people back to you — as when all return, only the man you are seeking; all the people will be at peace."
And the word was right in the eyes of Absalom, and in the eyes of all the elders of Israel.
But Absalom said: "Call, please, Hushai the Archite too — and let us hear what is in his mouth, he also."
And Hushai came to Absalom; and Absalom spoke to him: "According to this word Ahithophel has spoken. Shall we act on his word? If not — you speak."
And Hushai said to Absalom: "The counsel Ahithophel has counseled is not good this time."

And Hushai said: "You know your father and his men — that they are mighty men, and bitter of soul, like a bear in the field robbed of her cubs; and your father is a man of war, and he will not spend the night with the people.

Behold — even now he is hidden in one of the pits, or in one of the places; and it shall be, when some fall at the first, that whoever hears will say: 'There is a slaughter among the people following Absalom!'
And then even the son of valor, whose heart is like the heart of a lion, will utterly melt — for all Israel knows that your father is a mighty man, and sons of valor are with him.
Rather, I counsel: let all Israel be surely gathered to you, from Dan to Beersheba, like the sand by the sea in multitude — with your own face going into battle.
And we shall come upon him in one of the places where he is found, and we shall settle on him as the dew falls on the ground — and of him, and of all the men with him, not even one will be left.
And if he withdraws into a city, then all Israel will bring ropes to that city — and we shall drag it down to the wadi, until not even a pebble is found there."

And Absalom and all the men of Israel said: "The counsel of Hushai the Archite is better than the counsel of Ahithophel." For YHWH had ordained to break the good counsel of Ahithophel — so that YHWH might bring the evil on Absalom.
And Hushai said to Zadok and to Abiathar, the priests: "Thus and so Ahithophel counseled Absalom and the elders of Israel; and thus and so I have counseled.
So now — send quickly and tell David: 'Do not spend the night at the fords of the wilderness. You must surely cross over — lest the king be swallowed up, and all the people with him.'"
Now Jonathan and Ahimaaz were standing at En-rogel, and a servant girl would go and tell them, and they would go and tell King David — for they could not be seen entering the city.
But a boy saw them and told Absalom. So the two of them went quickly and came to the house of a man in Bahurim who had a well in his courtyard — and they went down into it;
and the woman took the covering and spread it over the mouth of the well and scattered crushed grain on it — and nothing was known.

And Absalom's servants came to the woman at the house and said: "Where are Ahimaaz and Jonathan?" And the woman said to them: "They crossed over the brook of water." And they searched and did not find them — and returned to Jerusalem.

And it came to pass, after they had gone, that the two came up from the well, and went and told King David; and they said to David: "Rise — and cross the water quickly; for thus has Ahithophel counseled against you."
And David rose, and all the people with him, and they crossed the Jordan; by morning light not even one was missing who had not crossed the Jordan.

And Ahithophel saw that his counsel had not been acted on; and he saddled his donkey and rose and went to his house, to his city — and set his house in order, and hanged himself. And he died, and was buried in his father's tomb.
And David came to Mahanaim; and Absalom crossed the Jordan, he and all the men of Israel with him.

And Absalom had set Amasa over the army in place of Joab. Now Amasa was the son of a man whose name was Ithra the Israelite, who had gone in to Abigal daughter of Nahash, sister of Zeruiah, Joab's mother.
And Israel and Absalom camped in the land of Gilead.
And it came to pass, when David came to Mahanaim, that Shobi son of Nahash, from Rabbah of the sons of Ammon, and Machir son of Ammiel, from Lo-debar, and Barzillai the Gileadite, from Rogelim,
brought bedding and basins and potter's vessels, and wheat and barley and flour and roasted grain, and beans and lentils and parched seed,
and honey and curds and sheep and cheese of the herd — for David and for the people with him to eat; for they said: "The people are hungry and weary and thirsty in the wilderness."

2sa.18.text: And David mustered the people who were with him, and set over them commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds.

And David sent out the people: a third under the hand of Joab, and a third under the hand of Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab's brother, and a third under the hand of Ittai the Gittite. And the king said to the people: "I too will surely go out with you."

But the people said: "You shall not go out. For if we flee outright, they will not set their heart on us; and if half of us die, they will not set their heart on us — for you are now worth ten thousand of us. And now it is better that you be a help to us from the city."
And the king said to them: "What is good in your eyes, I will do." And the king stood beside the gate, and all the people went out by hundreds and by thousands.
And the king commanded Joab and Abishai and Ittai: "Gently, for my sake, with the young man — with Absalom." And all the people heard when the king gave all the commanders charge concerning Absalom.
And the people went out into the field to meet Israel; and the battle was in the forest of Ephraim.
And the people of Israel were struck down there, before the servants of David; and the slaughter there was great on that day — twenty thousand.
And the battle there was scattered over the face of the whole land; and the forest devoured more of the people that day than the sword devoured.

And Absalom happened to come before the servants of David. Now Absalom was riding on the mule; and the mule came under the tangle of the great terebinth, and his head caught fast in the terebinth, and he was left hanging between the heavens and the earth — while the mule under him passed on.
And one man saw it and told Joab, and said: "Behold — I saw Absalom hanging in the terebinth."
And Joab said to the man telling him: "And behold — you saw! Then why did you not strike him to the ground there? It would have been on me to give you ten pieces of silver and one belt."

And the man said to Joab: "Even if I were weighing out a thousand pieces of silver on my palms, I would not stretch out my hand against the king's son — for in our ears the king commanded you and Abishai and Ittai: 'Keep watch, whoever you are, over the young man — over Absalom.'
Or had I dealt treacherously against his life — and nothing is hidden from the king — you yourself would have stood aloof."
And Joab said: "I will not wait around like this with you." And he took three darts in his palm and thrust them into the heart of Absalom — still alive in the heart of the terebinth;
and ten young men, Joab's armor-bearers, closed in and struck Absalom — and killed him.
And Joab blew the shofar, and the people turned back from pursuing Israel — for Joab held the people back.
And they took Absalom and threw him into the great pit in the forest, and raised over him a very great mound of stones; and all Israel fled, each man to his tents.

Now Absalom, while he lived, had taken and set up for himself the pillar that is in the King's Valley; for he said: "I have no son to keep my name in remembrance." And he called the pillar by his own name; and it is called the Monument of Absalom to this day.
And Ahimaaz son of Zadok said: "Let me run, please, and carry the news to the king — that YHWH has given him justice out of the hand of his enemies."
And Joab said to him: "You are not the man for news today. You shall carry news another day; but today you shall carry no news — because the king's son is dead."
And Joab said to the Cushite: "Go — tell the king what you have seen." And the Cushite bowed to Joab, and ran.

And Ahimaaz son of Zadok said yet again to Joab: "Come what may — let me run too, please, behind the Cushite." And Joab said: "Why should you run, my son, when there is no news to bring you a reward?"
"Come what may — I will run." And he said to him: "Run." And Ahimaaz ran by the road of the plain — and outran the Cushite.
Now David was sitting between the two gates; and the watchman went up to the roof of the gate, onto the wall, and lifted his eyes and looked — and behold: a man running alone.
And the watchman called and told the king. And the king said: "If he is alone — there is news in his mouth." And he came steadily nearer.
And the watchman saw another man running; and the watchman called to the gatekeeper and said: "Behold — a man running alone." And the king said: "This one also is bringing news."
And the watchman said: "I see the running of the first — like the running of Ahimaaz son of Zadok." And the king said: "He is a good man; he comes with good news."

And Ahimaaz called and said to the king: "Peace!" And he bowed to the king, face to the ground, and said: "Blessed be YHWH your God, who has shut up the men who lifted their hand against my lord the king."
And the king said: "Is it peace with the young man — with Absalom?" And Ahimaaz said: "I saw a great commotion when Joab sent the king's servant, and me your servant — and I do not know what it was."
And the king said: "Turn aside; take your stand here." And he turned aside and stood still.
And behold — the Cushite arriving. And the Cushite said: "Let my lord the king receive the news: YHWH has given you justice this day out of the hand of all who rose against you."

And the king said to the Cushite: "Is it peace with the young man — with Absalom?" And the Cushite said: "May the enemies of my lord the king, and all who rise against you for evil, be like that young man."

2sa.19.text: And the king shuddered, and went up to the upper room over the gate and wept; and thus he said as he walked: "My son Absalom! My son, my son Absalom! If only I had died instead of you — Absalom, my son, my son!"
And it was told to Joab: "Behold — the king is weeping; he mourns over Absalom."
And the deliverance that day was turned to mourning for all the people — for the people heard it said that day: "The king is grieved over his son."
And the people stole into the city that day, as people steal in when they are humiliated, fleeing from battle.
And the king covered his face; and the king cried out with a great voice: "My son Absalom! Absalom, my son, my son!"

And Joab came to the king, into the house, and said: "Today you have shamed the faces of all your servants — those who saved your life today, and the lives of your sons and your daughters, and the lives of your wives, and the lives of your concubines —

by loving those who hate you and hating those who love you. For you have declared today that commanders and servants are nothing to you; for I know today that if Absalom were alive and all of us today were dead — then it would be right in your eyes.

So now — rise, go out, and speak to the heart of your servants. For I swear by YHWH: if you do not go out, not a man will stay with you this night — and that will be worse for you than all the evil that has come upon you from your youth until now."
And the king rose and sat in the gate. And they told all the people: "Behold — the king is sitting in the gate." And all the people came before the king. Now Israel had fled, each man to his tents;
